# Thread: Would you eat this?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-04T20:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49402>

I have a freezer in the basement that used to be pretty much dedicated to liver - calf liver, pork liver, lamb's liver, chicken/turkey liver. I've known for a long time the nutritional value of liver, and have always bought it frozen from local farmers raising pastured animals that I was also buying. Trouble is, I really dislike liver, so I didn't keep up with making it. The liver freezer expanded in varieties. I could probably get to optimal 3 times over with the quantities of high quality liver I've collected... Then I started using the liver freezer for my many many ice packs... On 2 separate occasions, the liver freezer got left ajar overnight. The many pounds of pastured liver defrosted, fully, twice.

No I am thinking about all that liver, and how much I need to be eating it. Question is, would you, given it's been fully defrosted on 2 separate occasions?

### ealachan — 2012-09-04T20:41:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49404>

I wouldn't, personally...but what do I know?

edited to add: I wouldn't eat it because it had been thawed completely twice...assuming you don't know how long it had been thawed for, either. In terms of general food safety, I wouldn't touch it other than to throw it out...but I guess you never know unless you try.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-04T21:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49410>

Can't taste worse than Normal. Start mixing up meatloaf and balls that hide taste. iirc, nutrition should be intact.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-04T21:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49413>

I wouldn't be worried about the taste, but about what's in the liver after it defrosted twice.

I wouldn't eat it. But that's me...I wash my fruits and veggies!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-04T21:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49420>

I'm not worried about the taste or the nutrition, just whether or not it's rotting. It tastes bad to me regardless!! Freezer was open for a maximum of 24 hours both times. It would have been many hours before the liver was fully defrosted, and when I got to it, it was still "cold."

### vlynnb — 2012-09-04T22:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49428>

I've read that that if food has thawed it is not safe to refreeze it. Freezing slows down the growth of organisms in the food but does not totally stop the growth and if it is thawed more organisms begin to grow at that time and continue after refreezing. If there are still ice crystals after one thaw it is probably OK to refreeze if it is used a short time later but if there were no ice crystals left at all or it has thawed a second time I wouldn't take the chance, especially if you already have a leaky gut or compromised immune system. Even a healthy person would be taking an unnecessary risk to their health.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49452>

My local buffalo rancher has instructed me several time that it is perfectly okay to defrost meat, cut it into smaller portions, and then refreeze. The thing he told me to watch out for is to not let it get to room temperature. At that point, you need to either cook it or throw it out.

Some sort of raw dog feeder probably wouldn't worry even a bit about this.... so if you need a place to give it away, find a dog person who feeds raw.

Yeah, and the disclaimer is that I just had food poisoning, most likely. So don't listen to me. LOL

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-05T00:25:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49453>

> **Souldanzer;49981 wrote:** My local buffalo rancher has instructed me several time that it is perfectly okay to defrost meat, cut it into smaller portions, and then refreeze. The thing he told me to watch out for is to not let it get to room temperature. At that point, you need to either cook it or throw it out.
>
>
>
> Some sort of raw dog feeder probably wouldn't worry even a bit about this.... so if you need a place to give it away, find a dog person who feeds raw.
>
>
>
> Yeah, and the disclaimer is that I just had food poisoning, most likely. So don't listen to me. LOL

Food poisoning... The closest thing I've experienced in life to death... Hope you are feeling better! I think I will just throw it all out, remembering the hours spent on the floor in the bathroom years ago.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:27:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49454>

> **bigknitwit;49982 wrote:** Food poisoning... The closest thing I've experienced in life to death... Hope you are feeling better! I think I will just throw it all out, remembering the hours spent on the floor in the bathroom years ago.

somehow I didn't come up with sleeping on the bathroom floor.... that would've been a good idea. Seriously

### nonchalant — 2012-09-05T00:33:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49459>

I remember once DH and I got sick at the same time from food poisoning. Really bad. Heard the toddlers knock over the kitchen trash can, but didn't hear any glass break, and couldn't smell fire, so we didn't move to investigate. We were incapacitated for a good long while.

On the other hand, I once accidently left liver out overnight, and still the kitty ate it over the course of several days. She started balking after a while though, so I ended up tossing the rest. She's a pretty good judge of what's edible and what isn't.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T03:55:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-you-eat-this.3453/post-49669>

If it really was still cold, you are probably ok. Why not cook the living daylights out of a bit of it and try it? I would, but then I think we are all too concerned about bacteria.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cytomel.3454/post-49409>

My most recent thyroid labs (8/7/12) were as follows:

TSH â€“ 2.85 uIU/mL (0.50-4.50)

T4, Free â€“ 1.4 ng/dL (0.8-1.8)

T3, Free â€“ 2.8 pg/mL (2.0-4.9)

Reverse T3 â€“ 22 ng/dL (11-32)

My doctor prescribed Cytomel, 5 mcg. 2 x daily.

Does this sound reasonable?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-05T00:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cytomel.3454/post-49476>

Sounds reasonable to me! I am only a Hashis patient. Your rT3 ratio ([http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)) is 12.7 which means you have a rT3 issue going on which the Cytomel will help. Also you FT3 is way too low. It should be at the top of the range. But the big question you should ask yourself and your doc is what is causing this rT3 issue. Maybe adrenals dysfunction or iron out of whack or ????

The dose is small but is a good place to start. If you need more info read on the website that has the rT3 ratio calculator. Lots there based on patient experience.

# Thread: Soft food for a tooth extraction

### indigogirl — 2012-09-04T21:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49417>

I am having an infected root canal gone bad molar extracted tomorrow. Just learned today it is coming out tomorrow.

Not much time to plan and make soft food for the next few days.

I probably have candida and Hashis and am having a hard time figuring out what to prepare other than broths.

PurÃ©ed meat? Yuck!

Soft fish will be easier to chew (salmon, cod, etc.)

Any suggestions?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T00:32:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49457>

Sorry Indigo. Did this just go bad.... I remember you saying it was all over?

Soft foods..... maybe avocados blended? I like them with a bit of cinnamon and stevia.

Fish smoothies

I like pureed vegetable soups, I guess you could throw some meat in there if you wanted. Protein is hard to come by in soft forms.

Hopefully someone else will have better ideas.

The good thing is that tomorrow you will be on the mend

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-05T00:38:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49463>

I just had oral surgery three months ago. Two weeks ago I somehow got an infection in that same hole. I could only eat soft unchewable foods. Tried a smoothie - made me sick to my stomach. Here are my ideas:

avocado

cubes of fish or small scallops and just swallow (did this a lot)

unflavored good quality gelatin (I use Great Lakes)

bullet-proof broth

avocado soup - just found a recipe online

cauliflower soup - same as above - just pureed veggies like Soul suggested with layers of flavors

### indigogirl — 2012-09-05T00:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49469>

Thanks Pattycakes and Soul!

Soul- he finished the root canal a week ago and it was infected again within 2 days (now on the other side of the gum). Another round of antibiotics later I decided I needed another pair of eyes to see it. Was referred to a periodontist this AM by my dentist and he felt there is a hairline fracture and the tooth has to go. I've had this infection now for about 6 weeks and who knows how much longer as a low grade infection so I am actually glad to be rid of the tooth. Next step implant.

### AKMan — 2012-09-06T03:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49648>

You should try this! It would definitely be a great way to keep your teeth clean while you are healing without chemicals or having to brush/floss where the work was done. I've beed oil pulling for over a year now, every day, 15-20 minutes, using pure sesame oil. Try coconut oil if you like, but sesame is the original, thousand-year-old recipe.

Good luck!

Wonder if this is why I have been so exhausted the last 6 months?

I was adamant about not getting an implant. Doing my due diligence with research. Have a few months to think about it and haven't made up my mind yet.

Just read your blog and glad your tummy has settled down.

Do you believe in muscle testing? You could muscle test the food in fridge before throwing everything out.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-06T18:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soft-food-for-a-tooth-extraction.3457/post-49770>

>

Indigo, I did so much reading on implants in the body after your Ask Jack post.... thank you so much for this. I had no idea. It was my intuition to not get the implant for my PFO and a bit of anecdotal evidence. I kept referencing my various allergies and I was always shot down with titanium is safe and crap..... I'm still not sure how to convince insurance to pay for heart surgery if they can fix it for a fraction of the cost with a stupid implant that will kill me down the road.... but I'm collecting evidence for my case. Thanks for bringing this to my attention.

I was talking to a friend the other day and he said that one of his friends went to Turkey to have a tooth regrown there from stem cells. I haven't followed up on it since I don't really need to regrow a tooth right now (lol, but I broke one years ago in an accident which is holding up so far but you never know so I keep my eyes and ears open) but I guess it was actually not that expensive compared to the work she would have had to pay for in the U.S. Just something to think about

# Thread: vaccines - educate me, please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-09-04T21:34:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49418>

I'm posting this in the kids forum, because I figure the mamas in this group will have the most experience with this kind of thing. FWIW, I will preface by saying that I do not have kids, nor do I plan to have them.

So. What I would like to know is: what's the deal with vaccines? I've heard everything from "OMG, filled with heavy metals, will give your kids autism, don't vaccinate" to "OMG, people who don't vaccinate are idiots, diseases that were basically extinct before have made a come-back because people have stopped getting their kids vaccinated". I believe the one major study that linked vaccines with autism was debunked, right? So are vaccines still bad?

I apologize for my ignorance, but it's something I've never really had to read up on in the past, if you see what I mean.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-04T22:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49422>

I've worked infectious diseases, from the perspective of CDC and DOD. so my opinion is probably a bit skewed. I've also had several adverse event reactions myself (most likely b/c my immune system was in the tank - can we say BAD GUT!)

any way - Vaccines have their place, they provide herd immunity to the entire population, even if there are those who don't choose to vaccinate. the big up roar w/vaccines was the shoddy study by a brit Doc - who lost his license that said vaccines caused ASD. No such link. That said, no vaccine is 100%. Nothing in life is. so yes adverse advents do happen, and can be triggers for problems if the epigenetic switches have already been turned on.

both kids received all their vaccines on a delayed schedule - but that's how our ped does it. he thinks kids get too many vaccines these days - current standard for MMR is 12mth. back when I was a kid - it was 18mth. our ped's office gives MMR at 18mths. We delayed chicken pox on dd to see if we could find it naturally but no such luck.

I understand why so many people are against vaccinations - we haven't seen a major disease epidemic in close to 40 yrs. none of our generation remember polio, or small pox. none of the diseases we face today - are as scary as facing life paralyzed from a virus, or a virus that has an 20% survival rate like small pox, w/o vaccination.

Initially I was against vaccination until I read the book Demon in the Freezer. its a big wake up call. it lays out why vaccines are important, and what benefits they bring to the table.

now - flu shots - I've never had luck w/them. after reading the BG series, as a family we're skipping them, and we'll continue to do so in the future.

### ealachan — 2012-09-04T22:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49427>

I remember hearing about the shoddy study that was debunked later on, yes.

I guess what I have a hard time with is people who view the situation in terms of absolute black and white. I'm guessing it's likely easier to see abosolutes (to a certain degree, anyway) when one is suddenly responsible for a child's life as well as one's own...but still. It just makes me wonder.

Incidentally, when I was a kid (which, I'm 32, so not THAT long ago), they didn't vaccinate for chicken pox...at least where I grew up, anyway. Aside from saving your kid from a week of relative discomfort, what's the benefit to vaccinating for chicken pox? It can't actually make you terribly ill unless you get it as an infant, correct? Polio, small pox, serious diseases I can certainly see the value in vaccinating for...but chicken pox?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-04T23:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49437>

I think there are several variables here and this is a very complex issue in which context is, again, important.

1) The biggest worry in vaccines isn't the idea or process of vaccination but the adjuvants that are added... heavy metals, formaldehyde, etc. These things preserve vaccination material but are not necessary (in other words other preservatives could be used). However they are highly toxic. Which dovetails with....

2) If you have a leaky gut already and you get these highly toxic materials into your body, you become at further risk for accumulation. If you are a child with a developing brain, it is worse. This also has to do with having sufficient methylation pathways on board, but these are often compromised in the same children/people who have leaky guts.

3) To further complicate things, vaccinations are apparently dispensed in tubes that contain several doses, so the nurse/doc who administers, withdraws the dose for one child at a time until the tube is empty. However, the heavy metals sink to the bottom. If the medical professional isn't aware of this and/or doesn't shake the tube vigourously each time, the last person to receive the vaccine gets the full amount of toxic preservatives.

4) I do believe that Wakefield's research has been totally debunked. And if you listen to Dr K on this he suggests that the autism link is more likely the mother's DHA levels and health that allows the growing fetus to build the correct brain/body structures.

5) Not only is there questionable benefit about vaccinating for things like chicken pox, but immunity lasts for a limited time, so for some vaccinations, immunity ends at a highly vulnerable time (early adulthood when pregnancy could be likely). Children who are at greatest risk of adverse consequences from things like whooping cough or chicken pox are also the ones who are the most immune compromised to begin with. So this points to bigger issues than just vaccination. And the decisions about vaccination may need to be made in context of this person's overall health and status.

6) The most vulnerable age, for example, for whooping cough is under 3-6 months when the child is too young to vaccinate. Hence, there is big overlap with things like breastfeeding and overall health.

7) There is a strong argument in 'for the greater good' (it's public health thinking). However, if you work in other ways to insure good immune function in you/your child, you not only reduce your own risk of contracting an illness, but you reduce the risk to the population in doing so. (There is also good population data that small pox and polio were actually waning - in other words, we'd created our own species immunity through infection - before the vaccine came on the scene, so no clear way of knowing if the vaccine is what stopped outbreaks or not.)

8) A GP friend told me that most people do not need the full amount of vaccination to confer immunity... the amount given and the numbers of injections are another public health issue (so it's an estimate of averages). She suggests testing the person after each round to see if they have immunity. But this is another out-of-the-box issue that most GPs won't believe or want to do I'd guess.

It is a very complex and personal issue. When it comes to parents making decisions about their children, it's very hard to get good information because both 'sides' are highly charged.

### vlynnb — 2012-09-04T23:47:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49442>

I know you have probably looked this subject up elsewhere and are now asking mom's what they have decided but I want to refer you to this site: [http://www.everydayhealth.com/kids-health/dr-mallikas-5-fast-facts-why-kids-need-the-chickenpox-vaccine.aspx](http://http://www.everydayhealth.com/kids-health/dr-mallikas-5-fast-facts-why-kids-need-the-chickenpox-vaccine.aspx)

This Dr. states very clearly what I have read elsewhere and has lead me to believe in the immunizations. One reason is statistically 1% of those vaccinated have serious complications of the disease while 10% of those who contract the disease come down with serious complications. I don't want to take the chance that one of mine will be very ill from the disease or that after contracting the disease themselves they could be responsible for serious illness in another. I have had one sister-in-law and one niece in very serious condition, hospitalized with pneumonia, as a complication of chicken pox. Don't want to go through that again. Another acquaintance almost died and has neurological problems after acquiring whooping cough as a two week old baby and being hospitalized for 3 months. All being exposed by people who decided that not getting the vaccines was OK for them, they didn't get very sick, just those around them who didn't know they had a compromised immune system of some sort.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-04T23:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49444>

Since you were clearly about to ask. . .

I didn't want to do vaccines - but started to panic about it, so did them on a delayed schedule - plus the Docs in the family read us the riot act about it. I was so out of my depth with a baby (I had never even babysat before or held one) that I tended to freak out that I was going to kill him.

I'm all good and tiger mom now, so I don't know if I would do it again.

I think I would go a hybrid route and try and utilize family physicians to get preference and 1st dibs on a new bottle - and hope they felt strongly enough about it to humor me. . or I would look into see if there were options for kinder/gentler preservatives.

All the docs in the family are slathered in sunscreen and takin statins for the heart disease and god only knows what for their beeteees, so we are NOT on the same page with all this. It sure would make life easier if they were on board. . .

Hey XX I need a refill on my metformin

Hey XX I need a new hormone panel

The problem is they are good and well meaning people and would have to explain to us why we are the ones who are nuts.

Glad I got that off my chest.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-05T00:07:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49450>

Right now I have had almost as many vaccines as my kids have had... and that's from being deployed overseas with the military.

I'm also one of the few 70's babies that actually was vaccinated against small pox and I also received the old version of the TB vaccine when it used a live virus - every time I get tested for TB I test positive b/c of the vaccine - then I have to show my shot records etc... to keep from being put on treatment. I have received boosters since w/dead virus but hey - I had an old school ped was adamant about Small Pox and TB live virus vax  my SP Scar is on the inside of my left thigh (Which is one of the approved areas for vaccination of SP)

one of the reasons they're pushing chicken pox is b/c there are fewer and fewer of live cases going around, its a preventative measure so kids who weren't exposed as kids don't get really sick if a live case arises when they are teens/adults (CPx is much worse in adult hood than in kids, and far more deadly) The other thing the CPx vaccine is supposed to do is protect you from shingles... however, those who get the CPx vaccine will require boosters to stay current....

### Owl — 2012-09-05T00:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49455>

I am 53, and had chicken pox at about 6 years old. My younger sister had it at the same time. People back then wanted their kids to get it, because as Shijin said, it's much more dangerous to get as an adult. There was no vaccine for it then.

I also had the smallpox vaccine on my outer thigh, and had the polio sugar cube vaccine.

My DH is older, but has never had chicken pox, and will probably never catch it now, since he's not exposed to kids. That's a good thing, because he won't get shingles from it later. His mom suffered horribly from that when she was going through lung cancer treatment.

### kathylu — 2012-09-05T18:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49613>

I really go back and forth on the vaccine issue. In general I think they are helpful, certainly on a population wide level. However, if your child is the one that experiences an adverse event, what can you do? Nothing. So, I have been vaccinating my son on a very delayed schedule. No more than one vaccine per visit. Always the ones that have no mercury and the lowest level of aluminum or other adjuvants possible. By the way, aluminum from vaccines is deposited in brain and bone...scary. I'd certainly be relieved if he contracted "wild" chicken pox, but there is not a huge chance of that. I waited until he was 4 to give him MMR, because then a single injection will produce immunity instead of the required two doses. I won't give him the IPV unless we travel to an area where polio is an issue. Like that. Each vaccine is an individual decision. It's funny, I had the ped office nurse ask me why I was proceeding so slowly with this and I replied "maybe because I'm a scientist, I see potential for harm that others don't". Her reply? That "it is much easier being a parent if you are stupid"...sheesh!

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-06T03:31:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49657>

I think vaccination is the hardest decision any parent has to make. We chose not to vaccinate our kids after a lot of agonizing. The reasons we came to this conclusion was first off that mercury (thimersal), aluminium, phenoxyethanol, and formaldehyde all cause demyelinisation, plus liver damage. Many also contain a number of antibiotics. These all have an effect on health in one way or another. The argument of "herd immunity" also didn't sit well, we aren't a herd. We don't eat the same food, from the same sources, we don't have the same close interactions that herds do. Therefore, just because my neighbour comes down with the flu, doesn't mean I will (actually, I doubt I will).

My eldest is a teenager now and has brilliant health, she would be completely drug-free except she busted her foot badly and had a single paracetamol to get to sleep one night. My other 2 are completely drug-free, happy healthy, active amazing kids. We have done everything to ensure they are healthy, great diet, good lifestyle, plenty of sunshine, chiro, lots of laughter, hugs, kisses.

We have had plenty of exposure to childhood diseases, including my DH coming down with "man-mumps" (he is vaccinated), a number of exposures to chicken pox and WC, but every time their immune systems kicked in full on and they stayed well.

One last point is with looking at all the information that Dr Jack gives here, there is a place for "disease" in evolution and improved health. There is a difference between coming in contact and genetic/epigentic changes occurring to increase the strength of the population and surcoming to sickness and TBH I am not willing to intentionally expose myself to HIV to see what changes occur, but these childhood diseases are ones that we have been surviving for millennia. We can become germaphobic and reach new levels of poor health or we can embrace health and that is where germs are no concern to us.

I think with understanding that living with optimal health, our bodies were designed to deal with all this stuff. It has been 9 years since I was last sick. I am getting close to optimal, and I want my kids to stay with me here.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-06T09:45:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49697>

My kids, ages 18, 25 & 29 have not been vaccinated (with 1 exception) and we have never regretted our decision. My oldest son did receive one set of shots at 2 months of age back in 1983 but he had a bad reaction to them and this was before the number of vaccines grew to what they are now. He had a high fever, high-pitched scream then he fell asleep and was hard to wake up again. So we just said, "Well, that can't be good for him" and stopped vaccinating and never looked back. None of my kids has ADD, ADHD or Autism. They are all very healthy, creative and have high IQ's. Of course, they all nursed until they were at least 2 years old too. The oldest one nursed until he was 3 1/2.

I continue to keep up with vaccination news and I still haven't found a good reason to do it. By the way, Dr Wakefield's claim about the MMR vaccine causing Autism (which was completely related to gut damage) was duplicated by other researchers. But the Powers That Be don't want us to know about those studies. He rocked the "Big Pharma" boat too much with his recommendation that the MMR be divided and taken separately instead of clumped into one vaccine so he wasn't even against vaccines--just the MMR combo that was causing gut issues in his young patients. Here is one article that discusses this:

[http://www.naturalnews.com/031116\_Dr\_Andrew\_Wakefield\_British\_Medical\_Journal.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/031116_Dr_Andrew_Wakefield_British_Medical_Journal.html)

I'm also fascinated by info that discusses why so many kids are deathly allergic to peanuts these days. Turns out that Peanut Oil is used in many vaccines and that triggers an allergic response down the road. Same thing with latex allergies--they even stopped using latex in the vaccine bottle tops because so many people started having latex allergies which ironically affected a lot of health care workers who wear latex gloves in their profession. In other countries that use other types of oils instead of peanuts in vaccines, those places have allergy issues to THOSE oils instead of peanuts. And if you read the scientific info, you find that other foods have similar molecular weights as peanut oil so those severe allergies extend to certain fruits as well.

And, many of these dangerous diseases such as Whooping Cough are kept alive in society by vaccines. Whenever there's a breakout, it's mostly Vaccinated kids who catch it (since most vaccines don't really work as promised anyway). And the babies who are too young for the vaccines are at risk because they contract WC from newly vaccinated brothers and sisters. This happened to my friend's infant--she got WC from her older sister and nearly died. She didn't get it from an unvaccinated kid running around the neighborhood. I tried to keep my kids away from freshly vaxed kids because I saw them as toxic. I didn't rely on "herd immunity" by letting other kids take the vaccine risk. We all have choices and NOT injecting my kids with mercury or aborted fetus cells was MY choice and risk I was willing to take.

It's hard to think outside the box when everyone else around you only knows what mainstream medicine tells them to believe. My parents had a FIT that I nursed my kids! They thought I was a bad mother when I wouldn't shove a bottle of fluoridated water into their mouths all day long because "They Say" nursing babies need to drink water because there isn't enough water in breast milk. On and on it went fighting with relatives and so-called friends about so many issues including natural childbirth, no circumcision for my sons, nursing a baby older than 4 months old, no vaccines, no polyester flame-retardant sleepers (mostly cotton clothes), and a family bed to boot. Again, no regrets! I feel like we really dodged a lot of bullets going back to the basics with our kids even if it was against CW. Most of us are here on this forum now because we followed Conventional Wisdom and compromised our health.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T17:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-49749>

Well, I can't really argue with the decision not to vaccinate because I have had these thoughts as well. However, I want to bring up a slightly different point of view. First, the assumption that primitive man was supremely healthy and resistant to all viral and bacterial diseases is just false. Yes, perhaps immunity was better, but maybe not, we really can't answer that question. A few examples of populations where terrible epidemics have occurred are native Hawaiians and native Americans, supremely healthy populations who were almost wiped out by the introduction of measles virus into the population. Black plague (bacterial...Yersinia pestis) has repeatedly caused population decimation. Malaria, Dengue fever, smallpox, etc. Polio did not kill huge numbers, but paralyzed a certain percentage of people infected...that is almost totally prevented by vaccination now. To say that vaccines haven't reduced the incidence of many of these diseases is not true. And of course you are benefiting from "herd immunity". How could you not? If you are surrounded by a buffer of immune individuals, of course it lessens the possibility of spread of these diseases and therefore lessens your chance of contracting a disease. Viruses and bacteria are very good at what they do. They've been at it longer than we have been around, if they weren't very efficient at infecting a host, they would die out. Something to think about.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-08T02:34:42+0300

A few examples of populations where terrible epidemics have occurred are native Hawaiians and native Americans, supremely healthy populations who were almost wiped out by the introduction of measles virus into the population. Black plague (bacterial...Yersinia pestis) has repeatedly caused population decimation. Malaria, Dengue fever, smallpox, etc. Polio did not kill huge numbers, but paralyzed a certain percentage of people infected...that is almost totally prevented by vaccination now. To say that vaccines haven't reduced the incidence of many of these diseases is not true. And of course you are benefiting from "herd immunity". How could you not? If you are surrounded by a buffer of immune individuals, of course it lessens the possibility of spread of these diseases and therefore lessens your chance of contracting a disease. Viruses and bacteria are very good at what they do. They've been at it longer than we have been around, if they weren't very efficient at infecting a host, they would die out. Something to think about.

Epidemics of measles did kill off large numbers of native peoples, but think about where we go measle from? It came with agriculture and living in close proximity to cattle. The Indigenous Americans, Hawaiians and Australians had no agriculture and no passive immunity passed on for generations, until those that survived such epidemics started having children. Having said that, there has not been a disease that has 100% death rate, even Ebola there are those in the population that survive. What is the difference between those that survive and those that don't?

Look also at how some of these diseases are communicated, Polio is oro-fecally transferred. So it requires water polluted with effluent, or some serious poor hygiene practices to transfer.

As to riding off the back of the immunity of others, it makes sense, but reality is different. Where I live we had a whooping cough epidemic last year. The vast majority of people that came down with it were fully vaccinated, in actual fact those that seemed much less likely to get it were the unvaccinated. I did a lot of visiting of friends whos families were effected (because I don't get sick) to help save their sanity while being quarantined. I got not so much as a sniffle. Herd immunity only works if a)we are in fact a herd and b)vaccines have 100% efficacy (which we all no they don't). Recently in Australia they have even cut back our whooping cough vaccination programs after they not only didn't reduce numbers of cases but have been suspected of altering wild whooping cough to make the vaccine completely useless.

Yep, viruses and bacteria are awesome at what they do, but the most successful ones are those that leave their hosts alive and well. It is hard to keep procreating when your food sources keep dying off.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-09-08T05:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-50191>

It would be wonderful if the majority of parents could do the appropriate research and determine which vaccines, if any, to choose for their children. There are many good resources out there for parents, but most simply follow the advice of their pediatrician, most of whom follow the advice of the current federal and state guidelines. My pediatrician literally spent hours with me offering up her current opinions about vaccines and then letting me make my informed choices. We elected to do certain vaccines, and did them on our chosen schedule and ages. My oldest had an adverse reaction to the MMR vaccine, and I elected to wait until my youngest was 5 to vaccinate her. We also had local outbreaks of measles, with very serious consequences for those who got sick, and that also influenced my decision, as well as expected overseas travel.

I think that every parent needs to make an informed decision, even when that decision means zero vaccination or full vaccination. In the end it's a personal choice, but unfortunately it's not usually an informed one for the majority.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-11T23:01:14+0300

> **freshveggies;51625 wrote:** what about the tetnus shot. Any thoughts one way or the other?

We haven't done them. My son got a bad cut on his arm from a box cutter while cutting a shingle and when we took him to the ER, we were willing to let him have a tetanus shot but the doctor said it wouldn't really matter anyway to give him a shot or the booster so he didn't. And my son was fine. The article above did mention that you can give a tetanus shot immediately after an injury and it does work. And I've read that there currently are treatments available for lockjaw. It's pretty rare to contract because you need get it from sharing dirty needles or from stepping in affected horse manure with an open wound on your foot or from animal bites for example. If these are dangers for you or your kids, then by all means, vaccinate for tetanus.

Tetanus--According to Dr. Roy Dittman:

"The CDC reports that tetanus is not contagious and must be contracted from either an animal bite, shared needles from drug use, or other exposure. A tetanus shot can be obtained immediately after a suspected infection delivering a virtual 100% cure. You can check this one off your list." Tetanus is a difficult one. It is less of a vaccine and more akin to an antivanine. You aren't building an immunity to the actual bacteria, but creating antibodies to the neurotoxin produced by the bacteria. It would be one I would consent to under certain circumstances. The issue we have in Australia is there is no individual vaccine for tetanus, you have to get the whole DTPa just for tetanus. Because of this we use peroxide as antiseptic on wounds which highly oxygenates the area.

### Josh — 2014-03-28T05:52:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-118421>

Decent Thimerosol Effects review by Dr. Mullan, she also has free calls on Tuesday nights if one signs up.

> **_Keep Methylation Central_**
>
>
> Any one would have problems with Thimerosal...
>
> Hi, I am Dr Nancy Mullan,
>
>
> I help people with chronic disease who are open to working with dietary change and genetics based nutritional supplementation, and who want to go back to developing their lives as opposed to dealing with illness.
>
> Thimerosal is the mercury-laden preservative that is used in flu shots and other vaccines. Thimerosal damages your genes in three ways:
>
> Number Three Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal damages your genes by inhibiting key enzymes in the production pathway of methyl groups, which are central regulators of gene function, and which also repair gene structure.
>
> Thimerosal has the chemical structure and formulation described below:
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> Thimerosal releases organic mercury into your body. Organic mercury is an old and familiar poison. The Mad Hatter from Lewis Carroll's Alice in Wonderland demonstrates just how long it has been common knowledge that mercury causes neurological degeneration.
>
> The Hatter was mad, out of his mind, because people who made hats were known to use mercury and suffer neurological damage in the process.
>
> Mercury in even nano gram amounts inhibits a key enzyme in the biochemical pathway that makes methyl groups, a one-carbon, three-hydrogen molecule, that is central to the regulation of gene function and the repair of gene structure.
>
> Methyl groups help to turn genes on and turn genes off. Methylation is a regulator of DNA function. It also edits DNA transcription and repairs breaks in the DNA structure.
>
> Methylation silences DNA: it acts to keep viral DNA static, to keep it from reproducing and becoming an infection.
>
> Methylation is the attachment of a methyl group on to another molecule. If you do not have enough methyl groups in your body, inadequate methylation is the result.
>
> You may not have enough methyl groups because of an enzyme problem like MTHFR+, or because you've been exposed to an enzyme poison such as mercury.
>
> Lack of methylation allows DNA that should be silent to be expressed. Viruses can insert their DNA into your own body's DNA. When you do not have enough methylation capacity, that viral DNA can no longer be silenced, and can grow, express itself, and proliferate.
>
> The MMR vaccine is a live virus vaccine. Two out of the three viruses in the MMR are retroviruses. A retrovirus is a kind of virus that can insert itself into your DNA.
>
> Someone with adequate methylation may tolerate this vaccine because the injected and proliferating virus can be kept quiet. Someone without adequate methylation is at risk from any vaccine containing virus.
>
> Number Two Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal is very closely chemically related to a drug that is used to inhibit the growth of DNA.
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> The figure above depicts o-mercaptobenzoic acid. O-mercaptobenzoic acid is a drug which is used to treat HIV.
>
> HIV is a retrovirus so it can insert its DNA into your DNA sequences, both reproducing and living in your cell as a host by doing this.
>
> Many drugs that are used to treat HIV and cancer work by inhibiting the enzymes in key pathways for the synthesis of DNA and RNA bases.
>
> DNA and RNA bases are purines and pyrimidines. They are required for the production of new cells. This drug is used for both aids and cancer, in which diseases you have rapidly growing cells that you want to stop from dividing.
>
> If you inhibit DNA synthesis, the cells can't divide. In the case of HIV or cancer, that's a benefit.
>
> In the case of a child whose body needs to develop, or anyone wanting to have normal gastrointestinal mucosa, healthy wound healing, or good immune function, it works against you.
>
> Looking at the chemical structure of thimerosal in Figure 2, you can see that it is very similar to that of o-mercaptobenzoic acid. Cleavage can occur at the sulfur-mercury bond, and the release of ethyl mercury happens.
>
> Many researchers and clinicians have been primarily concerned about the release of organic mercury from thimerosal. But if you look at what is left behind after mercury is cleaved off, you see a molecule that is very close to the chemical structure of o-mercaptobenzoic acid, a chemical that can inhibit the growth of a retrovirus by acting on its ability to make DNA.
>
> The biochemical pathway that MTHFR+ affects is the same one that makes DNA bases. If you have a problem with MTHFR and someone injects you with thimerosal, you are going to have double trouble trying to make new DNA. In particular, you will not be able to make thymidine because thymidine has to be methylated, and MTHFR+ inhibits your ability to make methyl groups.
>
> Number One Kind of Damage:
>
> Thimerosal conforms to the basic structure of DNA bases and may substitute in the DNA for some bases.
>
> [](http://clicks.aweber.com/y/ct/?l=H.Ihk&m=42leCWo0Q9aPYn6&b=tGOqcHYSiRlafa8_l9oO5A)
> The DNA bases are pyrimidines and purines, the structures on the right in the above figure. The chemical structure of thimerosal is very similar to the DNA bases themselves. Look at thimerosal drawn two different ways in Figure 3.
>
> DNA bases are also called nucleotides. It is known that mercurated nucleotides, nucleotides with a mercury molecule deliberately put on it, will incorporate into your DNA.
>
> Thimerosal can form up like a DNA base, incorporate into your DNA and get stuck there. Your DNA is not going to work properly with that foreign molecule there.
>
> When are you going to say **enough** to thimerosal administration in vaccines, any vaccine?
>
> As a consumer, you have a lot of power. Just as boycott is going to finish GMO foods and accomplish what legislation does not, boycott can wrap it up for thimerosal in vaccines.
>
> Drug companies will not make what people will not buy. You can vote with your feet...
>
> **You have got to figure** that if thimerosal is so deadly to contaminants in a vaccine, **it might be bad for you also...**
>
> I hope this helps you understand the many ways in which thimerosal can interfere with your biochemical functioning.
>
> I hope this helps you 'just say no' to vaccines that contain thimerosal, like the flu shot, which is recommended for the young, the sick, the pregnant and the elderly, just the people who are most vulnerable to the problems of this drug.
>
> My Tuesday night call is your chance to learn what you need to know to get important areas of your health and biochemistry understood.
>
> I take your questions on many topics, among them the impact of your personal genetics on your biochemistry. I discuss how to balance your biochemistry with nutritional supplements that bypass problem genetics.
>
> **It is up to _YOU_ to avoid the hazards that are put in the way of your health.**
>
> **You need to know about the role of optimizing both methyl group production and your capacity for methylation.**

### optimeyes — 2014-11-18T07:37:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-147687>

Every \*body\* is different, and each parent needs to make up their own minds about their children.

First of all, Dr. Wakefield's license was taken away because he conducted his research in a supposedly distasteful manner. What he did was "pay" for his blood donations by giving candy and a dollar to children at a party, where he collected the blood, with the children and their parents' consent. So, because he did not collect the blood in a lab, and because he "bribed" his "volunteers," his methods were not considered valid. :-\\

Secondly, vaccines HAVE been tied to autism, AND so have wild viruses. Vaccines have been tied to other neurological issues, hence that little waiver you must sign when you receive a flu vaccine, stating you are aware that flu vaccine can cause Guillain Barre. Oh, here's the vaccine insert for one DPT shot that mentions autism: [http://www.fda.gov/downloads/BiologicsBloodVaccines/Vaccines/ApprovedProducts/UCM101580.pdf](http://www.fda.gov/downloads/BiologicsBloodVaccines/Vaccines/ApprovedProducts/UCM101580.pdf) Yep, that's on the FDA website.
And scroll to page 11 if you will to see:
"Adverse events reported during post-approval use of Tripedia vaccine include idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, SIDS,
anaphylactic reaction, cellulitis, autism, convulsion/grand mal convulsion, encephalopathy, hypotonia, neuropathy, somnolence
and apnea. Events were included in this list because of the seriousness or frequency of reporting. Because these events are
reported voluntarily from a population of uncertain size, it is not always possible to reliably estimate their frequencies or to
establish a causal relationship to components of Tripedia vaccine.

In other words, they know autism has been reported post-vaccination but are unable to reliably establish a link (for reasons I am about to mention in the following paragraph.) And if they cannot reliably establish a link, they cannot reliably prove no-link!

Third, no double blind w/ placebo trial will ever be conducted for vaccination. It would be considered unethical, therefore there will never be "undeniable proof" that vaccines cause autism. You'd have to not vaccinate a bunch of people, and then expose them to wild pathogens (the unethical part.) You'd have to have dozens of groups, with this group having one vaccine, and the paired group with the placebo of that one vaccine, then you'd need another group with a different vaccine, and its' placebo group. Then a group with two vaccines, and that matching placebo group, etc etc. Then you need the "known to be un-vaccinated" group, because you'd need that group to proof against all the placebo groups. And with all those groups, you'd also need to have subsets, where part of the group was exposed to pathogens and the other part just went about business as usual. Then, this study would have to go on over the course of a decade. So, who would pay for this massive-scale study? Usually the ones that pay for the studies are the drug companies. Do you see a reason they would want to find their products harmful or ineffective? Much easier to say they've done the testing, release the vaccine, and then "oopsy we've made a mistake" when there's an issue. I am not sure how many vaccines have been recalled, but it's been several in just the 10 years I've been researching vaccines.

Another point: no studies have been conducted to date to show the safety/effectiveness of the current vaccine schedule.

Finally, there are way too many medical personnel saying and doing things that just don't make sense. For instance, I was told twice (after two different pregnancies) that I should get my MMR shot since I was not immune to rubella, and that it was completely safe to receive this vaccine while breastfeeding. This is completely false. You cannot even have the MMR vaccine within 3 months of \*conception\* because it is considered unsafe. It is a live virus and it sheds. You would as much want to have MMR vaccine and breastfeed your baby as you would if you actually had the measles. I did not have the MMR after either pregnancy, and I thank goodness for that. (The rubella never takes in my immune system... I have had the MMR shot probably 6 times in my life - every time a doctor found me non-immune as a student attending public schools.)

My personal choice was to go slowly on our own schedule with the vaccines. My brother nearly died from a vaccination reaction (immediately in the office, right before the doctor's eyes) and therefore I already knew our family had sensitivities to them. My children have exemptions because I am against using the fetal components of aborted babies for vaccination, and these babies' DNA is in the live virus vaccines we use today. The babies were aborted \*because\* the moms contracted rubella while pregnant. Rubella in pregnancy can cause all sorts of problems \*including AUTISM!\* ( [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21592401](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21592401) ) According to this study approximately 16,000 ASD cases were prevented by vaccinating for MMR; however they didn't mention how many adverse reactions, including seizures and death, were recorded in this 10 yr period post MMR vaccine. I would like to see that data reported in the same paper.

### kovita — 2014-11-18T08:32:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-147688>

oh dear, I will have to go through this dilemma again... I think this time round there will me thousnads reason for me to avoid all of them.

### Jude — 2014-11-18T12:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vaccines-educate-me-please.3458/post-147702>

# Thread: Risky cooling therapy saves pregnant mom, baby, after cardiac arrest
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-09-04T22:59:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49431>

[http://todayhealth.today.com/\_news/2012/08/28/13529917-risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest?lite](http://http://todayhealth.today.com/_news/2012/08/28/13529917-risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest?lite)

anyone here surprise?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-04T23:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49439>

Not surprised but a bit angry/sad. They know all this stuff yet they don't use it in so many more cases... especially the ER. When I was there after my skiing accident I had to \*hide\* my plastic bags filled with snow that I was packing my head in. I'm mean, seriously, they use hypothermia on super serious TBI's why not try to protect my not so (?) super serious TBI brain cells.

I'm sticking with it: ERs need mostly tubs of ice instead of beds/stretchers. Probably most of the medical system needs that. INstead, they kept telling me I am going to give myself frost bite and noted "repetitive" in my neuro assessment. Yes - give me back my damn ICE already!!! Who cares about frost bite if your brain cells are at stake. I mean. Really.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-05T00:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risky-cooling-therapy-saves-pregnant-mom-baby-after-cardiac-arrest.3459/post-49447>

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-07T15:53:54+0300

When I was there after my skiing accident I had to \*hide\* my plastic bags filled with snow that I was packing my head in. I'm mean, seriously, they use hypothermia on super serious TBI's why not try to protect my not so (?) super serious TBI brain cells.
>
>
>
> I'm sticking with it: ERs need mostly tubs of ice instead of beds/stretchers. Probably most of the medical system needs that. INstead, they kept telling me I am going to give myself frost bite and noted "repetitive" in my neuro assessment. Yes - give me back my damn ICE already!!! Who cares about frost bite if your brain cells are at stake. I mean. Really.

it seems that only the docs who regularly step outside the box embrace cold...look at the NFL player from the Buffalo Bills who was paralyzed on the first play of the season opener several years ago... what did the team physician and Physical therapists do? They packed him in ICE on a back board before transportation to the hospital... not only saved his life...but he's now walking...

I really think I need to update my Road ID - so the first line says - IF INJURED PACK IN ICE...then my emergency contact info....

### AKMan — 2012-09-07T18:52:38+0300

>
>
>
>
> anyone here surprise?

It is pretty much the treatment of choice here in the local hospitals for cardiac arrest, especially those who arrest out in the field. Don't really consider it risky when you consider it is done only in ICUs.

I would be more surprised if I found a hospital that did not use the Arctic Sun and hypothermia protocols.

Nobody really will recommend cooling therapy in hospitals for pregnant women without studies. They are like this odd alien creature in pregnancy and so it is a big risk management issue. That would be more the risky part that would worry the staff.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-05T02:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49501>

I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"

For example:

value of .88 with a range of .4 - 4.5

What would be the excel formula for expressing that as X percentage of value.

Example.

Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.

I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-09-05T02:59:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49504>

You have to assume a distribution of the population of values in the range before you can establish quartiles. If the population is uniformly distributed over the range, the quartile divisions would be at the arithmetic quartiles of the range, if it is a normal distribution, the quartiles are approximately +/- .68 above and below the mean. This can be calculated from determining the area under the curve.

> **Cavemam;50030 wrote:** I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"
>
>
>
> For example:
>
>
>
> value of .88 with a range of .4 - 4.5
>
>
>
> What would be the excel formula for expressing that as X percentage of value.
>
>
>
> Example.
>
>
>
> Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.
>
>
>
> I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

.88 is about 21.5% of your given range?

Assuming you're just working with the even distribution. Forgive me if I'm wrong. Not the best brain today

Range is 4.1 divide by 100 for percentage points gives you .041. .88 divided by .041 gives you 21.5%. A little more than a fifth. At least that's the box for multiple choice I would check on this sick day

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-05T03:11:13+0300

I don't want quartiles, I didn't mean that - I want X% of range... It's on the tip of my neuron. I want the FORMULA, I can figure it out for any given range - but I want to set it up to pull in data and auto apply the forumula.

Make sense?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-05T04:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/math-minds-please-apply.3464/post-49534>

> **Cavemam;50030 wrote:** I want a formula for quickly establishing value to range . . .like when we are looking for something "in the top quartile"
>

>

>
> Value of 25, with a range of 1 - 100 would = 25% in the range.
>

>
> I clearly need a MHS, I used to be able to do this kind of stuff on mah toes. I just need the math, I can do the excel part.

here's how I would calculate it:

4.5-0.4 = 4.1

0.88-.4 = 0.48

(0.48/4.1)\*100=11.7%

top quartile would be 0.75\*4.1= 3.075 or higher

# Thread: Rapeseed oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jasmin — 2012-09-05T08:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49560>

Is rapeseed oil something that I should avoid when eating epi-paleo? I think that it is but I just need to be sure

### Michael — 2012-09-05T09:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49562>

I think it's pretty safe to say it would get a thumbs down.

AFAIK, it's modern use dates back to research in Canada aimed at trying to breed the plant so that it did not produce as much erucic acid. This is an unusual fatty acid present in the oil that seems to be linked with heart lesions.

Hence the oil has been sold in North America under the brand name Canola ("Can-" for Canadian).

I believe they weren't totally successful at getting that out. Besides, most of the oil will be produced at high temperature and pressure - because that's what the industry does with seed oils. That degrades them and makes them reactive even before they leave the factory. Even if that weren't so, I think you'd find that the omega 6mega 3 ratio was unhelpful, and that the total amount of PUFAs was excessive.

Googling around I find that Mary Enig, the lipids expert at the WAPF, has written an article on Canola/Rapeseed oil:

[www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/the-great-con-ola](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/the-great-con-ola)

However, the site's down at the moment for maintenance.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49623>

I believe they partially hydrogenate the oil to eliminate the foul odor. Since the purpose of the hydrogenation is not to make the oil solid, the USDA or whoever deems it unnecessary for them to mention the hydrogenation on the label. Pity our bodies don't understand our good intentions.

### shilohman — 2012-09-06T03:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49649>

Not sure what perceived health benefit you see in rapeseed oil, but the omega 3 is mostly ALA. Your body/brain needs DHA mostly and then EPA. My guess is that the erucic acid would be inflammatory at the gut level. Don't buy into the rapeseed industry's propaganda, rapeseed is sub-sub optimal.

### Jasmin — 2012-09-06T17:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rapeseed-oil.3466/post-49742>

THANK YOU SO MUCH there is no doubt im my mind now - no rapeseed for me

# Thread: Injecting BAT into abdominal fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-06T01:47:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49631>

Boy, theyare trying hard to figure out how to make this BAT thing profitable. Here is an article that describes how they injected BAT into mice and it worked in reducing the white fat. Incredible.

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-mice-heat-generating-cells-belly-fat.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-mice-heat-generating-cells-belly-fat.html)

From the article:

Researchers conducting the study were surprised to see that the injected cells even acted like "missionaries," converting existing belly fat cells into so-called thermogenic cells, which use fat to generate heat.

The scientists took advantage of the heat-generating properties of a so-called good fat in the body, brown fat, to cut back on the white fat cells that compose the visceral fat that tends to accumulate in the belly.

A closer look at exactly what was going on in the animals' cells showed that the injected cells produced high levels of a protein called Ucp1, which burns fat, suggesting that this protein assisted in the visceral fat reduction.

Because that creation of heat could be uncomfortable inside a human body, the researchers analyzed the treated mice further to see if the thermogenesis in the belly would produce effects similar to hot flashes.

"Heat production was higher in injected animals, but it was not dramatically higher. So there is some kind of response, but it seems not to be at a magnitude impairing a patient's well-being," Ziouzenkova said. "The animals were also moving less than noninjected animals, but in spite of that, they were still able to lose visceral fat. Their glucose tolerance improved, as well, which is probably related to reduced visceral fat."

Ziouzenkova said she hopes to design additional capsules to target a variety of diseases beyond obesity.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-06T01:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49635>

I'm glad. At least they are researching BAT, instead of new ways to reduce normal lipids, etc.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-06T05:04:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/injecting-bat-into-abdominal-fat.3470/post-49686>

Jay Leno did a joke the other night about "beige" fat and how it helps with the rest of your fat. (joke was something like, we're not fat enough, we need more fat of different colors?).

You know if Jay's joking about it, it's becoming "acceptable" to discuss.

# Thread: High Early Morning Adrenal Result
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T02:55:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49645>

I have been taking T3 only for five months. Last month I tested my FT3/rT3 ratio and it was 41. Woo-hoo, after a 14.3 ratio, I'm pleased. This month I tested the adrenals via a cortisol saliva kit from ZRT. The top range came back high at 13.6 (3.7-9.5), while all the others were in the perfect place in their respective ranges. Last year's early morning result was at the very bottom of the range so this is a huge jump.

I take my first T3 dose 2.5-3 hours before I get up and two other times during the day. I sure hope I didn't make a mistake with taking it that way before my first saliva sample although I'm not sure how this would make a difference since none of the other times appeared skewed and I had my other two T3 doses as usual. I'm not really having stress and slept good that night. Does anyone know any reason why the high reading would be so high?

I'm not really having stress and slept good that night. Does anyone know any reason why the high reading would be so high?

First, congratulations on the ratio! It also looks that you fixed your AF. Great!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T03:36:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49659>

I will most likely end up changing the time of the first dose to when I wake up but I thought it may be something else. Maybe I need to go back and check the book again.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T04:09:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49677>

### Destiny — 2012-09-06T13:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49703>

> **Patty Cakes;50213 wrote:** I have also been taking 200 mg pregnenolone divided into two doses. I didn't want to spend the money to test that yet but I would think that may have helped with the pregnenolone steal I had going on. Oh dear ....

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-06T18:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-early-morning-adrenal-result.3473/post-49774>

Most mornings I feel OK but not like I have that high of a cortisol reading...seriously. I fell hiking the other day (they kicked me out of dancing school when I was 7 so I am not the most graceful bunny around) and its hard not to lie on my deeply scraped up knee/leg so I'm not sleeping as well as I should be. But that should get better once the pain subsides and I can sleep straight through the night.

My BP, temp, and HR are all normal in the morning. I have felt a little wired feeling around 10ish a few times but not on a regular basis and this high reading was from my 6:30 am result. I think I may just keep doing what I'm doing and do a little more investigation to see what may need to change. It's also time for another consult with the doc!

### AKMan — 2012-09-06T19:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49792>

But back on track, my Winter plan is from Sep-Dec (Fall), stop eating summery, sweet fruit, veg and greens and incorporate potatoes sparingly, winter squash, homemade sauerkraut, apples, corn grits, etc... Starting this now--peaking on these kinds of things in mid-Oct, then phasing out by end of Dec. For Jan, Feb, Mar I intend to go full-on keto. My calories will come from mainly seafood, bone broth, wild game, frozen berries, coconut oil/milk, hard cheese, eggs, sauerkraut, and dark chocolate.

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-06T22:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49841>

> **kathylu;50291 wrote:** Wasn't it "Candygram"?

Yes, I had "Candygram" in mind when I typed my reply in artificial light way past my bedtime!! I was hungry and couldn't handle the thought of not getting Pumpkin Custard this winter which was allowed last winter. Yes, it was a confusing reply that was partly tongue in cheek and partly pizzed at giving up more carbs for mercury tainted seafood. We have Mercury warnings on the fish around here where I now live (WI) which is caused mostly from dentist office run-off waste according to Gov't info online. I just can't keep up with the menu changes here.

And since I no longer believe in Evolution, it makes it harder for me to sift through the good info on the blogs. I accept Kruse's health info when it's obvious it gets great results for whatever reason. I love how he's put all this info together for us in his blogs. It's when the explanation of WHY it works is all based on faith in the evolution theory that makes it hard for me to comprehend the info and apply it to my own health. If believing in Creation disqualifies me from posting here, then I can accept that. I still choose "Team Creator" and the possibility of eventually living forever in perfect health over believing in Evolution and having no hope of living past 122years unless I'm in ice cold water in the dark for the rest of my life. I give that thought process a passing grade of D- (for ambition) because why settle for living 122 years (though commendable) when the option of living FOREVER is still on the table for all of us???

WWED (What Would Eric Do?) lol!

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-06T23:00:55+0300

The bone "evidence" fits into a box the size of a casket.

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-09-07T00:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49866>

> **IceBarbie;50237 wrote:** But I didn't evolve around water (from Land Sharks or whatever you believe spawned you)--I was born to parents whose ancestors were created by a living God about 6000+ years ago. I was born in Minnesota which is the Land of 10,000 Lakes so maybe walleyes are still good for me! I did eat a banana the other day though. Will it kill me?

A way you might choose to reconcile the timeline discrepancy between your biblical/creation/faith based view with that of an evolutionary/science based view might be just redefining the variables.

Maybe a year can be a lot longer in the old testament. You get to interpret it however you choose. There is no description of the process/ingredients of how man was created, why would evolution not be a viable method for your chosen creator to utilize?

I'm not trying to be inflammatory, as I respect your beliefs. Just make sure you are choosing them, not having them chosen for you.

Personally, I'm more comfortable saying I don't know, which allows everything to be possible. I let the study and observation of nature help guide me to what's most probable.

\-doubting thomas

### IceBarbie — 2012-09-07T01:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49871>

x\_danny\_x....sorry my comment caused distraction from your question. I felt that it did tie in to the carb/seafood/evolution topics from Kruse and I was laughing when I posted it. I'm not a troll--I've been following much of Kruse's ideas for a long time now. I did the Leptin Reset (took 4 months) and still struggled with weight gain. Tried CT but was starving instead of shredding fat. I've made so stinkin' many changes in the last year I'm just getting a little jaded and starting to wonder why the heck can't I eat a stupid banana without feeling like I'm committing suicide. Thus my cynicism lately. Sorry to mess up your thread. I figured it would get closed anyway whether I commented or not. But now I realize it's not in the Ask Jack section. My bad.

And Tom, I do not believe that a "day" in scripture necessarily means a literal 24 hour period of time. A "day" can be thousands of years or refer to a specific time period. "In my day" would refer to a time period earlier in my life but not one single day for example. I do not believe in 6 literal 24 hour days for Creation to have taken place like some do. And carbon dating of bones thousands of years old isn't necessarily accurate if those bones have been inbedded in rocks that are billions of years old. Just wanted to clarify my opinion on that because I really understand how most here would consider me to be nutty even using the word Creation in the first place. Obviously, I'm in the minority here. And that's okay. I've been questioning things since I was young and this is just one of those topics that has been important to me. My questioning everything in Conventional Wisdom has also brought me to this site for tidbits of good health info. I just filter out the stuff that doesn't make sense and I have still benefitted. So it's all good. KumBaYah!!

Now back to our Regularly Scheduled Program--Winter Carbs!!!

### AKMan — 2012-09-07T02:01:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/okay-just-to-be-sure-no-carbs-during-the-winter.3475/post-49881>

> **x\_danny\_x;50408 wrote:** I took a look at AKMAN's post and the dude eats dark chocolate during the winter and a couple other carbs? Why is that?

I started this last winter and may change things this winter based on labs. I have a good baseline now to look at. As I get into full-fledged winter mode in December, the carbs will be sparse, like under 20g total/day for 3 months, I will be very strict about this and check labs at the end. If lipids, thyroid, testosterone, and hs-crp are all great, I will do the same the next winter. If they are off, I will reconsider some things. By dark chocolate, I mean 100%, baking type--not Hershey's Special Dark. I'm living further north than about 99% of the world's population, so doing what I do may not be the best for someone in SoCal.

# Thread: BG 11 question

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-09-06T16:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49737>

_I thought about posting this in Ask Jack, but I'm interested in a more involved discussion that I know Dr. K can't do in the Ask Jack section..._

So, in reading BG 11 this morning, I had a decidedly hard time wrapping my head around the BIG PICTURE in the face of all the minutia. Dr. K states that a person can't lose weight until they're leptin-sensitive. It is also inferred that they can't become leptin-sensitive unless they sleep well and get the resultant proper hormonal cascade. But then again, if one is leptin-resistant then it's impossible to get the proper sleep and hormonal cascade necessary to heal the body enough to regain leptin sensitivity?!

I feel like I'm chasing my leptin-resistant tail, here. Anybody have any insight they'd like to offer? I know the simple answer is "do what Dr. K tells you", but that doesn't entirely work for me, as I need to know why things work the way they do, and even having read all the blogs multiple times, I'm still getting a bit lost in my socks.

### kathylu — 2012-09-06T16:44:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49739>

Insight? Yes and no. Empirically I can see the truth of the ideas that Dr. K is weaving together. As I age, everything seems to be falling apart. My hormones have tanked, my FBG is creeping up, I am IR/LR, can't lose weight, memory issues, brain fog, I could go on, but it's too depressing! It's important to understand the principles, but I think it can be a trap to get bogged down in the details. That may be why you feel like you're chasing your tail.

It's actually pretty simple. Light has the greatest influence on hormones and metabolism, more than diet. To become LS/IS again you must correct the function of your circadian cycle. Best way to do that (in order of importance) is to obey natural light cycles, become cold adapted and adopt a seasonally ketogenic, seafood based diet. If you are obese, you must remain cold adapted and ketogenic year round (until you are "fixed"). If you are under 38, lucky you, as you may not need BHRT hormone tweaking like older women and men do. If you are obese, or have problems such as OSA or T2D, cycloset and metformin are helpful. However, I think Dr. K is right, controlling light exposure, lots of CT and the correct diet will work wonders for most.

Oh, I think he's right too, totally...I'm just trying to get a better understanding of the why's and wherefore's. You are stating that light control will allow one to become leptin-sensitive again, and that's the part I think I keep missing in Dr. K's actual writing. I can find all kinds of stuff about timing of meals helping to restore leptin sensitivity, but I guess what I'm really looking for is a definitive yes or no on whether controlling the light in our environment is effective in specifically improving our leptin sensitivity.

And, for what it's worth, he may have said just that somewhere and I was just on info overload and missed it...which is why I was asking the general reading public.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-06T19:48:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg-11-question.3481/post-49805>

It says light is the major factor in this blog......under the third grade example.......48% vs 10% Light controls 48% of the diet.......food control 10% What is the most effective? Most people do not know this because they do not understand how light effects metabolism. Moreover, until we started talking about this in educational consults no one realized humans are particularly sensitive to light because of how big their brains are and how the light wires things together.
