**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-09-08T06:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50196>

I asked my doc to test my DHEA levels, she complied.

I have been supplementing with DHEA fo rthe past 3 months.

I increased my dose form from 15 mg to 50 mg incrementally over these months. I am a 42 y/o male

Dr K stated that ideal is between 500-640 ng/dL

my test results were 761 ng/dL the range is 61-1636 ng/dL

should I continue with 50mg dosing or increase it? as my levels ar a little under 1/2 of the top range.

why would 500-640 be ideal when the top range is 1636?

am I missing something???

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T06:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50200>

I thought Dr K said ideal range is in the top quartile. It's in the BHRT webinar, I'm pretty sure. I'd think the top of the range is best since he said that it is a predictor of IL 6 levels in the body as well as gut health.

### shilohman — 2012-09-08T17:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50265>

> **PaleoDentist;50745 wrote:** I asked my doc to test my DHEA levels, she complied.
>
>
>
> I have been supplementing with DHEA fo rthe past 3 months.
>
>
>
> I increased my dose form from 15 mg to 50 mg incrementally over these months. I am a 42 y/o male
>
>
>
> Dr K stated that ideal is between 500-640 ng/dL
>
>
>
> my test results were 761 ng/dL the range is 61-1636 ng/dL
>
>
>
> should I continue with 50mg dosing or increase it? as my levels ar a little under 1/2 of the top range.
>
>
>
> why would 500-640 be ideal when the top range is 1636?
>
> am I missing something???

This is what I have:

Normal: - Ideal:

Men 280-640 μ/dL - 500-640 μ/dL

Women 65-380 μ/dL - 250-380 μ/dL

So the units appear to be off. μ is the Greek letter Mu which is the same as ug/dL.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-09-08T18:07:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50266>

my values are ng/dL is that the same as value as you listed???? I can't type in Mu symbol.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-08T18:40:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50275>

They are different units of measurement. You can convert the units here [http://www.onlineconversion.com/forum/forum\_1122666981.htm](http://http://www.onlineconversion.com/forum/forum_1122666981.htm)

Also I agree with what Lauren and Shiloman said. I just listened to the Hormone webinar again and Dr. K did say the top quartile. Plus I have the same numbers for optimal. I'm curious after conversion if the two optimal numbers will be close.

### JanSz — 2012-09-08T21:08:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-question.3519/post-50296>

> **PaleoDentist;50745 wrote:** I asked my doc to test my DHEA levels, she complied.
>
>
>
> I have been supplementing with DHEA fo rthe past 3 months.
>
>
>
> I increased my dose form from 15 mg to 50 mg incrementally over these months. I am a 42 y/o male
>
>
>
> Dr K stated that ideal is between 500-640 ng/dL
>
>
>
> my test results were 761 ng/dL the range is 61-1636 ng/dL
>
>
>
> should I continue with 50mg dosing or increase it? as my levels ar a little under 1/2 of the top range.
>
>
>
> why would 500-640 be ideal when the top range is 1636?
>
> am I missing something???

You are mixing DHEA with DHEAs

also it is good idea to describe what was tested and where and give units.

Preferably serum at LabCorp.

The

DHEAs(500-640) ng/dL

looks to me like "old" recommendation (just few months old).

Those recommendation on lef.org usually come from dr Dzugan.

Recently that changed to

Men DHEAs(350-490) μg/dL

Women 275-400 μg/dL

Dr Dzugan (in his book) also recommends (100-200)mg/day of 7ketoDHEA

and then plain DHEA as much as it takes to get to that range.

When I (initially) following 640) ng/dL got there, I have found having very high

5α-Androstane-3α

while

my DHT remained high but within range.

That have made me change the goal to 490.

\--------------------

TotalTestosterone= 761 ng/dL

is respectable

if it is good highly depends on SHBG level

also E1, E2 and progesterone are highly important.

..

Speaking of DHEAs

I am taking 7ketoDHEA and DHEA from lef.org

100mg/wakeup 7ketoDHEA

100mg/wakeup DHEA

100mg/4hrs latter 7ketoDHEA

100mg/4hrs latter DHEA

I came to that dose by frequent testing.

200mg/day 7ketoDHEA is my "permanent" dose, I do not know how to test it or evaluate required dose size.

If someone have idea about it, please speak up.

Dr Dzugan, in his book, describes use of either with no explanation why.

If I was (like you are now) in a process of establishing your DHEA dose,

I would use

DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology/Allergy Research

...

# Thread: Tough Mudder?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-09-08T10:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50212>

Has anyone here done one? I am doing one in 2 weeks time, just wondering what you ate, how you went etc.

We are down to start around 9:30-10am. I am trying to decide if getting up at 4:30 to eat something is a good idea or not. I normally just have butter coffee first thing in the morning and eat between 11 and 12:30.

Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-09-08T17:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50257>

MDA has a blog post about fueling for a Tough Mudder:

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-fuel-ultra-distances-the-worlds-toughest-mudder/#axzz25t9TRX6t](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-fuel-ultra-distances-the-worlds-toughest-mudder/#axzz25t9TRX6t)

### Danco3636 — 2012-09-08T17:29:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50258>

I did one last month. Went into it fasted.... About 16 hour fast and then didn't eat anything afterwards for about 2-3 hours. After that my meal consisted of bacon, seafood and kelp. I was just fine but I fast and eat mostly low carb daily. Some sweet potatoes and berries post lifting workouts. So it may all depend on your overall health, your past eating schedule, foods and how fat adapt you are.......

I think you will need to acces where you are currently. Worst case keep it like how you do your meals based around your current workouts.

Have a fun time......! I had a blast..... And my wife got to help hose me down with some CT afterwards.

\- Dan

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-09T02:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50342>

Thanks. I am quite well keto adapted I think I will just listen to my body on the day. I am thinking of making my own chocolate coated coffee beans with raw cacoa, coconut oil, glucose, cocoa butter etc. as well as the coconut water molasses etc hydration drink on the Marks daily apple page. Thanks for that link, it is great.

I am pretty excited about it. We have an awesome team. I should get through it no problems.

I am leaving DH and the kids at home, so someone else will have to hose me down after.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-10T03:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50619>

Hmmm maybe not as well keto adapted than I thought. I fasted from lunch yesterday (oysters, pastured homegrown beef, a little homemade coleslaw, spinach salad). I got up and had a coffee with butter and then went for a run after dropping the kids at school (18hrs since lunch yesterday) I felt awful running. I did some chin ups and monkey bars stuff and then walked about 2km. The walking was fine, the strength stuff not too bad (not optimal though) but the run was totally \*\*\*\*house. I felt foggy in the brain and weak in the muscles and damn hot! It is currently 21C, so not even that hot.

Ok, I have less than 2 weeks to get this sorted. Help!

### Danco3636 — 2012-09-10T16:22:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-50706>

Have a meal closer to the event. Perhaps protein and fats in the morning 3-4 hours before. Or eat dinner around 7-8pm so you don't have such a long fast. Cut the fast to 14-16 hours. Try doing different things during training but do what works on event day.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-25T11:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-54075>

So, the day before I ate as normal except I had a baked sweet potato with bolognase for dinner. Plus a few Litres of coconut water

In the morning got up had coffee, a can of oysters, some chia pudding (chia, cacao, coconut cream and water) and some home made coffee bean and goji chocolate with coconut oil.

Well, I could have run all day. It was so much fun and there was not a single moment where I felt drained of energy. I am planning on running back to back mudders (both the Saturday and the Sunday) next year.

### Endless — 2012-09-30T19:00:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tough-mudder.3521/post-55415>

Congratulations, and thanks for sharing! I am thinking of doing one in June, if I'm ready.

# Thread: Help me figure out what's going on!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-08T14:27:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50228>

I'm desperate and need some help here! Hoping some "outside eyes" will be able to point me to a resolution. For the last week or a little longer I've been having major stomach pains - almost feels like gas pain, but I'm not gassy at all. It's so bad that I'll be walking or even sitting and find myself totally bent over, unable to take a deep breath it hurts so bad.

I'm pretty sure I've had this happen in the past and it just eventually resolved, which is what I was hoping for this time. The pain did seem to diminish a few days after it started, but now it's back to being pretty intense. It happened as soon as I woke this morning even while lying in bed. I can't figure out if it's related to something I'm eating or not since I've been pretty much eating the same meals for the last several weeks and since it happened this morning 12+ hours after I'd last eaten it makes me think it's not food related.

The only changes I've made in the last 10 days were:

\- lower my DHEA (because my blood tests were very elevated)

\- I started using a progesterone cream about 2 weeks ago - don't know if that could somehow be related, but I did stop it for the last 2 nights and the pain hasn't stopped

\- I also started using 500 mg curcumin extract with my breakfast. I do have oxalate issues and turmeric is very high oxalate, but testing has shown the extract to be ok, so I thought I'd give it a go. I didn't use it yesterday morning though and like I said, the pain is still very present today.

Thoughts on any of the above 3 contributing to this?

I pretty much thrive on routine, so my diet really has stayed pretty consistent over the last 3-4 weeks:

BAB - generally red meat + offal, CO, tallow, bone broth, sea veg, mushrooms

Lunch - seafood (usually shrimp, scallops, fish), avocado, bacon

Dinner - about the same as lunch, but some days I'll add a few raw oysters or some roe

I drink about 8 oz of kombucha a day and have sauerkraut with every meal, but I am super strict with my diet and don't "cheat" or add any extras at all.

The other thing is that the pain generally starts after I've had my very first bite of food - which is usually sauerkraut since I eat that before every meal.

\*Gold star\* for the person who figures this mess out! I suppose I could go to my PCP, but I really can't see them being very helpful and I keep thinking it's something that will pass. I just need to figure out the cause.

Oh - should also mention that cold baths definitely help it, of course  Just with I could stay in the tub all day!

### Stavity — 2012-09-08T15:08:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50232>

I would look at the fermented food and the tea you are drinking. What you are describing to me happened when I was overdoing Bubbies pickles. I decided fermented foods were not for me. I think seafood is for everyone, but the rest we eat should be tailored to each individual.

Wanted to say it sounds like you keep offending your gallbadder by what you are eating. Let it rest.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-08T15:23:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50234>

w/the ferments... I have them every other day... gives my body a chance to do what it needs to do... how's your seafood intake? Especially raw?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-08T15:51:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50241>

gut flora is changing.......I would not have changed a thing.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-08T16:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50252>

> **Stavity;50785 wrote: I would look at the fermented food and the tea you are drinking. What you are describing to me happened when I was overdoing Bubbies pickles. I decided fermented foods were not for me. I think seafood is for everyone, but the rest we eat should be tailored to each individual.
>
>
>
> Wanted to say it sounds like you keep offending your gallbadder by what you are eating. Let it rest.
>
>
> Well, I've been eating loads of kraut since I started on the GAPS diet over 2 years ago. I guess it's possible, but it's hard to believe that it's just suddenly becoming a problem for me.
>
>
>
>
> > **Shijin13;50787 wrote:** w/the ferments... I have them every other day... gives my body a chance to do what it needs to do... how's your seafood intake? Especially raw?

>
>
> Seafood comprises at least 2 of my meals each day. I try to throw in some raw scallops or oysters or some sashimi several times a week.
>
>
>
> Jack Kruse;50794 wrote:** gut flora is changing.......I would not have changed a thing.

I doubt you'll see this again, but if you do - the only changes were lowering my DHEA since blood tests now show it very elevated, stopping the progesterone cream I was experimenting with and stopping the curcumin extract. My guess is you're referring to changing the curcumin and I should keep using that? Thanks!

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-08T17:25:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50255>

You say you eat sauerkraut before each meal. And you are drinking 8 oz. Kombucha. All great stuff! But for me, the timing of these is important. I eat my sauerkraut & drink kombucha WITH my meals, not before. So, I'll have a plate: a large piece of fish, some spinach, some sauerkraut (Bubbies, about 1/2 c), a Bubbie's pickle. I eat it all together, like on the same forkfull. And drink the Kombucha while I'm eating it.

I'm wondering if the probiotics are going down on an empty stomach, which is causing you pain?

### BJK77 — 2012-09-08T19:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50281>

> **PrimalPam;50809 wrote:** You say you eat sauerkraut before each meal. And you are drinking 8 oz. Kombucha. All great stuff! But for me, the timing of these is important. I eat it all together, like on the same forkfull. And drink the Kombucha while I'm eating it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-08T20:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50289>

> since it happened this morning 12+ hours after I'd last eaten it makes me think it's not food related.
>
>
> The other thing is that the pain generally starts after I've had my very first bite of food - which is usually sauerkraut since I eat that before every meal.

irritated stomach lining, gastritis..... acid (sauerkraut) on abrasions in your stomach = pretty bad pain

turmeric can irritate your stomach lining

I'd stop the acidic foods, add DGL, and think about what might have caused it (stress alone can do this, but also infection, too much acid in your food, gerd....). And empty stomach often will bring the pain on.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-08T23:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50311>

>

>
> I'd stop the acidic foods, add DGL, and think about what might have caused it (stress alone can do this, but also infection, too much acid in your food, gerd....). And empty stomach often will bring the pain on.

Y'know what Soul? I think you may deserve the gold star  That makes total sense. It did all start after I began the turmeric and it's definitely much, much worse on an empty stomach like first thing in the morning and about 3-4 hours after eating. It also seems to calm down after I do eat a meal even though that first bite of kraut causes some initial pain.

I do have some DGL here, but never took it before. I guess I'll give it a try and hope that it works to calm things down.

Now to figure out what caused it. I'm wondering if it could just be the turmeric. It's possible I had a bit of extra stress this week at work, but nothing major. My bet is on the turmeric. Darn! How do we get around that for those of us with sensitive guts?!?!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-08T23:30:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50313>

> How do we get around that for those of us with sensitive guts?!?!

Sandwiching it often works for me. I eat the offending items including supplements in the middle of my meal. Food below, food above, jump up and down to mix it up a bit (ok, don't do that ).... lessens the amount of concentrated acid or irritating whatever that will touch your stomach lining. Things will mix and be less offending. I have to eat my cysteine that way or it wouldn't stay down ever.

The occasional bottle of very acidic kombucha still sends me cringing sometimes though.

### Stavity — 2012-09-09T16:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-50470>

I keep forgetting to buy DGL but it is on my list. I use to take it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-14T01:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-figure-out-whats-going-on.3523/post-51537>

BJ, I tried to pm you but your box is full.... you need to be off the turmeric if that's what's causing the pain. And you need to be off it for a while...... if you have gastritis you need to heal the gastritis before you eat anything that will upset your stomach lining more. Healing gastritis can take weeks, months, years for some. It won't go away in a couple days off, unfortunately.

# Thread: Low T3, Low Carb, Low Life
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-08T19:52:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50282>

I feel like I am in between a rock and a hard place with my lack of a working thyroid gland.

I tried moving my T3 up this summer, and it was a disaster. After 3 months of trying to make the dose work, I gave up, and went back to T4 only meds. I think my symptoms were adrenal related (high heart rate, low blood pressure). Even though I was really struggling, my mood was still optimistic.

Now I am taking 150mcg T4 with 12mg T3. I am 3 weeks into this new regimen, and hoping my T3 levels will come up (they were below the bottom of the range before a added the T3). I've gained weight (8 pounds), and I feel a different kind of terrible. Almost depressed mentally, and no endurance. As in, I can barely make it up the stairs. Doing anything feels difficult. Last night it occurred to me that everyday is a struggle, and another 40 years of this sounds unbearable. I've started taking cycloset and getting am sunshine, and I'm hoping those things will boost my adrenals so I can eventually tolerate more T3.

Well, this morning, I was cutting up cantaloupe for everyone else, and decided to eat a small serving of it. And I feel a little better. Still not great, but not so deathly. I am able to do a little more...

I have been really strict about staying on the reset protocol, but I'm not really living. I am surviving the days waiting for it all to "work." It's always a struggle to not eat. I think I am in such a low energy state and my brain really, really wants food (even my regular epi-paleo stuff) as much as possible. Food gives a little pick-me-up, but I am never satiated.

So, I am wondering if there is any merit to increasing my carbs, just a little, at least until I can get a better hold of my thyroid status? Would this be counter-productive? I want to move towards better living as quickly as possible, and so I have made my choices that way. I still need to have a little bit of energy to do my basic living requirements, and I'm really wondering if a little more carbs would be safe, at least short term? Thoughts?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-08T20:11:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50284>

wait for BG 12.......

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-09T21:51:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50557>

Ready and waiting...

Up another 1.5 pounds on the scale this morning (probably from the cantaloupe, or possibly the vinegar that I used to marinate the steak..? I'm betting on the fruit though), and neuropathy starting in 6 of my FINGERS. I've felt twinges of it over the past few months, but nothing like today... Sigh...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-09T22:59:09+0300

BG 11 just got up.......as a precursor to BG 12......READ BG 1-6 about ten times. It is going to matter when you get BG 12. And when you get it.......brace yourself. Some major circadian knots will be tied nicely. Paleo beware.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-09-10T03:59:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50624>

Have you tried the Quelsen shake?

Quelsen has a great coconut milk shake - 1 can whole fat coconut milk, a little drop of stevia, 100% dark chocolate powder, 1/4c of HWC, cinnamon, and nutmeg - blend up and drink 3x/day w/meals - you'll be a fat burner in no time. I took the more gradual approach and did that 1x/day

It helped me with the muscles that feels like rocks and low energy, but I make no promises on the weight side. Omit heavy whipping cream if AI.

### AKMan — 2012-09-10T19:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50749>

[http://www.tpa-uk.org.uk/mgt\_of\_thyroiddef3.php](http://http://www.tpa-uk.org.uk/mgt_of_thyroiddef3.php)

Maybe something in here to find helpful. It sounds like you need to get some really good labs and find a really good doctor who can interpret them. Adrenal support is very important and conversion issues can make thyroid unmanageable with meds. Don't look to Jack to cure you and don't rely on BG-12 giving you the answers you need--use all that info, and what you have learned from Jack to find a good doc and know when to call 'bull\*\*\*\*' and fire the ones that aren't helping you. If they aren't talking adrenal support and getting lots of labs it's time to find a new doc. All the carbs in the world won't help if you have AI and don't know it.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-10T20:15:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-50756>

Love your advice AKMan! It took me years to find a doc who recognized that I had both adrenal issues and Hashis. Keep looking and find the one doc who can help you. They are out there. But keep researching to learn more and in turn a doc will be easier to find. I got to the point that I started interviewing the receptionists over the phone before I made an appt to see if they were aware of the issues I was facing. They know a lot more than you think they do. The right doc for you is out there.

My thyroid levels are ALL below where they should be. I had a consult with Dr. Kruse on Saturday and he told me I need to cut the occasional fruit. It makes me sad  Also said minding light and sleep was very important. Not sure if that helps at all?

### popcornsally — 2012-09-13T17:49:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-51443>

Bigknitwit-- I don't have a solution for you. Just wanted to express my sincere sympathy. What you describe sounds very similar to what I've experienced. It's all very confusing and finding a _good, pro-active_ doctor is much easier said than done. I resorted to ordering my own blood panel (and paying for it myself). I still don't know all my numbers, but the info I got from my most recent panel actually helped me move in the right direction. In my case, low pregnenolone and DHEA were present and replacing those has made the biggest difference of any single thing I've tried. I'm also on natural desiccated thyroid supplement plus adrenal support of various kinds. Compared to one year ago I feel about 80% better.

You can't sit back and wait for your doctor to actually CARE enough to keep trying things to make YOU feel better! If you feel crappy, you are going to be much more motivated than anyone else to figure out a solution. Either confront your current doc with what you think needs to be done or find a new one. What I did was threw away the lab order my doc gave me, ordered what I wanted (which included her stuff plus more) then gave her those results. Luckily, she's pretty mellow . I believe you WILL feel better. I hope it happens soon.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T17:51:12+0300

you are all assuming doctors learn or know anything about this.......they do not. You have to go and learn it on your own

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-14T23:25:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t3-low-carb-low-life.3528/post-64336>

For me as long as the dr. is willing to hear me out, then that is good enough for me. If it's my concern, then it's up to me to be armed with knowledge. As many people stated, if you go to a dr. with reason and rationale for certain tests you would like and they still give a hard time, then yes, you 'fire' that doctor and find a new one. You can't let someone else determine your health, at least not like that.

# Thread: Hello from Austin, TX
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nataliefroberts@gmail.com — 2012-09-08T21:55:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/post-50301>

Hello Dr. Kruse and company. My name is Natalie. I am 34, 5'5", 245lbs (!), married, no kids, small business owner... and a serial yo yo dieter! My goal is to lose weight (about 100lbs) and, more specifically, snow ski with my family this winter.

I've been reading and practicing Paleo lifestyle for 2 years, sometimes 100% paleo and sometimes 70% and sometimes not at all. I love Paleo food. I feel good when I'm 100% paleo. I love to cook. It's a dream come true. I read obsessively on the subject. Plus or minus 5 lbs, **I have not lost weight while Paleo**. I may have lost/gained inches, but have had a real tough time moving that scale. I'm not totally discouraged. I just keep reading and perfecting my food because I know/I feel I'm on the right path.

This is how I found Dr. Kruse: Primal Blue print -> MDA -> a bunch of other Paleo blogs -> Bulletproof Executive -> Jack Kruse

I am currently: Bulletproof IF, lifting heavy weights 2x week, trying to maintain keto and face dunking CT. I went straight for the Lepting Rx and CT Protocols. Started dunking my face. I also just went to a cryotherapy studio to try and ease some sore muscles. I'm curious if that can help with my road to cold adaption.

From what I can tell, I am showing almost all signs that I am becoming Leptin sensitive, per Kruse's Leptin Rx post. Meaning, better mood, better sleep, better energy, and less cravings. Not sure if I need to start Leptin Rx anyway? I'm concerned about this because I lift heavy weights 2x week. Per the L Rx I might need to stop.

I'm so thankful Dr. Kruse is on a mission to save us. No sarcasm. I am seriously thankful this information is free. I am having a hard time getting started with the Kruse informtaion. I just found this website last week and have since listened to every pod cast I can find. I'm trying to read blog posts, but I just can't keep up.

So, here I am. 100 lbs over weight. Feeling good. Blood work looks good, per my palo-loving/holistic ND. No real reason why I shouldn't be losing weight. Hoping Dr. Kruse can help me find out why I am not losing weight.

Questions:

1\. Where do I start? What do I do? What do I read?

2\. Will cryotherapy help with cold adaption?

3\. Should I start the Leptin Rx even tho I'm seeing signs of L sensitivity?

Thanks again for your good work, Dr.

Sincerely,

Natalie

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-08T22:24:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/post-50304>

The easy start is the place to start.

### caroline — 2012-09-09T11:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/post-50453>

Welcome to the forum and keep reading, reading, reading and keep us posted on how you are going

### nonchalant — 2012-09-09T17:20:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/post-50489>

Hi Natalie, always good to have another Texan here!

You didn't mention seafood. Are you eating seafood regularly? It's packed with nutrition, more than the usual Paleo choices.

About your lack of weight loss, I would say that a lack of hormone balance probably has to be the problem. You see the benefits of your efforts, just no scale moving. Most doctors won't see any extremely low numbers on the hormone tests, and therefore say everything is fine. But you need high numbers on some hormones to be optimal. And the ratio of one hormone to another has to be right.

Another potential issue is toxins. If your body has tucked toxins such as halides, excess extrogens, or pesticides/plastics away in your fat, it will fight tooth and nail to keep those toxins safely hid. You have to be sure to take Vitamin B complex, and add some dietary elements for detox.

How are you doing with the CT? Is the face-dunking easy? Does it feel good? Do you look forward to it? If so, you may want to progress further into the protocol. I'd be interested to see if after a few sessions of room-temp water, your body begins to adapt to the cold. That will tell you if your thyroid is stepping up and doing its job. Also CT will help change your hormones for the better. One thing that may help is a scale that measures body fat and muscle %. It helps when you CT and don't see the weight move, but see the body fat % steadily creep down and the muscle % steadily creep up, while not exercising.

About your weight lifting, does it make you feel really good? If it is a chore, you may want to stop it. It could be causing more stress to your body.

Welcome to the site! There are threads here that discuss every point I made above. Enjoy the process!

### shilohman — 2012-09-09T19:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-austin-tx.3530/post-50524>

3\. LeptinRx - You need to get blood work done before you can

answer that. Go back to answer one and start from there.

# Thread: Lights out after dark with a family...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-08T23:03:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50310>

So, I had a consult with Dr kruse and he really stressed this for me. I've been doing the diet and some ct, but this is a piece I haven't implemented yet.

How are ou implementing this with kids? I'm afraid my kids are going to seriously protest at no nightlights.

Another issue I have is my twin 15 month olds still wake a number of times a night. Sometimes to nurse, but sometimes... I don't know why. They just have not ever been good sleepers. It makes it difficult for me to get much sleep which I'm sure isn't helping anything. \*sigh\* any great suggestions on getting one year olds to sleep through the night?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-08T23:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50314>

Trisha, I've said this in other places before, but we've just been through an entire winter (plus a few months before) with no artificial lights. It got dark here by 4:30/5:00pm. My children were 7 and 11 when we began. So older than yours, to be sure. But I really think the worry about how much children will protest is unfounded. Sure, they will find it strange at first. But they adapt really quickly. We didn't get the blue-blocking glasses, just no lights/screens of any kind from dusk. Basically, we just never turned on the lights, so as it darkened outside we started to light candles. My kids play, do homework, cuddle, talk, make up games, etc all by candlelight. They have zero issues going to bed or falling asleep. And their sleep has improved.

If your little ones are having trouble in the night, can you not put them in a bed in your room? That way you don't need lights on. I remember listening to a TS Wiley podcast and her being asked what to do when someone was afraid of the dark. Her answer was simple: sleep with them until they aren't anymore. Especially if they are waking to nurse, I'd think just being able to 'find' them without lights would be a simple solution.

My guess is that once you get on the circadian cycles you'll find their sleep (and yours) will even out. You can't expect uninterrupted sleep at any age if you're getting artificial light input right up until you go to bed... and during!

Last thought... you could get a salt lamp and put it on the lowest setting in the hallway. It should cast enough light so that it's not pitch black but it would be orange/pink light.

We've had huge changes as a result of following the light cycles and I really recommend it. I know it's hard with the age your children are. But I really think you can do it... and see some pretty good changes right away. Good luck!!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-09T00:37:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50327>

I probably don't have much to add beyond what Lauren has already said. Her responses on other posts helped me get my kids on board with the whole light issue so far!

We've only had a "no-fake light" policy only for a little while now, but the kids are totally embracing it. They think the candlelight is cool and the last few nights they've been asking earlier and earlier to light the candles - before it's even dark! They're 12 and 8, so old enough for me to explain why we're doing this and have them at least somewhat understand.

As far as nightlights in their rooms, I gave them both a flashlight with a red lens that they can sleep with in case they wake at night and MUST get up for something. So far it neither has had to use it, but it makes them feel a bit more secure I think.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-09T01:16:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50335>

Thanks! I have the two 15 month olds, a 4 year old, and an 8 year old. I'm not worried about the 8 year old, but my 4 year old thinks he MUST have a night light. The biggest problem with having the 4 year old sleep with us is tht my husband and I are up and down all night long with the babies and it would be very disruptive to him.

Is there a type of light/ lightbulb we could use in their room as a night light?

I'm also not worried about the babies, I'm sure the darkness will only help them sleep. They have are the sweetest babies with the best dispositions, but sleep horribly! They get probably 7 hours of sleep in a 24 hour period. It's crazy.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-09T02:00:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50338>

Trisha, all I can suggest is that you take an experimental attitude ("I wonder what this will be like?"... "Let's try this and see what happens") versus one of certainty ("My four year old must have a night light."). This is totally new and, as a result, can yield very different results. My son used to be very afraid of the dark. He wouldn't even go to the toilet on his own at night, and that was with night lights. Now he happily wanders the house. Like BJK, he has a torch to use if he needs it, but he rarely does. In fact, both of my kids are addicted to evening face dunks. They fill the sink with ice water and do face dunking by candlelight. They say it feels great and helps them sleep! I didn't ask them to do this. They started on their own after watching me. So you may want to experiment with different things and see what works. I'm sure you'll figure it out.

### kathylu — 2012-09-09T02:07:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50340>

Trisha, consider trying this: [http://www.amazon.com/Cloud-Twilight-Constellation-Night-Light/dp/B000VZKGYY](http://http://www.amazon.com/Cloud-Twilight-Constellation-Night-Light/dp/B000VZKGYY) . It worked for my 4 (now 5) year old!

Very low light (we use red only) that projects stars and moons on the ceiling and it turns itself off after 45 minutes. Seemed like a good compromise on the night light issue.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-09T02:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50356>

> **Trisha;50889 wrote:** Thanks! I have the two 15 month olds, a 4 year old, and an 8 year old. I'm not worried about the 8 year old, but my 4 year old thinks he MUST have a night light. The biggest problem with having the 4 year old sleep with us is tht my husband and I are up and down all night long with the babies and it would be very disruptive to him..

Just make it happen. Eventually the 4yo will tell you to turn off your red light b/c it's too bright.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-09T15:27:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50464>

I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-09T16:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50477>

REading for school is one thing... reading for pleasure is entirely another... ONe of the generas I loved growing up was sci-fi - Has he read HGW Time machine? or War of the worlds? great books... Also anything by robert heinline is awesome too Stranger in a strange land was a trippy cool read in highschool still love it later in life... also philip k dick has great short stories... has he seen blade runner? if so ask him if he'd be interested in reading the short story that inspired it.

These are classics - yet most of them won't be read in school - trust me there are great books out there that he'll love. it only takes one really good one to snag them... even w/all the reading in school... plus the great thing about sci-fi is it usually tackles the things they cover in school from a perspective of other worlds...

other options... if he has not desire to read at all... knitting... eye hand coordination at night in the dark w/red light... talk about a skill builder... betting if you give him that or reading...he'll take the reading

eta I picked up the fountainhead and atlas shrugged in 7th grade - made a serious impact on me then (even if I didn't really grasp everything in both books )

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-09T17:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50496>

I just put red bulbs in their night lights. The ones I had in there before were blue LEDs! Ack! (they were cool looking though...)

My daughter woke up every night to nurse until I weaned her at 18mos so I feel your pain on that one!

### freesia — 2012-09-09T20:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50535>

16 - yes that would be difficult! LOL about him finding his shows on the internet. Resourceful kid - WILL get his fix.  How about audio books? I don't know what's available in that department but it might be worth a look. Public libraries sometimes have good selections. You could also see if he would get used to followig a guided relaxation CD just before sleep. They can be quite addictive...in a good way. Wish I had more ideas for you!

> **kjgraffin;51019 wrote:** I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-10T05:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50655>

Alrighty, so we're working on the no artificial lights after dark thing. We're not quite all the way there yet, but it was only day 2 so I'm sure we'll get there soon!

Yesterday, we didn't get lights out quite before dusk. My husband and I discussed we just need to maybe try to encourage the kids to keep lights out as much as possible even during the day. We need to make sure the shades are open for optimal sunlight in the house. I'll sometimes need light in the kitchen for meal prep and in the basement for laundry, etc.

We did really good tonight! We had all of the lights out before dusk. The older boys both thought the candles were really cool (especially my 8 year old). We have, for now, let them keep their small night light in their room, but they've been told that it will be going away soon. I actually may try doing what Jennie suggested and swapping the bulb out for a red light. The boys have always liked us to keep the hall way light on, that is OUT... and amazingly with no fussing!

A couple glitches we've run into and things we need to do:

\- Get a different bulb for the refrigerator - opened the door to get a drink of water tonight and got blinded by the refrigerator light!

\- It would be nice to have a reading light/lamp of some sort besides candle light. Maybe we can just get a bulb for our bedside lights?

\- We live in a subdivision and have outside lights all around us. Our neighbor in the back has a huge flood light (terribly irritating even prior to this). Anyhow, we already put light-blocking shades in our bedrooms. I was noticing last night (as I was up and down ALL night long with babies). That there is still a little bit of light that gets in between the edge of the shade and the windows. Do I need to be concerned about this, or will this little bit of light not cause a problem? If I need to be concerned about this, any good suggestions?

\- Any recommendations on good red/amber blue-blocking bulbs to use? I see there are bulbs on LowBlueLight.com, but they are quite pricey. Are the cheaper red/amber lights you can buy on Amazon or in our local store just as good? Is there something in particular I should be looking for?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T06:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50658>

If I need to be concerned about this, any good suggestions?

Yea for you! You're doing so great... so much progress in such a short time.

I hear you re: neighbours. It sucks. We literally have rooms we don't go into after dusk because I can't block out all the street lights and neighbour's lights in all the rooms. I have, however, worried about the light you've referred to in the bedrooms coming in through the edges. Our budget is super tight so I dealt with this by going to the local opportunity shop and getting a bunch of old curtains and blankets for $2 each and I stuff them above the curtains wherever light seeps in. I also use clips to keep the curtains back against the wall.

Oh... and the fridge! I know. It's amazingly bright. I try to never open ours after dusk. I'm thinking of just taking the bulb out.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T06:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50660>

> **kjgraffin;51019 wrote:** I have a 9 year old & 16 year old. My 9 year old is transitioning very well. I struggle with the 16 year old because I can't come up with alternative things for him to do other than be on the computer. I was able to convince them we don't need TV but he has found his favorite shows on the internet to watch. His circadian rythm is really off and I know getting off the computer and turning off the lights would be very good for him. I was an avid reader as a teenager so I can remember that is what I did at night, but he hates reading since it required so much at school. Any ideas on how to occupy a teen at night?

I used to work with teens (though have yet to live with my own, so all advice with a big grain of salt). I'd be engaging your 16 year old in some deep learning about WHY you are doing what you're doing. Listening to podcasts with JK, reading Lights Out and the blog, etc. The other thing is trying to get a teen to live in a counter-cultural way. They want to live counter to mainstream/parental culture, but not to teen culture. And teen culture is all about the computer and technology, so it's hard. In the long run, I don't think it's going to work if you have to occupy him. But you could do family things. We invested in a whole bunch of card and board games over the past year and we play them most every night. Maybe also get him to do an ASI panel... if he sees his altered circadian cycle on paper he may be more motivated and it may be more personal for him. Last thing: as he weans himself off the computer at night, you could get him blue blockers and make sure f.lux is installed. Good luck... not an easy task with a teen!!

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-10T15:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50689>

I went to the hardware store and got some amber flame looking lights for the dining room chandelier and a bunch of yellow bug lights. They had the red flame lights on clearance so I got the last four of those, might have to look at another store for more of those as long as they're on clearance (Lowe's). In my bedside lamp the bug light was too bright but the amber flame one too dark for reading so need to figure something out there. I think I'll put an amber one in the frig. I'm going to look for some orange cellophane from the craft store and see about covering lights from clocks, TVs, cable boxes, etc. with that. The stove light is another one I need to deal with. I also have a neighbor with a super bright light on at night, but it comes in the bathroom window which is covered pretty well.

My kids are pretty young (6 and 9) and they go to bed early so they haven't been too difficult about it.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-09-10T15:33:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-50694>

I was able to coax my 16 year old into playing UNO with me & my 9 year old last night. I asked him to just play 2 hands and then he could do what he wanted. He ended up playing until bedtime! It was great. He did promptly go upstairs and turn on his light though to get ready for bed. But at least he knows all lights have to be out before I come upstairs to say good night.

He is having an ASI done and I am hoping this will help. I've been talking about circadian rythm with him for months now. We both know that his is backwards, but sometimes seeing it in black and white causes it to hit home a bit harder. He keeps telling people we've gone Amish!

Wow Trisha! Sounds like you are well on your way  We ran into the blinding light from the fridge too . . . such a pain. We were just getting water from the outside and it's still a super bright light. I've just been trying to remember to fill up our water bottles before we go to candlelight. Then there's no excuse to open the fridge or use the water dispenser.

I'm getting into more of a routine with all of this now. I go around and light the candles and DH's propane lantern, fill the water bottles, make sure the kids have brushed their teeth (so much easier before it gets too dark!) and make sure everything is in its place for the next day. After a little over a week it just feels normal now

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-09-16T18:39:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lights-out-after-dark-with-a-family.3532/post-51936>

For bedtime reading, have you thought about candle sconces on the wall above the bed? We have a double one high above each of our pillows and, being high up, the light they produce is more than sufficient to see by. You can also buy sconces with mirrors behind where the candles go to magnify the amount of light produced.

# Thread: Pastured Bacon: So good, but so elusive.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RespectTheProcess — 2012-09-09T03:22:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50369>

Hi all,

Many recent blog posts hail bacon to be combined with seafood. Well, no one has to tell me that twice! However, here is my conundrum....

Where do you all buy pastured, completely GRAIN-FREE bacon? Despite hours spent on EatWild and researching local farms, all of them supplement their pastured hogs with some grain (per them, it's not "forced" but it's there and the hogs do supplement with it). I can taste the difference between local farms bacon and the "pastured bacon" at Whole Foods, so I believe nutrient-wise, the local stuff is better. I'm thinking that this could be considered "okay" bacon, but not "optimal."

Also, I have read some papers that hogs (who have a single chamber stomach, unlike the multiples of cows) can't fully digest all the fiber in the greens from the pasture and need some grain to supplement. I need to do more research, but what are other's opinions about this?

I'm co-eccentric to MD, PA, VA, WV, and D.C. so if anyone has a local supplier, please pass them along! I'm willing to buy in bulk and drive a bit to get it.

Thanks!

### nate.reik@yahoo.com — 2012-09-09T04:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50377>

I don't know of ANYONE feeding their hogs grain-free around me.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-09T20:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50537>

> **RespectTheProcess;50923 wrote: Hi all,
>
> Many recent blog posts hail bacon to be combined with seafood. Well, no one has to tell me that twice! However, here is my conundrum....
>
>
>
> Where do you all buy pastured, completely GRAIN-FREE bacon? Despite hours spent on EatWild and researching local farms, all of them supplement their pastured hogs with some grain (per them, it's not "forced" but it's there and the hogs do supplement with it). I can taste the difference between local farms bacon and the "pastured bacon" at Whole Foods, so I believe nutrient-wise, the local stuff is better. I'm thinking that this could be considered "okay" bacon, but not "optimal."
>
>
>
> Also, I have read some papers that hogs (who have a single chamber stomach, unlike the multiples of cows) can't fully digest all the fiber in the greens from the pasture and need some grain to supplement. I need to do more research, but what are other's opinions about this?
>
>
>
> I'm co-eccentric to MD, PA, VA, WV, and D.C. so if anyone has a local supplier, please pass them along! I'm willing to buy in bulk and drive a bit to get it.
>
>
>
>
>
> Thanks!**

**

Hi RespectTheProcess,

You will have a problem with finding a **totally grassfed hog**. Hogs like chickens are omnivores** and unlike herbivores cannot live on pasture alone. Hogs rooting around in the pasture will need supplementation or they will starve. (So, if you want to save the environment you eat mostly herbivores because that can live on pasture alone. Your pasture better be good though. If your pasture isn't any good your herbivores will be prone to sickness.)

Grow you own if you want to really understand this stuff. If that isn't an option and you want to find good hogs look for ones raised on pasture with GMO free feeds. It depends on your area but peas are a good plant protein source. Avoid soy feed at all cost. Hogs like chickens love to eat insects and mammals too. Hogs in the sun will have lard full of vitamin D because the hogs are like us and put down vitamin D in their fat.

Lastly, learn how to make your own bacon. The stuff that you buy is expensive and even the best artisan produced bacon can cause health problems for some. I used to smoke my own bacon until I found out salted pork bellies are just as good and much easier to make. Here is a recipe for salting pork bellies and making "bacon":

[http://eatkamloops.org/salted-pork-belly/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/salted-pork-belly/)

Here's some more recipes and what the "bacon" looks like after cooking:

[http://eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/specific-carbohydrate-diet-scd-recipes/)

### vlynnb — 2012-09-09T23:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50569>

Caroline,

Do you grow your own? I have quite a few acres of land that I currently lease to a grain farmer. I'd like to raise my own grass fed beef but never considered hogs. Do you have a good reference for doing beef and/or hogs? I have lots of questions like what do you mean by "good pasture"? I have done an internet search but if you know of a site with dependable info I'd like to know.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-10T04:33:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50633>

> **vlynnb;51124 wrote: Caroline,
>
>
>
> Do you grow your own? I have quite a few acres of land that I currently lease to a grain farmer. I'd like to raise my own grass fed beef but never considered hogs. Do you have a good reference for doing beef and/or hogs? I have lots of questions like what do you mean by "good pasture"? I have done an internet search but if you know of a site with dependable info I'd like to know.**

**

Hi vlynnb,

Yes, we grow our own hogs, beef and fowl. We also have a dairy cow and laying flock. We do gardening, permaculture and some forest gardening.

We have four hogs on pasture right now. We supplement their diet with grains, garden foods, and house food waste. We also have kelp, sea salt and minerals they have access to. If you have the land and have the interest just get out there and do it. Even if you screw up you will learn something. Just don't expect perfection the first year. Give yourself say... twenty years to get the hang of it!

Everything is based on the health and balance of your soil. There's a marketing tag line: "Healthy soil, healthy animals, healthy people." That is the damn truth. But where to start. That is an excellent question. I don't know how much you know already so I will give a beginner's reading list:

1\. Get a subscription to Acres USA:

[http://www.acresusa.com/magazines/magazine.htm](http://http://www.acresusa.com/magazines/magazine.htm)

2\. Anything by Joel Salatin but in particular: Salad Bar Beef and Holy Cow and Hog Heaven.

3\. Anything by Newman Turner but in particular: Fertility Farming, Fertility Pasture, Herdsmanship and Cure Your Own Cattle.

4\. Holistic Management: A New Framework for Decision Making by Allan Savory

[http://eatkamloops.org/brittle-grassland-pasture-update-photo-essay/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/brittle-grassland-pasture-update-photo-essay/)

[http://www.savoryinstitute.com/](http://http://www.savoryinstitute.com/)

5\. How to Grow Food in your Polytunnel: All Year Round by Mark Gatter

6\. Gaia's Garden: A Guide to Home-Scale Permaculture by Toby Hemenway

7\. Gardening When It Counts by Steve Solomon

Anything on the Weston A Price Foundation** website is great but they don't go into the details of pasturing. Acres USA has a conference every year. I've never gone but I bet its good. If you live near Joel Salatin go take a course. He is the master! If you live near Allan Savory, go see what he does or take a course. The man is brilliant.

For questions and support from crazy farmers and the bravest vet (Will Winter) I have never met:

[pasturedlivestockproducers@yahoogroups.com](mailto:pasturedlivestockproducers@yahoogroups.com)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=42AAZt0c0tw\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;42AAZt0c0tw\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=42AAZt0c0tw\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;42AAZt0c0tw)

[http://willwinter.com/](http://http://willwinter.com/)

Awesome list! I'm saving it - hubby and I keep talking about throwing it all in and homesteading. I really don't think we are homesteading people - neither of us are exactly the laboring type - but I wonder if it's like children: Until you've done it, you don't know what you are missing.

### maggie — 2012-09-10T05:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50643>

@ Respect: Did you say you were in or near Virginia? If so, try Babes in the Wood in Dillwyn, VA

[http://www.eatwild.com/products/virginia.html](http://http://www.eatwild.com/products/virginia.html)

_Babes in the Wood is a small, family-run pig farm in central Virginia. We raise our pigs in the forest, which is their natural habitat. With 2-3 pigs per acre there is plenty of room for them to roam and forage for food. This method is sustainable and benefits the woodland.

Our pigs are never confined (our sows even give birth in the woods!) Subsequently our pigs are not only happy, but they also produce superior pork. Because it is 100% free range, the texture of our pork is outstanding. It also has a particularly wonderful flavor as their diet of nuts, berries, etc. is reflected in the taste.

We specialize in a rare British breed called the Tamworth, which is known for its particularly rich and distinctive flavor and is especially suitable for raising outdoors. Our pork is free of hormones and antibiotics. We sell a range of cuts, plus sausages and bacon. In addition, we offer whole live hogs for spit roasts or for custom processing.

Customers can order by phone (434) 983-9721 or online at [enquiries@forestfed.com](mailto:enquiries@forestfed.com). We also run a buying club and have drop-off points in Northern Virginia and Southern Maryland. To join our buying club, email us and we will add you to our notification list.

We ship to anywhere in the United States and also attend the following Farmers Markets: Vienna Saturday Farmers Market (Saturdays, 8am-Noon, May-Oct), Freshfarm Market, Silver Spring, Maryland (Saturdays, 9am-1pm, May-Oct), Charlottesville City Market (Saturdays, 7am-Noon, April-Oct), Charlottesville Christmas Market (Saturdays, 10am-5pm, Nov-Dec), Alexandria City Farmers Market (Saturdays, 5-11am, year round), and Clarendon Farmers Market (Wednesdays, 2-7pm, year round). Please contact us as this information is subject to change. You can also contact us for a list of grocery stores and restaurants that we supply.

We thoroughly enjoy showing people around the farm. Please let us know if you would like to visit.

Babes in the Woods, Bill and Kimberly Jones, 327 Rodeo Lane, Dillwyn VA 23936. (434) 938-9721.

E-mail: [enquiries@forestfed.com](mailto:enquiries@forestfed.com). Website: [http://www.forestfed.com](http://http://www.forestfed.com).

_

I just noticed that they ship now. I'm going to contact them myself!

### vlynnb — 2012-09-11T00:40:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pastured-bacon-so-good-but-so-elusive.3537/post-50823>

Caroline, Thank you. That is just what I needed. I am not a livestock person, well I can ride a horse well and have been on round ups but that is not something that will be needed on my small acreage. I have an orchard and love plants so I guess I will now be transferring that interest to pasture. Before my husband died he was reading a lot about the subject and I know he was planning to divide the acreage into different plots to move the cattle frequently to prevent overgrazing. There, that is the extent of my knowledge, so I will be doing a lot of reading. I just found one of his books, "The Grassland Farmer." which I will begin tonight (before dark). Thanks again.

# Thread: The mainstream scares me
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-09T10:35:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-50450>

Headline in the Times (of London:

**Fatty food may harm the brain**

This apparently refers to "research" carried out at a Scottish university. When you read on you find sugar was actually involved too, but that fact's lost by the time you get to the headline.

A Dr. Williams apparently wanted to test whether obesity was related to "more than just calories". So they gave some mice a "high-fat diet" (which also appears to be high in sugar). Others they fed "fruit, veg and wholegrains". The former gained more weight.

So now it seems we're supposed to think a calorie is a calorie ... unless it's wholegrains.

It's nice that they now seem to be realizing that sugar is a problem. But there's apparently no inkling that they need to control separately for fat and sugar. Neither do they seem to realize that a human brain might require a little more than wholegrains and fruit to function adequately.

Times readers reading this and imagining that it's adequate are going to get sick. That's not a small thing.

The conclusion is:

> ... The high-fat diet \[causes\] damage to the hypothalamus ...

Apparently, throwing in the word "hypothalamus" makes a rodent-feeding experiment that might have been of interest about 80 or 90 years ago acceptable.

I haven't seen the paper, but if the Times is seriously mistaken in its report, then I have to conclude the paper is seriously badly written anyway.

I'd have hoped for better from a university in 2012.

### Stavity — 2012-09-09T16:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-50481>

Get use to being scared. I have to go into a trance when people make ignorant comments to me, on a regular basis, about bacon clogging the arteries. I have gotten to the point I won't discuss my diet, unless, I see the person is open to it which is not often. So far though I have 2 people thinking about the Epi Paleo. One is the fisherman/hunter who I buy my salmon from and the other is a friend I meet up with at the Farmer's Market. Just got a short email from the the fisherman and he said he is going to lay off the grains. He already knows the power of fish.

A person has to want to be optimal ,and literally, crave a better way of life. I could have given up a long time with all the bad advice I was given, but feel it with every inch of my being (body, mind, spirit), I have purpose to my madness about being healthy and staying healthy. I want to help other people also when the time arrives.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-09T20:03:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-50526>

I think Stavity is right. Face your fear. Have you ever read **Dune by Frank Herbert? Here is a quote from the **Bene Gesserit Litany Against Fear**:

_“I must not fear. Fear is the mind-killer. Fear is the little-death that brings total obliteration. I will face my fear. I will permit it to pass over me and through me. And when it has gone past I will turn the inner eye to see its path. Where the fear has gone there will be nothing. Only I will remain.”_

[http://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Dune](http://http://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Dune)

Listen to your body. There is a lot of "stupid" advice out there. Do a media fast**. Be careful about where your information comes from and realize YOU have to power to not let the information in. Watch the things that cause you stress. Reading that article is causing you stress and thus negatively effective your hormone statue. Take the needed steps to reduce stressful environmental forces from your life that you have control over. That means having self-control. If you do not have self-control work on developing some.

### Michael — 2012-09-09T20:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-50536>

> **Caroline Cooper;51081 wrote:** I think Stavity is right. Face your fear.

Well, I wasn't being literal.

I do feel somewhat concerned for the people getting this crappy advice, though. There. If we must be literal, will "somewhat concerned" do?

There are people who roll up at the paleo forums and say stuff like they have difficulty with sugar, and then it turns out they don't so much want it as genuinely crave it. Then multiple addictions, to substances or behaviours, start being brought out -- not that they make the connection. The connection here, of course, is that all these things, unbeknownst to them, are driven by brains that have been f---d up by inadequate nutrition.

It's damn near _criminal that people in Aberdeen are trotting out stuff like this when just down the road in London Professor Crawford is doing important work that they're presumably not aware of.

Somebody bleating about "wholegrains" in 2012 really ought to have read Cordain's pamphlet on the matter by now -- one of the best things he's done:

[http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf](http://http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf)

Maybe they could even be aware of Dr. Fasano's work at the University of Maryland on non-celiac gluten-sensitivity?

And here's a thought: could they have looked at what's been coming out for some time about carbohydrates and cancer?

But, no, I'm not actually "frightened" for the general public, although I definitely would prefer them not to have their health undermined by bad advice.

Nor am I "frightened" of the editor of the Times_, although I wasn't actually planning to go and punch him on the nose.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-09T20:55:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-50546>

> **Michael;51091 wrote: But, no, I'm not actually "frightened" for the general public, although I definitely would prefer them not to have their health undermined by bad advice.
>
>
>
> Nor am I "frightened" of the editor of the _Times_, although I wasn't actually planning to go and punch him on the nose.**

**

Hi Michael,

You have lots of company with your concern for the general public. That is why Sally Fallon started the **Weston A Price Foundation. That is why I started [www.eatkamloops.org](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org). Each of us must do what we can within the limited scope of our personal control.

Maybe you could learn from the Stoic's trichotomy as outlined in A Guide to the Good Life** by William Irvine. It will help you focus on the things you have "full or partial control over" and stop concerning yourself about the things you "have no control over":

[http://www.amazon.com/Guide-Good-Life-Ancient-Stoic/dp/0195374614](http://http://www.amazon.com/Guide-Good-Life-Ancient-Stoic/dp/0195374614)

You could write to the editor at the Times** if you think it is worth the time and effort, but he knows where his bread is buttered, or should I say "margarined". (Gee, I'm making up a new word again. What is going on with me this month?) Do what you can, friend. That is all any of us:

[http://eatkamloops.org/port-fool-you/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/port-fool-you/)

### Michael — 2012-09-19T21:09:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mainstream-scares-me.3539/post-52747>

Well, you all got me for sure.

I don't own a TV. I rarely turn the radio on. I don't buy a daily newspaper.

But I raise my head to draw attention for a minute to a sloppy story in the MSM that I did happen to come across and everyone descends on me with a crunch, tells me I'm a coward, and that I need to read some guy called Frank Dune.

OK.

Yes, I'm favour of turning a deaf ear to most of this stuff most of the time.

This guy is very good on that:

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/03/21/18-inches-the-difference-between-success-and-failure-w-td](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/03/21/18-inches-the-difference-between-success-and-failure-w-td)

I think he might be exhausting if you knew him, but by golly is he enthusiastic. I like the way he exudes positive mental attitude and blends physical fitness with some subtle "bodywork" ideas. Another of the good guys, IMO.

# Thread: My Story at 1 year
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-09-09T18:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50500>

Found Dr. K about a year ago broken physically and emotionally. I had just finished getting back surgery (at age 37) and had very weak muscles and and a fragile back. After being on benzos and ssri meds for 20 years for night time panic attacks, I was an emotional mess trying to be free from all the meds. I had a 1 year old at the time and needless to say had little sleep. Started reading Dr. Kruse's blog (with great difficulty) and was amazed at some of the claims. I realized I definitely had leptin issues and started his protocol. Within days, I lost my lifelong binge impulse. As long as I followed the plan, I could manage my diet. I wasn't really losing weight, but my body was changing and that validated it for me. My labs kept getting better. I started CT and my back pain was nearly gone! It also got stronger, without any training. I was very athletic most of my life and for 3 years, I was unable to even get to a jog. Post CT, I was doing cartwheels with my daughter, running, SPRINTING, playing on rope swings and doing leaps in the air. My sleep also got better around the time I started CT. But, the blue light blocking glasses also calmed me down and helped me go to sleep. I spent months doing the leptin Rx (not perfectly, fell off many times) but as time went on, weight became easier to lose. I went from a high of 150 to 134 pounds within this year. My body composition is better than before when I was this weight. I am so happy to be in the California sun now all the time and the coconut oil has made my skin better than I have seen it. My daughter who is extremely naturally white and I have been to the beach many times in the past few weeks. The sun has been strong! I haven't put one spray of sunscreen on her or me. We haven't even become red, absolutely no burn. This site has been life changing for me, with Dr Kruse'e insights, but also with the insights of the community here. The people in the forum have helped me just as much and I am so thankful for that. Thanks Dr. Kruse and wishing everyone the best! My next task is to tackle BIHT, hope to update on that too.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-09T19:28:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50518>

Success for sure! I am so happy for you and your daughter. She will have a much better life now that mommy is getting better. I love reading these stories. Just when things get worse, a great pick me up to keep me going. Please keep sharing...

### nonchalant — 2012-09-09T20:08:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50529>

Glad to hear of your strengthened back, Coldbren. It's just amazing.

Your daughter is one lucky girl to be able to take advantage of all this so early.

### freesia — 2012-09-09T20:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50532>

Congratulations on one year of improvements.......look at that list!!!

Body comp changes and increased fitness have been my big benefits from CT too. I just went through a whole winter feeling great (quite a novelty for me!) ...so I wish the same for you in the months ahead. Will you continiue on with the CT?

Another question...Did you manage to get off the benzos or is that a work in progress? I'm asking because I know that can be a real Biggie and I have a couple of friends who struggle!

Congrats again ColdBren and thanks for sharing your progress

### ColdBren — 2012-09-09T20:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50533>

Thanks! Freesia- yes I did get off everything completely, have been off for about a year. However recently my daughter has developed very troublesome tics and I am in a chronic state of stress over it. I took a benzo twice in the past week. Am really trying to be careful and dont want to get caught in that addiction again, but the stress of trying to figure out what is going on with my little girl is huge right now.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-09T20:36:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50534>

Thank you for sharing your story. I am always so inspired by the success of others. Good luck with your daughter. Keep looking and you will find the answer....it's out there somewhere. Could it be environmentally related: pesticides, chemicals of some kind?

### ColdBren — 2012-09-09T21:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50550>

My current theory (maybe even hope) is that yeast is the problem. She definitely has too much. I started her on nystatin and she is having horrendous die-off and increase of all her symptoms. I gave her a breathalyzer test and my 7 year old daughter blew a .01! This confirms yeast to me. Now, I am just hoping this is what is causing these awful symptoms, she throws her head forward many times throughout the day and is having extreme anxiety.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-09T23:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-story-at-1-year.3546/post-50566>

Good for you, Coldbren and inspiring!

What great news, Bren, and such amazing progress. You'll definitely tackle your next hurdles. Well done you! Thanks for sharing. Lauren

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-10T15:45:53+0300

awesome Coldbren.. great success!

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-10T16:15:22+0300

It's going to be great as more people hit their one year anniversaries with Dr k. and the leptin rx. Mine is coming up and I can't say enough. Actually I say enough. I drive people around me crazy, but no one can argue with the body comp results. After almost a year hardly a day goes by that someone doesn't comment on how good I look. I also have not touched sunblock for two summers and I don't burn at all. Carry on. Great news about your daughter. She will get all straightened out and have a long healthy life. What a gift!

### ColdBren — 2012-10-14T23:55:41+0300

When she goes off the diet, I see a difference...the only thing I dont know yet is if a perfect diet will allow total remission.

### SueC — 2012-10-15T17:54:38+0300

### TheKid — 2012-10-04T20:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-have-i-spawned.3547/post-56271>

Speaking I what I might have spawned. I am wondering how much protein your little ones eat.

I have a 21-month-old daughter who is only 50th percentile in height and weight.

We have an Elevation Burger in town (grass fed, organic beef) and we go there from time to time. It is not uncommon for my daughter to eat two patties. They are "kids burger" patties, but I was told that a kids burger is just an adult single.

My understanding is that those burgers are 1/2 pound in size. Is my little girl really eating a pound of meat? I mean, even if those burgers are 1/3 or 1/4 lb. I'd still be shocked.

Is that possible? Is that healthy? She won't touch most veggies. Loves fruit, but we're trying to cut back now that fall is upon us. And if there are no sweet potatoes around before the meat comes, she just makes massive amounts of meat disappear. Ou should see her go to work on a leg of lamb.

Help!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-04T20:44:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-have-i-spawned.3547/post-56282>

>
>
>

NORMAL! at least in my house... there are nights the Havoc (2yo boy) will clip off 2x 1/2lb burgers. some mornings he clips off 2-3eggs etc. Chaos doesn't eat as much but its pretty solid in the meat variety. There are nights she doesn't want veggies so she just eats meat. Its not that she doesn't like them...she just doesn't want them.

they'll each eat a whole can of sardines by themselves (no sharing) let her at it - she knows what her little body needs!

# Thread: Need Tips for keeping Cortisol in check on school mornings

**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
