### bigknitwit — 2012-09-10T01:29:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-tips-for-keeping-cortisol-in-check-on-school-mornings.3552/post-50589>

Does anyone else feel the strain, as you to help everyone get ready for school? Or church, or soccer, or wherever you're taking young people on a timeline?? I do my best to make the older 3 (11, 9, 6) as independant as possible (I'm going to have no problem if they forget their lunch (no cafeteria) some day). They are actively involved in packing their lunches (in the evening), and getting ready in the morning. But still... My 6 year old left the other morning without eating breakfast. She was served what she asked for, and decided she didn't like the look of it... I figure I have to let them fail to help them succeed independantly, but our mornings are stressful!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-10T01:36:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-tips-for-keeping-cortisol-in-check-on-school-mornings.3552/post-50590>

better off not to eat......IFing is good. But yet you stressed over something that was actually good for them. Another neolthic thought thta hurts you yet again. Being a mom is not always optimal. Said that to MJ in BG 8 and it hit her between the eyes.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-10T05:43:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-tips-for-keeping-cortisol-in-check-on-school-mornings.3552/post-50652>

Bigknitwit,

I'm past those years (my kids are 30 and almost 21), but of course I remember them and of course raising children is sometimes stressfully.

Jack says being a Mom is not always optimal, yet we'll be Mom's for the rest of our lives!

A friend once said to me that Mom's are only as happy as their

### philerup — 2012-09-10T05:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50644>

I have been doing CT for a while now, and this year I have been bitten more by mosquitoes than in any recent year. Has anyone else experienced this? I attribute it to the higher skin temperature. Just checking, thanks.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-10T05:24:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50648>

Don't know where you live...but, above average rain fall where I live=more mosquitoes .

### ealachan — 2012-09-10T16:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50711>

Interestingly enough, I've actually noticed the exact opposite this year. Usually I'm mosquito fodder all summer long, but this year they've mostly left me alone. There are plenty around and they're still annoying, but they don't seem to want to land on me anymore...and when they do, they don't seem to want to bite. I'm totally ok with this!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-10T17:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50720>

> **ealachan;51266 wrote:** Interestingly enough, I've actually noticed the exact opposite this year. Usually I'm mosquito fodder all summer long, but this year they've mostly left me alone. There are plenty around and they're still annoying, but they don't seem to want to land on me anymore...and when they do, they don't seem to want to bite. I'm totally ok with this!

I've been finding this... though they still bite me...though instead of the dollar size welts they used to turn into (Lack of DHEA) they're now normal size...

### ealachan — 2012-09-10T17:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50721>

> **Shijin13;51275 wrote:** I've been finding this... though they still bite me...though instead of the dollar size welts they used to turn into (Lack of DHEA) they're now normal size...

I got bitten by an ant a couple weekends ago, and I had hardly any reaction to it at all - just a little welt that shrunk after a couple hours and itched for maybe half a day. I used to get BIG welts that burned and itched for days from ant bites! Win!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-10T19:36:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50747>

> **ealachan;51266 wrote: Interestingly enough, I've actually noticed the exact opposite this year. Usually I'm mosquito fodder all summer long, but this year they've mostly left me alone. There are plenty around and they're still annoying, but they don't seem to want to land on me anymore...and when they do, they don't seem to want to bite. I'm totally ok with this!
>
>
> Shijin13;51275 wrote:** I've been finding this... though they still bite me...though instead of the dollar size welts they used to turn into (Lack of DHEA) they're now normal size...

+1 here too. I used to get eaten alive. This year I think I've had maybe 2 bites and they were hardly visible . . . and I live in the woods (very buggy) and spent a week along the bay in Maine. Haven't used any repellant either

### txritchies@att.net — 2012-09-10T21:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50759>

I am in TX(think West Nile) and unfortunately I am still getting mosquito bites. I have used 3 strikes and your out or 3 bites and I am back inside.

### ealachan — 2012-09-10T21:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50763>

> **Lacy;51315 wrote:** I am in TX(think West Nile) and unfortunately I am still getting mosquito bites. I have used 3 strikes and your out or 3 bites and I am back inside.

We have West Nile up here in VT, too.

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-10T21:33:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50764>

> **Shijin13;51275 wrote:** I've been finding this... though they still bite me...though instead of the dollar size welts they used to turn into (Lack of DHEA) they're now normal size...

Do explain? My wife gets massive welts from mosquito bites, and they seem to prefer her when out and about. She's a little apprehensive about our trip to Costa Rica in January because of that. I told her to try adding lots of turmeric to her diet. But how is DHEA related?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-10T21:55:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50767>

> **JoeBranca;51320 wrote:** Do explain? My wife gets massive welts from mosquito bites, and they seem to prefer her when out and about. She's a little apprehensive about our trip to Costa Rica in January because of that. I told her to try adding lots of turmeric to her diet. But how is DHEA related?

Low DHEA = big welts on mosquito bites and HIGH IL6 cytokines...

I bet your wife's DHEA-S and DHEA is low if you tested... mines been off the charts and I barely get a welt now...

### Jonathin — 2012-09-10T22:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50769>

I would agree that the metabolism of one's particular body plays a strong role in one's reaction to bug bits. This morning I was listening to the local public radio newscast and there was an interesting story about why mosquitoes are such a problem late in the year. The biggest issue has to do with the cyclic vectoring of west nile to the bird population and the consolidation of birds and mosquitos around the remaining watering holes. Start listening at about 2:50. This is out of western Colorado.

[http://www.kvnf.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/9\_10\_12.mp3](http://http://www.kvnf.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/09/9_10_12.mp3)

### melrito — 2012-09-10T22:25:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50770>

Fatigued adrenals/too low cortisol causes an overreaction to mosquito bites too.

### txritchies@att.net — 2012-09-10T22:25:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50771>

> **ealachan;51319 wrote:** We have West Nile up here in VT, too.

### txritchies@att.net — 2012-09-10T22:28:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50773>

> **melrito;51326 wrote:** Fatigued adrenals/too low cortisol causes an overreaction to mosquito bites too.

### ealachan — 2012-09-10T22:28:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50774>

> **Lacy;51327 wrote:** It is sad the number of cases out there and then number of deaths just keeps growing. I think the aerial spraying has helped in the areas that have been able to participate. No telling what that spray will do long term. How are they handling it in your area?

They're spraying in some areas here too, but more because of the EEE (Eastern Equine Encephalitis, which affects humans even though the name says equine!), which is also mosquito-borne and has become a major problem the past few weeks.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-09-11T14:10:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-50931>

> **ealachan;51319 wrote:** We have West Nile up here in VT, too.

I would seriously consider megadosing in vitamin C if the moment arrives, even [getting intravenous infusion if necessary](http://http://www.orthomed.com/Nile.htm) ([Dr. Levy has used it too](http://http://y2khealthanddetox.com/westnile-Levy.html)). Of course ridiculously low doses has already been proven ([once](http://http://injectablevitaminc.com/images/Ch10.pdf) —_Large doses parenterally are effective when oral administration fails (Youmans 1941)— and [again](http://http://www.quackwatch.com/01QuackeryRelatedTopics/pauling.html)) to be of almost no use against anything: if you don't want to waste time and health, you have [to titrate](http://http://www.orthomed.com/titrate.htm) and even go with [the lyposomal encapsulated one](http://http://www.canceraction.org.gg/system/files/Hickey-Liposomal%20vit%20C.pdf) in order to get higher plasma concentration.

About vitamin D, after reading Dr. Cannell on [_Blood Irradiation_](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2006/is-vitamin-d-an-antibiotic/) and on [Common Cold](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2010/self-inflicted-vitamin-d-toxicity/)_[http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2010/self-inflicted-vitamin-d-toxicity/](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2010/self-inflicted-vitamin-d-toxicity/), I get the impression that it is not going to be effective against staphilococcus aureus, rhinoviruses and influenza B at least. I don't know where West Nile virus stands, nor if another effect of CT apart its vitamin D receptor sensitization would take care of it (it seems the case with the staphilococcus aureus in Dr. Kruse's self-experiment).

[Antiviral medicines](http://http://www.drbriffa.com/2009/12/11/does-tamiflu-actually-work-and-is-it-safe/)... well, they do a lot of money.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-12T00:14:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-51070>

I got West Nile about 7 yrs ago when I lived in Houston!! For me, it was a series of symptoms one right after the other. Lasted almost 2 weeks. None terrible. Got to get "confirmed" by the CDC, which I thought was great. And it was on the news as "X-confirmed case of west nile"

Just felt like sharing.

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-12T00:24:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mosquito-bites.3555/post-51072>

I've been back on LR for over a week and have moved to mostly seafood for the past couple of weeks. Ditto on the CT (though I'm still not in the 50s except when I face dunk).

Mosquitoes usually love me and this year I've had few bites -- we have had some drought. Lately they are more numerous and I've gotten a few very small bites.

I can't believe I've been doing this good stuff for such a short time and would have this kind of effect. Usually I get large welts.

But on a related note, yesterday I cut myself a couple of times making my first MHS -- before I went to bed, one cut seemed to be a bit weepy and sore. I was really tired and thought that I'd use the H2O2 and tea tree oil this morning. When I awoke, the larger cut is completely invisible and the other one is just a faint red line. No broken skin. This has never happened before.

# Thread: DHEA  Vitamin D raise testosterone right?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jennie747 — 2012-09-10T15:15:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-50691>

So as a female with too-high testosterone I should not take those? I have been supplementing D for about 5 years as it was on the low end, now I'm wondering if that screwed me up? I bought some DHEA but then gave it to my husband after reading about how it raises testosterone. This is all so confusing.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T23:54:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-50806>

I think you need to supplement your Vitamin D until you get it high enough... and you need to make sure you get a lot of natural sunlight on you. I know that some of the men have talked about shirtless/naked sunbathing as a natural way, along with epi paleo and light cycles, to raise their T, but I don't think that implies that vitamin D will raise T in women.

DHEA can convert to T, but you can take 7-Keto as well. Why did you buy the DHEA in the first place? Were your lab values tested and low? I think it also matters in what form you take it and I believe Dr K has said that you can experiment and test to see what happens.

I guess the main thing is that supplementation should be in reaction to specific needs and the context of your labs. Does that help at all?

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-11T00:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-50822>

Thanks for your reply!

My D levels have been ok lately, after supplementing. My DHEA has never been tested as far as I know. I bought it after Dr. Ks blog on it because he made it sound so great & I thought I might try to make face cream out of it. It was while googling how to make face cream that I came across the testosterone comments.

What is 7-Keto?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T03:40:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-50881>

7-Keto is the precursor to DHEA. It doesn't convert to T, but can help build DHEA levels. I think testing for hormones is wise before using them. Maybe read the Hormone 101 blog, too, if you haven't. And if your T is too high, have you read about PCOS? And also tested E and P?

### Jennie747 — 2012-09-11T12:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-50920>

My E and P were in the normal range. I have read about PCOS and I have some of the symptoms (acne, weight gain, high cholesterol, hair loss, some hairs on chin) but not others (menstrual irregularities, ovarian cysts, elevated BP, infertility). I have Hashis so that accounts for some of my symptoms too.

I told my Doc about Dr. K at my last appt & he seemed interested, I hope he's been reading up! I'm going to call before I get my labs and see if we can add some more on there.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-12T00:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-51071>

Jennie,

I didn't have a baseline testosterone to compare - but I did get bloodwork done about 4 weeks after starting 50 mg's a day of regular DHEA. My testosterone was crazy high. 3X the max on the range. My hair started falling out - that's how I caught it. I stopped, and ran blood again last week - so I'm anxious to see if/how much it's gone down.

All other labs I get tend to be either in range or near range - so this one stood out by 2 docs, one who thought it could be a lab error. Long story short, I think it was the DHEA so I am now taking only 7-keto.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:08:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dhea-vitamin-d-raise-testosterone-right.3563/post-51135>

> **Jennie747;51484 wrote:** My E and P were in the normal range. I have read about PCOS and I have some of the symptoms (acne, weight gain, high cholesterol, hair loss, some hairs on chin) but not others (menstrual irregularities, ovarian cysts, elevated BP, infertility). I have Hashis so that accounts for some of my symptoms too.
>
>
>
> I told my Doc about Dr. K at my last appt & he seemed interested, I hope he's been reading up! I'm going to call before I get my labs and see if we can add some more on there.

I really think you'd love the webinars, esp the BHRT and the Epi Paleo one. Both give great info about hormones and also Hashi's. It's your P:E ratio you need to worry about. Ranges alone won't give you enough info. If you can, check out the webinars... it may help you heaps! Good luck.

# Thread: 1.5 t vs 3t MRI
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-5-t-vs-3t-mri.3564/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### danielarherman@gmail.com — 2012-09-10T15:57:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-5-t-vs-3t-mri.3564/post-50701>

Hey there everyone,

The nearest 3t MRI is at UCSF or there is 1 in Oakland, both of which is about 2 hrs from where I live. Other than detecting lesions, is there any reason I should not just start with a 1.5 MRI scan? We are looking into brain trauma and of course tumors.

Thanks!

### TheHolyCat — 2012-10-10T09:58:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-5-t-vs-3t-mri.3564/post-57265>

Kerowyn,

While I can't offer an expert opinion, I can tell you that a 3T MRI is supposed to offer significantly better resolution, & when done dynamically & used in conjunction with a contrast agent, can offer a much better means for detecting subtle damage & irregularities. In fact, I myself will be having a dynamic 3T MRI of my pituitary done with & without contrast this Friday, as I have finally convinced my ENT, Functional Medicine Doc, & Endocrinologist that something major is going on with my pituitary function. Do you mind if I ask what's prompted the brain imaging concerns?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-14T19:30:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-else-dying-for-bg-12.3567/post-51655>

One thing I keep coming back to, that I think is a missing piece to the whole-life-optimal picture, is auditory things.

It's known that rhythmic sounds can alter neuron firing. It's also known that music can alter the production of certain chemicals and hormones in the brain.

Just like the light/visual thing is relevant, might auditory input be as well? If we are going by the argument that visual input is 65% of the pic, then does that leave diet the only other relevant input?

Also, since our brain can get multiple overlapping signals (visual, auditory, taste, touch) all at the same time. . can you REALLY say at any given moment JUST visual is 65%

Mebby BG-32 will explain how dancin in rhythm keeps out little bacteria happy and pulsing together.

Happy Birthday, Caveman. The attachment works. Your thyroid numbers are low and as you know so is your iron.

"Iron forms part of the mechanism that transports thyroid hormone into the cells, and lack of it can lead to pooling of thyroid hormone and leave you metabolically hypo even if you have a good level of Free T3 in the blood stream, this is yet another form of resistance to thyroid hormone but has the disadvantage that you can't add more T3 to overcome it as the intolerance symptoms build up."

You need to fix your ferritin and saturation, then you can fix your thyroid.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-10T23:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-my-birth-day-im-old-hack-my-labs-pretty-please.3570/post-50802>

Ft3 is 8.5% in the range, optimal according to thyroid types is 75% of range or higher or 3.53 or higher

Ft4 is 44.3% in range, a bit low most thyroid types prefer to see it between 50-60% of the range.

Ft3/Rt3 ratio is about 11, should be above 20

your TSH is lowish but the fact that the FT3 is so low does not make me think you are hyper. unmedicated TSH below 1 in women with low FT3 may indicate something funky in the pituitary. I think I gave you a link in your journal that discusses what to check for. If your cortisol is low as well maybe get an ACTH test done.

### colleencoble — 2012-09-10T23:53:27+0300

Your free Testosterone looks pretty good. Your total is high but your free isn't high. Start working on getting your magnesium up too. It's on the low side. B12 isn't terrible but it could be better. I'm only mentioning these because some betaine might help both. I like mine in the 1000-1200 range. Definitely glucose issues. This was fasting?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-11T00:24:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-my-birth-day-im-old-hack-my-labs-pretty-please.3570/post-50819>

Happy Birthday!!!

You may or may not have Hashis based on the AB results. Keep testing and act/eat as if you do. Then after several tests your ABs go down and you are assured you don't have Hashis you lost nothing.

Yah, iron is so not good. Saturation s/b around 35%, Ferritin 70-80, IIRC, Serum 70-100 IIRC. Also your B12 could be higher...get it toward the top of the range.

Thyroid: FT3 is low (s/b near top), FT4 is low (mid to top) and you may have a rT3 problem but need units. Go here to calculate the ratio: [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)

Cholesterol sucks. You may have a fat malabsorption problem. Seems a lot of folks have that. Get the Metametrix GI fx test. That's how I found out I had it. Maybe something due to bypass????

Also that testosterone seems awfully high. I have in my notes that it should be between 60-76 optimal range for women.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-my-birth-day-im-old-hack-my-labs-pretty-please.3570/post-51134>

> **Cavemam;51586 wrote:** I had never heard of this, but several forum people from here implied it - googles then left some clues. I see much conflicting info, want to know more.

Hormone 101 blog:

Pregnenolone is made from cholesterol, and cholesterol is made from LDL. So anytime the body is stressed or inflamed, it up-regulates cholesterol production to make more lifesaving hormones. It requires T3 and vitamin A as cofactors to complete this step. Blocking cholesterol production will increase cellular stress. This is why biochemically, to me, no statin drugs have ever made any sense under any circumstance in medicine. Moreover, this is why there is a chronic association of cancer to low cholesterol levels in the literature. In times of infection or stress, LDL levels always need to rise to protect the cell.

### mambo — 2012-09-15T09:15:03+0300

When you had your gastric bypass, did you have your insulin resistance or diabetes reversed? Your fasting C Peptide seems a bit low. Had BG issues before and now no issues? Not sure if it's in right units but in ng/ml, that's seems to be close to what a T1/T1.5 diabetic would test at. In other words, not making much insulin, either because of autoimmunity or burnt out most of your beta cells. Not right if in wrong units.

# Thread: Why did I wait so long?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-09-10T23:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50790>

I finally tried some of my fish head bone broth today, for lunch. Lunches have been a problem, lately, because I find I am not hungry enough to make much effort to have something nutritious. I poured a small bowl of the broth, added salt and a bit more pepper, and wow, so good! I went back for seconds, thirds, and ended up consuming the entire quart of broth (minus the solid parts I strained out.) Dr. K had recommended bone broths for my gut health; itâ€™s about time I finally took that advice.

A couple days ago at lunchtime I bought a fish head from the asian market. Not the expensive salmon head, but the cheap one that had no species identification. I picked out one that had the nicest eyes. The man at the counter was a bit surprised when I didnâ€™t want it cut into pieces. He assured me it still had the brain. I took it home and popped it into the crock pot with water to cover, peppercorns, powdered spirulina, turmeric, bay leaf, and the rest of a package of fresh seaweed that was packed in salt. I rinsed the seaweed and artfully arranged it on top of the fish head, so if DH were to open the lid, he wouldnâ€™t be â€¦ startled.

After I got home from work I pushed on the fish head with a spoon, and to my surprise it easily crushed down to nothing. There were just thin triangular bones left that were all etched clean in the 5-6 hours it cooked. I put everything into canning jars & put them in the fridge, because we had other plans for the night.

I didnâ€™t follow a recipe, just tossed in stuff I had on hand. With the good results Iâ€™ve had, I may actually find some recipes for other ingredients to try next time. And I want to buy a whole fish, so I'll be sure to get the liver and thyroid, and filets to grill. Oh, and next time Iâ€™ll probably put the crock pot out in the garage. Whew, it was a bit pungent when I came home. And yes, DH did open the lid out of curiosity when he got home, but nothing stared back at him, thank goodness!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T23:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50800>

Sounds delish! I have made fish broth before, but recently have been doing bone broth. I stopped because I wondered about cooking the fish for so long... how do you gauge the temp? And the length of time to cook? I was under the impression (wrongly?) that cooking will destroy the DHA. I'm sure you've figured all this out. Can you say more?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-10T23:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50809>

I just cooked it on the low setting for the crockpot, whatever that was. A simmer. This was just an experiment. And the time was from my lunch break to when I could get back home. I figured I didn't have much to lose, with the cheap ingredients.

I'm sure I destroyed lots of DHA, especially compared to a MHS. But cooked seafood is good for us, and tasty, and the dissolved cartilage and bone was what I was after. It took me about 5 minutes to assemble the ingredients and start the cooking. Then I left the house. Easy peasy.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T00:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50810>

Thanks, makes sense! I wonder if dissolved cartilage and bone of fish are better for us than grass fed cows. Probably!!

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-11T00:02:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50812>

That sounds really good. Congratulations! I've added your 'recipe' to my fish broth recipe page.

I just had my own woo-hoo moment: I made my first fish head smoothie!!! And I loved it!!!

It was a scup -- not familiar with that kind. They are larger than mackerel, but the fish guy only had headless mackerel. I have good kitchen shears, but I had a really hard time cutting up those heads (I used 2). I kept saying, 'I can't believe I'm doing this'. The bony/cartilage parts were very difficult to cut through. But I did it. I added 3 lemons in my Vitamix. And some sea salt and a swig of lemon CLO.

I thought the vitamix was going to break at first. I added a couple of ice cubes because I didn't want a warm fish head smoothie. I first removed the tamper thingie -- and what I had was a really thick pudding! So I added some water, not much, and whirred it again for a while. It was still thick but not as bad, but it was definitely warm. So I added some more ice and some more water.

It still was pudding consistency. But at least it was very chilly. I had 2 glasses of this stuff, and it actually tasted like a lemon pudding. It was so very good. Ate it with a spoon.

My other part of dinner was 6 raw oysters that I shucked. I was going to add some veggie, but I'm stuffed. And I feel so incredibly energetic.

This stuff is all really great. I'm going to have to start a journal. I'm so proud of myself for doing this. You all don't really know me, but I'm a big wimp. Go me!

### caroline — 2012-09-11T08:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-50916>

well - I am good to go! I just went to the chinese store and bought a package of small frozen fish. I have lemons and coriander and a bottle of red wine to wash it all down! Now I just have to wait to be really hungry. Anyone else in? Cavemam?? We really have to just do this and get it over with before Inger disowns us for our wimpiness!!!! ### Primal Dream — 2012-09-12T00:01:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51068>

> **janagram;51448 wrote:** please tell us your recipe exactly, i.e., whole lemons?
>
>
>
> (Holy \*\*\*\*! I can't believe I just asked this!!!)

If you mean my FSH (non-mackerel fish): OK, I used 2 heads, they were bigger than I thought they were. Sorry, I didn't take pictures but I will next time. The fish was scup? Never heard of it but the fishmonger said it was like grouper.

So I got out my kitchen shears and started cutting the heads. It was not easy. Lots of cartilege. Next time I will use my kevlar gloves I use for shucking oysters. So it took a while. So I got out 3 lemons and juiced them. Put the juice and pulp in the blender (vitamix).

Just to make sure it was lemony, I put in a glug of lemon flavored CLO. Then some sea salt. And a couple of ice cubes.

I thought that the vitamix was going to break down. After I thought it might be ok, it looked really, really thick. So I added a bit of water. It was still thick and felt warm. I did not want warm. So I got some more ice and a bit more water. the result was still pudding like.

I got my courage up to taste it and it was lemony and not at all fishy. there was a lot of it. I filled 2 8 oz glasses and ate it with a spoon.

I really like the idea of adding cilantro. I'll do that next time. This fishmonger was at a farmers market and they are opening up a real store tomorrow. We really need another seafood retailer.

Priscilla

### philerup — 2012-09-13T10:00:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51395>

glad that people are starting to go to the Asian markets and get real food. The problem with most traditional American supermarkets and groceries is that they fix up the food to make it more salable and only include muscle meats etc.

I have found in all of the Asian markets that it is essentially an Epi-Paleo paradise. You can get whole fish, bones of any kinds, seaweed of all kinds, whole parts of animals including offal. That is just my input. I will never shop at a local grocery for this kind of stuff again.

### philerup — 2012-09-13T10:02:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51396>

Chicken feet rock! I know Dr. K doesn't approve of fowl, but the collagen that you get from chicken feet when making broth is unlike anything else you can get. I make sure to make chicken feet broth regularly.

### Qodesh — 2012-09-14T20:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51668>

The scup, Stenotomus chrysops, is a fish which occurs primarily in the Atlantic from Massachusetts to South Carolina. Along with many other fish of the family Sparidae, it is also commonly known as porgy.

They are fished for by commercial and recreational fishermen. **They are a fine fish to eat because of their light flavor** and are sometimes called panfish.

Primal Dream's fish has a light flavor. This could make it easier in our "beginner's" taste buds?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-14T21:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51675>

I recommend Primal Dream's pudding idea. I just had my second MHS, using one mackerel head. This was a different type of mackerel than I had before. I made sure to include the thyroid and liver this time. I added cilantro and limes. The first spoonful was awful. I ended up adding a third lime. I remembered to hold my nose. Wish I had more cilantro! I managed to get more spoonfuls down, spacing them out between kitchen cleanup. But a funny thing happened after I had eaten about half of it. I started realizing that it didn't taste so bad after all. It was like my body (mind?) was overruling my taste buds. I didn't have to hold my nose. I ended up scraping the bowl for the last spoonful. It still didn't taste good, but I wanted it.

It was better, for me, than holding my nose and chugging it down.

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-16T01:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51875>

OK, I'm sitting here with my MHS. Real mackerel this time. What a difference! The scup was more difficult to cut, but it was a 'cleaner' head -- don't know how to describe it. And those mackerel teeth are sharp. Ouch! I forgot to put on my kevlar gloves.

At any rate, it was a mess.

Scup is a lighter fish. This tastes gamier, if you know what I mean. it's still not bad, just not as tasty as the scup. And yes, Qodesh, it's from the Atlantic -- my fish guys fish off of the Hamptons, Long Island.

So: 1 mackerel head plus liver etc. 2 lemons, juiced, with pulp. cilantro -- a big handful. A glug of lemony CLO. And 4 ice cubes. Not bad, but I liked the scup better.

I'm still proud of myself.

Priscilla

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-16T01:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-51877>

MHS=BG12. Just saying

### caroline — 2012-09-18T03:57:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-52271>

Well ......I finally had my MHS! It really isn't bad at all. All I could really taste is lemon and corriander. I just used 1 whole frozen fish - about 6 inches long. I was really worried about getting it smooth enough because my food processor is crap so I used a hand held immersion blender and it worked great - except - I didn't notice that the lid wasn't on properly until it was too late. Lets just say - I had to change all my clothes!!! I have had a big couple of weeks - swimming in the ocean, eating fresh oysters and lots of other fish and now Inger's cocktail Inger is so right - just do it. It is also pretty nice to have a few swigs of red wine after your cocktail first thing in the morning. Every cloud does have a silver lining! I am really having a hard time eating beef now - does not appeal at all.

### Stavity — 2012-09-18T15:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-did-i-wait-so-long.3572/post-52332>

> **Jack Kruse;52491 wrote:** MHS=BG12. Just saying

Guess I will need to start looking for fresh Mackerel for smoothies. I have been in serious trouble, health wise, for years and need to do what is good for my brain.

# Thread: sinus SOS
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-10T23:49:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50798>

I'd love some help/input from all your great minds. I am trying to work out exactly what is going on with my sinuses and what I should do about it. I've basically got draining sinuses every day. It's not too intense, but it's low-level constant. Sometimes it's a bit worse, sometimes there is slight ear pain. The drainage is clear, I am otherwise well.

I'm assuming this is some kind of background inflammation, but I'm not sure if I should just be patient or if there is some specific action/testing I should do. (I've had hsCRP and most labs, am about to do re-testing since it's been 6 months, and am doing full hormone panel too.)

I confess that I get a bit freaked out about this sometimes and would love some input into how 'worried' you think I should be. I have been off all dairy for 6+ months, all eggs and nuts (except 1 Brazil nut per day) for about 3+ months and epi paleo (seafood, half raw, daily... the other main food is offal; no carbs/starches). I am religious about light cycles and have been for 6 months. CT is semi-regular.

Help?????

Thanks, Lauren

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-10T23:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50801>

Seasonal allergies? Your beginning to change seasons right???

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T00:02:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50811>

> **Shijin13;51359 wrote:** Seasonal allergies? Your beginning to change seasons right???

Oh, I should have clarified this. This has been going on for MONTHS. And in the past 6 months I've had to travel (by plane) twice for work on longer flights. Both times I got sick with terrible head colds/mild flu. After the second time I'm pretty convinced it's because of this background sinus congestion.

### caroline — 2012-09-11T02:28:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50850>

I was travelling a few months ago - along with a couple from New Zealand. He was having alot of sinus/blockage problems that nothing seemed to help. He was having alot of trouble sleeping. When he got home he went to his Doc and was prescribed an antibiotic. He then became seriously ill. Bad rash, lost alot of weight. They put him in an induced coma to try to figure it out. Family was called/ didn't think he would survive. He has recovered but they still don't know what it was. He was a very healthy and fit man.

I will try to find out more if you want. I wonder if airplane travel has something to do with it?

### ealachan — 2012-09-11T17:18:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50951>

> **KiwiLauren;51356 wrote:** I'd love some help/input from all your great minds. I am trying to work out exactly what is going on with my sinuses and what I should do about it. I've basically got draining sinuses every day. It's not too intense, but it's low-level constant. Sometimes it's a bit worse, sometimes there is slight ear pain. The drainage is clear, I am otherwise well.
>
>
>
> I'm assuming this is some kind of background inflammation, but I'm not sure if I should just be patient or if there is some specific action/testing I should do. (I've had hsCRP and most labs, am about to do re-testing since it's been 6 months, and am doing full hormone panel too.)
>
>
>
> I confess that I get a bit freaked out about this sometimes and would love some input into how 'worried' you think I should be. I have been off all dairy for 6+ months, all eggs and nuts (except 1 Brazil nut per day) for about 3+ months and epi paleo (seafood, half raw, daily... the other main food is offal; no carbs/starches). I am religious about light cycles and have been for 6 months. CT is semi-regular.
>
>
>
> Help?????
>
>
>
> Thanks, Lauren

That's exactly what I get when my allergies kick in. Sometimes it will stay like this for a week or two, sometimes it will progress to what feels like a full-blown cold. I know you said you've had it for months - has anything in your environment changed lately, that you know of? Is there anything that's possibly very dusty or moldy / mildewy near you (maybe not in your house, but nearby it or at work)?

### BJK77 — 2012-09-11T20:03:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50987>

Take this for whatever it's worth . . . I'm certainly no expert! Just thought I'd throw out there that my mom was dealing with a chronic sinus issue last year. She was having a lot of sinus pain, congestion, headaches and had already been on a few rounds of antibiotics. It all started to clear up somewhat and then we went on vacation - to Tennessee (unfortunately not to see Dr K!) and it got much worse. I had her start to use Oil of Oregano - both inhaling and orally and honestly it all cleared up for her in about 3 days.

Most of my family thinks I'm a total nutcase and just don't get my "natural, hippie ways," but this time she was totally amazed. That was last spring and she hasn't had a sinus issue since which is HUGE for her. She now calls me often when she has any little health concern to see if I know any natural remedies . . . I even had her doing cold baths for a little! She didn't stick with it, but I'm still nudging

### Jonathin — 2012-09-11T20:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-50999>

Discounting a chronic sinus infection or constant strong environmental allergen pressure, my guess is that the water balance in the body is off. How is your kidney function (tests)? Water and salt intake? Blood pressure? Do you have other body symptoms that are indicative of water balance/electrolytes issues? Seems like the body does not have only one isolated manifestation of dysfunction such as a sinus problem. Do you have other things about your body that are quirky even in a minor way that might tie in? Digging deeper is the only way to figure it out. I know that odd and persistent problems can be frustrating but worry is not the answer!! Be thankful that a high level of pain is not part of the issue.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T21:53:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51043>

This sounds like the way I pretty much lived my life before I quit wheat. I would clear up in summer, when pollen was low because of drought, but autumn, winter, and spring were miserable. The pollen allergies just added to the high inflammation that the wheat caused. It doesn't sound like you are eating anything that would possibly cause this inflammation.

Yes, an hsCRP would be a good idea. Could it possibly be a shellfish allergy?

I even had her doing cold baths for a little! She didn't stick with it, but I'm still nudging

Thanks, I may give that a try (being also of the natural-hippy variety myself!).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T22:13:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51048>

> **Jonathin;51567 wrote:** Discounting a chronic sinus infection or constant strong environmental allergen pressure, my guess is that the water balance in the body is off. How is your kidney function (tests)? Water and salt intake? Blood pressure? Do you have other body symptoms that are indicative of water balance/electrolytes issues? Seems like the body does not have only one isolated manifestation of dysfunction such as a sinus problem. Do you have other things about your body that are quirky even in a minor way that might tie in? Digging deeper is the only way to figure it out. I know that odd and persistent problems can be frustrating but worry is not the answer!! Be thankful that a high level of pain is not part of the issue.

This is very interesting. I will think about your question re: other quirky manifestations of tie-in issues. My blood pressure is typically low and I drink heaps (and use lots of sea salt) but I could experiment with this for sure. I have never even contemplated the idea of 'water balance in the body'. I will look into this for sure.

As for worry not being helpful, believe me I know it only adds to the problem (perhaps I'm allergic to worrying). It ties into my health history, but I will dig deeper and cut ties with the worry. Thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T22:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51049>

> **ealachan;51517 wrote:** That's exactly what I get when my allergies kick in. Sometimes it will stay like this for a week or two, sometimes it will progress to what feels like a full-blown cold. I know you said you've had it for months - has anything in your environment changed lately, that you know of? Is there anything that's possibly very dusty or moldy / mildewy near you (maybe not in your house, but nearby it or at work)?

Thanks for this. I'm pretty sure it's not related to allergies or changes (unless something has changed with the neighbours that I'm unaware of). But it's been pretty chronic, with ups and down, since March including going through a very cold winter. Thanks for the input - I really appreciate everyone chiming in.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-11T22:16:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51051>

> **Nonchalant;51614 wrote:** This sounds like the way I pretty much lived my life before I quit wheat. I would clear up in summer, when pollen was low because of drought, but autumn, winter, and spring were miserable. The pollen allergies just added to the high inflammation that the wheat caused. It doesn't sound like you are eating anything that would possibly cause this inflammation.
>
>
>
> Yes, an hsCRP would be a good idea. Could it possibly be a shellfish allergy?

Definitely no wheat and seems irrespective of pollen (we are supposed to be in spring but it snowed yesterday). Hadn't thought about a shellfish allergy. Could this be the only symptom?

### vlynnb — 2012-09-11T22:36:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51056>

I think the sinus issues really could be the only symptom of a shellfish allergy, I vote for checking this out, especially since your diet seems tight in other ways, though you may cry and experience higher stress if this is the case and you love shellfish.

BK - I also use oil of oregano for colds and sinus symptoms which I have quite often but I am just guessing at how to use it. I just take the lid off of the bottle and inhale about four times. How do you use it orally?

### BJK77 — 2012-09-12T01:07:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51078>

> **vlynnb;51627 wrote:**
>
> BK - I also use oil of oregano for colds and sinus symptoms which I have quite often but I am just guessing at how to use it. I just take the lid off of the bottle and inhale about four times. How do you use it orally?

I just put a couple of drops in water and drink up! The taste is terrible, but it's over quick enough

I actually think the most effective way is to boil water in a pan, turn the burner off, add a few drops of the oil of oregano and put a towel around your head while you breathe the steam in very deeply. Wipes colds and congestion out right away for my family. Just make sure to keep your eyes closed or they'll burn if you get the steam/oil in them.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T02:15:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51087>

> **caroline;51409 wrote:** I was travelling a few months ago - along with a couple from New Zealand. He was having alot of sinus/blockage problems that nothing seemed to help. He was having alot of trouble sleeping. When he got home he went to his Doc and was prescribed an antibiotic. He then became seriously ill. Bad rash, lost alot of weight. They put him in an induced coma to try to figure it out. Family was called/ didn't think he would survive. He has recovered but they still don't know what it was. He was a very healthy and fit man.
>
> I will try to find out more if you want. I wonder if airplane travel has something to do with it?

Caroline, in my exuberance at all the responses, I forgot to respond to you. I wonder, too, if airplane travel has to do with it. My sense is that the change of pressure is a trigger for things going haywire. I am VERY glad that I haven't ended up in a coma!! Lauren

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T02:16:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51089>

> **vlynnb;51627 wrote:** I think the sinus issues really could be the only symptom of a shellfish allergy, I vote for checking this out, especially since your diet seems tight in other ways, though you may cry and experience higher stress if this is the case and you love shellfish.
>
>
>

In thinking back to the genesis of this, it pre-dates the unveiling of epiPaleo so I wouldn't have been eating much in the way of shellfish. In terms of checking it out, how would you do so? Thanks to you both for the ongoing info around oregano oil, too.

### caroline — 2012-09-12T02:40:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51091>

we were on 5 flights over the course of 28 days plus high altitude - airplane travel might be worth looking into. I have had friends develop a shellfish allergy later in life - had always enjoyed it all their life and then all of a sudden things changed. Good luck!

### vlynnb — 2012-09-12T19:12:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sinus-sos.3573/post-51215>

To test for a shellfish allergy you could go to a traditional allergist. I have done this and had good results with some of his suggested treatment of seasonal allergies. However, he downplayed my food allergies, told me just to avoid what I knew bothered me to a great deal and not to worry about the test result that said I was allergic to wheat as it didn't seem to be bothering me. I didn't realize how allergic I was to wheat until I eliminated it from my diet. So my recommendation would be to go on an elimination diet, which you can do an internet search to find directions for, but basically you eliminate foods one at a time from your diet for a period of time, note any reactions or changes in you health, then add that food back to your diet and see what happens. The downside is that it takes a lot of time to go through a lot of foods, but if you hit on the worst offender on the first try, as hopefully zeroing in on shellfish will do, then you may choose not to test others.

# Thread: New Study finds 46% of obese people are metabolically healthy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-finds-46-of-obese-people-are-metabolically-healthy.3576/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-09-11T00:59:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-finds-46-of-obese-people-are-metabolically-healthy.3576/post-50830>

[http://www.examiner.com/article/new-study-finds-46-of-obese-people-are-metabolically-healthy](http://http://www.examiner.com/article/new-study-finds-46-of-obese-people-are-metabolically-healthy)

None of the links seem to point to the actual study. Also note the limited way in which they determine "Metabolically Healthy". I smell BS

> New research reported in the European Heart Journal on September 4, 2012, reports that a large subset of obese people is in fact healthy. The research was reviewed at the Eureka Alert web site the same day.
>
>
>
> The findings hinge on one fact. A person can be obese but be metabolically healthy. Metabolically healthy people do not have the same risk of developing diabetes, heart disease, cancer, and other chronic diseases that obese people that are not metabolically healthy do.
>
>
>
> Dr. Francisco Ortega under the direction of Professor Steven Blair at the University of South Carolina examined 43,265 participants in the "Aerobics Center Longitudinal Study" (ACLS).
>
> The researchers followed the ACLS participants from 1979 until 2003 and periodically measured every participants medical and lifestyle history, cardiorespiratory fitness on a treadmill test, height, weight, waist circumference, and percentage of body fat,
>
>
>
> The results indicate that 46% of the obese participants were metabolically healthy.
>
>
>
> Metabolically healthy but obese people had a 38% lower risk of death from any cause than their metabolically unhealthy obese peers, while no significant difference was seen between the metabolically healthy but obese and the metabolically healthy, normal weight participants.
>
>
>
> The researchers conclude that "There are two major findings derived from our study. Firstly, a better cardiorespiratory fitness level should be considered from now on as a characteristic of this subset of metabolically healthy obese people. Secondly, once fitness is accounted for, our study shows for the first time that metabolically healthy but obese individuals have similar prognosis as metabolically healthy normal weight individuals, and a better prognosis than their obese peers with an abnormal metabolic profile."
>
>
>
> Corroborating research that analyzed data from over 64,000 patients on the Swedish Coronary Angiography and Angioplasty Registry produced similar results.
>
>
>
> This is good news indeed for the 30% of people in Alabama that are considered to be obese.
>
>
>
> The researcher's recommendation, if followed, would reduce health care costs for half the obese people in Alabama and reduce the unneeded and unnecessary expenditure of Medicare and Medicaid dollars on a healthy population that has been deemed unhealthy by weight alone.
>
>
>
> Perhaps in it's zeal to heal and it’s need for government research dollars, the medical establishment in the United States and Alabama has been a bit overzealous in deciding that obesity is correlated with an “epidemic” of bad health.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T01:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-study-finds-46-of-obese-people-are-metabolically-healthy.3576/post-50835>

uh, yea - healthy in comparison to their SAD Peers. Everyone is in the same sinking ship!

_

The researcher's recommendation, if followed, would reduce health care costs for half the obese people in Alabama and reduce the unneeded and unnecessary expenditure of Medicare and Medicaid dollars on a healthy population that has been deemed unhealthy by weight alone._

Follow the money.

# Thread: My Vit D Experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-11T02:21:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-50849>

Jun 2011, my Vit D was 21ng/mL (ref range 30-100).

Started 10,000IU per day of D3 and retested:

Oct 2011: 41ng/mL

Jan 2012: 69ng/mL

May 2012: 80ng/mL

Stopped taking all supps, got min 30 minutes of full sun on bare chest/back/legs per day.

Sep 2012: 54ng/mL

Just wanted to post my observations of supplementing with D3, the lab result and time it took to raise/lower it in case anyone is fretting over taking too much or too little.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-11T02:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-50854>

Interesting.

My vitamin D in May was 44 (intermittent supplementing throughout the winter - not enough). I stopped all supplements and had lots of sun exposure - in a bathing suit for 1 -2 hours/day. My vitamin D last week was 54. Does vitamin D produced in the skin need pregnenelone, or is that bypassed?

### skline@peak.org — 2012-09-11T07:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-50907>

Didn't trust the sun in Oregon at all. Took 10,000 IU most days, missed some days. Stopped taking it for three weeks, then took it again for two more, FINALLY tested: 88 ng/ml.

I may try 5,000 IU and test again in six months.

### Destiny — 2012-09-11T13:39:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-50924>

When I did not pay attention to my D vitamin in the past, my GP always commented that I have a beautiful vitamin D !

I was supplementing 1,000 IU a day because I was totally avoiding the sun in the past 10 years since I am lightskinned.

Then, I increased my daily intake to 5,000 IU and my test was 113. I went down to 1,000IU and tested 78. Now, I am taking 5,000IU again.

For some reason, I can supplement my way up easy, not sure why.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-11T13:58:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-50929>

I'm just noticing that it's interesting that neither of us supplemented during the summer, and we both ended up at the same number - you on your way down, and me moving up.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-09-12T03:37:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-51102>

I stopped my vit d supplementation during the summer . I thought I was getting enough sun just tested at 22. now Im taking 8K IU. will retest in 3 months.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-18T21:40:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-vit-d-experiment.3578/post-52487>

November '11: 54 ng/ml

March '12: 124 ng/ml (via 20 - 40k/day sublingual drops) Dropped to maintenance supplementation of 10k/day.

June '12: Exatest shows elevated calcium so stopped all vitamin D supplementation. Eating fish roe and epi-paleo diet full of seafood. Some sunbathing.

September '12: Spectracell Micronutrient panel says I'm deficient in vitamin D  Took mega-dose of sublingual drops this morning.

# Thread: When inlaws give pretzels as a present....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-11T02:54:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50857>

Anyone have any ideas for what to do when inlaws give your husband a huge plastic jar of peanut filled PRETZELS?? I just want to scream.....because they know how important diet is to me so of all the things to give my hubby as a present for his birthday, they pick THAT!

Though he's not on the Leptin Rx, I try to have as healthy foods around as possible and incorporate as many of the principles in my cooking even for him which thankfully he is open to and is enjoying....I know I can't control others and what they do. It just makes me so frustrated. Maybe I just need to vent....

What do you guys do about stuff like this? Not stress I am sure....

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T03:07:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50864>

Re-gift! Take it to the office/church/school. Donate to charity.

Yeah, it's bad stuff, but the recipients would just be eating other grains if not for the pretzels.

### caroline — 2012-09-11T03:14:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50870>

I think a lot of people think pretzels are a healthy option. Just a thought.......... and venting is good! I cannot get a very dear friend to understand that I don't do wheat at all and she thinks everyone should have some sugar - need it for energy she says. Even tho she is very supportive she often thinks that just a little of something won't hurt. Now I just say no thank you - firmly - and grab an apple altho somedays I am winding up with way too many apples!!! She is a devoted follower of weight watchers and moderation is their mantra in all things.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T03:18:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50873>

Yeah, moderation. So you can get moderate relief from cancer/diabetes/heart disease...

### Destiny — 2012-09-11T03:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50876>

> **hope2conceive;51417 wrote:** Anyone have any ideas for what to do when inlaws give your husband a huge plastic jar of peanut filled PRETZELS?? I just want to scream.....because they know how important diet is to me so of all the things to give my hubby as a present for his birthday, they pick THAT!
>
>
>
> Though he's not on the Leptin Rx, I try to have as healthy foods around as possible and incorporate as many of the principles in my cooking even for him which thankfully he is open to and is enjoying....I know I can't control others and what they do. It just makes me so frustrated. Maybe I just need to vent....
>
>
>
> What do you guys do about stuff like this? Not stress I am sure....

How is the in-laws health? Maybe some educational intervention is due?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T03:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50879>

Don't be annoyed - gifting "treats" is such a part of our culture - you just can't expect everyone to be on our page. The tendrils of the idea of "moderation" and "now and then won't hurt" and "ok to do on a special occasion" go so deep, it would be an exercise in futility to expect others to really get it.

I think it is ok to smile and nod and show appreciation for thoughtfulness that WAS there. I really bet it wasn't purchased with "going against your wishes" in mind. Take it in the spirit which it was given.

hehe I know some will disagree, but that's my take - Family, goodwill, enjoyable moments are valuable to our health as well - why generate negativity that takes away from your health even further?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-11T04:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50890>

> **Nonchalant;51424 wrote:** Re-gift! Take it to the office/church/school. Donate to charity.
>
> Yeah, it's bad stuff, but the recipients would just be eating other grains if not for the pretzels.

That is real christian.......LOL

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-11T04:45:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50891>

People when they buy gifts don't necessarily think what you might like - they just want to buy something quick and get it over with. Rarely are people thoughtful with these kinds of gifts.

In 20+ years of severe anorexia, I was still being gifted with chocolates at all times. My very best friend would do this. Heck, my own boyfriend did. They knew I wasn't going to touch any of it. Yet it's the "accepted" thing to do, so they did. And everybody else, too.

What I'm saying is that gifts like these are symbolic. They have a meaning different from the actual item being given.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-09-11T07:23:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50910>

Make a wall decoration of them:

Take the pretzels, glue them to a large piece of posterboard in a design like a kaleidoscope. Add a little acryllic paint in the holes, like stained glass. Spray with varathane, so they don't drop salt on everything, and so that eating them is OUT OF THE QUESTION!

### caroline — 2012-09-11T08:13:39+0300

Cavemam - That is such a thoughtful answer. You can't expect others to be on the same page. I find that people do try to understand but don't quite get there.

### Michael — 2012-09-11T08:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50914>

> A leaky gut is a purely human adaptation.

[http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-1-who-are-we-really/](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-1-who-are-we-really/)

I take it a pretzel can't do a duck much harm.

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-11T20:09:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-inlaws-give-pretzels-as-a-present.3579/post-50989>

Y'all this is too funny. And Ealachan you're right - that's the issue....they gave it to my DH, not me, and why? Because he visited them before, ate them there and loved them (UGH! Probably because I don't buy this stuff...his snacks are usually trail mix, sometimes dark chocolate, and sometimes popcorn) so he loves it when away.

His family isn't all that healthy - the bro and SIL were infertile, never had a baby but tried till she went into menopause. She takes a drug every day for migraine prevention...

Nothing much I can do about it except make healthy foods palatable at home. But this thing just irked me and it was good to vent. Thankfully DH has been letting me feed him lots of ghee, Kerrygold butter, and EVCO, GF meats, all kinds of seafood, nut butters and he gained a nice amount of weight (was underweight before)...and hasn't complained about the lack of pasta, rice, potatoes at home. So it's not all bad...incidentally, he totally loves grass fed meat (always been a lover of seafood but growing up never had 'red meat' because the mom thought it was bad!).....

Anyway, thanks for all the input. Totally love this group

# Thread: cheated one day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-09-11T07:18:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-50908>

i went from 205 to 212 in one day of cheating. how is this even possible?? is it water weight?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-11T17:37:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-50954>

yes. it also depends on what you ate. didn't you just start?

### msclman — 2012-09-11T18:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-50961>

half way thru the 40 days

very discouraging. takes such hard work to lose and if you slip on one or two meals you gain it all back

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T18:42:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-50971>

> **msclman;51528 wrote:** half way thru the 40 days
>
> very discouraging. takes such hard work to lose and if you slip on one or two meals you gain it all back

Suck it up, buttercup,

Heh, just wanted to give tough luv. Nothing to do about it now but hit the plan again immediately. Hopefully it will drop as fast as it came.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-11T21:45:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51039>

a gain that large usually indicates a food intolerance

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-11T21:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51041>

> **msclman;51471 wrote:** i went from 205 to 212 in one day of cheating. how is this even possible?? is it water weight?

its called inflammation from sub optimal cellular signaling.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T21:51:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51042>

> **msclman;51547 wrote:** mmmm butter

Ok, if you are thinking of food like that - your dose is off. What/how much are you taking? Colleen would say skip a dose and see how you feel the next day.

This advice was a miracle worker for me. I fought the idea of skipping a dose for way to long (weeks!) but when I did it was like the sky opened up and angel's sang!

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-11T22:28:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51053>

> **Cavemam;51613 wrote:** Ok, if you are thinking of food like that - your dose is off. What/how much are you taking? Colleen would say skip a dose and see how you feel the next day.
>
>
>
> This advice was a miracle worker for me. I fought the idea of skipping a dose for way to long (weeks!) but when I did it was like the sky opened up and angel's sang!

definitely concur with this advice if you are feeling hunger.

### msclman — 2012-09-13T02:54:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51312>

doing 180 a day in the morning sub q in my abdomen. started at 140. skipped a day and got more hungry. thats why i went to 180. should i go to 200?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-13T17:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51442>

> **msclman;51897 wrote:** doing 180 a day in the morning sub q in my abdomen. started at 140. skipped a day and got more hungry. thats why i went to 180. should i go to 200?

nope, if you are hungry go down not up. it's counter intuitive but I would suggest skipping a day for dosing and if you are less hungry go down in your dose. if you manifest greater hunger consider upping then.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-13T18:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheated-one-day.3582/post-51451>

> **msclman;51897 wrote:** doing 180 a day in the morning sub q in my abdomen. started at 140. skipped a day and got more hungry. thats why i went to 180. should i go to 200?

the jump from 140 - 180 is too big. Make sure a week passed, then do another skip day. Going up or down should be from 10-20 at max

# Thread: Frozen shoulder syndrom
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frozen-shoulder-syndrom.3584/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-09-11T13:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frozen-shoulder-syndrom.3584/post-50927>

I had this happening on my left shoulder 4 yrs ago. Cortisol shots and physiotherapy helped. I gained most of my motion range back. Then, I started developing this on my right shoulder a few months ago. It was getting bad but now it got a bit better but it is not gone.

The getting better happened when I started Cytomel. That prompted me to google on it more. I was surprised to find that people with thyroid problems, diabetics, etc. experience this problem more often.

Anybody had any experience with this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-11T14:44:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frozen-shoulder-syndrom.3584/post-50935>

Interesting. I've had shoulder issues since I was about 13-14 from over use injury from training/competing at a national level in swimming. It became the worst my sr yr of college. Could swim freestyle w/o dislocating both shoulders. I mostly trained butterfly/breaststroke that yr. I had to stop competing in the mile that yr. the only time I swam freestyle was in the 200/400 IM. I wonder if that might have been my fist indication something was wrong.

### Stavity — 2012-09-11T15:39:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frozen-shoulder-syndrom.3584/post-50941>

I had this problem with my right shoulder a few years ago, and it just went away. I don't have any diabetes or thyroid problems. I believe it was my chiropractor that helped.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: I CANT eat cream anymore.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Qodesh — 2012-09-11T17:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-50958>

If I have heavy cream, I have stomach cramping about an hour after for a few hours. I'm nauseous and keep man-burping.

The 1/2&1/2 in coffee or cottage cheese has a very mild, hardly noticeable affect. I even had a milkshake about a week ago with NO problems.(I haven't had one of those in about 2 years)

I never had a problem with this until recently. Could it be the gut bacteria changing from high meat? And why some things are OK,........... but the HWC is killer!

After a few times of exploration and testing.................. I'm avoiding it.

Are those of you giving up HWC doing it _painfully_ voluntary? Or has anyone else had this happen to them and **had** to give it up to avoid pain?

### caroline — 2012-09-12T03:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-51095>

I have had this happen too - I ate a bit of a really rich dish with heavy cream and a bit of a very rich dessert \[social setting\] and I was sick all nite and most of the next day.

### Michael — 2012-09-12T14:23:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-51160>

> **Qodesh;51525 wrote:** If I have heavy cream, I have stomach cramping about an hour after for a few hours. ...
>
>
>
> I even had a milkshake about a week ago with NO problems.(I haven't had one of those in about 2 years).

I can't say I understand that. For most people if dairy products are problematic it seems to be either lactose or casein (or perhaps specific types of casein) that are responsible. The higher the level of fat, the lower that of these other components. Heavy cream ought to be better for most people, butter a little better than that, and ghee probably OK for most people.

So I don't know. Maybe it's the context in which you have it.

How are you with butter and ghee, since you didn't mention those?

Can you eat heavy goat's cream with no problem?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-12T14:55:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-51170>

You should consider BCM-7 AB testing if that is the case.

### Michael — 2012-09-12T15:30:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-51175>

> **Jack Kruse;51752 wrote:** You should consider BCM-7 AB testing if that is the case.

IOW, it's likely to be the source of his heavy cream.

While there's more casein in the milk in his milkshake, it may have come from a herd that quite by chance has more A2 cows?

[http://keithwoodford.wordpress.com/category/a1-and-a2-milk/](http://http://keithwoodford.wordpress.com/category/a1-and-a2-milk/)

### Qodesh — 2012-09-12T21:05:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-51228>

wow! thanks. This makes sense to me. It explains why the different reactions from different milk sources. Butter has no problem for me.

I will stick with goat milk until I can find some A2 cows.

### Momma28 — 2012-09-18T02:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-cant-eat-cream-anymore.3590/post-52254>

Hi Dr. K,

Is this a blood test? I read up on what BCM7 was and read that children with this can have cognitive impairment. My four year old deals wth this and I would like to have him tested. Is this repairable for him?

### Endless — 2012-09-11T19:34:15+0300

Can anyone help me with this? I really want to be able to somehow get the webinars onto my ipod so I can listen to them repeatedly until they sink in! I drive a lot for work and it's a perfect time to do it, but I don't know how!

### Pamela — 2012-09-18T18:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinars-on-ipod.3591/post-52376>

I agree but they are only in webinar format which is MP4. I have requested they be made into MP3 for downloading to iphone, ipod etc. Not sure why they are in Webinar format since on some it is only voice. Hopefully Misty or Dr. K will see this and reply.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T19:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinars-on-ipod.3591/post-52389>

The older webinars, including the hormones one (thankfully!) I was able to convert to mp4 with a program I got, and can do that on my idevices. Unfortunately the file is ginormous and my emails won't go through to send them to anybody. I'm going to burn a thumb drive I guess to take to my docs.

The newer quicktimes - I've not been able to figure how to "put" them on the device for easy listening - but you can DL it as needed, so it's POSSIBLE - but very impracticle when out exercising or whatnot.

### Endless — 2012-09-20T09:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinars-on-ipod.3591/post-52870>

What is the program? At this point I would be happy to just save them to my computer before the new website deadline....I need help with that too!

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-22T16:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinars-on-ipod.3591/post-53389>

I was able to save them all to my computer. Right-click the webinar link, and "Save target as".

B/c I know I'm going to forget it again I'm going to post my own instructions here  so my bad brain can find it:

putting webinars on iTunes:

\- download webinar (right click while holding down either option or control... can't remember ), wait for download to finish, click on webinar in downloads, it will open in quicktime

\- click on SHARE in the top bar in quicktime

\- choose iTunes

\- choose iPod/iPhone

\- export will start automatically

### tellmisty — 2012-09-28T02:20:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinars-on-ipod.3591/post-54905>

We went out of our way to communicate the changes coming so that everyone could prepare for them. It is not our intention to gyp anyone out of what they were expecting; that's why we made announcements via email, during webinars, and in the forums--to give people the opportunity to buy consults and download webinars before the switch.

I had many, many members email me about downloading the webinars, and got a lot of confirmations that they were able to download all of the webinars. I was not aware of anyone who was actively trying to download webinars but was unsuccessful before the switch... I believed we helped every single person who reached out for help to ensure they were able to download successfully.

If you contacted me because you were having issues before the new site switched over and we did not successfully help you download, open a support ticket and we will consider the circumstances on a case-by-case basis.

If you did not reach out for help before the new site switched over, there is not anything I'm going to be able to do for you. I got HUNDREDS of emails leading up to this change, so I feel that we communicated well and were very available to helping anyone who struggled or reported issues.
