# Thread: Hysterectomy advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2012-09-11T20:15:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-50993>

Hey all, here we go again. Another friend in need. She is having a hysterectomy this Thursday due to continuous bleeding for the last 2 years and no answers from any docs. What can she expect afterwards? They are leaving the f. tubes and ovaries. What will/won't happen without the uterus?

I know this has been covered somewhere but I have no idea where. Thanks for any advice

### janagram — 2012-09-11T20:30:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51000>

I had that...yrs ago. It did not send me into menopause, if that's a worry. My ovaries did their job. It's not great to lose an organ, but it's a last resort sometimes....

### vkiernan — 2012-09-11T20:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51011>

She is determined to go thru with it. I tried my best to talk her out of it. Just hoping I can help her down the road with some advice if things go awry.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-11T21:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51044>

I also went through this 12 years ago. I kept my ovaries and cervix. The cervix is really important for female orgasm. It didn't throw me into menopause.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T02:20:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51090>

> **indigogirl;51615 wrote:** I also went through this 12 years ago. I kept my ovaries and cervix. The cervix is really important for female orgasm. It didn't throw me into menopause.

That is great advice. I wonder if she thought about the cervix. I forgot about that. I will ask. Thanks.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-12T02:52:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51092>

> **MsYo;51573 wrote:** Dr Kruse refers to hysterectomy as "female castration". My OB/GYN strongly recommended to me and I refused and fired her.

Me too. It's unfortunate that your friend is insistent on going through with this barbaric surgery. I practically ran out of the OB/GYN office screaming. This was about 3 years ago; I found low-carb, then paleo, then Dr. Kruse, now epi-Paleo + CT 4-5 times/week = no more excessive bleeding or pain. And I still have all my parts.

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-12T03:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51113>

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ff5IOSj1l7w&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Ff5IOSj1l7w\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ff5IOSj1l7w&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Ff5IOSj1l7w)

It is her body, but she needs to make an informed decision about it. It is not a useless organ that once it is past it's used by date can just be discarded with no effect.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T04:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51117>

> **PrimalPam;51666 wrote:** Me too. It's unfortunate that your friend is insistent on going through with this barbaric surgery. I practically ran out of the OB/GYN office screaming. This was about 3 years ago; I found low-carb, then paleo, then Dr. Kruse, now epi-Paleo + CT 4-5 times/week = no more excessive bleeding or pain. And I still have all my parts.

I'm finding that with most people, healing themselves naturally is tooooo much effort and they won't go there. I have another friend who has ulcerative colitis and according to her, there isn't anything worse than this and diet will NOT help cuz it is autoimmune. I gave up trying to lead her down a different path. I will heal myself naturally and come out way better in the end. I might not find relief today, but it is coming. The friend who is having the hyst. could have tried thermal ablation and was going to but then realized it might not work so opted for major surgery. This thought process really confuses me! You'll take major surgery over something way less invasive and non-destructive!

2 of my sisters watched my brother and my mother die right along with me and you'd think they would get it. Not! One of them is even a nurse. I give up on them too. I have spent soo much time explaining and teaching and after a while it got to be why waste my time when they didn't want to make any changes anyway. They listen but don't really hear.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T04:30:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51124>

> **MsYo;51694 wrote:** You are right to heal yourself. I remind myself everyday. Once healed I can give a show/tell testimony with facts and data.
>
>
>
> My OB/GYN went as far as to make a snide remark regarding my GIANT uterus and how sex must be uncomfortable. "Just get those things out" is how she said it. Mind you I've had 2 myomectomy surgeries and not once have I done a hormone panel until I requested one from my primary care physician. I had already started taking DIM and in one month my clotting was almost non-existant. She still did not do any horomone testing. SHE'S FIRED. All she can do is my Ultrasounds every 6 months and that's it!!
>
>
>
> Here is some reading for your friend. Reference only...not recommending. The reviews are enough to make anyone reconsider and look at other options. [http://www.energeticnutrition.com/vitalzym/fibroid\_tumors.html](http://http://www.energeticnutrition.com/vitalzym/fibroid_tumors.html)

Very interesting. I wonder if any doc actually even checked her for them.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-12T05:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51139>

> **TribalSpice;51687 wrote:** [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ff5IOSj1l7w&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Ff5IOSj1l7w\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ff5IOSj1l7w&feature=youtube\_gdata\_player\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Ff5IOSj1l7w)

>

I am becoming more and more disgusted with conventional medicine. If patients can read and understand these studies and stories - why can't doctors?? I just don't get it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-12T05:30:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51140>

> So, IMHO, I OWN my health and just because I am a patient doesn't mean that the recommendations are in my best interest.

I'm finding out that in this country I don't get to decide what is in my best interest. Apparently. Doc knows better.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:52:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51142>

V, what I try to remember is that 2 years ago I was largely ignorant and pretty sure my doctors were right when I was facing major surgery. Dr K always says the teacher appears when the student is ready. I guess we all get to ready at different rates... and some never. I don't try to 'educate' anyone who isn't interested. And when their eyes glaze over even after they've asked, I just change the subject. It takes energy to get optimal and I'm going to save that energy for me and those who are committed. Otherwise, it's just wasting energy and increasing my own stress. At the same time, I try to sympathise with my friends who are dealing with health issues but refusing to stray outside of CW. I say what is honest (e.g. 'I'm really sorry you're having to go through this' etc) and offer compassion but sometimes that's all you can do (as well as remind myself that I, too, was once totally blind to this reality and wouldn't have seen it no matter how well someone spelled it out).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51143>

> **Souldanzer;51717 wrote:** I'm finding out that in this country I don't get to decide what is in my best interest. Apparently. Doc knows better.

This is NOT limited to the US unfortunately. I think you can reduce it to the Fat Head movie's mantra.... 'follow the money.'

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T14:24:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51161>

> **KiwiLauren;51719 wrote:** V, what I try to remember is that 2 years ago I was largely ignorant and pretty sure my doctors were right when I was facing major surgery. Dr K always says the teacher appears when the student is ready. I guess we all get to ready at different rates... and some never. I don't try to 'educate' anyone who isn't interested. And when their eyes glaze over even after they've asked, I just change the subject. It takes energy to get optimal and I'm going to save that energy for me and those who are committed. Otherwise, it's just wasting energy and increasing my own stress. At the same time, I try to sympathise with my friends who are dealing with health issues but refusing to stray outside of CW. I say what is honest (e.g. 'I'm really sorry you're having to go through this' etc) and offer compassion but sometimes that's all you can do (as well as remind myself that I, too, was once totally blind to this reality and wouldn't have seen it no matter how well someone spelled it out).

You are right and next time I will do a better job of distinguishing those who are seriously interested and those who are not. I did meet a gal Sat. night at that meeting who was truly interested and she was able to share back; needless to say, the conversation was very interesting to both of us. It is very rewarding when someone "wants" to know rather than those that feel like you are shoving something down their throat. Thanks Lauren, I hadn't thought about it like that before. After all, it is 'their" life not mine they are ruining. That in itself changes everything.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T14:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hysterectomy-advice.3592/post-51162>

Lauren, You just made me realize that I know a lot of sick people. This is very sad. Everywhere around you someone has something. In my little dept. at work along, one has thyroid, one has asthma, one has diverticulitis and Lymes, my boss just lost his gall bladder and everything I have. That leaves 1 person and he is knew so I don't know if he has issues yet or not. We all range from 37 to 52. Not old in my book. The one that has ashtma thinks she is completely healthy cuz her doc told her so; so of course, she keeps doing everything she is doing and doesn't change a thing. What does doc k say in hormone 101, if you have any of these 10 diseases, you have some major problems. I agree with Soul's comment about us getting it and docs not but I think that stems from them not reading outside the box. They don't have the issue and they feel they were taught correctly. Can't really blame them. It usually does take something happening in your own life to trigger a response.

# Thread: Underweight with severe ME, chronic infections  adrenal dysfunction
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underweight-with-severe-me-chronic-infections-adrenal-dysfunction.3594/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### moongirl — 2012-09-11T21:16:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underweight-with-severe-me-chronic-infections-adrenal-dysfunction.3594/post-51018>

Hi folks,

I'm new here...I joined the forum as I just recently heard about CT and have been considering doing it to see if will help with my longstanding health issues. I used to do alternating hot & cold showers in the past but nothing really intensive. Anyway this morning Ive sort of gotten started...i had a pretty cold, longish shower, and definitely felt an increase in body temperature afterwards and a lowering of brain inflammation which was good as thats one of my worst symptoms. Later on I felt weaker, my b/p was lower than normal (85/55), and body temp was 35.4 C (usually low anyway), and I also had what felt like some kind of detox reaction - I felt extremely irritable, which for me might have indicated die-off/herx (similar to when Ive taken antibiotics). Ive been ill for a long time (19yrs) with ME/CFS and should mention that through testing Ive found out that I'm harbouring several intracelluar infections including lyme, chlamydia pneumoniae, and I tend to get a lot of inflammation, particularly in my brain and have poor detox. I'm also quite adrenally insufficient (have both low and high cortisol at different times of the day), and adapt very poorly to stress....I'm also a bit underweight and don't have a lot of fat on me...so Im wary of the CT promoting more fat/weight loss.

Ive not been tested for leptin and dont follow the leptin diet strictly, as my adrenals are too weak and can't reduce my carbs too much. I follow a moderately paleo diet.

Anyway I'm just curious to know if the CT has helped anyone out there with similar health problems? Are there any potential dangers of blood pressure, cortisol, dropping too much? Ive read conflicting info about cortisol...I'm a bit confused as to whether CT raises it or lowers it, or regulates it?

Thanks!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T21:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underweight-with-severe-me-chronic-infections-adrenal-dysfunction.3594/post-51036>

Hi Moongirl, welcome to the forum.

You may want to slowly ramp up the CT. Another way of doing CT could be an ice pack under your neck as you sleep.

When is your cortisol high? In the morning or evening? How is your sleep?

I've found myself that CT in the morning is great, when my cortisol is highest, but also I do very well with CT at night, when my cortisol is quite low. I sleep well either way.

### moongirl — 2012-09-11T22:15:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underweight-with-severe-me-chronic-infections-adrenal-dysfunction.3594/post-51050>

Hi Nonchalant,

Thanks for your reply and the welcome  yeah I'll definitely take things slowly.

Last time I was tested my cortisol was high (over the range) in the morning, but the low during the day and evening. Its interesting that the CT helps u when your cortisol is both high and low. I'll definitely experiment with CT at different times of the day, and will consider trying the icepack too. My sleep is not \*too\* bad....i dont get into stage 4 very often and dont wake up very refreshed, but I do manage to stay asleep most of the night, most nights.

cheers


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Mercury/yeast advice please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-09-11T23:02:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51060>

My 7 year old daughter is having tics and they are getting very aggressive, my heart is breaking for her. I suspect yeast is part of the problem and she is taking Nystatin (and having horrible die-off symptoms) I just got back her Mercury numbers (from blood) and they are 20 (elevated) I guess 10 or 15 is normal. I feed her packed tuna many times a week. Would seafood be enough to cause these or is it most likely from her vaccinations 3 years ago? SHould I take her off seafood? Anyone have advice on chelating while getting rid of yeast? I am a mess trying to find the right answers. Thank you

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-11T23:52:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51065>

Bren,

I'm so sorry you and your daughter are going through this. If it were me, I would totally cut the tuna - yes we all know seafood is good, but she's eating it now and that doesn't seem to be helping her. I have zero experience in all of this, except a friend who's son has tics (he spins and grabs his crotch). They went paleo for awhile, cutting grains - and it seemed to lessen, but came back with stress.

I would take special care with any chemicals etc (food dye, shampoos etc) and start talking to different professionals and see if you can find one that has treated these with success.

Have you done a GAPS type thing with her yet? Might be something you can do NOW while you look for medical help.

So, why do you think this is mercury?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-11T23:58:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mercury-yeast-advice-please.3595/post-51067>

Most canned tuna has soy in it. Starkist, Chicken of the Sea, etc. Also tuna is relatively high in mercury.

Maybe try sardines or salmon?

To rid the body of mercury, add selenium (brazil nuts) to bind with mercury and cilantro to eliminate the bound mercury.

[http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS\_graphic.html?tid=sm\_gplus\_wap](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/2012/04/03/gIQABd16sS_graphic.html?tid=sm_gplus_wap)

### TribalSpice — 2012-09-12T03:37:18+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-12T05:19:56+0300

Bren, I'm dealing with my own Hg issues and +1000 on ditching the canned tuna. (Though I'm guessing it's harder to face one's own toxicity issues than those of your child!!!) Not only is it canned (so full of BPA etc) and a big fish, but it's cooked at such high temps before canned that she's probably getting very little benefit from it. Find something with cilantro she loves (like the pesto TS suggested) and get her to eat heaps. And yes, make sure she's getting selenium from Brazil nuts. Also, lots of seaweed or iodine, too. I'd also be working hard on her gut - you can't get rid of toxins if you re-absorb them. Supplement with B vitamins, too, to help the methylation pathways. (You can also test for how well these detox pathways are working.)

In the meantime, try not to stress and take things one step at a time. Easier said than done, I know. But if you stress a lot about it, she will too and this will worsen things for her health.

Hang in there. XO

# Thread: Sarcolipin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-12T01:36:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/post-51081>

Don't recall anyone talking about sarcolipin before, but maybe another path to CT.

[http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html](http://http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html)

Muscles that burn energy without contracting have yielded new clues about how the body retains a constant temperature â€“ and they may provide new targets for combating obesity.

Traditionally, the body's main thermostat was thought to be brown fat. It raids the body's white fat stores in cold conditions to burn energy and keep the body warm.

Muscles also play a role in keeping the body warm by contracting and triggering the shiver response â€“ but this is only a short-term fix because prolonged shivering damages muscles. Now it seems that muscles have another way to turn up the heat.

"Our findings demonstrate for the first time that muscle, which accounts for 40 per cent of body weight in humans, can generate heat independent of shivering," says Muthu Periasamy of Ohio State University in Columbus.

Surviving the chill

Through experiments on mice that had their usual thermostat â€“ brown fat â€“ surgically removed, Periasamy and his colleagues proved that a protein called sarcolipin helps muscle cells keep the body warm by burning energy, almost like an idling motor car, even if the muscles do not contract.

All of the mice had their brown fat removed, but some of them had been genetically engineered to lack sarcolipin too. These rodents could not survive when held at 4 Â°C, and died of hypothermia within 10 hours. By contrast, mice that could make sarcolipin were able to survive the chilly temperatures and maintained their core body temperature â€“ despite having no brown fat.

Periasamy also showed that an inability to make sarcolipin made mice 33 per cent heavier than normal when fed a high-fat diet. This suggests that idling muscles might also help combat obesity by burning off excess energy. The search is now on for drugs that perform the same role, triggering idling muscles to burn off excess fat.

"The most interesting finding is that mice unable to make sarcolipin are more susceptible to obesity," says Andy Whittle of the University of Cambridge, who is testing spicy dietary treatments to ramp up the fat-burning activity of brown fat. "The research demonstrates that muscle is an important component even in mice, which have comparatively more brown fat than humans. In humans, burning fat in muscle is likely to be even more important for proper energy balance."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-12T03:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcolipin.3596/post-51106>

Very interesting, yet predictable response to the findings...produce another drug!

Why not just get cold?

# Thread: Looks like someone else is starting to recognize circadian rhythms in Obesity..
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BenG — 2012-09-12T10:14:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/post-51146>

[http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868)

Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity

Hadas Sherman, Yoni Genzer, Rotem Cohen, Nava Chapnik, Zecharia Madar and Oren Froy2

\+ Author Affiliations

Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot, Israel

↵2 Correspondence: Institute of Biochemistry, Food Science, and Nutrition, Robert H. Smith Faculty of Agriculture, Food, and Environment, The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Rehovot 76100, Israel. E-mail: [froy@agri.huji.ac.il](mailto:froy@agri.huji.ac.il)

Abstract

Disruption of circadian rhythms leads to obesity and metabolic disorders. Timed restricted feeding (RF) provides a time cue and resets the circadian clock, leading to better health. In contrast, a high-fat (HF) diet leads to disrupted circadian expression of metabolic factors and obesity. We tested whether long-term (18 wk) clock resetting by RF can attenuate the disruptive effects of diet-induced obesity. Analyses included liver clock gene expression, locomotor activity, blood glucose, metabolic markers, lipids, and hormones around the circadian cycle for a more accurate assessment. Compared with mice fed the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet restored the expression phase of the clock genes Clock and Cry1 and phase-advanced Per1, Per2, Cry2, Bmal1, Rorα, and Rev-erbα. Although timed HF-diet-fed mice consumed the same amount of calories as ad libitum low-fat diet-fed mice, they showed 12% reduced body weight, 21% reduced cholesterol levels, and 1.4-fold increased insulin sensitivity. Compared with the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet led to 18% lower body weight, 30% decreased cholesterol levels, 10% reduced TNF-α levels, and 3.7-fold improved insulin sensitivity. Timed HF-diet-fed mice exhibited a better satiated and less stressed phenotype of 25% lower ghrelin and 53% lower corticosterone levels compared with mice fed the timed low-fat diet. Taken together, our findings suggest that timing can prevent obesity and rectify the harmful effects of a HF diet.â€”Sherman, H., Genzer, Y., Cohen, R., Chapnik, N., Madar, Z., Froy, O. Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity.

### Destiny — 2012-09-12T13:26:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looks-like-someone-else-is-starting-to-recognize-circadian-rhythms-in-obesity.3600/post-51151>

> **BenG;51725 wrote:** [http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868](http://http://www.fasebj.org/content/early/2012/05/16/fj.12-208868)
>
>
>
> Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity
>

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Disruption of circadian rhythms leads to obesity and metabolic disorders. Timed restricted feeding (RF) provides a time cue and resets the circadian clock, leading to better health. In contrast, a high-fat (HF) diet leads to disrupted circadian expression of metabolic factors and obesity. We tested whether long-term (18 wk) clock resetting by RF can attenuate the disruptive effects of diet-induced obesity. Analyses included liver clock gene expression, locomotor activity, blood glucose, metabolic markers, lipids, and hormones around the circadian cycle for a more accurate assessment. Compared with mice fed the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet restored the expression phase of the clock genes Clock and Cry1 and phase-advanced Per1, Per2, Cry2, Bmal1, Rorα, and Rev-erbα. Although timed HF-diet-fed mice consumed the same amount of calories as ad libitum low-fat diet-fed mice, they showed 12% reduced body weight, 21% reduced cholesterol levels, and 1.4-fold increased insulin sensitivity. Compared with the HF diet ad libitum, the timed HF diet led to 18% lower body weight, 30% decreased cholesterol levels, 10% reduced TNF-α levels, and 3.7-fold improved insulin sensitivity. Timed HF-diet-fed mice exhibited a better satiated and less stressed phenotype of 25% lower ghrelin and 53% lower corticosterone levels compared with mice fed the timed low-fat diet. Taken together, our findings suggest that timing can prevent obesity and rectify the harmful effects of a HF diet.â€”Sherman, H., Genzer, Y., Cohen, R., Chapnik, N., Madar, Z., Froy, O. Timed high-fat diet resets circadian metabolism and prevents obesity.

Dr. Kruse has better study subjects ... people! It nice to see that it works for mice too !

My question is what will I notice when it is working and how will I know if I am taking enough or not. Any info on this or fat mal. is great appreciated. I did a lot of searching the last few nights and there is a lot more I'd like to know. The web ties this to celiac in almost every article I've read which makes sense. I have also read that as I get celiac under control (let my body deflame), my fat mal. will go away.

Thank you all for any input!

### Destiny — 2012-09-12T15:23:24+0300

> **vkiernan;51748 wrote:** I recently found out I have fat malabsorption and ox bile salts were mentioned to assist with that. My question is what will I notice when it is working and how will I know if I am taking enough or not. Any info on this or fat mal. is great appreciated. I did a lot of searching the last few nights and there is a lot more I'd like to know. The web ties this to celiac in almost every article I've read which makes sense. I have also read that as I get celiac under control (let my body deflame), my fat mal. will go away.
>
>
>
> Thank you all for any input!

I started taking ox bile salts when I started Leptin RX simply because of the change to the fatty diet last November. Since I had no gallbladder, I had many fat intolerance symptoms. At one point I thought, I will never tolerate more than a small amount of fat. The ox bile did not work right away even though I was taking it 3 times a day. I carried on and eventually realized that I started tolerating fats much much better. Now, I am down to 1 capsule in the morning and doing great. I have no symptoms of fat intolerance anymore. I can eat any fat!

This is what I have been using:

[http://www.amazon.com/Jarrow-Formulas-Bile-Factors-Capsules/dp/B0013OSHHK/ref=sr\_1\_5?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1347452487&sr=1-5&keywords=ox+bile](http://http://www.amazon.com/Jarrow-Formulas-Bile-Factors-Capsules/dp/B0013OSHHK/ref=sr_1_5?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1347452487&sr=1-5&keywords=ox+bile)

### vkiernan — 2012-09-12T15:41:56+0300

Thanks Destiny. I think you are saying you would get D. I have the opposite problem from a bad colon (like Jack says, everything in context right) and I still have my gallbladder. Although, most of my family doesn't. This prompts me to work harder at it. So, I am trying to figure out what will happen. TMI, but I think it will no longer float. But that is all I can figure out. Glad to hear it didn't work right away(ykwim) cuz I have not noticed anything yet. I have the same one that you recommended. I am taking a couple with each meal and more if it is fattier.

### ealachan — 2012-09-12T16:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51186>

> **vkiernan;51760 wrote:** Thanks Destiny. I think you are saying you would get D. I have the opposite problem from a bad colon (like Jack says, everything in context right) and I still have my gallbladder. Although, most of my family doesn't. This prompts me to work harder at it. So, I am trying to figure out what will happen. TMI, but I think it will no longer float. But that is all I can figure out. Glad to hear it didn't work right away(ykwim) cuz I have not noticed anything yet. I have the same one that you recommended. I am taking a couple with each meal and more if it is fattier.

Major TMI ahead - you have been warned:

Floating poo is a big sign of fat malabsorbtion, I believe. I have no gallbladder and was getting urgent runs right after eating a fat-heavy meal, and a combo of digestive enzymes and ox bile helped a LOT. I used to sometimes get floaters as well (on the rare occassion my poo was actually solid). Now I don't usually get runs, I don't have floaters, and my poo is usually well-formed rather than loose / ragged.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-12T18:42:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51213>

Hi V! I got the same results on my Metametrix GI fx test. My doc said to start taking ox bile with meals and use a high poptency one. Well I got a 500 mg one and it was toooooo much and gave me the big D every time I took it. So I went down to the 125mg and adjusted from there. I usually take 4 HCL 15 min before BAB and then w/meal take 250mg ox bile+digestive enzyme+lipase (to help w/fat), lunch (if I eat anything I just take the enzyme and then dinner I take 3 HCL+250 mg ox bile+one enzyme.

I think you should start and play around with the dosages until you find what works. I like the 125mg ox bile because I can adjust on the fly. When I travel I look like a card carrying pill pusher. I have little plastic bags (1/3 snack baggie size) that I use for my 3x day supps and then I have all these digestive aids I have to bring with me. I laugh when my bags go through the xray (I decline xray, of course) and wonder what those guys looking at the contents of the bags are thinking!

### Jude — 2012-09-13T04:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51355>

> **Destiny;51922 wrote:** I had to stop beatine hcl after a few days only. It gave me a serous stomach pain like when I had a gastritis. I am fine just on ox bile.

Same happened to me. Have been through the D from too much -(500mgms). The interesting thing that I have found is that I've really been able to reduce the amt to 25mgms daily ox bile in the past year. No gall bladder and absolutely no toleration for fat for 10 yrs-gave me such gut pains 2 hrs after eating and such bad burping etc.

What I've found is that now I can tolerate a BAB with no ox bile supplement. No ill effects,-and everything comes out alright! Presume that must have sufficient bile for breakfast. Only take it if meal looks extra fatty--and hasn't been that often lately- especially since I've been eating epi paleo.--what a change.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T05:32:34+0300

I also had teh cyrex test for cross-reactivity of foods that act like gluten in our system. I have a few things I cannot eat due to that but most of them are grains anyway. Thank goodness coffee and chocolate weren't on my list! Coffee is a treat to me these days though. I stopped it a year ago to help heal my adrenals.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-13T05:43:58+0300

Thank goodness coffee and chocolate weren't on my list! Coffee is a treat to me these days though. I stopped it a year ago to help heal my adrenals.

Congrats on being able to have coffee and chocolate. I am trying to convince myself to give them up and I am struggling. That would be my only treat too. As for the gluten, it can show up for a year or two. That is why that test is good and also it shows up a lot sooner in your stool than blood, etc. I was doing a lot of reading and if you read on Enterolabs, they give very good explanations. I am probably going to do their test that consists of the gluten (2nd opinion) but also quite a few foods like dairy so I know exactly what I shouldn't be eating. If I can add back to the menu, that would be a happy day.

Do you think you have celiac? Everything I have read points hashis's to celiac and fat mal. to celiac. Sounds like all 3 are working off each other.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T06:37:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51387>

> **vkiernan;51967 wrote:** Congrats on being able to have coffee and chocolate. I am trying to convince myself to give them up and I am struggling. That would be my only treat too. That is why that test is good and also it shows up a lot sooner in your stool than blood, etc. I was doing a lot of reading and if you read on Enterolabs, they give very good explanations. I've wondered about the Cleiac myself and have heard many connections but I figured at this point it is mute for me since gluten is definitely a thing of the past. I don't cheat at all when it comes to gluten and I am quite cautious. Even eating sushi we go to this one place 90% of the time and the sushi chef/owner knows we can't have any rice or flour so he makes everything special for us. Lots of sashimi and rolls with no rice.

Are there other things you have read about that we Hashis/Fal Mal/Maybe celiac should be concerned about? Are you going to take any other special precautions if you are diagnosed? I researched celiac a couple years ago and since I didn't have any symptoms I stopped researching. My head has become filled with hormones and other stuff these days!!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-13T14:44:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51404>

> **Patty Cakes;51973 wrote:** I've wondered about the Cleiac myself and have heard many connections but I figured at this point it is mute for me since gluten is definitely a thing of the past. I don't cheat at all when it comes to gluten and I am quite cautious. Even eating sushi we go to this one place 90% of the time and the sushi chef/owner knows we can't have any rice or flour so he makes everything special for us. Lots of sashimi and rolls with no rice.
>
>
>
> Are there other things you have read about that we Hashis/Fal Mal/Maybe celiac should be concerned about? Are you going to take any other special precautions if you are diagnosed? I researched celiac a couple years ago and since I didn't have any symptoms I stopped researching. My head has become filled with hormones and other stuff these days!!

Ok, this is my own bad thinking here and I'm sure someone is going to hammer on me for it. For myself, if I have celiac, I know I am eating poison. Otoh, if I only have a gluten sensitivity, then I don't feel like I am eating poison just something not very good. Perhaps I need to read more on the diff. TMI warning, I DO know without a doubt that I have glut sens. as everyday after breakfast (yes, good ole' whole grains) I would bloat up so damn bad that my pants were ridicuously tight and eventually the gas would come out that was created from it and I would start a new day the next day. This went on for years. I had no clue as this was my life and the only way I knew it. It took me years to realize I was lactose intolerant. If I made a roast, carrots and potatoes for a meal along with a glass of milk, within 15 min. of dinner my stomach went into overdrive and the gas was bad and non-controllable. It took me a long time to make the connection. I also couldn't eat cheap ice cream. Same effect. Eventually I put it all together and quit the milk. I did not have that problem with cheese, custard, etc. Cream was ok but milk no way. I have learned that my poor health history goes way back. All this time docs have been telling me I am sooo healthy when in fact I was slowly killing myself right under their noses. If your blood pressure is fine (actually mine was always low 80/50 but that didn't matter) and you don't have a weight problem, you are HEALTHY!

I'm pretty sure I am kidding myself about the diff. between celiac and glut sens. I have probably even stirred my dh in that direction as he is under the impression that I can eat some gluten if I am only sens. to it. I am going to have to do some serious reading on the diff. I think that is why labeling me celiac carries a lot more weight for me for him to get it. I know he has to be struggling with all of this. I work for him a couple evenings a week and we would usually grab something to eat out. This can't happen anymore and he thinks it only can't happen if I have celiac.

I have a new friend that has been going thru all of this for a long time and she and I basically have the same stuff going on. Diff. is she has no money worries and is actually currently in India for 3 weeks getting some special ayurvedic treatments 2-4 times a day. But, she is very intelligent and insightful and is really helping me take a different stance on all of this. She also owns a gluten free restaurant and her dh is a chef. She, like Jack, is looking at all of this for health and doesn't take the approach like I do as in just a little on occasion please!!! Yes, I always want to be healthy but I want that little cheat every now and then. I'm not talking even monthly; I think when you tell me I cannot do or have something, I fight all the harder. Mind thing for sure. That is an honest response and I know it sucks and my thinking needs to change or I will never get to level of health that I'd like. She even recommended a few docs in the Chicago area and I might just try the one out. Sounds like she has had everything we have and then some and managed to heal herself. Good person to have in my back pocket.

I think we are all very trained to having "treats" and when you say no more "treats" it is sort of penalizing in a way like you've done something wrong. Like a kid who didn't clean their room and can't have a cookie. I have to get over this thinking!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T18:55:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51452>

You are so lucky to have a friend who is in the same place as you are. And someone who is a researcher and looks for ways to get healthy. I wish I had someone local who is like that. I look to my forum friends for that because most people think I am a freak! But if sounds like you definitely may find a good new doc in Chicago.

I have one close g/friend who has Hashis and another on thyroid meds who I think has Hashis but she has never been tested. One g/friend has had a double mastectomy due to breast cancer, one has had radiation due to breast cancer (twice) and two who "seem" to be healthy but are just now getting into the peri-meno phase of life. Looking at that I see a group of gals who are not healthy. My Hashis friend listens to me but it takes her about 6 months to start something. She is now paleo and completely gluten free. She just recently has been doing this detox thing from her naturopath and she now has her antibodies so low they are within range. I'm not totally sure what that means as far as Hashis goes, but at least she isn't in an active attack all the time. I hope she will heed the light cycles by the first of the year. MHS probably will probably never happen!

Maybe treats could be something else for you. A healthy treat..... I grew up with a health nut mother and that was her philosophy. We could have healthy treats, those things with no sugar. Most kids would get real yummy treats coming home from school. Not me. I got a bowl of raisins and sunflower seeds to eat or a piece of fruit. Change the thinking to make the "good for you" item your treat. Whatever that is for you. I'm not sure if I'm supposed to eat chocolate but I found this fabulous 91% smooth dark with no soy lecithin in it and one square is all I need to feel like I had something treat-like. Can you have chocolate? Maye I'm enabling you by suggesting other treat ideas. Oh boy.....I may not be helping here!!!

### Lyndra — 2012-09-13T20:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-malabsorption-and-ox-bile-salts.3602/post-51474>

I have a friend with celiac disease (and lupus and too many others to count). She is extremely sensitive to gluten in any form. She can't be in a house where someone is baking because the flour in the air affects her. She had to switch to shampoo without gluten and that was very hard for her to find. I wonder if you're being exposed to gluten in ways that you hadn't imagined?

# Thread: Making Fish Head Soup
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-09-12T15:59:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51179>

I've been cooking fish head soup in my crockpot on low for 18 hours and some of the bones, skin, and eyes haven't totally dissolved. Will they ever? How long should I cook for them to dissolve?

This is a leap for me. I do not like fishy tasting fish : (

I added clam juice, carrots, celery, onion, kombu, fresh parsley, thyme,3 red snapper heads and water to cover. Anything I could have added to make it taste less fishy? I hope to cultivate a taste for fishy tasting fish...

### Michael — 2012-09-12T16:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51191>

The eyes you should probably eat raw.

The bones and skin are unlikely to dissolve as far as I know, although maybe they'd get soft enough to chew. In practice you probably wouldn't cook them for long enough.

Fish broth is traditionally cooked for far less time than meat broth. I think something like a couple of hours is usual. I don't know the reasons - probably based on flavour. Traditional practice is probably a good guide unless one's sound information to indicate not.

I've made meat stock a few times but rarely fish. With the former you include a tablespoon or so of cider vinegar to help pull the minerals from the bone. Probably the same deal with the fish.

Sally Fallon will have directions in "Nourishing Traditions". There she quotes a South Anerican proverb to the effect that good fish broth can even "raise the dead"! But someone is bound to have put the instructions online, so try googling something like:

"sally fallon fish stock"

Or try a more general search - but she is pretty reliable. Or have a look at some of the WAPF or paleo sites. I think there's one called "The Nourishing Gourmet".

### indigogirl — 2012-09-12T22:33:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51248>

Thanks, Michael.

I probably cooked everything good out of it cooking it as long as I did!

### Michael — 2012-09-13T08:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-fish-head-soup.3604/post-51392>

> **indigogirl;51831 wrote:** Thanks, Michael.
>
> I probably cooked everything good out of it cooking it as long as I did!

I expect so. The WAPF is saying 4 to 24 hours. I'd understood chefs generally cooked fish stock for quite a bit less than meat stock, but maybe I was mistaken.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)

# Thread: Unable to sweat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unable-to-sweat.3606/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bennyha — 2012-09-12T16:10:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unable-to-sweat.3606/post-51181>

Hello. In the past year, I have gradually been feeling worse and worse to the point now where I feel awful everyday. I have very little energy and absolutely crash around 2:00 every day. Also, during the time, I lost my ability to sweat. I mean I cannot sweat at all. I am a 30 year old male who has always been athletic and energetic and had no problems sweating. In the past year or so, I have totally lost my ability to sweat, which coincides with me feeling horrible the past year. I was working outside on a deck this summer in 100 degree+ temps, and I didn't drop a bead of sweat. And the two guys I was working with were absolutely soaked all day and both mentioned that it can't be normal for me to not be sweating at all on a day like it was doing the work we were doing.

The two doctors I went to both said everything is fine, despite me feeling awful and having a long list of symptoms that I've never had in my life previously, such as:

\-inability to sweat

\-very low levels of energy

\-suddenly looking bloated and soft despite being very muscular and fit my entire previous life

\-low body temps (I avg. 97.5 degrees)

\-no sex drive (I mean none at all-despite being a 30 year old male)

\-very depressed/pessimistic toward all things in life

\-horrible acne (probably due to my inability to sweat and release toxins from my body)

I could go on forever. Also, I took some labs and my T3 came back low (2.5 on a 2.5 to 6.0 "normal" range) and my TSH high and out of range (3.5 on a range that says above 3.0 is high and out of range). Doesn't this along with my symptoms suggest I probably have some kind of thyroid problem? And why can't I sweat any longer?

### quelsen — 2012-09-12T16:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unable-to-sweat.3606/post-51183>

no sweating??? think lack of signal to keep water balance properly.... think a lack of aldosterone.... realize how low on the hormone chain that is ..... know your hormones are fraked

start with Vitamin D and DHEA and Pregnenolone....

if you cant afford labs eat 2 million iu of Vitamin D and start going to bed with the sun, take enough dhea to keep you asleep all night, work with pregnenolone until you get serious morning wood every freaking day....

take control and ignore dumb doctors who tell you everything is fine when you damn well know it isnt

Thank you for your replies. I did get some lab work done and I have no idea how to interpret the results and where to start. My DHEA was in the very end of the high range according to my labs. My testosterone was low as was my T3 and my TSH was out of range high. I don't know where to start like I said. ### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T05:20:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unable-to-sweat.3606/post-51377>

> **bennyha;51763 wrote:**
>
> \-very low levels of energy
>
> \-suddenly looking bloated and soft despite being very muscular and fit my entire previous life
>
> \-low body temps (I avg. 97.5 degrees)
>
> \-no sex drive (I mean none at all-despite being a 30 year old male)
>
> \-very depressed/pessimistic toward all things in life
>
>
>
>
>
> I could go on forever. Also, I took some labs and my T3 came back low (2.5 on a 2.5 to 6.0 "normal" range) and my TSH high and out of range (3.5 on a range that says above 3.0 is high and out of range). Doesn't this along with my symptoms suggest I probably have some kind of thyroid problem?

This definitely says thyroid problem to me but I am not a doc. Read all you can about it. [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) is a great resource website. There could be a multitude of reasons why you have a poorly functioning thyroid as well as the sweating issue. Another lab to test (I know you are low on funds but keep this in mind) is the TPO and TgAB antibodies test to see if you have the autoimmune version of thyroid disease, Hashimotos. You really need to find a doc...maybe at a local clinic where they charge based on your income???

Sounds like your testosterone is tanked too from pregnenolone steal syndrome (hence the low energy/depression)

# Thread: A convenient way to get your CT on and get lots of other stuff done too!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-12T17:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51201>

Hey guys, I just wanted to share something I was introduced to recently- Cool Fat Burner. My husband and I have started experimenting with it and are going to document our experience on our blog. The thing I like best about it is that I don't have to figure out creative ways to keep ice packs in place. I'm very active throughout the day with 4 boys, so it can be tricky. I've even found I can nurse my twinsies while I am CT'ing with this. I'm sure this can't take the place of complete submersion, but I think it's a fantastic option for those who have been trying to do spot CT throughout the day.

Here is the blog post: [http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/09/12/gettin-our-ct-on-with-the-cool-fat-burner/](http://http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/09/12/gettin-our-ct-on-with-the-cool-fat-burner/)

Hope this helps someone out

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-13T02:05:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51294>

This looks cool! (pun intended...lol). However, it says "Sold Out" unfortunately. I will check back though, as this is a great idea!

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-13T06:13:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51384>

I just got one in the mail today and the ice packs are in the freezer (had to get creative to fit them in there with the 30 1-quart freezer containers full of water to freeze for tub CTing, haha). Will try it out tomorrow and let you all know how it goes.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-14T22:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51686>

> **CuiviÃ«;51970 wrote:** I just got one in the mail today and the ice packs are in the freezer (had to get creative to fit them in there with the 30 1-quart freezer containers full of water to freeze for tub CTing, haha). Will try it out tomorrow and let you all know how it goes.

How did it go? We are loving ours!

### Endless — 2012-09-15T06:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-convenient-way-to-get-your-ct-on-and-get-lots-of-other-stuff-done-too.3607/post-51765>

# Thread: Question about Free vs Total Testosteron in Ladies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-12T18:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/post-51210>

Ok, so got some hormones back a few weeks ago. . .

Testosterone was 152, with a range of 7-46. So 3X the high of range.

Free Testosterone was 1.4, with a range of .2-2, so in range.

I stopped all DHEA supps, thinking that was effecting testosterone. . . and we re ran it 3 weeks later.

Docs office just called and said my testosterone didn't go down, it DOUBLED! Panic Panic Panic. Said other hormones were in range (for what that means)

I'm going in to talk to him in a few hrs. . .can anybody point me to the relevant blog posts or information on what I need to know about Free vs. Total in ladies - or anything else that might be relevant I need to brush up on?

I have none of the other PCOS signs, in fact have ruled that out based on other stuff. I will hopefully get the numbers on the rest of my hormone panel when I'm there today.

The other major symptom I'm having is hair loss - it's falling out. Could have been the 25 lbd weight loss, but this testosterone thing is a bugger.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-12T19:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-free-vs-total-testosteron-in-ladies.3609/post-51218>

> **Cavemam;51793 wrote:** Ok, so got some hormones back a few weeks ago. . .
>
>
>
> Testosterone was 152, with a range of 7-46. So 3X the high of range.
>
> Free Testosterone was 1.4, with a range of .2-2, so in range.
>
>
>
> I stopped all DHEA supps, thinking that was effecting testosterone. . . and we re ran it 3 weeks later.
>
>
>
> Docs office just called and said my testosterone didn't go down, it DOUBLED! Panic Panic Panic. Said other hormones were in range (for what that means)
>
>
>
> I'm going in to talk to him in a few hrs. . .can anybody point me to the relevant blog posts or information on what I need to know about Free vs. Total in ladies - or anything else that might be relevant I need to brush up on?
>
>
>
> I have none of the other PCOS signs, in fact have ruled that out based on other stuff. I will hopefully get the numbers on the rest of my hormone panel when I'm there today.
>
>
>
> The other major symptom I'm having is hair loss - it's falling out. Could have been the 25 lbd weight loss, but this testosterone thing is a bugger.

FWIW you don't have to have all the PCOS symptoms to be dx. my experience was that in general my concerns about my issues were pushed to the back of the pile... until I paid for a full hormone lab and included my both free and total T. my VA PCP looked at it last September and said WHOA - why haven't you been Dx'd w/ PCOS before this? especially after we discussed my symptoms. Some PCOS symptoms can be confused w/thyroid. You can have PCOS and not be infertile btw.

not saying this is you... don't forget CT can also raise T.

They weren't completely numb, just starting to feel that way. Same with the fingertips. These symptoms are all extremely recent, however, the interior sode of my large toes have been numb for several years (or "unknown" cause). I'm wondering if it's the cycloset I started taking. A1c 5.1

fasting Insulin < 2

Fasting 73

1Hour 134

2Hour 80

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-14T03:23:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51571>

Do a quick experiment, maybe. Stop taking Cycloset for a week and see what happens. (Keep monitoring blood glucose and the feeling in your fingers and feet.) I think that could be a quick first step maybe.

### mambo — 2012-09-15T09:01:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51774>

Those diabetic #s look good, especially fasting insulin. I don't think it's diabetic neuropathy. Did your neuro consider whether you may have autoimmunity? Lupus, Sjogren's, RA could all cause neuropathy. I would get ANA tested and see if you're positive for any autoimmune diseases.

> **bigknitwit;51804 wrote:** A1c 5.1
>
> fasting Insulin < 2
>
>
>
> Glucose Tolerance Test
>
> Fasting 73
>
> 1Hour 134
>

### maggie — 2012-09-15T23:55:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51872>

Your A1c looks great. There seems to be a wide range of opinion about what's acceptable for post prandial readings. I'd get upset about a post prandial number of 129 and would take a serious look at what I ate for lunch. I think the neuropathies start at much lower readings than generally thought.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-16T13:53:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-have-diabetes-type-i-ii-other.3613/post-51910>

I didn't like that post-pranial either. I had my regular lunch - fish sauteed in tallow, scallops, sauteed cabbage and carrots. Maybe I ate too many carrots. Or maybe my meter was giving inaccurate results.

# Thread: Epi-Paleo Impossible ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-impossible.3615/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-09-12T20:57:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-impossible.3615/post-51226>

I've been allergic to multiple foods over 20yrs, Ige positive and elevated eosinophils. Tried every allergy treatment that exists and traveled to the top allergists in the US. I've been unable to sustain any allergy treatment for long without developing a severe reaction.

Since reading about epi-paleo I've tried shrimp, cod, lake trout, whitefish, salmon, tuna and had either an allergic reaction or anaphylaxis. Ironically.. I live on Lake Superior and have access to fresh fish daily. I also test positive to crab & lobster but haven't tried them for years.

Otherwise I'm at a normal weight. I sleep 9-10hrs and get natural sunlight and CT. I have no symptoms of hormone issues or any signs of menopause. I take probiotics and try to follow the suggestions on the leaky gut forum.

Any advice for me?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-12T21:42:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-impossible.3615/post-51242>

yes......go to an alergist and get tested and ask about desensitivity training.......but when I hear this story I think low DHEA, Hashmoto's or some other oxidizing gut condition is present like an AI.

# Thread: Is there such a thing as a healthy balance?

### BJK77 — 2012-09-12T21:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51233>

I find myself seriously stressing out lately over not being absolutely perfect with CT and minding the light cycle. Thankfully diet is not an issue for me at all. Ever since I started on a real foods diet over two years ago when I began GAPS I can honestly say I havenâ€™t cheated once. Following the light cycle is proving to be a bit more difficult for me though and Iâ€™m afraid Iâ€™m so used to striving for perfection that every little failure is stressing me out to the point of detrimental health.

When I believe in something I go â€œhog wildâ€ with it. In my mind, there is no point in putting time and effort into anything unless youâ€™re going to give it all youâ€™ve got. I still wholeheartedly believe in that, but Iâ€™m beginning to wonder if itâ€™s possible to achieve some sort of healthy balance. I agree with Jack that moderation is not optimal and I donâ€™t want to shoot for mediocrity, but I donâ€™t know how to live life in this day and age and stick 100% by the light cycle and get in my CT. Iâ€™ve been doing a 30-minute cold bath every morning before work, but had to skip a day this week and a day last week because I had to go out of town and had to leave the house much earlier than normal. I found myself in near tears over it, feeling like a complete failure! Maybe some of itâ€™s just hormones, I donâ€™t know! Iâ€™m also stressed over the fact that I still canâ€™t manage more than 25-30 minutes in a 50-degree tub. I know itâ€™s related to my low T3 that I just canâ€™t seem to get figured out, but it still adds to the stress.

My main concern is dealing with the light cycle. Seriously, how does one actually LIVE in the modern world and not be exposed to light after 5 PM in the winter? Honestly Iâ€™d probably love nothing more than to be in a deep, dark, cold cave with just my family and not deal with the outside world, but thatâ€™s just not practical. We actually have been doing really well the last 2 weeks with implementing candles at sunset, no fake light/electronics, bedtime soon after, etc, etc. BUT with school just starting and my kids having activities they go to in the evenings and winter juts around the corner I donâ€™t know how weâ€™ll be able to continue that. Do I tell the kids they canâ€™t participate in any activities? They do things that help them grow and enjoy life in other ways so I donâ€™t think thatâ€™s an option. My son does karate and just started cub scouts. My daughter does a weekly book club, dance and participates in a youth small group that I actually lead. These are all worthwhile and enriching activities for them. For the most part theyâ€™re home by 7:30-8:00, so itâ€™s not even super late, but of course theyâ€™re going to be exposed to fluorescent lights the whole time theyâ€™re away from home.

I know my hormones are off now, so that could explain my needing to vent and my total stressing out here  I guess Iâ€™m just curious how others plan to manage it. If it were just me, I think I could easily give it all up, but I canâ€™t do that to the kids. I want them to be healthy, but I want them to enjoy life too. Make sense? Not to mention my daughter is at a really critical point socially. She just started middle school and I think itâ€™s a tremendous help to make new friends who share similar interests. There has to be a balance somewhere, right?

I found myself in near tears over it, feeling like a complete failure! Maybe some of itâ€™s just hormones, I donâ€™t know! Honestly Iâ€™d probably love nothing more than to be in a deep, dark, cold cave with just my family and not deal with the outside world, but thatâ€™s just not practical. We actually have been doing really well the last 2 weeks with implementing candles at sunset, no fake light/electronics, bedtime soon after, etc, etc. BUT with school just starting and my kids having activities they go to in the evenings and winter juts around the corner I donâ€™t know how weâ€™ll be able to continue that. Do I tell the kids they canâ€™t participate in any activities? They do things that help them grow and enjoy life in other ways so I donâ€™t think thatâ€™s an option. My son does karate and just started cub scouts. My daughter does a weekly book club, dance and participates in a youth small group that I actually lead. These are all worthwhile and enriching activities for them. For the most part theyâ€™re home by 7:30-8:00, so itâ€™s not even super late, but of course theyâ€™re going to be exposed to fluorescent lights the whole time theyâ€™re away from home.
>
>
>
> I know my hormones are off now, so that could explain my needing to vent and my total stressing out here  I guess Iâ€™m just curious how others plan to manage it. If it were just me, I think I could easily give it all up, but I canâ€™t do that to the kids. I want them to be healthy, but I want them to enjoy life too. Make sense? Not to mention my daughter is at a really critical point socially. She just started middle school and I think itâ€™s a tremendous help to make new friends who share similar interests. There has to be a balance somewhere, right?

I think the first step is evaluating (and getting honest) with what you want to do right now. For some of us, the weakness will be diet, for others CT, for others light, etc etc. You are 100% on diet, but you are not ambivalent about this issue. There are many others who are (taste, cost, giving up things they love, etc)... and they will construct a similar argument about why they shouldn't stick 100% to the diet.

\>For me, the point is to get aligned with yourself. The stress comes (I think) when we are pushing ourselves to do something we don't want to. It sounds to me like only part of you wants to be perfect with CT and lights; another part of you wants to prioritise other things. This is NOT a judgement. I think you need to know where you are at. Then you have a starting point to work from.

So... for example... you say to yourself 'these evening activities for my children are important to them and to me and I want to support them happening'. Then you can do things like get blue-blocking glasses or just start with candles/darkness when you arrive home, etc. If you deny the truth of what is important to you (realising that this may change over time) then you'll always be fighting with yourself (I am speaking from experience here!).

I really believe that ANYTHING we want to make happen, we can. We can live in our 'modern' world however we want. My family is proof that you can make it through a winter and many other months without any modern electricity or screens after dusk. But... my weakness is CT. I still dislike cold and I had to modify how I did CT over winter because I already live in a cold, unheated house and getting into an ice bath was sending my own stress sky high. That doesn't mean I couldn't have made it happen. But I didn't want to. So I went back to face dunks and taking walks without a jacket. It that perfect? Hell no. Did it work for me? It did.

I don't think you need to compare yourself to anyone else nor justify your lack of perfection. There are a lot of variables and it may help to put them in order of priority and then see what fits.

One last comment: I prioritised making sure that I got my own needs met. I was/am the sickest and the one who has the most ground to make up. And like that blog MJ wrote, I'd put my own needs aside. So when my children had evening activities, they went. I, however, did not go pick them up - I arranged for other parents to do that. I just wasn't willing to compromise for myself, but was willing to work out a compromise for them. Does that make sense?

If it's causing you stress, it's probably a signal that you are a) being too hard on yourself (which is not the same thing as being totally committed to a goal) and b) not aligned to what you really want right now.

Hang in there. We're all in this together

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-13T00:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51269>

I think you need to stop thinking in negatives and start thinking in positives.

If you fill your mind with all the things you can't, won't, don't want to do then your life with look exactly like that. Try it the other way around and watch yourself become happy and successful at what you do.

The other thing I want to say is that I think some people need to stop idealizing Dr K as the Optimal Angel that he isn't. He's got family, he's got kids, he's got a job that requires quite a few Optimal violations as far as I can judge that  Dr K uses computers at night, he works under bright artificial light a lot, he's got a less-than-optimal past. The difference is that he makes the best of it with the options at his hands - adapts - and doesn't get his cortisol up about the rest. Maybe he takes an extra dose of Resveratrol to make up for the rest

There's some things we don't have control over. Make your best choice, be happy, and hope for the best.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T01:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51286>

your perspective colors your reality.......and your perspective is not a healthy one to get to optimal. The journey is the part to experience. Instead of making this a task make it a challenging game to get back what you have lost. That is really what the primal sense blog post is all about. Thoughts can change our DNA. If your thoughts suck.......well you can figure out the rest.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-13T02:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51302>

ok, and now for a lesser opinion.

I think you are on a path and your speed along that path involves too many variables for anybody but you to judge what is acceptable. I "chilled the \*\*\*\* out", and let me tell you sister - it is the best move for me. I have found it important for my perspective to back up and try to live today in a reasonable way. If you are internally flipping out when you can't do what you "think" you need to do, it MIGHT be a sign that you need to figure out a way to adjust your life.

I realized, the adjustments I had to make were not more restrictions and getting freaked out over missing a bath or workout or BAB. Back up and take a 30K foot view of your life. If you are near tears and can't enjoy the good things in life that you DO have (interaction with family? whatever it is) then that doesn't sound right either.

I have been there on the "complete freak out over something petty". You can just give this up.

My perspective is that is unhealthy and inflammation generating. . and who knows what else you make worse with this.

I know "moderation" is a dirty word around here - but your life is a complex quilt, you can't just pull out one panel and only cover up with that. You have to have the entire quilt sewn together and in balance before you can snuggle up under it. Sometimes that means you give attention to health, sometimes it means you skip a bath for something else.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-13T02:31:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51307>

I learned something recently that I will share as it has helped me do some things I either didn't think I could do or didn't want to do. Everyone must come up with their personal "WHY?"? Why are you going to swim the English channel or summit Mount Everest or choose a particular lifestyle component or why you dip your body in a cold ice bath or .....? What is the real reason, it may be underneath the surface, that you are choosing to do something. It may be for your health or at least that is what you think it's for but maybe deep down it's because you want to be able to play with your unborn grandbabies. Ask yourself WHY are you doing something, get an answer and then ask yourself WHY to that answer to help you go deeper. I learned the "Five WHYS" while getting my MBA but honestly I have used that technique more to get in touch with me.

I appreciate your response Lauren. I always enjoy your posts!

I think you've really hit on something for me - I suppose I do need to learn to make myself more of a priority. I feel like I do this in some ways and maybe I slack in other ways. It's tough to get past the guilt - of not being there for every one of my children's performances, for not being the one to take them to all of their activities, for letting down 12 teenage girls who have come to depend on me (some of which see me as the only stable figure in their life). These are the things I feel obligated not to fail on and I think a lot of that is because I couldn't do many of these things 2 years ago. I was too ill to go out and I missed a lot and now that I finally can, I want to embrace it and cherish it all.

I love that you were able to make arrangements that worked for you while allowing your children to still lead their normal lives. I've been thinking about how I can work that out all day and spoke to a few parents. We are going to work out some carpooling, but I just don't have it in me to not take the kids at all, so I'll be taking them about once every 2-3 weeks . . . not so bad. I need to give some more thought to the small group I lead for junior high girls, but even that is only one night a week and the more I think about it, the more I feel like I'm going to be getting so much out of it that just maybe it's worth dealing with some fake light one night a week. Maybe in this case the benefits will outweigh any damages.

Thanks again for your encouragement!

### LinD — 2012-09-13T02:58:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51315>

I hear ya BJK777.

I started the Fall Bible study on spiritual warfare this Monday night AT 7PM (!) and realized, crap, there are lights on after sunset and a video to watch. Hmmmmm.

While I might be screwed fior this Monday night, I CAN order the frames I looked at last week (and couldn't decide on), but vow to get back to my eye dr office and decide and order the blue blocker lenses asap--and begin wearing them, as needed. I'll use those from time to time more than likely, given life circumstances. I will not stress, but do the best I can and then some.

At least you are 100% on your diet. I'm getting there. We are all a work in progress. Give yourself the grace to get there, too. You will if you want.

Blessings.

Got it. My thoughts were sucking earlier. It's a new hour now. Ha! My thoughts seem to change rather frequently these days. Thanks to this great forum I'm feeling a renewed sense of purpose. I will enjoy my journey to optimal and I'll do it while being the best me I can be living life to the fullest  I've never been one to turn down a challenge anyway!

Cavemam;51887 wrote:** ok, and now for a lesser opinion.

I like this Cavemam. Thanks for daring to share the 'unpopular' opinion. After I posted this I kind of expected to get hit pretty hard (though that hasn't happened). I suppose that's because truthfully we're all trying to figure out how to make this work for ourselves. It's easy to sit around on our laptops and say we're doing this or that and everyone else should be too. Damned be anyone who doesn't eat a raw fish head or sit in a 40-degree tub of ice for an hour every day! The truth is we all just need to do the best we can. And I'm realizing I need to accept that and not push myself for perfection - it's only gonna backfire on me and I'll never get to the great land of Optimalness

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T04:28:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-such-a-thing-as-a-healthy-balance.3616/post-51367>

Pressure? Nonchalant........time is not promised to you. Every day you waste is a day you will never recover in the road to optimal. Time is our most valuable asset. If your thoughts of beliefs subjugate you......who really pays? You do.........swim in that pool for a period of time. Embrace your discomfort. It is the best that you may ever get.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-13T06:14:35+0300
