# Thread: ok... please hack my smoothie!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-09-14T16:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51638>

Finding myself without the Inger classic formula ingredients (mackerel head and lemon) I have done the following for the last couple of days:

\*2 whole frozen portuguese sardines (6oz)

\*4-5 stalks of frozen asparagus

\*2-3 pickles OR c 1tbsp capers

\*a splash of apple cider vinegar or pickle "juice"

\*a splash of olive oil

\*1/2 cup or so of water

This all goes in the ninja until the chunks abate.

Then, I get to choke this down.

I follow this with a chaser of a 1+ cups of cold shrimp and wakame salad and then floss the scales and whatnot out of my teeth

verdict: I've kept it down... but i didn't want to.

Here is my dilemma:

I REALLY enjoy seafood, but this I approach with the gusto of eating pickled puppy face or the like.

I can't see this being sustainable for me.

Is there something that I get here that I won't in another way?

I am trying to determine my ideal juice vs. squeeze ratio.

Thoughts?

### Inger — 2012-09-14T17:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51641>

I would not use frozen sardines.. because they are so fatty and they get rancid fast. Even if frozen. IF you get any other fish, buy fresh. Fresh is best for sure, you can use any fish heads. If you do want to use the frozen sardines, check that they are as fresh as possible, and I would not puree the whole fish, why? Purreed fish is yucky.. lol. Why use the delish parts too in the smoothie? Too much yucky stuff to drink..lol.

I would puree only the part that is hard to eat or I do not like otherways. The meat / filet I always turn into something very yummy raw, like tartar. I wrap the tartar in nori sheets and it is so very yummy. For spices I use organic white wine vingegar (or lime), wild raw honey, fresh organic ginger and seasalt and chopped onion. I only puree the heads and liver and roe.

I do not care how my smoothie tastes like, I never add anything. It wan't get yummy anyways I guess... so who cares. I always drink the smoothie first and then I get to enjoy the rest of the fish as a delish dish. Works for me very well!

### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-09-14T18:03:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51645>

This makes a lot of sense.

Thank you, Inger!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-14T21:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51674>

Re: the smoothie, I too would recommend using just the heads (though I looked up fish anatomy and figured out where the liver was so I could cut just below there - it's sort of the top 1/3 of the fish). And I would add as little extras as possible. I just squeeze in 2 lemons and enough water to make it into slush. The more extras you add (esp stuff like pickles and asparagus) the bigger that smoothie gets. Mine fills just a small/medium size glass. By the way, I also use sardines (frozen, caught fresh) all the time and they don't cause me any problem. Good luck!

### caroline — 2012-09-15T00:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51704>

> **KiwiLauren;52272 wrote:** Re: the smoothie, I too would recommend using just the heads (though I looked up fish anatomy and figured out where the liver was so I could cut just below there - it's sort of the top 1/3 of the fish). And I would add as little extras as possible. I just squeeze in 2 lemons and enough water to make it into slush. The more extras you add (esp stuff like pickles and asparagus) the bigger that smoothie gets. Mine fills just a small/medium size glass. By the way, I also use sardines (frozen, caught fresh) all the time and they don't cause me any problem. Good luck!

I bought frozen "small yellow croaker" at my Asian store. Do you think they would be okay? I thought I would just use 1 of them per smoothie. Anyway - I thought - even if we bought fresh - we should freeze them first. waddayathink???

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-15T02:13:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51732>

> **caroline;52303 wrote:** I bought frozen "small yellow croaker" at my Asian store. Do you think they would be okay? I thought I would just use 1 of them per smoothie. Anyway - I thought - even if we bought fresh - we should freeze them first. waddayathink???

I do always use frozen when doing the smoothies. I'd guess what you bought would be fine, but I'm not a a fish expert. I thaw mine on the counter for a few minutes (otherwise my blender has fits). You can start with just the heads to make it easier. Good luck!! And don't forget your lemons!

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-15T14:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51782>

I had my first smoothie this morning!! I did use 3 whole fresh sardines from portugal which where pretty big. I put four lemons and a bunch of cilantro, celtic salt, ice and a bit of water. It was actually very tasty...it just looks so awful you have to keep your eyes away.

I can see how you might want to concentrate on heads and save the flesh for other more elegant eating, but this way I figured it wouldn't put me off if my first smoothie was tasty.

I was going to try it with a giant cod head I have from a 7 pound fish...one look at the bones and I knew my cuisinart wouldn't hold up, so it's on the stove turning into soup.

We've been trying to catch trout in our pond for the last few weeks, but no luck. They just aren't biting at the flies, lures, worms ....whatever we are using. There are a lot of late bug hatchings going on, so we'll keep trying. Fresh heads would be great and my husband can have the bodies...(that sounds kind of strange doesn't it?)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T19:16:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51825>

When I can get a Vitamix...I will try a fish head smoothie. Seems like a quick and easy on the go 'meal replacement' which is not some gross 'protein powder'. LOL

I am satisfied with soups though in the meantime...hehe.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T19:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51826>

Btw, I don't use protein powders at all. I do some integrated exercise, as in I swim in a lake, walk, and bike. I don't do any other 'intended' exercise.

I eat so much seafood (like everyone here). People always ask if I 'work out'. I say no. They don't believe me. It's true, when the body is able to repair itself during the night (sleep) then it can maintain a decent musculature on it's own. I am not wasting away or anything.

Also, I feel like I don't need to eat that much. I get satisfied from eggs, fish, avocadoes, bacon, coconut oil, seaweeds, etc. (I like sweet potatoes, sqaush, pumpkin during this season). I suppose if I wanted to train Olympic level I'd have to eat more but I am not an athlete so I just eat what I feel like.

I think I am 'chemically-reduced' (as Jack refers to).

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-15T20:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-please-hack-my-smoothie.3656/post-51834>

Does my signature show?

# Thread: Jack your thoughts?  IF The Warrior Diet

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-14T17:46:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-your-thoughts-if-the-warrior-diet.3657/post-51644>

Most of the articles I read I find agreement with on most of the issues. This one...seems to conflict quite a bit with things I've read here.

[http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/09/14/intermittent-fasting-benefits.aspx?e\_cid=20120914\_DNL\_artNew\_1](http://http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/09/14/intermittent-fasting-benefits.aspx?e_cid=20120914_DNL_artNew_1)

At first I thought they were typical Mercola articles...but didn't think Mercola wrote something called The Warrior Diet...than I noticed the author is Ori Hofmekler I guess hes a guest author for some of Dr. Mercola's articles?

So I'm curious about the discrepancies and why the mismatch. He seems to think Dinner is the most important meal and breakfast is the one you should skip. He mentions Circadian Rhythms as the reason sorta interesting.

Maybe I need to reread Leptin posts. As I don't really remember why it was important to eat three meals. I have been skipping dinner quite a bit and figured it might be ok as long as I eat breakfast. The three meals a day was important so that you aren't digesting all day long...and the breakfast was important for some reason....definitely need to reread as I'm forgetting all the important things I have a feeling.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-14T22:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-your-thoughts-if-the-warrior-diet.3657/post-51685>

Once your LS you can skip breakfast........but if you do it alters the gastrin cycle. CT 7 has all that.

# Thread: Light at night
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-14T19:00:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51652>

I am trying to figure out the issues of artificial light at night for my household. I am looking into Jack's sites about how light disrupts circadian cycles, and everything I pull up seems to emphasize that blue light after dark is the worst. Of course we already know this, it suppresses melatonin production, and disrupts the hormone cascade into the night, and the next day, etc. However, I can't find anything compelling that says I need to turn out the incandescant lights as well. Do we need to turn out the incandescants as well? Can someone point me to some compelling study showing it? I personally, want to get this done for my family (candles after dark). But my (technology loving, stay-up-late) husband needs more data before he's willing to get on board for the time before the kids go to bed.

### ealachan — 2012-09-14T20:10:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51665>

I believe that the quick-and-dirty is that normally your brain doesn't start producing melatonin until you've been in complete darkness for 4 hours. When you sit there with the lights on in the dark, you're basically giving your brain a whole lot less time to produce the melatonin that you desperately need.

Oxytocin appears to be a short-cut on the 4 hour thing. If you have an orgasm and your brain releases oxytocin, you then go straight to melatonin production without having to wait for 4 hours of darkness to pass.

Again, just my rudimentary understanding.

edited to add: In regard to your husband wanting articles - Dr. K has posted several links to good ones on his FB feed lately, including one to a monster paper (over 100pgs) done by the European Union back in the spring of this year. It's dense reading, but interesting stuff.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-14T22:24:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51683>

Your understanding is fine.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-14T22:51:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51687>

Okay, so I need to look for sites that say we don't produce melatonin unless we are in complete darkness.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-15T00:09:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-51698>

He doesn't want to read Jack's interpretations, he wants to see the cites.

I sent him this one ([http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/48682547/ns/health-mens\_health/#.UC5BG47T1UQ](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/48682547/ns/health-mens_health/#.UC5BG47T1UQ)), but he took home that you need to sleep in darkness (okay, this is a good first step!), and that people who sleep more have less cancer, less depression. I also sent him the article about blind women getting less breast cancer (here: [http://m.medicaldaily.com/articles/7369/20111011/cancer-sleep-cancer-epidemiologist-steve-jobs-antioxidant-melatonin-cortisol-hydrocortisone.htm](http://http://m.medicaldaily.com/articles/7369/20111011/cancer-sleep-cancer-epidemiologist-steve-jobs-antioxidant-melatonin-cortisol-hydrocortisone.htm)). He said, the study failed to remove the fact that the blind women got more sleep than the women who weren't blind. Sleep has been shown time and again to help longevity, cancer, etc. So it "could" be simply that the if the non-blind women got the same amount of sleep as the blind ones, they would also get less cancer. And, quite frankly, he has a point.

I'll look at CT-7 cites and all, next.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-17T18:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52132>

> **bigknitwit;52743 wrote:** How did you handle the kids? Mine are young, and I can't "just remove myself to a dark room" in the evenings when they are awake. If kids were not part of the equation, I wouldnt have an issue.

Kids - well, back this spring/summer I didn't worry about the kids. couldn't had to worry about me and me only. that didn't mean I didn't want them to be sitting in that light. but as Jack has said you've got to fix you first, then you can worry about others.

my situation was different than yours b/c dh actually had an appt in Jack's spine clinic - and basically dh walked out of his office as a changed man. - threw out all the grains and crap out when he got home, and asked me for orange glasses. Its only been recently since we've really started pushing the lights at night. initially it was just at story time, then it became at bath time and now its dinner time. when I get home from work I no longer turn on any lights in the house. as soon as it gets hard to see in the house on go the candles. we try to be outside during dusk if we can - b/c it makes it easier - cooking on the grill, etc limits the need to use lights inside. we've also taken advantage of the nice weather to use the fire pit at night and do story time there instead of in the house.

both kids have their own orange glasses, and we try to use them when the lights are on at night - but both parents are using this.

parenting is about compromise - why not approach DH about doing it his way for a month (already doing this) then trying a month your way - your question to DH is what can one month hurt? why not try it and see what happens Call it an experiment in your children's behavior, and ask him to see if he notices a difference in the kids. also ask him to see if he's noticed a difference in his own patterns. you might be surprised - the kids will get into it, and may not want to go back to DH's way... mine now refuse to eat at night unless the lights are off and candles are lit. and yes occasionally DH grumbles - b/c lets be honest - 2yo aren't the neatest eaters on the planet. (its a lot harder to clean up a toddler mess in the dark - but we're working on it - good thing the dog likes what's left of the floor!)

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-17T18:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52148>

Thank you for this... I'm not worried right now about my kids either - just fixing me (as selfish as that sounds - 2 of mine have autoimmune conditions). Dh is against them living in the dark after sunset. I could make a dark room(s) for myself, but the kids still have free reign in the house. They are 11, 9, 6, 3. The older 2 spend a lot of time working on their lego league challenges after school and after dinner. They have a dedicated room for this, so I could potentially let lights be on in there and just not go in, and not allow the younger 2 in there after dark, because I can't go in after them (lit environment), and they're the ones most likely to cause chaos requiring my intervention! The younger 2 want to be where I am, so in theory I could watch them and keep them entertained in dark lit rooms. They will adjust to this. Dinner could be before dark (usually already is before 5pm, so not too bad to do). I won't have free reign of the house, especially after dh gets home from work (lights on)... So all preparations for the next school day, dinner clean-up, etc, has to happen before he returns. That's a challenge, but doable. I still have after-school activity driving to do (piano lessons, gymnastics drop-off/pick-up, soccer, etc). I am responsible for this. In these circumstances, I could use the blue-blockers while out and about. Maybe I should get an attractive pair... Often we have to wait from piano lessons to finish (1 hour), and I take my youngest shopping to pass the time. Or, i can see about moving their lesson time earlier after school (if it's available). We'll still be in the dark in Nov/Dec/Jan returning (dusk is at 4pm near solstice), but it will be closer. No way around the gymnastics pick up time (6pm) If I fill in the imperfections with the blue-blockers, am I still making moves that will effect my health positively, or is it closer to an all or none deal?

> **Shijin13;52749 wrote:** Kids - well, back this spring/summer I didn't worry about the kids. couldn't had to worry about me and me only. that didn't mean I didn't want them to be sitting in that light. but as Jack has said you've got to fix you first, then you can worry about others.
>
>
>
> my situation was different than yours b/c dh actually had an appt in Jack's spine clinic - and basically dh walked out of his office as a changed man. - threw out all the grains and crap out when he got home, and asked me for orange glasses. Its only been recently since we've really started pushing the lights at night. initially it was just at story time, then it became at bath time and now its dinner time. when I get home from work I no longer turn on any lights in the house. as soon as it gets hard to see in the house on go the candles. we try to be outside during dusk if we can - b/c it makes it easier - cooking on the grill, etc limits the need to use lights inside. we've also taken advantage of the nice weather to use the fire pit at night and do story time there instead of in the house.
>
>
>
> both kids have their own orange glasses, and we try to use them when the lights are on at night - but both parents are using this.
>
>
>
> parenting is about compromise - why not approach DH about doing it his way for a month (already doing this) then trying a month your way - your question to DH is what can one month hurt? why not try it and see what happens Call it an experiment in your children's behavior, and ask him to see if he notices a difference in the kids. also ask him to see if he's noticed a difference in his own patterns. you might be surprised - the kids will get into it, and may not want to go back to DH's way... mine now refuse to eat at night unless the lights are off and candles are lit. and yes occasionally DH grumbles - b/c lets be honest - 2yo aren't the neatest eaters on the planet. (its a lot harder to clean up a toddler mess in the dark - but we're working on it - good thing the dog likes what's left of the floor!)

### Lyndra — 2012-09-17T19:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52152>

I won't use candles either. I know too many people whose houses have caught fire because of candles, including one who died. I'm making do with orange light bulbs everywhere and blu-blockers. Is that optimal? I think risk/reward has to factor into a determination of optimal. I've forgotten to turn off a burner when I removed a pot, sometimes for hours. I can easily imagine letting a candle burn somewhere unattended, maybe even overnight. I have pets which greatly increases the risk of something untoward happening with an open flame. My assessment of my personal situation is that candles are not worth the risk. I think it's great that you are making it work, as that seems to be more true to the principle of no lights after dark. But I can absolutely see why someone cannot get behind that practice.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-17T20:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52164>

> **Lyndra;52769 wrote:** I won't use candles either. I know too many people whose houses have caught fire because of candles, including one who died. I'm making do with orange light bulbs everywhere and blu-blockers. Is that optimal? I think risk/reward has to factor into a determination of optimal. I've forgotten to turn off a burner when I removed a pot, sometimes for hours. I can easily imagine letting a candle burn somewhere unattended, maybe even overnight. I have pets which greatly increases the risk of something untoward happening with an open flame. My assessment of my personal situation is that candles are not worth the risk. I think it's great that you are making it work, as that seems to be more true to the principle of no lights after dark. But I can absolutely see why someone cannot get behind that practice.

I'm with you, I've been too close to someone who died in a fire - and I have a healthy amount of care and knowledge that "crazy bad things DO happen". Candles are ok now and then, but I don't want my less than careful child thinking we do things with candles - as the next step in his independence will be to do it himself and set himself on fire. I've had a lot of people in my family die from freak accidents - I'm taking no chances! Lighting strikes the same people over and over!

edit: He is also not driving till age 25, nor is he going to be riding with anybody under the age of 25. We have said this to him over and over again since birth so it's not a surprise. I'm fully prepared to move to a city where nobody has cars if that is what it takes.

### Jonathin — 2012-09-17T20:12:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52170>

I have set up a variety of homemade amber LED lights for reading and general room lighting in our small house. The amber LEDs are a very narrow band of light and contain no blue or green light. I have done it in a way so that the level of light is purposely low but sufficient for normal activity. Of course, the power cost is minimal and they last for about 50,000 hours. If you reduce the voltage or add a diode to a regular incandescent bulb they take on an orange-ish color. A turnkey solution is to install standard dimmer switches. I think there is a do-able solution for every situation. Depending on the space and the air circulation, even the cleanest burning candle will lower oxygen levels. I guess that might help with sleep.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-at-night.3659/post-52185>

A lot of the lights in my house are already on dimmer switches, and very orange on the lower light settings.

# Thread: The 4th most important thing you need to know about diet and fitness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-09-14T19:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51653>

Before I start, I should emphasize that these posts are "in my opinion"... and I realize my opinion comes from the other side of the bridge. I just want to make that clear before people start complaining about me not seeing it from their perspective etc etc... this is not the debate. This is just my opinion, thats all.

No.4: Create your own orchestra with the best conductor.

To recap

1\. There ainâ€™t noâ€¦ nothing is more important than the other, everything counts

2\. Listen to your own body and go on feel

3\. Optimal does not have to be extremism

This leads me on to no.4. What sparked me to write this was a recent ted talk I watched about the paradox of choice. You see most people think choice leads us to freedom and happiness. However, the opposite is often true. Too much choice can make us paralyzed and this results in us becoming dissatisfied.

It is this paradox of choice that I see happening everyday when it comes to health and fitness. You can go paleo, ketogenic, epi-paleo, VLC, LCHF, etc etc. You can do crossfit, movnat, tabatha, metcon, eccentric loading, high rep, low rep, run/walk, cycle/swim. These are all choices and they all have their merits. However, the amount of choice and the sheer number options is what can lead to paralysis. You simply cannot decide on what is right for you due to you not being able to figure out what is the best choice! The other problem that arises then is when you do actually make a choice and it doesnâ€™t work, or more importantly, you think it doesnâ€™t work as good as it should. This then leads to dissatisfaction and regret.

Let me put this into perspective for the people following this forum. You now have the choice of light cycles to follow, blue light glasses to wear, fish smoothies to make, veg to ferment, temperatures at which to CT, hours to intermittent fast, oils to cook with and tests to take. These are all choices. You might CT at 55F, eat salmon, use ghee and fast for 14hrs. But you donâ€™t lose weight and your health doesnâ€™t improve. You then look and read further and realise that shrimp is better, you should cook with avocado oil and not ghee, CT at 45F is better and so is fasting for 16hrs. If this keeps happening, and you are provided with even more choice as you go along, you are on the road to paralysis and dissatisfaction. This is obviously completely counterproductive and is the antithesis of what you are trying to achieve. Contentment, happiness, positivity and self satisfaction are what you want with the aim of reducing the very thing that is likely causing your problems in the first place i.e. stress !

An analogy that can be used is that all these choices can make up your own orchestra. You canâ€™t have all the best musicians in the world in the same orchestra. So lets say you choose a certain number of them. Being content and satisfied that you have the best you can get is first base. However, regardless of how good these musicians are, they donâ€™t play well together unless you have the best conductor. The conductor in this case is your thought process. As long as you have the right thought process, the choices will work well for you. More importantly, even the most average musicians can play well together if the conductor is top class. Thatâ€™s the key. Everything relates back to the central command centre, Epigenetics.

So create your own orchestra. Donâ€™t worry too much about how big or how talented your orchestra is, just make sure youâ€™ve got the best conductor.

â€œBeyond the walls of intelligence, life is definedâ€￾

### Inger — 2012-09-15T07:29:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51768>

Good points Barry. To me Jack has limited my choises greatly..lol

Now I go shopping.. directly to the fishcounter.. I know what to pick. It is very easy.

I know.. minimal fake light 24 / 7.

As cold as possible, what I feel comfortable with.

The adaption occurs.. slowly but it is fun. I start to crave MHS, oysters (sadly I get them not too often).. cold..

I thought my cold adaption is sooo slow, it barely happends. I do baby steps, swim in the river 3 to 10 minutes every day. River is around 50-52 degree F. I do facedunks most mornings for 3 minutes. Thats it. Have it cool in my appartement.

Now, these babysteps must have done something because yesterday my brothers were here and we all got in the river together. One could barely do 20 seconds and the other that already have done some CT could do only 2 minutes. They said it hurts! I feel no pain. I could not understand. Then I realized I must have adapted to some degree! I can swim for 7 minutes before I start to feel stiff. Ok I thought it was nothing because others here do 1 hour in icebath.. but in comparison to my strong and fit brothers I still did very well. I guess we need to not compare with others, just do as good as we can and enjoy. We need to enjoy the trip otherwise it makes no sense. Our thoughts. Exactly as you says Barry. The toughts are so very important. I often wonder about why someone struggles so bad and have these negative thoughts. Jacks concept is not hard to do. It is very easy. It is much about to letting go. Letting go of choises. Everything becomes so much more simple.

My brothers have not read Jack sites at all.. but they do so well!!! 2 of them use only candlelight at nights so far, and go early to sleep.. and eat lots of seafood and MHS when I make them one.. I am so proud of them. They do not know ANY of the science behind, but they intuitively know it is the right thing to do. They still have the mind of a child in some way even if they are 22 and 26 yo. Like.. "if we do not become like children, we can not enter heaven.." I think I know what this is about. It is huge. It is so beautiful. It makes one so happy and free.

Life is so much about letting go... very paradoxical. Who tries to save ones life will loose it... You need to experience this to know what it is about. It is magic for sure.

### Barry — 2012-09-15T17:23:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51813>

Apart from choice , the message I am trying to get across relates to the other point that the author of the book makes .. Life is about creating your own fish bowl.

What I see happening here is that people are taking the advice and recommendations to the extreme. Most then realise that modern life, family, work and just general hassle means that implementing properly everything is actually more trouble than it's worth.

This is the issue I have with chasing "optimal" . You are more likely to reach paralysis and dissatisfaction if you continue the chase. So create your own fishbowl and chase contentment not perfection.

Again , just my 2 cents...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T17:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51816>

I dont see it as extreme.......i see it as looking at life through our biochemistry. When you do that, what you thought was extreme because of socialization and culture then become bizarre. How you feel trumps what you are taught to believe is true. Once you get in tune with that it becomes pretty easy. Just my two cents.

### ealachan — 2012-09-15T18:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51821>

I have to agree with Inger's post. I don't see "chasing optimal" as having given me MORE choices, but rather, as having simplified my life. I am one who truly used to get paralyzed by choice. Now, I have a few things I know to shoot for: fish, cold, sunlight, darkness. Everything else is just kinda...background noise, now. I know I'm not perfect, and frankly, I'm not worried about ever BEING perfect. I spent a whole lot of years feeling really effing \*\*\*\*ty, and starting to feel GOOD now is more than enough to keep me motivated to keep "chasing optimal".

Optimal, to me, is like nirvana - it's something you strive to acheive and you'll probably never actually GET it, but that's ok because it made you a better (or healthier) person through the striving. It's a life's work. It's not a quick fix.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-15T21:51:31+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T23:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-51866>

I think I understand what Barry is alluding too......I agree with him that all of our present biochemistry is a puzzle......so everyone has their own fishbowl.......but where he and I dont agree.......there is an epistemologic basis of how the laws of biochemistry apply to our species.......and that is universal for our species. I am trying to show you how it works.......and not how we think it works. I guess that is where Barry has his issue..........so I get that.

### Linz — 2012-09-17T12:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-52082>

Barry, Ten years ago nothing was extreme in my fishbowl. I lived a normal, happy life with a great deal more respect for the natural environment than most. Brought up with home grown meat and veg and plenty of holidays camping on the coast and catching fish to cook for breakfast, healthy athletic children who were not afraid to go out in the freezing cold in winter or race to be first into the sea on holiday, they even enjoyed eating raw shellfish on occasion and no home computer. I enjoyed an athletic, outdoor lifestyle. My doctor even asked me what I did to stay so healthy!

Chronic illness puts a whole new perspective on life. A few symptoms and a hospital visit sent me sliding down a horrible slippery slope 'but don't worry this can be kept under control if you keep taking the medicine'. Just what I wanted to hear so I didn't listen to the tiny voice in my own head. Seven years ago when it became obvious that the drugs were not even masking the problem effectively and were themselves causing metabolic havoc I took a leap of faith and changed my diet radically. Thanks to Jack I now know the real reason why it worked (probably why it happened in the first place), why some things went wrong on the way, and I am making much more progress. When you face a lifetime (probably short) of chronic disease cold baths and dim lights don't really seem so extreme - and the raw fish was already an occasional part of normal life.

Could I run 100k....... I'm going to need a lot of good science first. Curing ourselves by taking our natural lifestyle to more extreme than is usual is hugely preferential to any kind of medical intervention. Would I have taken that more extreme action before seeing the effect of chronic disease and artificial steroid chaos on myself? Ice bath or a few little white tablets?

Some of the players in my orchestra still need some serious retraining, and all our children urgently need proper scientific education on their natural envronment so they never need to go extremes to rebalance.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-17T14:12:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-52085>

Adaptation reduces extreme to normal.

### Barry — 2012-09-20T23:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53014>

> **Jack Kruse;52480 wrote:** I think I understand what Barry is alluding too......I agree with him that all of our present biochemistry is a puzzle......so everyone has their own fishbowl.......but where he and I dont agree.......there is an epistemologic basis of how the laws of biochemistry apply to our species.......and that is universal for our species. I am trying to show you how it works.......and not how we think it works. I guess that is where Barry has his issue..........so I get that.

not quite. My issue is that what applies to our species now has several variations. This is due to adaptation, gene expression and mindset. Ironically, I think you are possibly overlooking the very thing you are focusing on.

In my opinion, the biggest thing the paleosphere and krusosphere has overlooked is how people have managed to live long healthy lives that don't conform to the "epistemolgic basis of the laws of biochemistry" ... why aren't we looking at the these people ?

Okay, so the Inuits prove that high fat/zero carb diets don't cause heart disease. Fine. But what about the french village, where the people ate croissants everyday, and never died from heart diseases, not given the same attention ?? What about the italian families that eat fish once a week, yet have never had any cognitive decline or disease and lived into there 90's ?? What about my Irish grandmother that hits 100 next week and is nowhere near senile, yet has lived on diary and starches ??

I am on the same side as the fence with you when it comes to the biochemistry. Its simply the application and the variation where my thoughts differ. You seem to believe that in order to be healthy, disease free etc that you need to follow the exact ancestral epi species specific way of eating and living. In terms of ethnicity, evolution and epigenetics, how can this not have changed ?? And aren't the people that I just described living proof of this ??

My 1-4 blogs are just spin-offs of the sort of mindset that this creates. Chasing perfection leads to extremism. There is no perfect. Choice leads to dissatisfaction. Ideology raises expectation. High expectation distances us from happiness. Happiness is the goal.

The questioning continues, thats all this is...

### Inger — 2012-09-21T14:03:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53143>

### Barry — 2012-09-21T18:36:46+0300

### Inger — 2012-09-22T08:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53368>

>
>
>
> My goals are the same , real science that helps people. I am just looking at it from a completely different angle so my perception is different . I'm not claiming to be right and I'm not saying Jacks perception is wrong. I'm simply thinking out loud like you , but my thinking is just different to most on here.

I think I got it Barry!

Your perception is different from most of the forum because you are very healthy. That is why you think different..?

Most on here are very broken that's it. I think I see you now Barry.

Now, to regain health you need to change things dramatically. It won't work going on like you did before because that is what made you sick after all. But people are often afraid of change. That is when it becomes painful and a horror. When forced. It does not need to be. If we loose our fear for the unknown it is exciting to make changes, very much so. Then we start to feel like flying.. But it starts all - with our thoughts. Like you said.

And yeah.. I think it is very important to feel allowed to do baby steps. There is only so much we can take, not more.. but it gradually increases. I do very small babysteps all the time. And once in a while a big one..lol. But I have also quite a good health in comparison to many here (but.. when I see what I can reach I am so far from that - this fact makes it only so much more exciting to me). The sicker the more extreme/strict one needs to be... or the faster the healing. Kind of backwards huh. But I guess, when one is suffering grately one might also be more willing to do extreme things to feel well again soon. I hope so at least..

It requires a lot of willpower to turn the wagon.. when it is heavy. Where does the willpower comes from?

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-22T20:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53441>

> **Barry;52251 wrote:
>
> It is this paradox of choice that I see happening everyday when it comes to health and fitness. You can go paleo, ketogenic, epi-paleo, VLC, LCHF, etc etc. You can do crossfit, movnat, tabatha, metcon, eccentric loading, high rep, low rep, run/walk, cycle/swim. These are all choices and they all have their merits. However, the amount of choice and the sheer number options is what can lead to paralysis. You simply cannot decide on what is right for you due to you not being able to figure out what is the best choice! The other problem that arises then is when you do actually make a choice and it doesn’t work, or more importantly, you think it doesn’t work as good as it should. This then leads to dissatisfaction and regret.
>
>
>
> Let me put this into perspective for the people following this forum. You now have the choice of light cycles to follow, blue light glasses to wear, fish smoothies to make, veg to ferment, temperatures at which to CT, hours to intermittent fast, oils to cook with and tests to take. These are all choices. You might CT at 55F, eat salmon, use ghee and fast for 14hrs. But you don’t lose weight and your health doesn’t improve. You then look and read further and realise that shrimp is better, you should cook with avocado oil and not ghee, CT at 45F is better and so is fasting for 16hrs. If this keeps happening, and you are provided with even more choice as you go along, you are on the road to paralysis and dissatisfaction. This is obviously completely counterproductive and is the antithesis of what you are trying to achieve. Contentment, happiness, positivity and self satisfaction are what you want with the aim of reducing the very thing that is likely causing your problems in the first place i.e. stress !
>
>
>
> “Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined”**

**

Hi Barry,

I have enjoyed the conversation you have started. In a lot of ways you are talking about a common problem that we all face. Where do we put our limited time and resources? **Chris Kresser** talks about focusing on your weakest link** not focusing on what we might know the most about or have formally studied:

[http://chriskresser.com/reflections-on-the-9-steps-the-biggest-obstacle-to-perfect-health](http://http://chriskresser.com/reflections-on-the-9-steps-the-biggest-obstacle-to-perfect-health)

From your website you know a lot about biochemistry, nutrition, and exercise. You also seem to have an interest in the power of the mind to heal. If we take Chris Kresser's post at face value, it would be of little use to you to focus on these areas for self-improvement. Your conductor has the orchestra playing beautifully in these areas. What are your weakest links?

Before I came to this forum, I didn't think very much about artificial light, though I knew about its dangers. I just had never done anything about it. (I haven't had a TV in 30 years and I am careful about EMF exposure.) After experimenting, I find artificial light at night does negatively effect my depth of sleep. Cold thermogenesis has had a less positive effect on me in the short term. I can not common on long term exposure but it does fit into what I would like my new life to look like. (I want my next house to be off-the-grid.) I do sleep in a naturally cold room and try not to fear getting cold when I do. My diet has been good for a long time; more seafood is a easy change but I don't go crazy because seafood is not a locally available food in my area.

I think my weakest link is I work way too much and I don't have enough fun. I also don't "work" my passions. How do I want to spend my most precious resource - my life energy? I only have one fragile life. Do I want to be remembered as a Jimmy Patterson or a Wendell Berry?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim\_Pattison](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim_Pattison)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendell\_Berry](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendell_Berry)

Some of my best work, or most meaningful work, is unpaid work. But I have realized where I am going the cash economic means little. I have decided to sell my business which might take some time. My business is my creation. You could call it a prison of my own making! The only problem is that I will just build another prison somewhere else. Or will I?

_The longest journey you’ll ever make is from your head to your heart._

**Sioux saying**

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T23:27:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53485>

I am more in line with B on this one. . .my spin is this:

1\. When you take the 30K view of your life as a whole, perfect optimal for whatever you think that is - might not be the perfect path for the specifics of your life - the one you want, not the one you have.

2\. Practically speaking, does it make any sense to create stress and inflammation over minute details when there are big low-hanging fruits to pic?

Everybody chill, thnx!

### Barry — 2012-09-23T02:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53519>

> **Jack Kruse;54141 wrote:** I dont disagree........but when you read ask Jack questions.......that is not how most human view their problems or their life. This site is about them getting answers they cant get at the doc.........there in lies the paradox.

The "Ask Jack" questions are proof of my 1-4 blogs. People don't go on feel, they chase magic bullets, they focus on the tree's instead of the woods and they don't create their own fish bowl.

For the record, I'm not looking for any fans here. I'm just telling it how it is. That's it.

I think my view is skewed though in terms of what people are dealing with here. I'm looking at health from a health body and mind. Most on this site are looking at health from a sick body and mind. This means my biochemistry works differently so my points are not equivocal in that sense. Therefore, I question the continuation of this series in terms of its relevance... I don;t think people see where I am coming from, apart from a few and those that have DM'd me.

So I might just put an end to this, not because its wrong, but more because it is likely to cause more confusion to those on this site than constructiveness

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T02:35:27+0300

It should not end...........Never let people behavior's interupt your stream of consciousness........especially when your white hot. If they cant handle it........that is on them. I like where your taking this.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-23T03:09:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53534>

> **Barry;54161 wrote:** The "Ask Jack" questions are proof of my 1-4 blogs. People don't go on feel, they chase magic bullets, they focus on the tree's instead of the woods and they don't create their own fish bowl.
>
>
>
> For the record, I'm not looking for any fans here. I'm just telling it how it is. That's it.
>
>
>
> I think my view is skewed though in terms of what people are dealing with here. I'm looking at health from a health body and mind. Most on this site are looking at health from a sick body and mind. This means my biochemistry works differently so my points are not equivocal in that sense. Therefore, I question the continuation of this series in terms of its relevance... I don;t think people see where I am coming from, apart from a few and those that have DM'd me.
>
>
>
> So I might just put an end to this, not because its wrong, but more because it is likely to cause more confusion to those on this site than constructiveness

What I like about your posts, Barry, isn't about whether I agree or not (often I don't know what I think!). I like that you are articulating a clear position and that you question. I don't want to blindly follow anything or anyone and I like that you come from a 'healthy' position with a combined understanding of the science. I may be trying to make up health ground that you are not, but that doesn't invalidate what you say. I like that you are provocative, too.

For me, the thematic link between those who 'chase magic bullets' and those who 'create their own fishbowls' is fear. The 'chasing' of an answer (or even believing there is an answer) comes mostly from a place of fear and anxiety. I know that place well. It is perhaps the most toxic thing that we inherit from the CW mindset. And it may be your position of health that means you don't have such intimate experience with this sort of fear. When you create your own fish bowl (as I understand it from what you've explained) it revolves around self-empowerment and self-design, to create something that makes sense and works for you in a positive way. Inherent in that when you are 'broken' means taking a huge leap of faith in yourself. If that isn't something that comes naturally to a person, you have to dig really deep because it will never be encouraged by those who are your medical support out there in the world. It's hard to convey in words what this is like when you are not healthy. You turn your back on everything that is set up to help you and essentially tell them they are both wrong and potentially harmful (and incompetent to help you in the way you now understand you need to be helped). Dr K often says that others in the paleo community struggle to understand where he comes from because they've never been obese. I think the same is true for people who have never had a health crisis. It's hard to make a bridge to connect the positions and tension emerges. For me that bridge is fear and how much it guides what we see, what we can't see, and what we're willing to see.

I can embrace your position while also wanting to learn and understand more because my life is not a sole focus on my physical health. I was diagnosed with cancer but during the entire thing, I was able to remain authentically grateful and happy, even while scared and sad, to. My fish bowl is constructed in spiritual terms and my physical/medical health resides within that. Learning through Dr K has meant that I have let go of a lot, changed a lot and questioned a lot. But it is a process that is integrated within my larger value system and who I am in a very genuine way.

Last thought: another reason I appreciate your different point of view is because I, too, hope to be able to sit from a position of total health and re-evaluate my life. I don't expect to always be in catch-up/recovery mode. And I like the view you offer, even if it isn't mine.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T03:14:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53537>

perspective colors your life.........KiwiLauren paints with that brush.

### Barry — 2012-09-23T19:22:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53624>

> **Jack Kruse;54166 wrote:** I agree with Barry..........I dont think your skewed........I think your the pot at the end of the rainbow that they are all striving to find. It should not end...........Never let people behavior's interupt your stream of consciousness........especially when your white hot. If they cant handle it........that is on them. I like where your taking this.

I'm not afraid of others opinion or afraid of taking criticism. My consciousness won't change and I'lll stick to my guns. The problem is more related to the scientific basis of my arguments. Just like the physiology of a cold adapted person versus a warm adapted person is different, the same applies for a sick/obese person versus a healthy/fit person. Therefore, the function and understanding of hormones, cell signalling, partitioning, inflammation, gene expression etc... works differently for me. Thus, what I recommend and what I know works for me may not apply to the biochemistry of people on this forum. So my information is skewed.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-23T19:44:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-4th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-fitness.3660/post-53628>

Have never been sick for a day in my life and i am also a ultrarunner. Not racing anymore, just for the pure love of motion. To have a good dynamic in this forum your voice is needed because with time many others will soon be seeing things more from your point of view. Keep your thought coming...

//Peter

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T20:15:15+0300

I would also like to say that as a person who has quested spiritually with no impressive satisfaction dropping into circadian life is one of the most profound experiences I've ever had. As I surf the diminishing light of autumn I feel a sense of peace I have never encountered. It's all here if you let it guide youl. I'm not sure what it is, but I am following.

# Thread: Slow Cookers

### Lyndra — 2012-09-15T00:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51712>

I'm in the market for a new one. I love mine (an old Corning ware version) but it only holds about 3 quarts. I'd like something double that size. Every time I start looking there are too many choices (per Barry's recent post) and I get frustrated and stop looking. The only hard-and-fast requirement is not teflon (or other non-stick) coating. I'm willing to pay more to get quality and durability. Anybody have one they love and can recommend?

Every time I start looking there are too many choices (per Barry's recent post) and I get frustrated and stop looking. The only hard-and-fast requirement is not teflon (or other non-stick) coating. I bought a big 7 q one at Kohls, on sale for $19. Hate it. It's low is too high, it boils. My ancient (from college!) one which is small is great. Let me know if you find something, I keep eyeing the pricey ones at Williams and Sonoma, but that seems crazy to spend on one of those. ### Souldanzer — 2012-09-15T02:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51741>

> **Jack Kruse;52330 wrote:** The girls in my office bought me a stainless one for Xmas last yr.......I already need a new one.

I got the same Crockpot Stainless Steel and it committed suicide a couple nights ago. Boiled over or something. And the handle of the lid melted off.  Lasted all but a few months. Going back to Bed Bath and Beyond.

### tweety — 2012-09-15T03:34:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51749>

It's not 7 qts, but I got a vita clay slow cooker (really for rice) last year and I LOVE it! My only complaint is I should have gotten the bigger one. It was about $70, and so worth it. I don't worry about any toxins, and it makes wonderful broths and stews.

For bigger items I bought a large basic crockpot with the black enamel coating. I think I found it at target for around $50? It's good for larger batches of broth or big roasts. But the flavor doesn't compare to the clay cooker.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-15T05:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51758>

Yeah, a time or two I've been close to buying an expensive one online (Amazon or W&S) and then I read the reviews and get discouraged. Nobody seems to like them, the insert breaks, etc. I should have also said I want one that is safe to leave cooking for hours while we're not home. I'm using a pot on the stove these days but I really don't want to leave the house with a burner turned on.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-15T15:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51796>

I have a Hamilton Beach and I absolutely love it. It has a ceramic insert and cleans very nice. I love that I can program it for how many hours I want and then it automatically goes to a warm cycle until you shut it off or 24 hours have passed. It also has a thermometer to cook by temp. So far, no downsides.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T16:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slow-cookers.3666/post-51800>

We replaced our "crockpot" brand this past spring. Found a 7-9qt oval one on amazon w/free shipping for around $30

That thing gets a workout at least once/week

### JanSz — 2012-09-15T15:16:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fatty-acid-profile-how-does-it-look.3667/post-51789>

Scan each page of your Fatty Acid Profile test report, post it here.

Name the laboratory that did the testing.

Scan and post all other labs (on this thread) if you wish).

Post information about your self.

...........

You are presenting 6 fatty acids on your post.

Krieger lab (that dr Kane uses) tests for 43 fatty acids

Genova Diagnostics tests for 24

\-----------------------------------------------------------------

The good thing about your laboratory is that it presents upper limits.

EPA 3.1% (.1%-2.5%)

too much

if you reduce EPA, AA will go up.

You need DHA & AA.

It is better to be in mid range on all fatty acids than have excess of EPA.

If you eat fish oil or krill oil they usually contain way too much of both, but specially too much of EPA.

...

Your lab is using considerably different ranges when compared to Kriegers lab that I compare to.

...

Some transfats are natural (and we pay $$ to get them).

Which trans fats have you tested?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans\_fat](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans_fat)

> The process of hydrogenation adds hydrogen atoms to unsaturated fats, eliminating double bonds and making them into partially or completely saturated fats. However, partial hydrogenation, if it is chemical rather than enzymatic, converts a part of cis-isomers into trans-unsaturated fats instead of hydrogenating them completely.
>
>
>
> Trans fats also occur naturally in a limited number of cases: Vaccenyl and **conjugated linoleyl (CLA)** containing trans fats occur naturally in trace amounts in meat and dairy products from ruminants, although the latter also constitutes a cis fat.

# Thread: I have 2 mackerels . . . what to do?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-15T01:53:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51724>

My local farmer's market has just added a seafood stand and this is the first time I've seen whole mackerel, so I felt compelled to buy some . . . now I'm wondering what the heck I was thinking! On the drive home I figured I'd make some fish broth, but I just looked in my Nourishing Traditions book and see they don't recommend using a fatty fish for the broth.

These were fresh and never frozen. I know I could probably go ahead and do the MHS, but I don't quite think I'm there yet. Plus, if and when I do that I think I'll want to have the fish frozen first for the whole parasite issue.

So, can I make broth with this? Any other ideas? I suppose I could just grill it whole, but again I don't know if I'm at the point where I can actually eat the eyes and 'stuff' yet!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-15T02:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51742>

Cut off the fillets and freeze the heads for when you're ready

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T02:59:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51745>

> **Souldanzer;52341 wrote:** Cut off the fillets and freeze the heads for when you're ready

This. Then take the rest of the fish butterfly it (see Julia child's cook book or joy of cooking for how to)

Then prepare your grill. Get some smoker chips soak them in water then place them on your coals (or tinfoil if you have gas grill) put fish over top of smoker chips Season w/salt pepper avocado oil. Nom nom nom

### Inger — 2012-09-15T08:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51772>

Just do it.

If you want to get healthy, _what are you waiting for...!!!?_

Don't wish, just do.

This is my motto.

You can do it. Everyone can. \*\*\*\*hugs\*\*\*\*

(parasite-issue.. when you open the mackerel you will soon see if there are worms, they are small rolled worms, anisakia-worms. If nothing, they have no worms. I have been eating unfrozen mackerelk with these worms without issue, I just pick the worms I can see away. These worms cannot live longer hat 3 weeks in human anyways. They can give some digestive issues though, according to my research. Soem are allergic to them to and they suffer more. I had no issues. But read it up yourself that is the best. We all have to know our risks and do he research.)

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-15T09:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51775>

after a surfing attempt this evening, i had fish. including a raw, small cut up japanese mackerel, sashimi style from a local japanese market. they included the head, bones, and tail, which are usually set aside for deep frying. i decided to consume a whole raw fish as is, those extra parts just took longer to chew. the head was tasty, actually. who would have thought

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-15T14:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51783>

Was that your first? Way to go!

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-16T08:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-2-mackerels-what-to-do.3669/post-51909>

had whole sardines before but they were still partially frozen, which felt like a safety net cuz it takes some edge off the experience lol. this was first time pure naked raw to every last bit.

there is a particular organ in the guts that's way harsh bitter. its at the bottom just behind the head/gills... maybe the gall bladder? best to skip that whatever it is

# Thread: Dr Who's Newby Questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-whos-newby-questions.3672/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-15T07:03:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-whos-newby-questions.3672/post-51766>

Not sure why it got closed, but it was a question that keeps popping up. Look here for some more info, easy to miss: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?816-Quickstart-guide-to-Cold-Thermogenesis](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?816-Quickstart-guide-to-Cold-Thermogenesis)

I recommend the face dunking for newcomers to CT. It invokes the mammalian dive response and preps the body for more serious CT. This is especially good for people who live in warm climes and never really get cold.

Once you can comfortably keep your face in icy water while holding your breath for about a minute, you are good to go for cold showers and baths. I think we discussed using a snorkle, but decided that defeats the MDR which is about hypoxia.

Start out with cool showers if you like, don't sweat the temp at first, just get way below your comfort zone. Eventually you'll be able to shower at the lowest setting you can get. If that turns out to be 50-55 deg F, you are good to go. If it's only 60+ degrees, you may want to try the ice bath with ice added to get the magical 55 degrees. 55 degrees is the temp where skin cold receptors are maxed out and full activation of BAT and uncoupling protein-1 (UCP-1) is fully working. People on here have workied their way down to 32 degree water for 45 minutes or more. If you go that extreme, make sure you have a spotter and watch for hypothermia.

The extremes you go to should be dictated by your individual circumstances and goals, not just mimicing others here. If you just have a few pounds to lose and are otherwise healthy, an ice bath after a hard workout or a workout wearing an ice-pack vest may do wonders for you. If you are morbidly obese or metabolically a wreck, you may want to work your way down to 'deep CT' as we like to say.

good luck, post about your experiences. I will be posting when I start CT again in another month.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Seriously stubborn kid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-09-15T07:16:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51767>

I started adding seafood into my kids' diets about a month or so ago. I try to have one meal a day with some form of seafood. It doesnt happen every day but about 5 days a week. My 8 year old doesn't love it but eats it to be done with it. My 5 year old has a complete and total fit about it EVERY SINGLE TIME. I'm talking tears, screaming, gagging, the works. It can't be healthy to have this kind of scene every night at dinner. Anyone have a similar issue?

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-15T15:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51798>

Yep 2yo did this initially. Then we did the whole if you didn't eat breakfast then it becomes lunch if you didn't eat it for lunch it becomes dinner & so on. No snacks either. Eventually they'll get it & they'll start eating

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T16:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51804>

You think it is healthy to give in to what he wants at 5? you control his environment, you are the parent. IF he does not like dont let him eat. Gretchen is spot on here.

### Sem — 2012-09-15T17:34:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51814>

That's exactly what I'm doing. I just worry that I am creating negative emotions around food. But I guess I just have to wait him out. Last night he went to bed with no dinner and total hysteria. I'm just surprised it has lasted this long!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-15T17:41:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51815>

who cares about WHAT might happen when you already know what happens when you eat bad food. That is how bad thinking leads to suboptimal living. Eradicate that thinking and you will have optimal kids.

### Sem — 2012-09-15T20:14:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51831>

> **Jack Kruse;52428 wrote:** who cares about WHAT might happen when you already know what happens when you eat bad food. That is how bad thinking leads to suboptimal living. Eradicate that thinking and you will have optimal kids.

Good way to think about it! That helps. Thanks.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-15T21:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51842>

> **Sem;52427 wrote:** That's exactly what I'm doing. I just worry that I am creating negative emotions around food. But I guess I just have to wait him out. Last night he went to bed with no dinner and total hysteria. I'm just surprised it has lasted this long!

What really turned the corner for me was realizing eating disorders are brain chemistry disorders. You don't "give" someone an eating disorder.

We did that whole tantrum and no dinner, and get served it for the next meal. Worked pretty quickly too. Still get fusses every now and then. . but he is 6, they come with a built in "I dont' like it" for everything right?

The reality is they are not going to starve themselves at this age, if their bodies need food - they will eat what you give them!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-16T04:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51893>

> **Jack Kruse;52417 wrote:** You think it is healthy to give in to what he wants at 5? you control his environment, you are the parent. IF he does not like dont let him eat. Gretchen is spot on here.

Sem, I agree with what has been said. But I think there is another piece to the puzzle that can help. That is the relational piece. When we as parents have to insist upon something and be in charge, that's just a fact of parenting life. There are a lot of times we have to set limits with our children or enforce rules that make them upset but we know it's important to do. Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.

As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-16T19:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51939>

> **KiwiLauren;52507 wrote:** Sem, I agree with what has been said. But I think there is another piece to the puzzle that can help. That is the relational piece. When we as parents have to insist upon something and be in charge, that's just a fact of parenting life. There are a lot of times we have to set limits with our children or enforce rules that make them upset but we know it's important to do. Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.
>
>
>
> As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

Just Awesome Lauren... true parenting from a primal spirit.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-16T20:14:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-51953>

Lauren, that was wonderful advice, not just for parenting, but as a way to treat all human beings.

We all just want to be acknowledged.

Our children must be able to count on us to do what is right, regardless of how much they don't like it. However, the relational piece means that we can do this with compassion. The ability to see things from the child's perspective and to empathize helps them regulate their emotions and bodies (and HPA axis) and can deepen their connection to use, even in the face of something they don't like. So while you insist that this IS dinner, you can also say 'I can see how much you don't like this. I can see how angry you are that I won't fix you something else. It's really hard to have to eat something you don't want to, isn't it?" We don't have to be stern or punitive when we are setting limits.
>
>
>
> As parents, we often think we have to solve our children's problems. But sometimes (most times), what they need is to be seen and understood. If we move to fix everything instead of staying with the experience, they don't learn how to tolerate difficulty and negotiate it. And to learn that they are always - always - connected to us in a heartfelt way, no matter what sort of issue we are dealing with. You can retain your parental authority AND be kind and compassionate. This sort of combo can heal a lot of ills.

Thanks so much for this Lauren, Such a nice reminder to me that guiding and correcting behavior does not have to be me bossing and bitching at my child! Sometimes in efforts to be stern, I find myself using a stern tone, and jumping in with my fast talking speech (instead of honoring his slower pace). This was perfectly said.

### Sem — 2012-09-17T08:25:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52074>

Couldn't agree more Lauren. I have a problem walking that line sometimes. I'm right there with them - the model of patience and compassion. And they keep pushing and pushing and bam! I lose it. All out the window. Very hard.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-17T21:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52192>

> **Sem;52691 wrote:** Couldn't agree more Lauren. I have a problem walking that line sometimes. I'm right there with them - the model of patience and compassion. And they keep pushing and pushing and bam! I lose it. All out the window. Very hard.

Sem, I think we ALL have trouble walking that line. It's really important to be kind to yourself and also to 'stay with' your own experience. As you are talking them through theirs, you can talk through your own (internally), reminding yourself that it's bloody hard to be a parent, not to mention one pushing an epi-paleo template!  I also think the psycho-neurological concept of 'rupture and repair' is critical here. Not only does an apology mend things, but it shows our kids that we can get reconnected even after big breaks in connection. Relationships are never smooth and easy, no matter how much we love someone. It's important for kids to learn how to negotiate the rough patches; and it's important for us to know this can be done! Good luck. I really think we're all in this same boat with you, so hang in there.

### Linz — 2012-09-20T02:30:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-52825>

I love your thoughts Lauren. When my children were small I always told them to taste everything we ate but never made them finish it so they didn't feel threatened by something they really disliked. Often they surprised themselves (and me) and usually ate anything they were given but I always let them have have something else healthy but not a favourite if they had really tried our meal first.

A little of the thing they thought they were not going to like usually became a whole meal without protest quite soon and I was rapidly overtaken as far as shellfish were concerned - catching up now but MHS did make me consider a screaming tantrum! Seeing it back on my plate at breakfast wouldn't have made me want to try again.

### Sem — 2012-09-21T06:55:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-53120>

Thanks ladies. Appreciate all the reassurance. I'm glad some people have made it through to the other side. We had another terrible experience at dinner tonight with him over eating cod. I think it's become a huge power play or something. I hope to see some positive changes soon.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seriously-stubborn-kid.3673/post-53188>

One thing that became a family habit for us was "Courtesy Bites". You must take courtesy bites of any food you are served - one day you may be having dinner with the president or a dictator, and refusing could get you killed! We play it up like a game, it's been fun.

It goes right into "you have to eat it 7 times and then your tongue will like it".

So, when we know he likes the food -we make him eat it, or serve it for the next meal, this isn't a restaurant. . if you are hungry you eat your food. Whoever pays gets a say! and on and on.

But when it's a new food or one he doesn't like - we go the courtesy bite route! I put my hand to my forehead and swoon and say. . .you MUST, I can't have my baby being killed by a crazy dictator over fish eyes (or whatever).

More than working for him, making it fun keeps ME on track and not gravitating to that bossy dictator tone that sets up a power play.
