I seem to recall a listener who asked him about hair in poor condition _since going paleo. IIRC, after casting around and saying that most nutrients should be in better supply in a paleo diet, he did say that iodine can be low on a paleo diet. I think he then said that he could, however, see no reason why that would have an effect on the condition of someone's hair, and suggested the listener made some exhaustive analyses of her diet pre- and post-paleo and look for anything_ that might be lower.

I think he also made a comment to the effect that he had started wrapping hamburgers in sheets of nori once.

My guess is a recognition that "iodine can be low on a paleo diet" has got onto his radar one way or another. But presumably he doesn't think that's of great moment or he'd talk about it much more.

If nothing else in Cunanne's book shows we've missed the significance of iodine, then I'd think that figure of 700 million people worldwide deficient in it, despite the compulsory iodination of salt and other foodstuffs in many countries, makes the point.

### AKMan — 2012-09-17T23:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52213>

So I got to wondering where wild animals in Nebraska or Poland or other places far from the sea get iodine since all animals have a thyroid and need iodine for T4 production.

I found an obscure study that linked deer antler size to iodine availabilty. It seems that these deer got all the iodine they needed from plants they ate and water they drank. Some areas have more than others resulting in deer with larger antlers and giving wild deer more iodine resulted in larger antlers.

Another study found seasonal variances in iodine availability which effected the reproductive capacity of wild desert goats. Their main source of iodine was blown in as dust and settled in their drinking water and on their food. Seasonal wind patterns made more iodine locally available in the fall which coincided with their breeding season.

So, apparently, we are getting iodine from more sources than I had considered. I guess this little bit of research I did helped guide me through this particular rabbit hole. I'm not going to go out of my way to supplement with iodine unless I have reason to believe, and labs to prove, iodine deficiency.

If the rabbits in my backyard are getting enough iodine from my lawn and the few carrots they nibble, and if the chickadees are getting enough iodine from the mosquitoes they eat, then I am probably getting enough from the fish and oysters (and everything else) I eat most everyday.

### AKMan — 2012-09-18T01:18:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52236>

From the BG-12 comments:

“In summary the results of the review show the following order of geometric mean iodine content

for the main food groups (Table 3.4):

Marine fish (1455.9 μg/kg) > Freshwater fish (102.8 μg/kg) > Leafy vegetables (88.8 μg/kg) > Dairy

(83.9 μg/kg) > Other vegetables (80.1 μg/kg) > Meat (68.4 μg/kg) > Cereals (56.0 μg/kg) > Fresh

fruit (30.6 μg/kg) > Bread (17.0 μg/kg) > Water (6.4 μg/l)”

So it looks as if 'most vegetables' contain about 80 micrograms per kilogram. I would have to eat about 5lbs of 'vegetation' to get my rda of 150 micrograms. That makes it very understandable how a moose, who eats 100lbs of leaves and twigs a day, gets his iodine.

I would need about 5lbs of 'meat', so that explains where the fox, who eats a couple rabbits, gets his iodine.

If all else fails, I can drink about 5 gallons of water to get my iodine.

The fact that there is iodine in water also raises some questions: What kind of water? Rain? Bottled? Well? Municipal? I'd love to see a chart.

I'm also curious about the RDA of 150micrograms...wonder what the real optimal amount is.

### AKMan — 2012-09-18T01:28:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52237>

From: [http://www.circumpolarhealthjournal.net/index.php/ijch/article/view/17491](http://http://www.circumpolarhealthjournal.net/index.php/ijch/article/view/17491)

Objectives. The iodine intake level is important for the occurrence of thyroid disorders in a population. The iodine intake in Greenland has been proposed to be more than ten times the recommended level. However, no measurements have been performed to determine the iodine content of Greenlandic food items, drinking water, and beverages available in East and West Greenland.

Study desing. Food samples were collected at the local market, kalaalimineerniarfik, in Nuuk and Ammassalik, and tap water was obtained from all towns in Greenland. Beverages were purchased at Kalaallit Niuerfiat KNI Pisiniarfik.

Results. Iodine content of seal, whale, wild fowl, reindeer, and musk ox varied between 4 and 195 μg/kg with low values for terrestrial animals (< 10 μg/kg) and higher values for marine animals (10 - 195 μg/kg). The iodine content of fish varied from 9 μg/kg in freshwater fish to 1,380 μg/kg in a sample of cod. The iodine content of sea mammals was: blubber 130 μg/kg; viscera 70 μg/kg; meat 21 μg/kg. No difference was observed between animals from East Greenland and West Greenland (P > 0.1). Iodine content of tap water was below 3.3 μg/l for all towns. Two sorts of beer had a high iodine content, up to 240 μg/l. The iodine content of all other beverages was 5 - 38 μg/l.

Conclusion. We found a relatively high iodine content in marine animals but low iodine content in tap water and beverages in Greenland. The food and drinking water evaluated in the present study indicate adequate iodine intake in this area and do not sup-port the notion that Greenland is an area of excessive iodine intake.

### AKMan — 2012-09-18T01:40:18+0300

from the paper:

Iodine concentrations in groundwaters (and surface

waters) largely lie in the range 0.01–70 μg/l,

depending on geographical location and local geology

and soils. Higher concentrations can be found in

saline waters such as coastal and arid or semi-arid

areas. The principal sources of iodine in groundwater

are aquifers and soils and the atmosphere.

### AKMan — 2012-09-18T06:35:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52299>

> **Jack Kruse;52899 wrote:** Why would the RDA be hgh for a moose or a cow.......their brain is 10% the size of yours and way less complex.

My thinking was that they have such large bodies and large thyroid glands, they would need large amounts of iodine to support all the cellular activity--not so much brain wise, but muscle, bone, digestion etc... A moose thyroid is massive, and since this gland contains nearly all the bodies iodine, it would stand to reason they need lots of iodine.

I found a website devoted to horse nutrition [http://www.ker.com/library/advances/239.pdf](http://http://www.ker.com/library/advances/239.pdf) , horse breeders know a lot about what it takes to keep their animals healthy. Turns out a horse's RDA for iodine is about 1-6mg--ours is .15mg or 150mcg, Here's an excerpt:

Iodine (I) is an essential nutrient for reproduction and normal physiological

function in the horse. Thyroxine (T4) contains iodine and this hormone, along

with triiodothyronine (T3), has powerful effects on the overall health of the horse.

These hormones influence nearly every process in the body, from heat regulation

and feed utilization to proper bone growth and maturation. Based on data from

other species, the 1989 NRC estimated that the iodine requirement of horses was

0.1-0.6 mg/kg of diet. Normal diet is 10kg/day.

Overfeeding iodine also causes problems in horses:

Baker and Lindsey (1968) reported that goitrous foals were born on three

farms which were feeding mares high levels of iodine. The incidence of goiter

was proportional to the level of iodine fed and equaled 3% on one farm feeding

48-55 mg I/day, 10% on a farm feeding 36-69 mg I/day and 50% on another farm

feeding 288-432 mg I/day. A neighboring farm which did not have any goitrous

foals fed iodine at a rate of 6.3-7 mg I/day. In a separate paper, Sipple (1969)

reviewed a case in which 11% of the foals born on a farm had goiters. Analysis of

the diet revealed that the mares received between 160-400 mg I/day. Coincidentally,

the author discovered that the manager of this farm was the brother of the

manager of one of the farms in Baker’s study in Florida. Apparently, the Florida

horseman had prescribed the same iodine supplement for his brother’s horses

1,000 miles away.

Toxic dietary iodine concentrations may result from adding excessive supplemental

iodine, such as from ethylenediamindihydroiodide (EDDI), to

concentrates or from using feedstuffs high in iodine. A common feedstuff that

may contain excess iodine is kelp (Laminariales), a specific family of seaweeds

that may contain as much as 1,850 ppm iodine (NRC, 1989). Unfortunately, people

have a tendency to classify all seaweeds as kelp just as the layman might

consider every breed of horse a Thoroughbred. There are numerous other

specific seaweeds, including Fucaceae, Palmariaceae, Gigartinaceae,

Bangiaceae, and Ulvaceae that contain considerably less iodine than kelp.

### AKMan — 2012-09-18T19:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52419>

> **Anna K.;52970 wrote: Here is an interesting link wich contains the amount of iodine in food:
>
> [http://iodine4health.com/research/iodine\_in\_food\_table.htm](http://http://iodine4health.com/research/iodine_in_food_table.htm)
>
>
>
> and explains the fish head smoothies! "a significant part of the iodine is in the head of the fish (where the thyroid is)"**

**

It seems to be CW, and I've seen this posted around a lot, that the body has no way of storing iodine--so we must ingest it every day. However, the thyroid is a rich source of iodine and the T3/T4 circulating in the body have iodine in their chemical make-up. Maybe we store more than we realize.

This from a endocrinology text:

Of the many minerals that may affect thyroid function, iodine is the most important. It is an essential substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis and also interacts with the function of the thyroid gland at several levels. Acute administration of increasing doses of iodide enhances total hormone synthesis until a critical level of intrathyroidal iodide is reached. Beyond this level, iodide organification and hormone synthesis are blocked (the acute Wolff-Chaikoff block). Chronic or repeated administration of moderate to large doses of iodine causes a decrease in iodide transport resulting in a decrease in its intrathyroidal concentration. The latter relieves the Wolff-Chaikoff block and is known as the escape or adaptation phenomenon. Although the exact mechanisms of the block and escape remain unknown, they appear to be autoregulatory in nature since they are independent of pituitary TSH secretion. Iodoloactones may play a role in the induction of the Wolff-Chaikoff block.80 One mechanism through which iodide acts is via desensitization of the thyroid gland to TSH. In TSH stimulated glands, iodine rapidly reduces the level of the mRNA for thyroid peroxidase (TPO) and the Na/I symporter (NIS) but not for thyroglobulin (Tg) or the TSH receptor (TSHr).80a Iodine also antagonizes TSH stimulated thyrocyte proliferation.80a In FRTL-5 cells, iodine blocks the TSH stimulation of Tg synthesis but does not alter the level of the Tg mRNA.80b These actions occur without a change in TSH receptor number, and may, in part, be via an action on adenylyl cyclase.80c More detailed description is provided in Chapter 2. Another effect of large doses of iodine, apparently independent of TSH and hormone synthesis, is the prompt inhibition of hormone release. It has been exploited to achieve rapid amelioration of thyrotoxicosis in Graves’ disease and toxic nodular goiters (see Chapters 11 and 13). In normal persons, the inhibitory effect of large doses of iodine on thyroid hormone release produces a transient decrease in the serum concentration of T4 and T3. It causes, in turn, a compensatory increase in serum TSH, which stimulates hormone secretion and thus counteracts the effect of iodine.81,82 The mechanisms of thyroidal autoregulation are believed to serve the purpose of accommodating wide and rapid fluctuations in iodine supply.**

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-18T20:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52469>

\[pre\]\[/pre\]

> **Michael;53079 wrote:** To some spirits the manufacturers add brown colouring derived from burnt grain - to hide the fact that they're not actually barrel-aged. That _might_ be what's going on.

in some cases yes but all you have to do is cruise a celiac forum to see this is a common topic of discussion right up with whether the skin can absorb gluten through lotions/shampoos. (again according to CW no, but practical experience is yes you can)

my n-1 with vodkas (so no colouring to cause cross contamination) is that Absolut, Stoli, Grey Goose (wheat)/Van Gogh (corn and wheat)/Finalandia(rye) all make me nauseous and I wake up feeling like cr@p no matter how little I have.

Luskowa/chopin (potato), ciroc (grape), Paua (pineapple) do not cause this reaction at all.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-18T20:45:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52471>

> **janagram;53072 wrote:** ColleenM...Thank you for that interesting info!! Wow! and Dang!
>
> (sounds like a homeopathic effect!)
>
>
> perhaps but what is interesting is the folks that report the most obvious reactions as I said tend to have HLA-DQ 1 types and the research according to Hadjivassiliou is that with this variant of the gene show neuromuscular manifestations first rather than intestinal manifestations of gluten intolerance.
>
>
>
> This can occur without any signs of intestinal damage, which is the cornerstone for a diagnosis of Celiac Disease. Sometimes, intestinal disease will follow several years later. It is possible that neurological disease is the sole manifestation of gluten sensitivity.

for me the weirdest manifestation of having alcohol that is distilled from gluten grains is that I feel it in my quad muscles first (they feel weak after drinking wheat based vodka) and then I get nauseous.

otoh traditional celiac types (HLA DQ 2 and 8) often do not have any perceived reaction at all to grain based alcohol.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-09-18T22:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52515>

Regarding iodine supply for wild animals far inland, before we built a bunch of dams tons and tons of salmon would migrate far inland, and their remains brought marine minerals to whole regions.

I'm beginning to wish we'd take out almost all those dams, and get our electricity from wind and solar. Trading salmon for megawatts (which we can either get from solar and wind or just do without) was a bad bargain.

### cookworm — 2012-09-20T19:59:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52953>

For what it's worth (I'm no expert!), but I like dulse flakes, and Jack mentions it in his list of sea vegetables at the [Brain Gut 6: Epi-Paleo Rx](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/). For only one tablespoon (3g), the nutritional value listed on the packaging says "330% of the daily value", but does not list mg or mcg unfortunately. I find it to have has a mild taste with a pleasant saltiness that I sprinkle on things like I would use salt, as it doesn't require any soaking (making it really convenient). It's fairly affordable too in comparison with other sea vegetables, though I do add a few other varieties of the sea vegetables to get more complete nutrition.

> **Michael;52573 wrote:** [I seem to recall a listener who asked him about hair in poor condition since going paleo.](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4000-Best-sources-of-iodine&p=52573&viewfull=1#post52573)

I can say from personal experience that this can be true--I noticed this with my hair, and Dr. Kruse [verified](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3790-Hair-loss) it. I read elsewhere that zinc and magnesium along with iodine were important for regrowing hair--being a Kruiser, I already knew how important magnesium was  and had been taking it, and I'd already been taking zinc, but did not realize that iodine might be lacking.

### Michael — 2012-09-20T21:13:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-52975>

> **piano-doctor-lady;53139 wrote:** Regarding iodine supply for wild animals far inland, before we built a bunch of dams tons and tons of salmon would migrate far inland, and their remains brought marine minerals to whole regions.

I guess if you ate bear-meat it would be high in the minerals and oils from salmon at the right time of year.

Sally Fallon quotes an old author on bear-meat in _Nourishing Traditions. A bear-meat diet was apparently used by some American Indians as a remedy for infertility. I can't locate the passage. IIRC, the author said that the diet makes the man "very importunate to his wife" and ' 'tis odds that's she's a mother very soon".

Reading it one tends to think of the bear-meat being rich and not past that, but I guess what the bear is eating is relevant too.

I like the way Cate Shanahan draws that out in Deep Nutrition_, saying that food is really information and a really good piece of food links you back to a whole properly functioning eco-system.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-21T00:00:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-53019>

> **Nonchalant;53597 wrote:** Very interesting, I hadn't heard about the burnt grain for coloring, but I have heard of liquors irritating celiacs.
>
> So now I wonder about moonshine and other homemade goodies.

depends on processing like any other alcohol. Bacardi Rum can be clear and you would think gluten free but it is allegedly aged in oak barrels and lots of celiacs react to bacardi. Mount Gay is aged in steel vats and isn't known for it's reaction.

Big Rock Brewery in Calgary AB has a great cider but it's not gluten free because they make it in the same lines as their beer.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-09-21T04:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-sources-of-iodine.3688/post-53103>

@Wanna -- Wind and solar may not be totally steady, though added together and supplemented with geothermal they get awfully close. Certain parts of the U.S. are windy almost non-stop, 24/7, year round. Another way around the intermittent problem is to set up battery banks. Batteries are luckily improving fast. And just to stop OVER USING electricity for trivial purposes!

And in a pinch, one can change one's lifestyle to use the power when it's there and do other things when it isn't. Any other options are better than using coal or nuclear (generating waste dangerous for 30,000 years just isn't a practical approach.)

For me, a stronger argument for keeping the dams is flood control, which is the only thing keeping me from wanting ALL the dams on the Columbia and Snake Rivers removed. The salmon and the nutrients they bring inland are far more valuable than mere kilowatts wasted destroying the night with light pollution.

@Michael -- very interesting about the bear meat, and I think you may be right! And I like Cate's Deep Nutrition book just like you do.

# Thread: My dogs are smarter than I am
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-09-16T17:50:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-51929>

When I first started reading Dr. Kruse's blogs, I have to admit they were WAY over my head. But recently I think I've started to get it....

I read BG 4 and BG 5 yesterday, and then BG 12 was posted last night. I'm SO glad I read the other 2 again, as it really helps for everything to start coming together. Diet, light, timing of meals, hormones. I'm not all the way there, but it's starting.

It's also opened my eyes to follow some of the things that my dogs have been doing instinctually. I have a small front and back patio, and whenever we go outside, no matter what the temp is (and some days this summer have been HOT), they will always find that sliver of sun and lay in it. Even if they curl themselves like a doughnut (and these are 85 lb dogs LOL). When people say to keep your dogs out of the sun in the summer - wrong! My advice is give them choice - they will know when to go into the sun and when to go into the shade. They are way smarter than us.

Then, my oldest dog started to going to her bed in the bedroom much earlier than me. I noticed that in the winter, she would go around 8, in the summer at 9:30. At first I thought it was to get away from the sound of the TV, but now I'm realizing she's going into darkness - out of the light.

I'm sure that their systems are different than ours, but since I did a consult with Dr. K, I've been changing my habits, and I've noticed that they are getting leaner and more muscular. I've been eating earlier, which means they have too, and we've been timing as many of our walks as I can after our meals to make sure that they are within 30 minutes of waking and 4 hours before bed.

I also did a nutritional consult for the dogs about a year ago. I have been feeding them raw for over 3 years (combination of grass fed beef and turkey with chicken feet), but wanted to make sure they were getting the right nutrients. Although I didn't really think much about it at the time, she added sardines, kelp, B vitamins and zinc to their diet. Hmmm....sound familiar! So when I started the epi-paleo diet, I already had kelp in the house! I've started to up the seafood in their diet, and I've noticed how amazing their coats have been lately. Plus they've been so much happier - probably because I have been too!

It really strikes me how intuitive their bodes are - and how much I can learn from following them. Plus, I am so diligent with making sure that they are eating what's right for them, getting the right exercise and meeting their needs - and here I don't do it consistently for myself (i've gotten better for sure, but not always consistent). That's the lightbulb that went off when I read BG4 - I really loved it. It took me a very long time to read but I made sure I got as much as I could. Now I see how I am so out of whack - and almost all of my labs showed that - low melatonin (extremely low), low DHEA, low cortisol, low progesterone - almost all of it was speaking to me.

Thank you Dr. K, for helping me to learn. Now for me to follow the dogs' example!

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/52544=463-diesel sun.jpg>)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-16T17:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-51931>

Diane the reason is simple.......go back and listen to the podcast I did with Sean Croxton. The answer is there. We totally control our environment and dog have to live by their's unless we change it for them.........

### diane — 2012-09-16T21:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-51960>

Thank you! I went back to listen to it - heard the part about the mountain lion. Funny - the dog that goes into the dark bedroom is leaner than the younger male dog, even though they eat the same thing. Now I'm thinking that he's being more affected by my habits than she is. I also read a lot about the obesity epidemic in dogs. Yes, we've introduced unnatural diets for them (kibble), stuff them full of treats - but they are also subject to our mismatches more and more. And a dog's body composition often mirrors the owners. Hmmm...really making me think!

The one thing you said was you changed your thoughts which changed your DNA - which I also read in I think BG4 and BG12. I'm definitely going to have to spend more time figuring that part out!

At least I'm heading in the right direction - guided by what I'm doing for my dogs, in some part. I've used the bones I bought for them for bone broth, and today, for the first time, cooked up some of their liver for myself. And I actually like it! The only thing I can't get them to eat is raw fish. But I bet if I gave them raw fish day after day they'd soon eat it! LOL.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T00:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-51996>

My dog used to do all of this - raw fed, too. It cured her dander, greasy coat, itchiness - her fur was the nicest ever. A little after dark each night she'd sit in front of the closed bedroom door asking to be let in while I was still up. I think dogs still have full access to their primal voice unless we mess with it..... the only thing she never understood was that you don't eat garbage (=grain and sugar) from the streets and given by strangers  She was wild for grains despite it making her feel really badly each time she got into them.

Btw, dogs don't get enough zinc from meat and fish alone.... they need shellfish I found out. My dog got a weekly can of oysters and her paw pads got so soft from it

### diane — 2012-09-17T00:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52001>

Thanks Souldanzer! I've been giving them sardines - but just got a few more cans of oysters this week as I was planning to try them. Now it's my turn to share.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T01:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52007>

You can feed a lot of fish... really really good for them especially since your meat is O6 heavy. I never messed with canned always frozen whole fish with guts and heads and bones. Sardines are good, mackerel, anything fatty really. My dog hated white fish, absolute nogo she rather starved herself. I also added a good doggie multi and a little vit E to her diet b/c her meat wasn't grassfed and the fish from the Asian store  but she did great either way. Beautiful coat and defined muscles, super strong, great stamina, and always alert and ready to learn (for food, lol).

### diane — 2012-09-17T02:41:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52030>

I went to canned because they wouldn't eat anything raw. But I will try again. I bought some frozen sardines and mackerel yesterday - especially after all the threads on MHS and raw fish LOL - so will start giving them some too. I did get some salmon bones, and they've eaten that raw. It's a start!

I do get grass fed beef - a friend's parents have a cattle farm, so I get a 1/4 beef which we share.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T03:05:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52035>

> so I get a 1/4 beef which we share.

You're my kinda dog person

My dog wouldn't eat fish at first. Tough love..... down on feeding spot for 15min if not eaten back in the freezer (my dog preferred frozen fish) to be re-served for next meal. No treats until fish is eaten. I cheated a little and some fish I would defrost halfway, chops up into bite size, then pour coconut milk over or cover in yummy doggie vitamin powder.... but she learned that way to eat most fatty fish. I used whole frozen smelts for training treats a lot. I love how you feed your dogs  They're not meant to eat garbage in frosted flakes shape

### diane — 2012-09-17T05:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52056>

> **Souldanzer;52652 wrote:** You're my kinda dog person

Thanks!

Yes, I have no problem with tough love - I can get them eating fish LOL. I don't use treats for training - but they do get beef, bison and other various types of bones for chewing every now and again. I don't let people give my dogs treats (most of it is crap, and I don't want them to beg) and when I say "no" people give me the dirtiest looks or tell me I'm mean. I'd like to know how it's so mean to feed your dog the best possible food, give them healthy bones to chew, spend lots of time with them walking, playing, hiking, my dogs can go anywhere off leash from the downtown streets to hiking in forests. Yes, they're so hard done by!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-17T06:12:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-dogs-are-smarter-than-i-am.3691/post-52066>

Cat Hijack:

I have no trouble tough loving the pets (or children!) either. The last cat we had, I was on-board with keeping him trim and food restricted of the good stuff. Damn thing lived to 23!!!! his last few years were not pleasant for us - but he just kept on hangin on. He was a super cranky vengeful beast and we decided the next cat gets to be fat and happy and go outside a lot. hehe.

### diane — 2012-09-17T14:30:09+0300

# Thread: Epi-Mediterranean Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-16T21:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-51961>

If this is the standard mediterranean diet, how do we make it "Epi-Medi" (I should trademark that):

Wikipedia say:

1\. abundant plant foods,

2\. fresh fruit as the typical daily dessert,

3\. olive oil as the principal source of fat,

4\. dairy products (principally cheese and yogurt),

5\. fish and poultry consumed in low to moderate amounts,

6\. zero to four eggs consumed weekly,

7\. red meat consumed in low amounts,

8\. wine consumed in low to moderate amounts.

AKMan's Epi-Medi:

1\. Abundant, non-starchy plant foods, especially seaweed

2\. Fresh fruit in season

3\. Olive oil, coconut oil, and pastured butter as principal source of added fat

4\. Low amounts of dairy

5\. Fish consumed in high amounts, poultry rarely

6\. Zero to 24 free-range eggs consumed weekly

7\. Grass-fed beef, pork, wild game, offal consumed moderately

8\. Wine, dark chocolate, coffee consumed moderately

starting to sound like epi-paleo, but anyone else want to play?

### Michael — 2012-09-16T22:04:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-51966>

> **AKMan;52576 wrote:**
>
> starting to sound like epi-paleo, but anyone else want to play?

Sounds interesting. It had occurred to me too that some kind of low-carb version of "Mediterranean" might be pretty good - mostly on account of the seafood.

Of course what Wikipedia says is a "Mediterranean" diet doesn't necessarily have much to do with what people actually eat in that area.

Sally Fallon and Mary Enig did a pretty shrewd demolition of the claims dieticians have made:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/mediterranean-diet](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/mediterranean-diet)

What I think is this: that if you're going to call a diet "Mediterranean" then what's in it has to bear some relation to what's available in the area. So they don't grow coconut palms round there, as far as I know, and I don't think it's much of an area for dairying. (Not saying you can't eat those fats named in your (3), but I don't think it makes sense to call those "Mediterranean" fats.) OTOH, I expect there has traditionally been a wider use of lard and duck and goose fat than Wikipedia would care to admit.

Socrates famously said that "we" (meaning the Greeks, I suppose) live around the Mediterranean "like frogs around a pond". I think historically those "frogs" -- the people all round that sea -- were hardly not using it as a resource. This makes Wiki's criterion (5) damn silly. Why "low"? Only because the "diet dictocrats" say so.

In general what people are told to go low on by Wikipedia doesn't seem to have much to do with people actually do, or did, in that area .... except perhaps during and just after the Second World war. (I'm reminded of claims that have been made about the Okinawans.)

I doubt people in that area avoided "too many" eggs or "too much" meat when they had them. As for "low to moderate amounts" of wine - maybe that would be best, but I doubt it's what they've ever done except in case of dire shortage.

### AKMan — 2012-09-16T22:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-51967>

The diet was popularized by Ancel Keys, if that says anything...

### freesia — 2012-09-17T00:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-51989>

I'd add (in the fine print) to grow any carbs in well cared for, mineralized soil and not to overwash them before eating. Maybe some ferments made by natural exposure to the environment. And I'd probably specify some dairy from goats??

### Michael — 2012-09-17T08:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-52075>

> **AKMan;52582 wrote:** The diet was popularized by Ancel Keys, if that says anything...
>
>
>
>
> Indeed. As Sally and Mary have it in the link I gave:
>
>
>
> Dr. Keys returned to Oxford where, as an underpaid visiting professor, he and his wife endured an unheated house and got by on food rations. He then had the brilliant idea of visiting sunny Naples to check out Professor Bergami's claim. Once there, he discovered the trotterias and dined on "simple pasta and plain pizza." Keys says he discovered that heart attacks were indeed rare in Naples, "except among the small class of rich people whose diet differed from that of the general population-they ate meat every day instead of very week or two." His wife amused herself by measuring serum cholesterol concentrations "and found them to be very low except among members of the Rotary Club." After this exacting research, Keys was able to conclude that "there seemed to be an association between the diet, serum cholesterol and coronary heart disease."

I think that sarcastic "after this exacting research" is fully justified. Based on no real evidence and little systematic and honest inquiry, these people have been leading most of the developed world on an exhausting and potential fatal dance for much of the past fifty years.

I suppose in deference to the supposed connections with the Med one should call it a tarantella.

I think it would be interesting to explore the traditional cuisine of, say, places like Provence and Catalonia. There's no evidence Keys ever did that or would have been interested to. I wonder if he even knows a word of [Occitan](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occitan_language).

I think people in that area probably do eat too largely of cereals and pulses. However, I don't see them, as implied in Wikipedia criterion number 5, saying things like: "Make sure that's a small serving of bouillabaisse, Henri; really I prefer dry bread, so long as it's wholemeal, and some lentils."

It's to Sally's credit both that she understands this, and that she is genuinely interested in what people eat, how they source it, and how they prepare it.

Jimmy Moore had a guest on who had written a book of recipes for a low-carb Mediterranean Diet:

[http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/the-llvlc-show-episode-522-conner-middelmann-whitney-beats-cancer-with-a-low-carb-mediterranean-diet/12543](http://http://livinlavidalowcarb.com/blog/the-llvlc-show-episode-522-conner-middelmann-whitney-beats-cancer-with-a-low-carb-mediterranean-diet/12543)

That book would probably be worth having.

### Linz — 2012-09-17T11:34:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-52079>

In a recent television programme about food in France (I think it was Raymond Blanc) they said the French take pride in using the local source of fats - olive oil in growing areas and butter in dairy farming areas. In addition, their dairy produce is much more likely to be from A2 cows and the cheese made from raw milk.

### Michael — 2012-09-17T12:58:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-52083>

> **Linz;52696 wrote:** In a recent television programme about food in France (I think it was Raymond Blanc) they said the French take pride in using the local source of fats - olive oil in growing areas and butter in dairy farming areas. In addition, their dairy produce is much more likely to be from A2 cows and the cheese made from raw milk.

Interesting points.

I think also the dairy is more likely to be from goats or sheep in this region. I think you've probably got lush grass in, say, Normandy for the cows, but that would be less true farther south. Goats are browsers rather than grazers and can manage where other livestock couldn't.

It would be interesting to see the data Keith Woodford has for type 1 diabetes projected as a map.

### AKMan — 2012-09-17T18:51:00+0300

Something tells me that the Mediterranean Diet they are selling us is way different from the diet of Mediterranean people...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T01:48:23+0300

### skline@peak.org — 2012-09-23T04:21:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-53551>

hmmmm,

Well, while you guys are having fun, maybe I can devise an Epi-Oregon diet.

Drive west one hour to the coast. Buy local seafood (fish and shellfish -- Dungeness crab, for instance) down

by the harbor where the boats come in. Lightly poach the fish, then make soup from the water.

Collect seaweed at low tide. Learn the kinds. If kelp meal for the garden becomes hard to get, collect extra seaweed for the garden.

Grow a few non-starchy veggies, and lacto-ferment some, like small pickling cucumbers. Keep for winter in the garage. Supplement the soil in raised beds with plenty of kelp meal and compost. Greens like kale and mache and parsley can be grown all winter long. Garlic will grow, but don't count on it for selenium. There is hardly any in the soil.

Eat fresh fruit in modest quantities from the yard only, especially unusual fruits not bred for high sugar, nor polluted by agribusiness's ugly hand, nor picked by underpaid labor.

Make vinegar from apples in the yard, and from pinot noir grapes in the yard.

Have a few local eggs and some filbert-fed side pork from three miles down the country road. Local grassfed lamb from people who only keep a small herd of sheep. Be sure to get the liver, heart, and kidneys. Make bony soups in the crock pot from all the tough parts.

One or two filbert bushes in the yard, just for fun, but most of the nuts shared with friends.

Blueberries, copious blueberries in season, small servings of frozen blueberries the rest of the year.

One jar of coconut manna per year, and four or five lemons, mostly squeezed over fish or into the

filtered drinking water.

Another exception to local eating: lots of avocados from California, and a little olive oil from there,

until the small olive tree I've planted gets big and I figure out how to press the olives. This will

provide a natural limit for how much olive oil to eat.

Rule: when cutting and digging out the incredibly invasive Himalayan Evergreen Blackberries,

eat any which are ripe.

Winter: go out in the rain lightly clad, but with a rain hat. Keep the house warm enough to avoid mold and mildew, but do CT in cold water and/or by going outside.

Steadfast rule: supplement Vitamin D3 and selenium all year long.

No dairy, no nightshades. Tomatoes hardly ripen here anyway.

I guess that will do for going on with ...

### Joann — 2012-09-23T05:54:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-53557>

Well. I can tell ya' what my grandparents ate.......conch, sardines, eggplant, bitter greens, olives, anchovies, clams, peppers, lots of eggs,liver and onions, salads, semolina bread, pasta with ? (vegetable), cheese (for grating, ricotta) coffee, tea, wine, anisette .....Im hungry!

oh, steak ...porterhouse, seldom chicken, turkey. ground sirloin. chick peas, peas, escarole was big, broccoli rabe, potatoes with something....like eggs or stew, not alone. pork...bacon, ham, garlic, basil, oregano, tomatoes, zucchini, veal, capers. only olive oil.

one of my grandmother's ate a raw egg every morning at 6 am, she never, ever went to a doctor or dentist! she was 86 when she died. had all her teeth, worked until about 80 yrs old. loved her dogs and cats.

I think its more than diet, its a different attitude toward living and what you eat.

word that comes to mind is JOY.

### AKMan — 2012-09-23T20:14:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-mediterranean-diet.3696/post-53652>

> **Joann;54199 wrote:** Well. I can tell ya' what my grandparents ate.......conch, sardines, eggplant, bitter greens, olives, anchovies, clams, peppers, lots of eggs,liver and onions, salads, semolina bread, pasta with ? (vegetable), cheese (for grating, ricotta) coffee, tea, wine, anisette .....Im hungry!
>
> oh, steak ...porterhouse, seldom chicken, turkey. ground sirloin. chick peas, peas, escarole was big, broccoli rabe, potatoes with something....like eggs or stew, not alone. pork...bacon, ham, garlic, basil, oregano, tomatoes, zucchini, veal, capers. only olive oil.
>
> one of my grandmother's ate a raw egg every morning at 6 am, she never, ever went to a doctor or dentist! she was 86 when she died. had all her teeth, worked until about 80 yrs old. loved her dogs and cats.
>
>
>
> I think its more than diet, its a different attitude toward living and what you eat.
>
> word that comes to mind is JOY.

My grandparents/great grandparents were German immigrant settlers of the Black Swamp in NW Ohio. They had no access to seafood other than freshwater fish. I know from the stories that they ate lots of brains, organs, and lard along with raised animals and wild game. They were said to eat the brains of everything they killed. We all laughed at this when we were kids thinking, 'Wow, must suck to be that hungry'. Who's laughing now?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T20:38:51+0300

# Thread: stuffy nose/allergies....thinking of getting nose surgies,  dont want too though
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-09-16T21:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-51963>

Hello, I have been suffering from allegies and asthma for years, since I was in the 6th grade actually. For so long I was following the usual doctor advise since being a kid until I found Shawn Croxton with Underground Wellness which in turn I found out about Dr. Kruse.

I fear that the damage of eating unhealthy though thinking that I was eating healthy with eating grains, avoiding fat and cholesterol, etc....that I have damaged my breathing.

I am using AFRIN to help me breath, I know it is not good thing to do, but its soo stuffed that I cannot breath at all to my nose.

I was wondering if any you guys know what type of foods or anything else for that matter that I can try to help make me breath normally again. Doctors just give me pills and more pills and shoving up water with salt up my nose.

If there is nothing else then I thinking of heading under the knife to open my nose up.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-17T00:03:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-51988>

Danny, I know all too well your issues. I've been a lifelong mouth breather with asthma and a deviated septum. If your septum is truly deviated, you may need surgery. But before that, try the true epi-Paleo template for about 6 months.

The one thing that's been SO amazing to me out of all the things this template has done for me is the clear breathing. I, too, lived on decongestants. Guess what I just did yesterday, in fact? I cleared out my medicine cabinet of all my expired meds, because I just haven't needed them. I threw out bottles of Nyquil and packages of Sudafed.

I've lost weight, my skin issues have cleared up (eczema), my sleep is sound. But the absolute winner for me is that I can BREATHE.

I lay down at night, while I'm drifting off to sleep, and it still amazes me how clear and quiet my breathing is now. Like, oh, this is how a normal person is supposed to breathe?

I grew up with a vaporizer in my room and Vick's on my chest. Literally, for decades. So I hear you.

### chocolate — 2012-09-17T00:09:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-51991>

Hey Danny,

we are just wowed by the sublingual biotin from solaray. It is sweet... which is bad... but it makes your nose clear very quickly. Its pretty consistent in our family.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-17T17:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-52110>

PrimalPam, Im just going to start that, hopefully eating seasonally will do wonders for me, that is long term though since I have to follow the plan.

chocolate i will try that, hopefully that will work, beats afrin.

how fast is pretty quick? I am assuming in minutes??

### ealachan — 2012-09-17T17:40:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-52115>

If you're still eating a lot of dairy, you might also consider cutting it out for a while to see if it helps. Cutting way down on dairy helped my asthma quite a bit.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-17T18:16:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-52131>

The only dairy I drink is A2 type Raw Milk. A2 type is the healthy milk. so far my Ketosi diet is very plain and i am trying to add things.

### shah78 — 2012-09-17T18:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-52135>

find a good neuromuscular therapist, neurosomatic therapist or rolfer and have them do the "Nose routine" on you. They will open your nasal cavity up with their pinky! I was constantly digging wet tissues out of my pants pockets after the washing machine for the first 37 years of my life. Now nada. A pinky may sound extreme, but it is a hell of a lot more gentle than a scalpel.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-17T21:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-52186>

use a pinky? that sounds painful! might just request scalpel

### mafixit — 2012-09-21T03:17:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-53068>

I found that my food allergies were causing me to suffer. I cut out the foods I was allergic to and I haven't needed any allergy meds in months. I also use Sinusense (a contraption that works like a water pic) to rinse out my sinuses.

I used to live on claritan, allegra, sudafed.... Now I only have an occasional attack and if I use my rinse, I am usually good to go.

### Destiny — 2012-10-02T14:03:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55783>

> **x\_danny\_x;52578 wrote:** Hello, I have been suffering from allegies and asthma for years, since I was in the 6th grade actually. For so long I was following the usual doctor advise since being a kid until I found Shawn Croxton with Underground Wellness which in turn I found out about Dr. Kruse.
>
>
>
> I fear that the damage of eating unhealthy though thinking that I was eating healthy with eating grains, avoiding fat and cholesterol, etc....that I have damaged my breathing.
>
>
>
> I am using AFRIN to help me breath, I know it is not good thing to do, but its soo stuffed that I cannot breath at all to my nose.
>
>
>
> I was wondering if any you guys know what type of foods or anything else for that matter that I can try to help make me breath normally again. Doctors just give me pills and more pills and shoving up water with salt up my nose.
>
>
>
> If there is nothing else then I thinking of heading under the knife to open my nose up.

Danny,

My husband lived on Afrin and Claritin-D for over 10 yrs. When he started taking cold showers twice a day since February, all of a sudden he could breath. He slowly removed Claritin and Afrin. He has been Afrin and Claritin free since April.

### steve@wity.net — 2012-10-02T15:04:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55792>

I would go along with Destiny's suggestion. I would also suggest starting the 50 degree water CT face dunks if you haven't already.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-02T17:42:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55830>

i take 30 to 45 minute showers, cold water from the tap. I have been doing it for a month so far, I will give it more time. Hopefully besides eating Epi Paleo this would be enough.

### Adria — 2012-10-02T18:47:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55847>

Most likely your problem is food allergy. Stop all dairy and do an elimination diet. Start with 1-2 basic foods, no drink but water, slowly add one food at a time. Anything you ingest is suspect, including spices.

Afrin is addictive and will only make your sinus condition worse over time, you should wean yourself off this and all allergy meds.

### BenG — 2012-10-02T18:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55848>

Have you talked with an ENT about a turbinectomy? I know it isn't fun to have surgery, but I was a chronic mouth breather until I has the surgery to reduce the swollen/inflamed turbinates in my nose. I've been breathing through it fine ever since although it took about a year for everything to settle down to where it works great.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-02T19:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffy-nose-allergies-thinking-of-getting-nose-surgies-dont-want-too-though.3697/post-55855>

Yeah, I talked to my doctor about that, but she wants me to do the stuff that other people have mentioned. I will try the cold, and also start fresh with dairies. I really dont want surgery but will go that route if everything else fails.

# Thread: Tell me about Hmart
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-16T23:50:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-51981>

I remember reading some posts about Hmart, an Asian grocery chain and thinking I wish I had one near me. The closest is an hour and a half away. Just realized there is an Hmart very close to where I have to go for work tomorrow!

I looked at their online sales flyer and am psyched! I was wondering how you all found shrimp with the heads still on . . . now I know. They have them for $3.99 lb this week and full mackerel for $1.99 lb. I'm stocking up  What else should I be on the lookout for?

For those that do shop there, are you pretty convinced the quality is fairly decent? Is it mostly farm-raised or wild-caught stuff?

Oh, and will they gut the mackerel or do I not want it gutted? I'm heading into uncharted waters here (pun intended) and need some suggestions/guidance!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T00:10:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-51992>

At the fish counter you get to decide what gets gutted. Look for some fish roe but be sure to buy pure and not adulterated. Quality wise is a guess - they label wild caught vs farm raised and I go with that. Not sure that is honest though. Their shrimp are all farm raised.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-17T02:48:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52031>

Thanks Soul. I didn't even think about roe. I've been buying it online at a hefty price. I hope I can find some decent stuff at Hmart. My budget has been taking a major hit lately and I had to stop ordering it.

Good to know they do label the stuff; hopefully it is honest. I'm not a big fan of farm raised shrimp. Have you bought some there? Any issues with it?

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-09-17T02:48:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52032>

Hmart has a large selection of sea veggies .... packages are much larger than I found in Whole Foods and prices are much better.

### JoeBranca — 2012-09-17T03:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52034>

if you're interested in kimchi, look out for white kimchi (jarred i think) which should be good to go without excessive spice or MSG. if they have an in-house kimchi making operation, try to find out if MSG is added before you pick some up. the packaging does list ingredients but i suspect it doesn't list _everything

_

might be worth checking the frozen section as well as dried foods section for smaller whole fish like smelts, anchovies, etc.

sometimes there's sushi grade shellfish (scallop, surf clam) that isn't too expensive.

and be on the lookout for off the beaten path items that may show up at the seafood displays. i scored some shredded jellyfish for a great price!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T03:12:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52036>

> **BJK77;52648 wrote:** Thanks Soul. I didn't even think about roe. I've been buying it online at a hefty price. I hope I can find some decent stuff at Hmart. My budget has been taking a major hit lately and I had to stop ordering it.
>
>
>
> Good to know they do label the stuff; hopefully it is honest. I'm not a big fan of farm raised shrimp. Have you bought some there? Any issues with it?

I have and I ate them all whole heads shell antennae etc. My biggest issue was that they came in 4lbs frozen blocks with preservatives added. I just can't eat 4lbs with the shell before they go bad.... the shells upset my digestion if I eat more than a bowl full a day.

Definitely get the roe. 7 bucks a lb I buy A LOT of it and can easily eat 1/3lb in one sitting. I try to eat some every day. Make sure it is unadulterated. Salmon roe is expensive so I rarely get that. Mine is called Sweet Fish roe. Tiny little beads.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T03:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52038>

I also buy frozen shucked oysters there. I go through about 3-4lbs a week..... but I think I might have gotten sick from those when I ate them raw. Oppsie. I still eat them. Cooked

### kathylu — 2012-09-17T03:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52040>

Be careful of quality at Hmart. Sometimes the fish sits quite a while before it sells or is thrown out. Fresh fish should not smell like fish...just a clean, ocean scent. Eyes should be clear, not sunken. Gills should look moist and red, not dry and pale. If they have a fish price you like and it doesn't look as fresh as you would like, ask for fresher fish from their walk-in cooler. They will prepare the fish any way you like...I usually have mine filleted and ask them to bag up all the trimmings for fish broth. As for roe, I get flying fish roe (fresh, not seasoned). Yummy, tiny orange eggs that are served in sushi restaurants. You can generally get great prices on cleaned squid too, make ceviche from squid rings or marinate for a seafood salad. Good stir fried too.

> **Cavemam;52684 wrote:** ok, I'm going to mackrel head smoothy. I think. Soul - where did you get yours? I didn't see anything at WF that looked appropriate. . .I don't even know what a Mackrel looks like!! Also, anyplace besides scary asian markets to get roe?

### BJK77 — 2012-09-17T14:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52091>

Thanks so much for all the tips! I can't wait to see what all I can find! I'll be like a SAD kid in a candy store

### Glamazon — 2012-09-19T08:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52646>

> **Cavemam;52684 wrote:** ok, I'm going to mackrel head smoothy. I think. Soul - where did you get yours? I didn't see anything at WF that looked appropriate. . .I don't even know what a Mackrel looks like!! Also, anyplace besides scary asian markets to get roe?

I see whole mackerel at my WF almost every time I go and I am over there almost every day. The guys have a lot of frozen stuff in the back like salmon heads for 1.99 a pound. They told me if I call a few days ahead they will put special orders on the side so I can make fish bone broth. They said they would put skin back for me too. Just talk to the guys at the seafood counter. Same goes with the beef. The head butcher behind the meat counter says they often store the offal over in the pet food freezer because they don't have enough space. Interestingly enough the beef offal is more expensive than the seafood offal, like 5.99-3.99 over 1.99 at the seafood dept.

I never thought to ask them about roe if they have any fresh frozen after they clean.

Hmart's roe does come frozen. It is kinda scary looking to me sitting in the chest freezer. LOL It comes still in the sack instead of like the fresh sweet roe which is masago or a flying fish or smelt type egg which comes loose in a plastic tub. I love the little crunchy orange stuff.

You have to be careful over there and shop like kathylu says because I see plenty of sunken eyed fish over there at the end of the day after work. Maybe if I went over in the AM it would be a different story. There's a hell of a lot of frozen fish and frozen shell fish selection over there too. You would not believe the diverse sea weed selection. Can come in big sheets like a pillow case in a package.

I am also very hesitant to buy the beef/pork because I know it comes from the big ag industry meat places even though the selection of different types of cut is huge. They have every kind of fowl you can think of besides chicken.

They have an incredible selection of exotic fruits and veggies. You can actually buy fresh bitter melon if you choose. I had fresh bitter melon in a dish when I was in Bejing a few years ago. It was cooked with these hot peppers and the dish was neither hot nor bitter with the combo of the two. My fav veggie from there is lotus root. It has a cabbage like taste and you can eat it raw or cooked or pickled. In Chinese medicine, lotus root is said to cure lung problems.

### kathylu — 2012-09-19T17:55:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52710>

If you have a dehydrator, dried bitter melon (sprinkled with kelp granules) is really good!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T18:14:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52714>

Glamazon, where did you find the frozen whole roe at Denver Hmart? I've only found the tubs with Sweet Fish roe.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-19T18:33:44+0300

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-19T18:34:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52723>

### Glamazon — 2012-09-20T04:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-hmart.3702/post-52851>

> **Souldanzer;53342 wrote:** Glamazon, where did you find the frozen whole roe at Denver Hmart? I've only found the tubs with Sweet Fish roe.

It is in one of those long open freezers in the center of the aisle to the left of the fresh fish as you face towards the back of the store. The ones I saw were in plastic clear bags or wrapping I think. They are still inside the huge egg sac if I remember correctly. I had no idea what they were, because they are awfully gross looking frozen, tan in color, until i got close and read the tag. The sacs are probably about the size of a mango. Maybe over by the frozen shell fish? More in the furthest part of that particular freezer in relation to the closest part being near the fresh fish area.

Hope that helps.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-17T20:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-lets-hear-about-yours.3703/post-52183>

> **bigknitwit;52636 wrote:** Does anyone know how long pregnenelone (or DHEA) takes to stabilize in serum (for testing purposes)? I know typically endocrinologists wants 5-6 weeks for TSH and thyroid hormones to stabilize (wondering about oral T3 as well here though). I'm wondering how long to hold a dose before testing to see where it's at.

Don't know about DHEA or preg but I tested after 8 weeks and there was definitely a noticeable increase.Although I don't know if they have a half life or not which is why you would need to wait, I think.

TSH is a practically useless test when you are on thyroid meds IMHO. If you want to know how you are doing on one you should be monitoring your FT3 & FT4 and know where your antibodies are. There is a half life associated with T4 so you need at least six weeks for the T4 to be in your system. T3 only lasts approx 8 hours in your body so you don't need extra time to see where you are at. However, if you are switching form a T4 to a T3 med you have to wait 6-8 weeks to get that T4 completely out of your system before you can test how you are doing on the T3.

# Thread: Meg from Georgia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meg-from-georgia.3704/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Meg — 2012-09-17T00:47:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meg-from-georgia.3704/post-52002>

I had my first consultation with Dr. K on Friday and found myself shaking as we ended our session. Iâ€™ve wanted answers for so long on why I feel so crummy but I must admit there is some truth in the saying that ignorance is bliss. And as most of you know Dr. K is not one to promote any kind of ignorance and hold back on truth- no matter how bad it might hurt.

I do want the truth because I know that the truth will ultimately set me free to find more truth. But some of the truth on Friday hit a place in my heart that is raw and afraid. The truth that if I donâ€™t fix what is wrong with me I will end up like my mother- and my mother, at 71 years old canâ€™t remember bits of a conversation we just had, or my birthday or much else that would make her able to function without the constant attention of my father. The Alzheimerâ€™s showed up at age 67. All my life I have fought to not be like my mother â€“ which is another story in itself, and here I am coming to the realization that physically I really am currently headed down a very similar path.

The past 8 months have been hard, lots of physical ailments. But the hardest part has been the mental side. The anxiety, the lack of clear thinking, the fear and irrationality that has taken hold of my mind and has made me feel like a crazy personâ€¦... made me feel like my mother. It is hard to be an advocate for yourself when you are in this kind of state of mind, but somehow I was led to Dr Kâ€™s site and have been eating up as much information as my poor brain can hold. And implementing as much as I can. This Gut/Brain series was written for me! I feel like I know a lot of the regular participants on this forum because I have read and reread many of your entries. The fog has lifted enough for me to pursue last Fridayâ€™s appointment and now to write these words and start to pursue help from those on this site who are running hard after optimal. Because I want nothing less than optimal for the rest of my years and I know I can change my future.

I have much work to do. I have Candida and gut flora issues, wacked out hormones, glucose and cortisol problems. I will be pursuing metformin, cycloset and more BHRT and new doctors. I hope to dig in and start asking questions and I look forward to becoming friends. I know that I will find the answers I need and find the health that I want.

Hi, my name is Meg. I am a wife and mother to four awesome children. I live in Peachtree City GA (ATL area), Iâ€™m a real estate agent, and am a very creative upbeat person. I have an addictive personality, I used to be addicted to sugar but now I am addicted to finding health and truth and optimal!

Today I ate a dozen raw oysters & a half a pound of fish for lunch. There is no turning back!

PS. Dr. Kruse was nothing but kind and sensitive while dishing out honesty during our consult- I would never want to give the impression otherwise and make anyone to hesitate to seek his help.

### caroline — 2012-09-17T01:41:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meg-from-georgia.3704/post-52014>

welcome to the forum and optimum! I am so glad you had such a positive experience with Dr. K. and I look forward to hearing all about your journey. When you post your successes \[and failures\] it helps everyone. There are a lot of really smart,kind people here that are very happy to answer questions or discuss whatever....Good luck

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-17T01:56:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meg-from-georgia.3704/post-52024>

Welcome Meg...all the best with your journey!

# Thread: 2-4am wakings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T02:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52027>

Sorry if I am asking a question here that has been answered over and over again  I still wake up almost every night exactly 4 hrs after falling asleep. I'm wide awake within minutes and I'm pretty sure this is a misplaced cortisol spike. I stay awake for hours, usually at least until 6am, then sleep in late. Still a zombie during the day despite sleeping in.

So my questions:

1) Reasons for waking during the typical 2-4am period...?

2) How do I turn off the cortisol spike if that's what's waking me up?

PS is the only thing that comes to my mind but I would have to take it during the night when I wake. I've also tried it during the past with limited success. I've also tried oxytocin, melatonin, GABA, Kava root, Ativan, valerian/sullcap tea in all sorts of combinations... no success.

Still a zombie during the day despite sleeping in.

I'd love to know this too. My wake up is 5 hours after falling asleep.

### Destiny — 2012-09-17T02:24:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52029>

### chocolate — 2012-09-17T03:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52039>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22330017](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22330017)

Protein ingestion before sleep improves postexercise overnight recovery.

Res PT, Groen B, Pennings B, Beelen M, Wallis GA, Gijsen AP, Senden JM, VAN Loon LJ.

Source

Department of Human Movement Sciences, NUTRIM School for Nutrition, Toxicology and Metabolism, Maastricht University Medical Centre+, Maastricht, The Netherlands.

Abstract

INTRODUCTION:

> The role of nutrition in modulating postexercise overnight recovery remains to be elucidated. We assessed the effect of protein ingestion immediately before sleep on digestion and absorption kinetics and protein metabolism during overnight recovery from a single bout of resistance-type exercise.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> Sixteen healthy young males performed a single bout of resistance-type exercise in the evening (2000 h) after a full day of dietary standardization. All subjects were provided with appropriate recovery nutrition (20 g of protein, 60 g of CHO) immediately after exercise (2100 h). Thereafter, 30 min before sleep (2330 h), subjects ingested a beverage with (PRO) or without (PLA) 40 g of specifically produced intrinsically \[1-C\]phenylalanine-labeled casein protein. Continuous intravenous infusions with \[ring-H5\]phenylalanine and \[ring-H2\]tyrosine were applied with blood and muscle samples collected to assess protein digestion and absorption kinetics, whole-body protein balance and mixed muscle protein synthesis rates throughout the night (7.5 h).
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> During sleep, casein protein was effectively digested and absorbed resulting in a rapid rise in circulating amino acid levels, which were sustained throughout the remainder of the night. Protein ingestion before sleep increased whole-body protein synthesis rates (311 Â± 8 vs 246 Â± 9 μmolÂ·kg per 7.5 h) and improved net protein balance (61 Â± 5 vs -11 Â± 6 μmolÂ·kg per 7.5 h) in the PRO vs the PLA experiment (P < 0.01). Mixed muscle protein synthesis rates were ∼22% higher in the PRO vs the PLA experiment, which reached borderline significance (0.059%Â·h Â± 0.005%Â·h vs 0.048%Â·h Â± 0.004%Â·h, P = 0.05).
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> This is the first study to show that protein ingested immediately before sleep is effectively digested and absorbed, thereby stimulating muscle protein synthesis and improving whole-body protein balance during postexercise overnight recovery.

I'm not backing off on night time protein. It helps a little when I wake up. I just eat a couple of bites and pass right back out. I know it doesn't align with the study, but if my tummy is talking or my brain wants me up, I figure its for a reason. I don't do so much fat at night. I seem to need more protein. Its usually cold chicken. I keep a kind of decent one in the fridge to hit hard if I don't have time for fish. Im not uncomfortable with hunger, I just can't sleep and want to. I hope I get enough gut flora and veggies to make my own butyric acid someday. Then I can sleep through again. I don't even have to pee usually, just eat a couple of bites.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-17T03:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52042>

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-17T18:20:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52134>

> **Shijin13;52701 wrote:** Increase to a couple of times /day see if that helps.

Already doing that  I take a supplement "shot" with BAB and dinner. I'm going to increase it anyways though I think. Going to make some dandelion/milk thistle concentrated tincture to add throughout the day and see where that gets me. Upping all the liver stuff to at least 3x/day.

I slept a bit fitfully last night but I slept and didn't spend hours awake in the middle. So I'm going to continue refining my current plan of attack with this. Yesterday was:

\- lots of NT support during the day,

\- increased L-theanine in the AM,

\- ginseng and gingko in the AM to increase ATP during the day (which will help with adenosine increase in the PM)

\- milk thistle/dandelion root tea + 400mg mg chloride with dinner

\- valerian/skullcap tea an hour before bed

\- 1hr CT before bed (tap water temp)

\- GABA+oxytocin+melatonin while going to bed

\- new rule of staying out of bed during the day.... bed is for sleeping only

\- new bed sheets... I think those made all the difference

\- I ate dinner right before 8pm. I know this isn't optimal (got a late start into the day yesterday and didn't eat most of the day so it was necessary....) but I think it's better for me to have a "snack" or later dinner if that helps me sleep than not sleeping at all.

Took forever to fall asleep. I've switched back to regular green tea from decaf in the AM (trying to wake myself up a bit for the day) so if the trouble with falling asleep stays I will have to go back to decaf. My heart was pounding once again.... going to try to fix that with increasing my electrolyte support today.

### atkarlovsky — 2012-09-18T12:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52312>

I joined just to drop some information by for you all.

[http://www.polyphasicsociety.com/polyphasic-sleep/overviews/segmented-sleep/](http://http://www.polyphasicsociety.com/polyphasic-sleep/overviews/segmented-sleep/)

From what I understand segmented sleep is actually a natural sleeping pattern. I tried to change my sleeping patterns to segmented sleep without knowing about lighting conditions that cause melatonin disruption, and I struggled. As soon as I stopped leaving overhead lights on and using the no-blue bulb lamps at night I started naturally sleeping segmented.

Those of you who wake up after 3.5-5h sleep normally, you are actually lucky, because you are doing something right!

There are also documented polyphasic sleeping indigenous peoples, so polyphasic sleep is a natural ability for humans. TMYK

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-18T19:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-4am-wakings.3705/post-52386>

I find the balance of blacking out my room (b/c of a little bit of street lights, but NOT bad like how it was in Burlington) to getting morning sun in the room. I close my curtains and put my blanket like a fort so it's 'black' where I'm sleeping. I'm lucky though that my room is south facing (window faces south) and the other side is east. I was staying in a west facing bedroom for 2 weeks at my new place to 'test it out'. I was noticing I was starting to sleep later, like past 7am...almost 8am despite 10pm bed time. Yikes. Now that I've moved 'back' to my east-south room I'm waking up before 7am again. I wonder though, I never really manage to get up at 6:30, but rather 6:40 or :50. This might be due to changing light patterns of the Fall. Anyways, I wonder if blacking out a room is better if it means no 'morning sun' shining in the room to 'wake' you up.

Hmmm...

Blackout curtains on top of the cover. My room is pitch dark at night but the tiny bit of morning light still wakes me up.... which tells me how important even the least bit of light exposure is. So I do wake up with the sun now and how been for a few weeks if I get a decent night.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T17:53:15+0300

I have not been awake at night for 3 nights in a row now and the last one was the best sleep of all of them so I'm hoping this is a new pattern awakening. So maybe try some of the things I listed earlier. Really honestly, I think this is the liver-related issue which gets helped by eating carbs before bed..... I don't remember the name for it. Personally I have decided I'm going to do what I need to do to get sleep for now, if that involves eating a snack/dinner right at 8pm so be it. I was feeling really, really bad the last few weeks on no sleep and all that's supposed to happen by keeping your insulin down before bed won't happen anyways if you're chronically awake during the night. I'm going to keep trying the move the last food earlier and less insulin-spiking. But slowly. I need to refresh my memory about what exactly causes this to happen but I remember it being discussed before.

If you already are awake, I know this is a no-no, but I used to fix that (before Kruseism) by eating a snack when I wake. At this point, anything for sleep in my case, even snacking in the night. Until I know how to fix it.
