# Thread: ASI,   and salivary cortisol things
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T18:10:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52365>

Ok,

in getting my hit list together on "what's next", big at the top is the ASI/salivary cortisol stuff.

My question for you folks that have done it - is are there any big changes in your life that you've made since getting sub-optimal results that made it worth it?

I'm all for having the info, but what actions would one take if it were good vs bad?

For me, I'm already living as epi-paleo, circadian focused as I can. Is there some other magic that one would do to fix a bad asi?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-18T18:18:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52369>

Cavemam, how is it bad? Low in the morning? High all day?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T18:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52370>

> **Nonchalant;52992 wrote:** Cavemam, how is it bad? Low in the morning? High all day?

Dunno! I'm trying to triage what tests to do next! I have no sleep/energy issues - and frankly, don't think it's "that bad". But, then I started to think - ok, lets assume it IS bad. .then what would I do different in my day to day life.

### MartiD — 2012-09-18T18:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52380>

I'm going to try to get this done with new doctor in December but after reading so many posts I would love to see what is "optimal" readings. Most people who post their results are in range but not "optimal'? What is the best morning, noon, evening readings to have?

### MsYo — 2012-09-18T19:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52411>

I had an ASI test done in late April but I didn't get the results until a couple of weeks ago. I am taking a new test in a couple of weeks to have a comparison. My Cortisol spiked in the afternoon likely due to stress. I did not have melantonin results and my DHEA did not appear optimal (increased dosage) I am working diligently to reset my circadian clock as well as reduce stress. I am researching TM techniques and I have purchased Phosphatidylserine to help with the afternoon cortisol spike.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T19:40:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52420>

After getting my results last year and finding out they were all in the low part of the ranges I made some changes. Only walked for exercise (no more boot camp workouts), this was when I stopped eating gluten, made sure i was in bed by 10pm and slept at least 8 hours, meditate, try to remove as much stress as possible, and also took some supplements from my doc for adrenals. Then I started taking my T3 early in the morning a couple of hours before waking and BAM! with all that my adrenals (except my high morning reading) are practically perfect.

I think it is wise to know what is going on. Sometimes you may not feel a dysfunction and it's best to catch it before it goes worse. To me the ASI test is a biggie!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T19:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52423>

> **Patty Cakes;53043 wrote:** After getting my results last year and finding out they were all in the low part of the ranges I made some changes. Only walked for exercise (no more boot camp workouts), this was when I stopped eating gluten, made sure i was in bed by 10pm and slept at least 8 hours, meditate, try to remove as much stress as possible, and also took some supplements from my doc for adrenals. Then I started taking my T3 early in the morning a couple of hours before waking and BAM! with all that my adrenals (except my high morning reading) are practically perfect.
>
>
>
> I think it is wise to know what is going on. Sometimes you may not feel a dysfunction and it's best to catch it before it goes worse. To me the ASI test is a biggie!

Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T19:48:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52426>

> **Cavemam;53046 wrote:** Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

hehehe, the worst thing I do for my health now is read this damn website!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T19:56:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52433>

> **Cavemam;53046 wrote:** Hmmmm. These are all changes I've already made. Was your HS-CRP high? Mine is not, I've already re-engineered my life to be very low stress, very happy inducing and no longer boot camping it etc. I just don't know that there is anything ELSE I can do that this test would prompt.

My hs-CRP is in the 2 range so it's high. But I'm going into menopause and have Hashis so that is kinda expected even though I am doing everything to lower it. Another thing to consider is stress is not always an outside force. What if something is going on inside your body that is causing internal stress and you didn't know about it but it is affecting you? Just a thought... I was on thyroid meds and that had stopped the fatigue. I didn't know I had an adrenal issue and if you asked me I would have told you I didn't. I got up at 5:30am every morning, felt great, rarely drank coffee, etc. The test is what warned me I had an adrenal problem. For me this test was invaluable but may not be for you. Everyone is different.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T19:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52434>

> **Patty Cakes;53056 wrote:** My hs-CRP is in the 2 range so it's high. But I'm going into menopause and have Hashis so that is kinda expected even though I am doing everything to lower it. Another thing to consider is stress is not always an outside force. What if something is going on inside your body that is causing internal stress and you didn't know about it but it is affecting you? Just a thought... I was on thyroid meds and that had stopped the fatigue. I didn't know I had an adrenal issue and if you asked me I would have told you I didn't. I got up at 5:30am every morning, felt great, rarely drank coffee, etc. The test is what warned me I had an adrenal problem. For me this test was invaluable but may not be for you. Everyone is different.

So let's say I do it, and find out it's an issue - what would I do (take?) that I'm not already? Is this used WITH the thyroid panel to decide if you want to treat with t3 etc?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-18T20:11:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52440>

It is connected to thyroid where usually one gland goes whacky then the other. IIRC, the thyroid usually goes first. I don't know if you would _take_ anything or just become more aware of things around you and make some subtle changes. Being more mindful in everyday life can be very helpful especially in adrenal distress.

So what is keeping you from getting the information? Are you reluctant to get any testing? Why is this different from say a thyroid test that shows everything is normal except your FT3 is slightly low? Do you want to take something for that? Maybe not. But it may make you aware of other changes such as maybe I should eat shellfish 3x/day to get that iodine in my system to help raise the FT3 production. Awareness creates thoughts that will help you make the most appropriate choices....again, mindfulness.

I hope I'm not sounding argumentative but that is surely not my intent. My intent is just to make you think deeper about this test. This is just one piece of the pie. I view this test as another diagnostic on the road to optimal. Obviously I'm a believer!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-18T20:27:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52459>

This really is a big piece of the puzzle. It tells you if you are on track or not. If one of them is out of rhythm, then you need to dig a little deeper to find out why. But, you said you feel well. Maybe wait for your gut test to come back and see how it fairs. Ins. usually doesn't pay for this one as far as I know.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T00:13:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-52537>

The ASI test you can get for $129 (IIRC) through STTM (stopthethyroidmadness) and mymedlab. It's done through ZRT. I just did it and you also get DHEA-s. I don't know if you can do an add-on of melatonin though to the special STTM kit.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-24T01:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-53706>

I think you ask a great question - would your lifestyle change based on the results of the test? Are you already doing everything advisable to live optimally? If you wouldn't do anything different, then it seems to me it's not an essential test. It could still be useful as a baseline for comparison down the road. Or, it could be useful for peace of mind if you are a worrier. But honestly, if you feel you wouldn't make any changes regardless of the outcome, then why bother?

It was useful in my case because it confirmed the adrenal fatigue and made me realize I had to stop all exercise. That was really, really hard to let go of and I wouldn't have done it without the test results and a lot of reading & getting doctors' opinions.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T01:33:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-53708>

ASI changes the most when you have an unstable thyroid situation of a really screwed up gut. I love following it with a salivary melatonin for people with BG 11 issues.......I also like trending IGF-1 to get insights into what people are really doing over what they think they are doing.

### caroline — 2012-09-25T06:01:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-54043>

I thought I was relatively okay until I had a consult with Doctor K. the other day. I couldn't have been more wrong! He said he hadn't seen a hormone panel that bad in a long time. The one thing he said - actually more than once - mostly because I kept saying I don't feel that bad at all - was .... You \[me\] have been living in bad for so long you \[me\] don't know the difference. and now as I keep re- listening to the consult - I realize how very right he is and I can't understand why I didn't see this for myself. I kinda think when you grow up in an abusive environment - your reality and perception is different and you just push thru. My 2 cents worth anyway. Love to know your thoughts.

### CarolMorris — 2012-09-25T20:38:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/asi-and-salivary-cortisol-things.3736/post-54188>

> **Cavemam;52988 wrote:** Ok,
>
>
>
> in getting my hit list together on "what's next", big at the top is the ASI/salivary cortisol stuff.
>
>
>
> My question for you folks that have done it - is are there any big changes in your life that you've made since getting sub-optimal results that made it worth it?
>
>
>
> I'm all for having the info, but what actions would one take if it were good vs bad?
>
>
>
> For me, I'm already living as epi-paleo, circadian focused as I can. Is there some other magic that one would do to fix a bad asi?

I haven't done the ASI yet(that's next) I did the Diurnal 4X cortisol..morn, noon, 5pm and bedtime. I was sure it would come back high in the evening and bedtime because my sleep was so bad...it came back exactly the opposite, high in the am and noon, I was shocked.

Jack's educational prescription was a fun one! First in am..sun..second...sex/orgasm...third BAB.sooo...sometimes a bad test has a great solution!

# Thread: Kids, Obesity and BPA's
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-18T18:23:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/post-52371>

Might be of some interest to you...

[http://xfinity.comcast.net/articles/news-national/20120918/US.MED.Obese.Kids.BPA/?cid=hero\_media](http://http://xfinity.comcast.net/articles/news-national/20120918/US.MED.Obese.Kids.BPA/?cid=hero_media)

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-09-22T19:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/post-53430>

Here is another post about BPA in coconut milk, the traditional paleo diet's favorite milk substitute:

[http://chriskresser.com/3-reasons-why-coconut-milk-may-not-be-your-friend](http://http://chriskresser.com/3-reasons-why-coconut-milk-may-not-be-your-friend)

Chris Kresser also has a great post on the protective effect of selenium from mercury in seafood:

[http://chriskresser.com/is-eating-fish-safe-a-lot-safer-than-not-eating-fish](http://http://chriskresser.com/is-eating-fish-safe-a-lot-safer-than-not-eating-fish)

In the comment section of this post is how to make your own coconut milk and avoid all the additives. By the way, The Brazil Coconut Candy is loaded with selenium and would be a good "dessert" after your epi-paleo feasts:

[http://eatkamloops.org/brazil-coconut-candy/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/brazil-coconut-candy/)

Just say no to using plastic if at all possible. Even if it is not on your food, using plastic will get into the environment, and in the long term will get into your food and water or the food and water of someone you love:

[http://eatkamloops.org/solidarity-of-a-sort/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/solidarity-of-a-sort/)

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-22T21:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/post-53454>

I recently stopped pretty much all coconut milk. Initially I just had overeaten it and plain didn't want any any longer but now, that the "addiction" is over, I'm not going to put it back into my diet. If I want coconut, I'm going to eat coconut..... not canned coconut milk. I've always had concerns about the cans and I'm trying to move away from that in my diet. Just eat the real thing....

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T23:22:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/post-53484>

Initially I just had overeaten it and plain didn't want any any longer but now, that the "addiction" is over, I'm not going to put it back into my diet. ### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T00:05:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-obesity-and-bpas.3737/post-53492>

> **Cavemam;54125 wrote:** Let me know some best practices with this! I keep trying to get those damn coconuts out of the shells, and it's always too difficult to make it worth the effort (which then makes me wonder if nature is trying to tell me something that I should be listening to)

Same problem, same thoughts. Winter is coming and coconut will not be on the menu most likely for the most part. Possibly as CO a little bit. But in the summer I go at it with a hammer. First pierce the eyes with a screw driver, get out the water, then put the whole thing into a plastic bag and then put that elevated testosterone to good use while you go at it full force. (outside)

Dried is always a step above canned.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-teenies-poop.3738/post-52476>

Wow look at all this commenting here, Jen maybe I will always have you post things for me LOL

I had a big fat pity party for myself all morning. on to focusing on things I can actually change right here and now....

No, I have not had MHS, Cavemam.

And I paid for this OOP, my GI guy didnt think I had any reason to get this done. I kinda want to hand it to him triumphantly, like HEREYAGOSUCKAH-INYOURFACE! but since I want his cooperation....

My understanding (limited LOL) is that the fat maldigestion requires enzymes etc, so I picked up pancreatin with bile salts, super digestaway enzymes and betaine HCl. the betaine is for the protein digestion, I could do better there as well.

Basically I should be in the 3rd and 4th quintiles with the bacterias on first page, and I'm either way too high or too low. So I'm gonna look at this as blasting all these dudes away with ABx, and start from scratch. Also gonna take antifungals (diflucan?) for parasite. Gonna lobby hard to NOT take PPI (nexium etc) because the digestion issues also tend to relate to low stomach acid, and the PPI lowers acid. ay yi yi.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T20:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-teenies-poop.3738/post-52743>

> **vkiernan;53366 wrote:** Whew, my memory is so terrible somedays that I wonder. Thanks for throwing that out there. Btw, why don't you like timed-released T3? I am considering it as it supposedly only gives your body a little bit at a time like it needs. Or????

It's totally a personal thing. I did the circadian method of T3 dosage to help heal my adrenals and you cannot do that with SRT3. I have read that many people on the thyroid boards did not like it. But there were some who did like it. As a recovering control freak, I want to decide when to administer. The regular T3 gives you the control for when you need more or less.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-20T14:37:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-teenies-poop.3738/post-52879>

> **Patty Cakes;53371 wrote:** It's totally a personal thing. I did the circadian method of T3 dosage to help heal my adrenals and you cannot do that with SRT3. I have read that many people on the thyroid boards did not like it. But there were some who did like it. As a recovering control freak, I want to decide when to administer. The regular T3 gives you the control for when you need more or less.

I am really bummed. The c. pharmacy called and told me I am only getting 40 mg a day of T3 and timed release. Because I know no difference in the tr, I am ok with that for now but the pharmacist and my doc knew I needed to be able to titrate it up. I also told here that no one ever raised my Armour so what I am on may not be enough anyway. This is so MADDENING!!! You start thinking someone finally gets it and then Bamn, stops you in your tracks. I am seriously tempted to buy Cytomel on the net.

Another thing I don't understand is if T3 is mostly synthetic, why not just use cytomel and save myself the added expense of compounding???

Patty, How much T3 are you taking? I know it is all in context, but just curious. I was up to 60 mcg before my other doc shut me down and I couldn't tell I was taking any.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:54:01+0300

Try the SR and see how it goes. If after a month you don't like it call the doc and ask for either the compounded T3 or the Cytomel. Although the 40 mcg may not be enough. I don't know how the SR matches up to the cytomel for dosage comparisons. You want to keep the script here in the US since it's awfully hard to get a good T3 online. I'm struggling so I'm entertaining the thought of going back to NP Thyroid (NDT). If anyone knows of a good place online to get T3 PM the url please.

I'm currently taking 87mcg of T3. In Cytomel terms that's probably around 70mcg. I've cleared the rT3, healed my adrenals and now need to figure out what I should do for long term. I really like the T3 if I could get it. My doc wasn't keen on scripting it as she said my rT3 wasn't that high. She didn't look at the ratio. I need a new doc. I may have found one but can't afford him until my new job starting in January.

When you finally cleared enough so the t3 started working, what did you feel and what did you do at that point with your t3 dosing?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T20:13:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-teenies-poop.3738/post-52957>

> **vkiernan;53586 wrote:** NT is probably good as long as it doesn't convert to rt3. At least, that is what I have read. I am going to add milk thistle to my regimen again. That seemed to be helping last time to clear it out too.
>
>
>
> When you finally cleared enough so the t3 started working, what did you feel and what did you do at that point with your t3 dosing?

I feel a thread hack coming.... Sorry Teenie!

I didn't notice a big surge feeling like some people do when the rT3 clears. My ratio was at 16.5 so not that bad to start. But I watched my temps, HR, BP every hour the first few weeks so I knew when to raise. My BP and HR would go up when I either needed to raise or needed a dose. But I think it is the opposite for most folks though not sure. I'm not sure how the SR works. I switched from 2 1/2 grains of NP Thyroid to T3 doses of 12.5mcg at 4am, 6.25 at noon, and 6.25 at 4pm. There is approx 9 mcg of T3 in a grain of NDT so I converted and started there. Needed the T4 to convert all that was left over from the NDT (either to FT3 or rT3) so didn't want to go hyper. Then jsut upped the dose based on my symptoms and vitals. If I was getting fatigued, draggy or darn right tired during the day I checked my vitals and could tell I needed more. It's easy but time consuming.

But since you are on SR things may vary for you. Have you researched to see if SR is good for clearing rT3?

Back on the MDA monster thread I thought when Jack posted the blip about t3 and the thyroid he talked about that. I have no idea how to even find that blip. It was a good one and I should have printed it out or something. I think that will be my homework for tonight.

# Thread: I miss my green tea with coco milk....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-09-18T21:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52485>

Has anyone found a replacement for all those sat fat creamers yet? I need something to go with my seafood and my green tea.... don't like plain green tea without some sort of creamer.

### ealachan — 2012-09-18T21:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52486>

Could you maybe just drink the green tea with coconut milk at a time when you're not eating seafood? I've been wondering lately - Dr. K said not to eat sat fats WITH seafood, but do we actually know how much "clearance" time is needed before / after the seafood until we can have sat fats without it effecting DHA absorbtion?

Wow, that is the most awkwardly-worded post in the history of the internet...sorry...LOL

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-18T21:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52491>

Well since we're not supposed to snack I'm assuming it's just per meal for "clearance" time.

I mostly eat seafood. I also remember Dr K commenting that for people with EDs sat fat is needed as well. But then he also said you should eat seafood all day every day. I can't remember why I need sat fat but don't want to ask that question until I've tried to figure it out on my own and my aching brain is at capacity for now.

I really really like to wake up and have a cup of green tea with coco milk. I just really like it and my body appreciates it to kind of get my tummy going for BAB. If I don't do this I'm likely to get sick from BAB. I only use a couple teaspoons in my huge mug. Though I would like to use more. I also like to have my seafood in the morning (and usually again at night). I guess I'm complicated. A morning without green tea with coco milk is just.... kinda sad.

### ealachan — 2012-09-18T21:55:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52493>

Nope, I totally get it. I started eating my fish at lunch because I'd rather eat an extra meal (I've been on BAB and dinner for months now) than give up my coffee w/ CO and butter. It's not even that I need the caffiene. It's more the comfort thing, that it tastes good, and like you said, it kind of wakes your stomach up.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-18T22:21:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52505>

I've started eating lunch again b/c I don't sleep 12-14 hours a day anymore.... lol. But BAB is still a major meal that I'd like to keep seafoodish. I guess a couple teaspoons of coconut milk won't ruin it all..... though that means my second cup has to be plain.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-18T22:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52511>

I'm not perfect. so somedays I have seafood for BAB and BPC. while not optimal - its a hell of a lot better than the way I used to eat. right now I shoot for having BAB being something other than seafood - unless I'm only going to get one meal in w/seafood.

I do however try 98% of the time to keep the SAT Fat away from the seafood. I'm usually pretty successful...but not always.

today I'm only getting one seafood meal in... that's b/c tonight's dinner is leftover jambalaya (said in Newman's voice after leaving the soup nazi - FWIW the kids now say it that way too!)

I have found that there are days I just need more fat - and the easiest way - HWC and Coconut milk and BPWhey smoothie (I've discovered when I have that at some point during the previous day my FBG the next morning is closer to the lower 90s)

### ealachan — 2012-09-18T22:51:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52516>

Jam-bah-laaaaaaaaaayyyyyaaaaahhhh!!

::giggles::

### kathylu — 2012-09-18T23:09:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52523>

> **Souldanzer;53109 wrote:** Has anyone found a replacement for all those sat fat creamers yet?

Ok, call me crazy, but what about lemonnaise? Use extra light olive oil or macadamia nut oil and an egg to make a basic mayo...leave out the salt, and mustard (or other spices), but use lots of lemon. It sounds like it might be good in tea, lemony and creamy. You could make a bulletproof tea too...in a blender for more froth.

For coffee, I'd leave out the lemon.

I'll try it tomorrow morning and let you know!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T01:07:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52553>

It sounds like it might be good in tea, lemony and creamy. You could make a bulletproof tea too...in a blender for more froth.
>
>
>
> For coffee, I'd leave out the lemon.
>
>
>
> I'll try it tomorrow morning and let you know!

I've tried the macadamia nut oil and wasn't too thrilled... the bottle is still sitting in my fridge. I shouldn't be having it in the first place b/c of AI protocol. No eggs for me, either

Maybe the extra light olive oil.... I'll try that  The other thing i was going to try is avocado oil. How about avocado itself since Cavemam is now blending pumpkins into her coffee??

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T01:10:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52554>

> **Shijin13;53135 wrote:** I'm not perfect. so somedays I have seafood for BAB and BPC. while not optimal - its a hell of a lot better than the way I used to eat. right now I shoot for having BAB being something other than seafood - unless I'm only going to get one meal in w/seafood.
>
>

>

>

I've been getting wicked fat cravings from time to time. I then give into chocolate and nuts which makes me feel terrible (shouldn't am on AI). I think it's b/c I naturally eat less fat with seafood and then it catches up with me suddenly....

### johnnyb — 2012-09-19T01:33:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52562>

It's best to go with the how evolution packaged it. If you are eating plenty of seafood with lunch and diner then you are probably optimizing DHA with those meals. Having the sat. fat with breakfast is only sub-optimal for getting the DHA from the seafood with breakfast. If you need the sat. fat then eating it with one meal even with seafood may make your diet on the whole more optimal even if not optimized for DHA for the one meal. I think that's how the math goes.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-20T17:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-miss-my-green-tea-with-coco-milk.3745/post-52904>

I've found out that mac nut oil actually works really well for BP green tea - I think it was the quality of my (decaf) tea that made things go bad (too acidic in taste which is really what I'm trying to get rid of by adding the fat). I buy the best quality decaf green tea I can find but it still comes in teabags and I'm a snob with my green tea

Now that I've switched back to my yummy yummy regular loose leaf organic green tea  mac nut oil works great suddenly. Once the bottle is gone I will switch to avocado oil and see how that works since I'm not exactly supposed to be eating mac nut oil on AI protocol. Or I will try the extra light olive oil since Spectracell just complained that my oleic acid is a little low....

Yum yum. My world is whole again

# Thread: New Fav Beverage treat!   Pumpkin time. . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-fav-beverage-treat-pumpkin-time.3746/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-18T21:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-fav-beverage-treat-pumpkin-time.3746/post-52490>

I roasted the seasons first pumpkins last week - and then saw a recipe for a pumpkin latte that was paleo. . and I've been experimenting.

HOLY MOLY, is this stuff good.

The basics, roast the snot out of a pumpkin by sticking the whole damn thing in the oven at 350 on a pan on aluminum foil. Stab it a few times with a chef's knife. This is for you, it's fun. Probably not necessary for the recipe.

When it turns black in spots, 2-4 hrs depending on size. . .and is completely soft everywhere, take out and let cool. It can sit out all day, no biggie.

pull it apart, scoop out seeds and then scoop out flesh. I did it with my hands. Quick and easy. Store in freezer for later use.

Take about 1/4 cup of pumpkin and heat it with your coffee additive of choice (HWC, Butter, Coconut Milk) and blend with stick blender over heat. Add pumpkin pie type spices to taste. Add a touch of cocoa. Then add coffee or espress. . .

Boooya, Take that Starbucks - you is my beeeatch! I no longer miss your fall pumpkin concoctions - I've done better!

Coffee maker is a technavorm moccamaster - it's one of the only ones that get the water to the supposed right temp. I said somewhere else, since this is an appliance we use everyday I splurged. If you want to become my client, I usually give them as closing gifts!

The grinder is a breville, I like it because it can store a couple of days worth of beans, so I don't have to bean up for every single pot. It's a burr grinder (that's important for coffee snobbbery).

ahhhh Idaho/Colorado/Utah/Wyoming how I miss you. The aspens in fall, four seasons in one day, crisp clear blue skies (the exception being Denver), Winter from October to April, the chance for Snow in June and the best part NO ALLERGIES AT ALL.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-19T17:37:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-fav-beverage-treat-pumpkin-time.3746/post-52701>

ok, here is the best recipe ever.

Thanksgiving Thank God!

1 tbs unflavored gelatin

Â½ cup pure maple syrup (adjust to taste, as some point you may need to up the gelatin the further down you go on syrup)

Â½ tsp salt

1 tbs pumpkin pie spices. . .you can go much lower on these depending on taste. All depends on what flavors you grew up with I suspect! This amount is the spicy the way I like it.

16 oz pumpkin puree

3 eggs separated

Â½ cup raw milk (or if you are insane you could use unsweetened almond milk or coconut milk)

Â½ cup raw HWC - I say raw, but I know you canâ€™t always get it. Totally worth the splurge tho!

MOST IMPORTANT PART â€“ Make your own slow roasted pumpkin puree.

1\. In saucepan combinegelatin, syrup salt, spices and milk with big whisk.

2\. Cook till it boils, remove from heat and cool.

3\. Put in fridge to set

4\. Beat egg whites till stiff. Real stiff.

5\. Fold whites into set filling

Pour into whatever, or stand in front of bowl and eat and moan. Chill till serving.

CRUST FOR PIE AND WHATNOT

We make a nut crust and make tarts. Iâ€™ve made little nut cookies and piped this onto them for partays. My favorite nutty crust below â€“ when you combine the two, itâ€™s the damned best pumkin pie EVER. I gave these out last Tgiving and I have people wanting orders.

150 g almond flour

60 g pecans roughly chopped

Pinch o salt

65 ml oil or butter (You can use CO, all butter, a mix. . I like half CO and half Macadamia)

2 tbs honey (optional depending on what crust is used for

1 tsp vanilla

1\. Mix wet ingredients in small bowl

2\. Mix dry ingredients in big bowl

3\. Mix wet into dry

4\. Use fingers to press into whatever you want. Bake for 12-14 mins at 350 until golden. Remove and squish down any puffy parts and move it around as needed. When it cools it hardens. Cool before filling.

> **Shijin13;53299 wrote:** ahhhh Idaho/Colorado/Utah/Wyoming how I miss you. The aspens in fall, four seasons in one day, crisp clear blue skies (the exception being Denver), Winter from October to April, the chance for Snow in June and the best part NO ALLERGIES AT ALL.
>

>
> If I could figure out how to do my job from one of these states I'd move in a heart beat... haven't figured that out yet.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T19:56:26+0300

Every now and then I kick around the idea of just getting into the fed system so I could go where ever I wanted - but the problem is my benefits pkg, kicks serious but - and there's no Job in this region that would match everything I've got going now except executive level - which I don't want that responsibility I want to leave work NLT 4-430. heck there's a hiring freeze here in DC for all FED jobs as it is. ppl are retiring and they're not replacing them

> 3 eggs separated
>
> Â½ cup raw milk (or if you are insane you could use unsweetened almond milk or coconut milk)
>
> Â½ cup raw HWC - I say raw, but I know you can’t always get it. Totally worth the splurge tho!
>
> MOST IMPORTANT PART – Make your own slow roasted pumpkin puree.
>
> 1\. In saucepan combinegelatin, syrup salt, spices and milk with big whisk.
>
> 2\. Cook till it boils, remove from heat and cool.
>
> 3\. Put in fridge to set
>
> 4\. Beat egg whites till stiff. Real stiff.
>
> 5\. Fold whites into set filling
>
> Pour into whatever, or stand in front of bowl and eat and moan. Chill till serving.
>
>
>
> CRUST FOR PIE AND WHATNOT
>
> We make a nut crust and make tarts. I’ve made little nut cookies and piped this onto them for partays. My favorite nutty crust below – when you combine the two, it’s the damned best pumkin pie EVER. I gave these out last Tgiving and I have people wanting orders.
>
> 150 g almond flour
>
> 60 g pecans roughly chopped
>
> Pinch o salt
>
> 65 ml oil or butter (You can use CO, all butter, a mix. . I like half CO and half Macadamia)
>
> 2 tbs honey (optional depending on what crust is used for
>
> 1 tsp vanilla
>
>
>
> 1\. Mix wet ingredients in small bowl
>
> 2\. Mix dry ingredients in big bowl
>
> 3\. Mix wet into dry
>
> 4\. Use fingers to press into whatever you want. Bake for 12-14 mins at 350 until golden. Remove and squish down any puffy parts and move it around as needed. When it cools it hardens. Cool before filling.

Thanks, Cavemam! I'll add ground cloves, because pumpkin pie spice tends to forget this expensive ingredient.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-14T21:44:49+0200

> **Shijin13;53299 wrote:** ahhhh Idaho/Colorado/Utah/Wyoming how I miss you. The aspens in fall, four seasons in one day, crisp clear blue skies (the exception being Denver), Winter from October to April, the chance for Snow in June and the best part NO ALLERGIES AT ALL.

And as to allergies ... you don't want to be here when there are fires raging all over the West. last summer was the worst one I have ever EVER experience as far as allergies go.

but other than that ... yeah. Oh yeah, you left out Montana. they don't call it the Big Sky state for nuthin'.

Now excuse me while I go dig through the freezer for that cooked pumpkin I froze up last winter.

MT is nice to visit but not too sure I want to live out there. I swear Cody WY is a bigger city that Butte

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-15T00:01:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-fav-beverage-treat-pumpkin-time.3746/post-64344>

> **Shijin13;66335 wrote:** I25 was that way when I was at USAFA back in the 90's. only difference now it that there are more ppl to the east of it.
>
>
> "more people" is a true understatment, LOL. My home town wasn't much bigger than Sheridan is now (about 16,000 pop.) when I was a kid. It started growing shortly after Colorado A&M became Colorado State in the late 50's and it has not slowed down since. At one time, late 60's early 70's, Fort Collins had the distinction of being the fastest growing city in the US. It saddens me no end. Luckily, Wyoming hasn't quite yet been outed.
>
>
>
> MT is nice to visit but not too sure I want to live out there. I swear Cody WY is a bigger city that Butte
>
>
>
> I think I could dig SLC. I've always loved it. Yeah fires suck. We get them back here and it makes regular allergy season hell. I'm really looking for easy access to jobs & snow & close access to ski/snowboard resorts. If both DH & I could jobs in Sun Valley ID ID move back in a heartbeat!!

Actually Butte is a really cool place (larger than cody by far) and has a 5 star restaurant downtown that we found years ago by accident. We have been known to drive to Butte (about 5-6 hours) just to have dinner there. It's rich with Western history and of course, there's that monumental lake of poison left over from the Copper Kings.

SLC? Wayyy to big and way to mormon for my tastes (no offense intended to any Mormons on the forum). But there are some stunningly beautiful little towns surrounding it. Plus there's the Lake and the skiing and Zion National Park.

Love Sun Valley but too rich for my blood. Idaho has some really interesting landscapes -- specifically thinking of Craters of the Moon -- WOW! I have always wanted to go back there and camp for a few days so we could go hiking and explore all those lava tubes. Also all those waterfalls in Eastern Idaho are just too cool. We spent many days driving back and forth over the years to visit DH's Mom who was living with his sister in Meridian. We took about every back road you can take going and coming. Salmon, ID is a pretty place but really isolated.

I also like parts of Eastern Washington and Oregon. Actually I just love the West. The vast tracts of desert to high plains desert rolling right up to the Rockies ... it has a wild, raw beauty like nothing else.

I like a big city feel in the mountains. SLC isn't as big or crazy as Denver -and I do love Denver The plus about SLC is its a shorter drive to the slopes & I could probably swing a weekend job teaching lessons!! Paid time on the mountain!!!

ID is where I feel at home. It's my connection back to everything that I am and everything that I ever will be. I would live in Boise I'd if I could talk DH into it. But I doubt that will ever happen. My dad is from payette and his dad moved there after college & WW2 from burley. I do love Stanley but that would require an investment in a pilots license & a plane. Not interested.

Your last two sentences struck a cord w/me. You described exactly why I love & miss the mountain west. I know optimal wont fully fall into place until I'm back on native soil.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-15T03:11:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-fav-beverage-treat-pumpkin-time.3746/post-64387>

Idaho is really nice. We considered retiring there for a while. Thought about Montana as well. We both like Belfry (home of the Bats ... really!) since it's out in the country but close enough to Billings. Arizona I thought about for 2 minutes. Too many people! See I'm a small town girl at heart. I don't like big crowds. I can tolerate 'em for a bit but to live in that? No way. No how. 2 years ago I had decided I really wanted to move back home. DH even agreed. Then the real estate market took a nose dive and here we're going to stay. But it's OK. I made peace with it especially after my last trip back down to Loveland. (insert a choice few 4 letter words) the TRAFFIC!!! Bumper to bumper from Wellington on South. BOTH directions! It was like LA in the early 60's. Nope. I'll stay in Wyoming thank you very much.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-52540>

Ok... instead of hijacking Nonchalants thread : [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4033-Jack-is-ruining-my-reading-enjoyment](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4033-Jack-is-ruining-my-reading-enjoyment)

What are everyone's favorite books/generas etc....

1) Atlas Shrugged - Ayn Rand

2) The fountainhead - Ayn Rand

3) The Razor's Edge - Sommerset Maugham's

4) Stranger in a Strange Land - Heinline

5) Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy - Douglas Adams

6) Any Short story by Philip K. Dick

7) Dune -Herbert

8) Hobbit/LOTR trillogy - JRR Tolkin

9) Starship Troopers - heinline

10) 1984 - Orwell

11) The Handmaidens Tale - attwood

12) Call of the Wild - Jack London

13) Time Machine -HG Wells

14) War of the Worlds - HG Wells

15) A wrinkle in time - Madeline Engle

16) Tess of the D'bervilles

17) Le Miserables

18) The Great Gatsby

19) the Heart of darkness - Conrad

20) On the Road

21) Zen and the Art of Motorcycle Maintenance

22) Way of the peaceful warrior

23) Old Man and the Sea - Hemmingway (this is the ONLY HEMMINGWAY Book I like)

24) To Kill a Mockingbird - Harper Lee

25) The Power of One - Courtaney

as I said before I love me my Action/Suspense Romance Novels: bullets, guns, bad guys, good guys, kick ass women and good sex scenes: So basically anything by Cindy Gerard, Lori Foster, Lisa Jackson, Laura Griffin, Maya Banks, Pamela Clare, JL Saint, Kat Martin, Dana Marton, Linda Howard, Sandra Brown, Roxanne St. Claire, Collen Coble, Stephanie Tyler, Johanna Lindsey, Jacquelin Carey, Cherry Adair, Lora Leigh

Speaking of throw up in your mouth a little, cognitive dissonance, and irony. . . I spent 20 yrs in marketing and advertising - BROADWAY SHOWS. Les Mis haunted me my whole damn career (along with cats). The irony part is aside from 2 shows, I absolutely hate broadway musicals. This one tops the list, I think the happiest day of my life was when I quit and realized I did not have to sit through the frigging singing french revolution one more time (I probably saw it 30 times).

### Michael — 2012-09-19T09:29:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-52648>

I quite enjoy novels about journeys. On one level the external journey may also be about an internal journey - and about homecoming. But they're enjoyable to me simply on the level of space and ease and elbow-room, and who knows what might come along? ... and there's room for humour along the way.

The classic one there would the _Odyssey, of course. I like the Robert Fitzgerald translation. My copy has what must be a quotation from a review:

> "A strong and salty flavour of its own. And it makes you see things" --C.S. Lewis.

_Kudos to Fitzgerald. I think it does.

In the same vein, I found _Tom Jones hugely enjoyable and Sir Walter Scott's _Waverley_.

I also like historical novels. Having mentioned Walter Scott - he was the pioneer here, of course, and stuff like Kenilworth_ and The Heart of Midlothian_ and The Fair Maid of Perth_ pleases me. I'd like to hear some of his Scottish novels read by a really good Scottish actor who'd be able to do all the different types of Scottish accent in them.

I do also enjoy some contemporary historical novelists, though. As a kid I read a lot of Henry Treece and Rosemary Sutcliffe. Now I'd enjoy anything by Bernard Cornwall. Those Sharpe books are good knockabout stuff, although some of his other series seem to me to be better crafted. [His series on the American Civil war](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Starbuck-Chronicles-1-Rebel/dp/0006179207/) has a very strong period feel as well as some historically and psychologically convincing characters.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-20T20:39:45+0300

Loved chapter one. Chapter two not so much. Three was good, and four was stupendous. Today I get chapter five. We're still in port, but soon we'll go to sea!

### maggie — 2012-09-21T05:01:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-53112>

Great thread. I'm really going to comb through to find good reads. Here are some of mine:

I'm addicted to Ann Perry's Victorian novels and I'm a bit worried that she isn't writing them fast enough since I think I've read almost everything now. Also like all of Alan Furst's novels--he writes about WWII in Europe, wonderful historical detail and atmosphere and stories I always find very moving. Neal Stephenson's _Cryptonomican also about WWII. J.G. Farrell, _Singapore Grip, about the Japanese takeover of Singapore during WWII, awesome portrayal of Singapore society at the end of the British colonial period. Robert Graves, _I, Claudius

Peter Hoeg _Smilla's Sense of Snow--I've read it numerous times and bought the DVD, don't know why it gets to me so. Maybe we're all innately attracted to the Far North-- Stef Penny wrote _The Tenderness of Wolves while she was suffering from agaraphobia and couldn't leave her house, much less fly from Scotland to Canada, where the book is set. She wrote it all from research and imagination. I devoured Jane Smiley's _The Greenlanders (this one's fabulous, I think), also loved David Poyer As the Wolf Loves the Winter_.

Some Sci Fi: William Gibson Pattern Recognition_. (OK, that one's not really sci fi, but it reads like it is.) Margaret Atwood, Oryx and Crake_ and her sequel (I think it's called After the Flood_), and A Handmaid's Tale_; Greg Bear Darwin's Radio_(it's about Factor X)

### Michael — 2012-09-21T15:21:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-53148>

> **Maggie;53747 wrote:** Robert Graves, _I, Claudius_

A memorable televisation, too. I can still almost hear Derek Jacobi going, "Clau-, Clau-, Clau-, Claudius." Brian Blessed as Augustus, Sian Phillips as Livia, and John Hurt as Caligula ...

Derek Jacobi made a good Cadfael, too.

The clumsy young monk who assisted him in compounding for the infirmary used to amuse me. He'd drop and break things or fall asleep while supposedly watching the medicines simmer. I liked the one where he was escorting a young woman around the market and this old woman selling simples on a stall looked at him and the young lady and said with a leer, "Try this young man: it'll keep you up all night."

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-22T02:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-53336>

OK... here's a old childhood favorite that I can't help deconstructing from an epi-paleo perspective...(couldn't help throw in a little lit.crit lingo, but I'm over it now)

Have any of you worried about the Ingall's family in The Long Winter? There they were during the winter of constant blizzards without proper supplies and for months they only had WHEAT to eat. The wheat saved the town from starvation, but at what cost? Actually I think this is a case where some of the old ways kept them pretty optimal... they had very little light and heat and Laura Ingalls Wilder wrote about the cold and dark in great detail. Also the wheat wouldn't have been modernized at that point so perhaps less inflammatory over all. Still, just the thought of only living on bread alone makes me quiver...I'd be a goner in a week...and there lies some of the appeal of these books....those generations of pioneers were made of pretty stern stuff.

Back to regular programming..

### Michael — 2012-09-22T11:47:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-books.3747/post-53375>

Yeah, that's interesting. A story like that would make you doubt the notion that everybody needs to eat like the Kitavans, if you hadn't already. If it's dark enough when it should be - and perhaps cold enough at night, too - maybe the food doesn't matter so much. I already pointed out that there's no electricity on Kitava.

There are so many factors in play. Maybe the Kitavans are (relatively) healthy _despite_ their diet. Not but what I think one could do a lot worse in terms of diet, but that's hardly likely to be the only factor in play. And that's before we even ask if their diet might be more appropriate for their latitude and perhaps their genetics.

Here's another thought: has anyone inquired into the level of antibiotic use on the island?

The notion that one can take hold of a single thread in the loom of life, pull it out of the web, and make incontrovertible conclusions by looking at it!

I heard there's a doctor who says that we need to begin by by looking at the human brain, investigating how it came to be how it is, and what noting what its requirements are. Seems like a good place to start to me.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Cod Liver Pate Recipe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-pate-recipe.3749/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T00:38:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-pate-recipe.3749/post-52544>

I finally played around and came up with this recipe for cod liver pate. I could have done so many different things and may change this up a little as time goes on but this one is pretty good and doesn't have the fishy liver taste as much.

1 can cod livers (approx 7 oz)

1 shallot, chopped finely

1 lemon, juice and zest

2-3 Tbsp fresh grated horseradish

salt to taste

Mash the cod livers with a fork until fairly smooth. Add the next three ingredients and mix thoroughly. Add the salt to taste. Serve with celery sticks or nut crackers.

Enjoy!

You can also add pepper but DH has a throat swelling problem with pepper. :-(

# Thread: Pool is cooling down...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-19T03:03:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52580>

Living in Michigan our pool hasn't gotten but just a couple months of use...until 2012, when I started reading Dr. Kruse's blog! We used our pool quite a bit this spring for CT. We fell off the CT bandwagon this summer. I did the occasional spot CT, face dunk, and cool shower but nothing too hardcore. We are trying to get back with the program. I've been wearing my cool fat burner vest every day and we are getting in the pool as much as we can. I did 30 minutes at 66 degrees today! Fall has arrived in Michigan and the cool air and pool is invigorating!

### Birdy — 2012-09-19T03:48:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52591>

jealous!...........................

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-20T18:08:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52912>

What I've always been curious of is what temperature does it have to get to see the beginning of beneficial effects? Considering the range of beneficial effects some start sooner while others later.

My pool is low 70s at night for now. In the day its closer to 80. Is there any benefit to 80 or low 70s? Or do I need to get into 60s before its really useful?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-20T18:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52922>

anything below 98.6 does something. Obviously, the colder the better as long as you do it safely.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-20T19:07:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52935>

My house is normally 75-78 so even that has a benefit? I would like to cool it down more but my air conditioner sucks...or at least it seems to. Can't seem to get it to go lower...but its getting colder. Too bad I don't think my family will allow me to keep the heater off.

### kathylu — 2012-09-20T19:55:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52950>

Can't remember where I read this, but skin temps have to be below 65 degrees F to stimulate the conversion of WAT into BAT. Below 65 is also where you start to see the synthesis of uncoupling proteins...

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-20T20:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52956>

Good to know. My question was more about benefits in general involving colder temps not just the WATConversion though it is one of the things I wanted to know. Thanks

Beneficial effects being

Extra calorie burn, WATBAT conversion, Immune system improvement etc...etc...

If someone has a better list of beneficial effects feel free. I know I'm probably missing quite a few and I'm guessing the temperature at which you get those benefits varies. Some may even require going lower than 50-55, but which wouldn't be safe until your cold adapted and would need greater caution to use.

Also if anyone has read anything in relation to what temperature those benefits start feel free to include them in the listing.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-20T20:48:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-52968>

Yes, with water anything below body temps is good. I'd definitely take advantage of anything under 80F. The longer in the water the better.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-21T23:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-53303>

In my understanding and in my personal experience, you don't get the cold thermogenesis that Jack's talking about--not just activating BAT, but switching over to a winter-mode metabolism--until the skin reaches a sustained 50-55F while the core remains stable. Anything above that and you may get the uncoupling and BAT effects, but not the switchover to the leptin-melanocortin metabolic pathway.

That's just my reading of the CT blogs, however. I wasn't able to make it for the webinars. Someone else who did should chime in.

Personally I find that I only get the skin-on-fire/turn-lobster-red effect when the water is in the mid-to-high 40s so that my skin can get down to a sustained 48-54. Anything \*warmer\* and I don't get that reaction at all; rather, I turn pale white and get severely fatigued (hypothermia) and have a ravenous appetite for days afterward that totally screws up my eating cycle.

### Inger — 2012-09-22T08:56:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-53371>

> **Raiken3712;53570 wrote:** My house is normally 75-78 so even that has a benefit? I would like to cool it down more but my air conditioner sucks...or at least it seems to. Can't seem to get it to go lower...but its getting colder. Too bad I don't think my family will allow me to keep the heater off.

Oh.. that is warm! I keep my appartement at 60 degree F, sometimes lower sometimes a little higher depending on the temperature outside.

The river is now 48 degree.. I swim about 100 meter (sometimes more) in it instead of showering every day. Sometimes I stay in for 10 minutes. I cannot wait for the winter!

### MonteD — 2012-09-22T19:30:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-is-cooling-down.3752/post-53429>

Water and air have different conducting properties. 80f air is nothing like 80f water.

> The generic number I came up with about three years ago when working with Tim on The 4 Hour body is that the energy to melt, and raise to body temperature, 66 lbs of ice = 1 lb of fat (3500 Kcals).
>
>
>
> later I did a one dimensional solution to Founier’s law and then a more sophisticated model using mathematics that took into account skin thickness, adipose tissue, and muscle.
>
>
>
> **It turns out that sitting in 27C water (80F), which is where mild cold stress begins, burns about 2.4x RMR or for the average 100 watt human, or about 240 watts.** This was compared to actual measurements of metabolism that range about 230-270W, so I considered it solved. (btw, using watts here as that takes into account time so that 1 watt is .86 kcal/hour ). --Ray Cronise

Enjoy the cool Michigan temps!

# Thread: Sub-optimal fitness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sub-optimal-fitness.3754/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### enyaw — 2012-09-19T03:55:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sub-optimal-fitness.3754/post-52593>

I know, this thread doesn't belong in the Optimal Fitness forum. But I'd like to get some insight from some other forum members. I've noticed there's a wealth of knowledge on these forums.

I've spent some time in the Marine Corps infantry. That is a sub-optimal life. I know this. Running 3 - 5 miles, three to five times a week. Running operations, or even training, through the night. Eating a SAD. I guess we can add sub-optimal lifestyle to the list of sacrifices that our men and women in uniform make for us.

I've since left the Marine Corps and am now in the Michigan Air National Guard. What a difference! When I'm at home, I do what I can to strive for optimal. However, I just got back from a three-month TDY, I have a six-week TDY coming up, and another three-month TDY next summer. This upcoming six-week TDY is very regimented. I will be force to eat whatever is served in the chow hall. These options are always less than optimal. I remember one day, I had to choose between breaded chicken parmesan, chili mac, or spaghetti. I could've had a big salad loaded with (most likely) soy based salad dressing. Fortunately, I had the option of going to my barracks room and enjoying a can of kippered herring filets and a salad with EVOO and red wine vinegar. I won't have that choice this time.

On my upcoming TDY I will be required to run 2-3 miles about 2-3 times a week. Far less than in the Marines, but still not optimal. Does anyone know how bad that amount of running is? Does it significantly raise cortisol levels? Should I supplement with melatonin on nights after a run?

Optimal isn't a choice; at least for six-weeks. I will be able to take vitamins, so I'm thinking about supplements. I should be able to take cold showers as well (stall showers, so no baths). Hopefully, I will have control over the A/C in my room and roommate that likes the cold.

Does anyone have any suggestions on how to minimize the negative affects of this lifestyle?

### Michael — 2012-09-19T15:58:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sub-optimal-fitness.3754/post-52675>

Well, with your background you'd know more amount fitness than me. And I only looked at this board, because having made an earlier post I was interested to see what people had thought about that.

But, FWIW, looked at from the point of view of anthopology I'd have thought you're not necessarily way out of what would be normal for a young fit man. I think there's increasing evidence that long runs repeated frequently are quite physiologically harmful, but I'd have thought you were way beneath that threshold in both duration and frequency. People certainly had to run sometimes when following game. They seem also to have run when on journeys. And if warriors carried out raids on another tribe, they didn't walk away afterwards. All that seems to be amply attested.

The guy at Harvard - Liebermann is it? - has this theory that "persistence hunting" was the main form of hunting in paleolithic times, and that the whole tribe, men, women, children, and old people, all did that all the time. Well, I don't know of a single reference to modern-day hunter-gatherers ever having been seen to do that, and I don't think he's ever offered one. I think the young men did it and probably not that often. But this is where the _Born to Run_ book was coming from, and the unfortunate comment on that was the death of the mysterious "White Horse", who at autopsy was found to have massive heart damage.

Liebermann even wants to explain our upright stance by saying that it evolved for running. Cunnane quotes an physical anthroplogist who says, "why would a four legged animal stand up in order to go faster?" and leaves it at that.

I think the notion of man as "the creature who runs" is dead. Sure, they (or the young men) did sometimes run game down, which some people in paleo, Mark Sisson, for example, seem reluctant to admit. What I guess they didn't do was do that every day. However, man has certainly been a creature who runs when it suits him. I don't think anyone should go out and do 10 or 15 miles every day. But I doubt a medium distance 2 or 3 times a week is way out of the evolutionary norm. I think some people in paleo are averse to anything other than weights, sprinting, or walking, because they feel anything else tends to get in the way of "getting big" - lift and rest in the days between. Might be true, but if that's not your aim, and if what you want to do requires different ...

### AKMan — 2012-09-21T20:11:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sub-optimal-fitness.3754/post-53235>

Hey, MooseMan - I think you are over-thinking things a bit. I spent 21 years active duty Air Force. Retired at 39, obese, hypothyroid, gout, high cholesterol, bad liver. Within 2 years of retiring, I had gained 50 lbs and was taking a handful of pills a day. Once I decided to get my life in order, I came across paleo, then Dr. Kruse.

During my 21 years in the AF, I ate whatever I wanted, drank as much as I could, and exercised only when told to. I traveled all over the world, and worked all kinds of crazy shifts. I had sleep apnea, and couldn't tell anyone for fear of getting kicked out. I suffered the poorest sleep you can imagine for years.

Long story short, I'm now about as healthy as one can be. If I could give you some advice:

1\. Learn to eat right. This may mean eating some bad stuff while out in the field, but you can make the best choices from what's available. Learn to fast, so you can get through the worst of it--Look to Barry on this forum for help!

2\. Get strong. Focus exercise on core and upper body. Learn to squat heavy. Only run when you have to. Sprint a couple times a week. This will keep you in shape for those 2-3 mile runs without overdoing the cardio.

3\. Don't drink or smoke. 'Nuff said.

4\. Sleep ya big dummy.

5\. Read the Quilt and let it sink in.

# Thread: Sherpa's Love Bacon!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpas-love-bacon.3756/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-19T04:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpas-love-bacon.3756/post-52607>

Leave it to Jack to shed light on the fact that nitrate-soaked bacon can turn us into Sherpa rockstars!

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1089860312000730](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1089860312000730)

Residents of the Tibetan plateau appear to produce more nitric oxide (NO) compared to lowlanders as indicated by enhanced NO concentrations in exhaled breath and elevated levels of NO metabolites (incl. nitrite, nitrate and nitroso species) in their blood. This adaptation may help offset the consequences of life-long exposure to reduced atmospheric oxygen at high altitude by improving oxygen delivery and/or utilization and thus hypoxic tolerance. Recently, lowland participants of two independent research expeditions to Nepal have been demonstrated to also respond to environmental hypoxia with enhanced NO formation, suggesting this to be an integral part of human physiology that contributes to the process of acclimatization to reduced oxygen availability. Furthermore, dietarynitrate has been demonstrated to reduce the oxygen cost of exercise by improving mitochondrial efficiency at sea level and suggested to be sequentially reduced to nitrite and NO in hypoxia. This pathway could theoretically complement NOS-mediated l-arginine oxidation by providing an alternative source of NO under conditions of reduced oxygen availability. We hypothesized, therefore, that dietarynitrate administration may improve oxygen efficiency at high altitude, and this rationale has been tested in a randomized, double-blind field study carried out during summer 2010 at the Margherita Hut at 4,559 m (Xtreme Alps). This presentation will highlight some of the key results of this study and discuss their potential implications for the treatment of patients suffering from hypoxemia during critical illness.

# Thread: Intelligent opinions and wild guesses, please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-19T05:12:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/post-52628>

In March 2012...

my T4 was 15 (10-24)

my T3 was 3.6 (3.5-6)

my TSH was 1.51 (.40-4.00)

my hsCRP was 1.2

my D3 was 162 (50-15)

and

my DHEA S was 4.3 (0.5-12.0)

NOW

my T4 is 14 (10-24)

my T3 is 3.0 (3.5-6.0)

my TSH is 4.7 (.40-4.0)

my hs CRP is 0.80

my D3 was 162

and

my DHEA S is 2.9 (0.5-12)

so... T3 is falling, TSH is rising, DHEA is falling, but so is inflammation.

I have not been on any thyroid meds and have been taking 7-Keto since March.

I'm curious as to WHY this pattern is emerging. Any takers?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T05:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/post-52632>

you went VLC but didn't have enough iodine so your T3 decreased and with that your LDL to pregnenolone conversion which then affected your downward steroid hormone cascade including DHEA. TSH went up b/c your brain is trying to upregulate thyroid function despite low iodine.

Inflammation went down b/c of cold/VLC/epi-paleo and you might want to try regular DHEA instead of keto version

Wild guess here  I honestly have no idea.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-19T05:26:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/post-52633>

DHEA can be affected by stress a lot..... so it could just be you were more stressed right before the second blood draw

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-19T17:48:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/post-52707>

No reverse T3, Lauren?

Soul's theory is clanging around in my formerly foggy brain...things that make you go hmmmmmm. I used to eat a lot of yogurt, milk, cheese, eggs and bread in my pre-paleo epoch...all good sources of iodine, but just started eating a significant amount of seafood. Also switched from common iodized table salt to non-iodized. I am, almost certainly, iodine deficient. Interestingly, dough conditioners have a lot of iodine.

T4 is midrange, but T3 is low...conversion issue? According to the WHFoods website, zinc, iron, and selenium are important in the conversion. Bet I am low in all of these.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T01:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intelligent-opinions-and-wild-guesses-please.3757/post-52811>

Thanks, everyone. And I second the 'wow' from your response, Soul. Makes total sense and explains the apparently contradictory trends of improvement and worsening.

WBO, I can't get a RT3, though am awaiting my ASI results. I know I've been iodine deficient, too, and probably still am. My Se is top of the range, but my Zn was low when I last tested and my Fe good. I know I've had a hypothyroid problem for years (I have had all the symptoms much worse than I do now even). Now to find a doc to give me some thyroid support in addition to the rest of what I'm doing. (Btw, I think dough conditioners used to have iodine... they are now made with bromide as far as I know, which would further deplete iodine in the body.)

### Michael — 2012-09-19T07:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52645>

This is an interesting post that's just gone up at Mark's Daily Apple.

It's an Australian who's complaining:

> I'll get cramps every now and then, or pain in my calves (sometimes at night - I also seem to be waking up a few times throughout the night, the worst time was about 12 times in a 10 hour period, but is currently averaging about 5 times).

He seems to be doing everything that someone like Mike Eades would advise on a low-carb diet: water, seasalt, magnesium, potassium. Yet he's still waking in pain.

Interesting that he gives a food-log. And, yes, there's no seafood in it at all - although, AFAIK, really good quality diverse seafood is easily available in Oz:

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread66220.html)

### Michael — 2012-09-19T19:05:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52729>

No comments from anyone on this one? I'd have liked to help the poor guy (except I lost at least 2 MDA logins - and besides I still don't fully understand). Can anyone here post a response for him? I don't know what people at MDA are going to say: either nothing or "safe starches" I guess. There must be a better answer.

I have a vague memory that DHA is what's important here. But I don't really understand why.

I've had cramps in my calves, and they woke me once or twice, and they did hurt, but up to ten times a night ... I feel both paleo and low-carb are letting this bloke down. But the Australian Aborigines who lived in that area pre-contact could probably have put him right immediately.

Cramping came up here a few months back, seems like some started cramping when adding in CT. I also notice if I drop my magnesium for too many days in a row it tends to hit.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-19T23:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52776>

Mike Of course there is no seafood in it.......MDA is 100% paleo........they don't talk about seafood much.

ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14527626 This was written before Mark had a blog or a book.

### Glamazon — 2012-09-20T04:52:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52852>

I take mag maleate before I go to bed. And I also spray magnesium oil around my calves and across the top of my feet. If I neglect to do so BAAAAAD, crippling cramps return during the night. Then I have to take some mag maleate and spray and it takes about 20 minutes before the cramps calm down.

### Michael — 2012-09-20T08:26:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-and-cramping.3759/post-52868>

> **Glamazon;53483 wrote:** I take mag maleate before I go to bed. And I also spray magnesium oil around my calves and across the top of my feet. If I neglect to do so BAAAAAD, crippling cramps return during the night. Then I have to take some mag maleate and spray and it takes about 20 minutes before the cramps calm down.

Thanks. I have got some "magnesium oil" spray, so I'll try that direct on the calves and feet if it returns for me.

And this seems to be what others in the thread are saying they find, too - Mg.

I do take magnesium malate pills regularly. Maybe that's why I don't currently have problems, though I don't recall if I'd run out or something when I did get it. I'm pretty sure it _was when I'd decided to reduce carbs a bit more than usual.

If the cramping is mainly down to low magnesium (when carbs are pretty low) then it's still a pretty interesting phenomenon. It seems to be a very_ common problem for people doing "low carb paleo" or low carb in general. When someone like Dr. Eades says "take some magnesium" it's good advice, but it kind of begs the question: "Where was the magnesium coming from in hunter-gatherer diets?"

From seafood?

If it's simply that depletion of magnesium from the soil has become so bad, because of intensive farming methods, acid rain, and so on, then by golly a low-carb diet throws the problem of soil mineral-depletion into sharp and painful relief.
