# Thread: Circadian stuff....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chrystin.comeau@live.com — 2012-09-19T17:13:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-52695>

Posted this in my journal, but wanted to throw it out here for opinions/impressions/discussion

"Something else I've been delving into is the major move we made December 31st 2007. We moved from lat 34 degrees to 43 degrees (SC to NH). In the middle of winter. I was pregnant. From the charts I'm looking at, it looks like about an hour of light difference at that time of year. Wondering aloud about what impact that may have had..."

also, a story...

"My husband told me a really interesting story this weekend. Something that he didn't attach any significance to until now, after listening to My Call With Jack (like My Dinner with Andre, but less boring LOL). I capitalize it because I consider it a pivotal event in my life. So, my husband was a skinny guy after boot camp. Seriously. Skinny. Then he entered the submarine service. Gained 40 lbs. No changes in diet or exercise (non existent b4 and after). THE LIGHT! He also shared the story of a nurse that he recently hired. She had been working nights at her previous job and complained that she got fat while working that job. Now, she's back to regular shifts. Lost the weight."

### TheKid — 2012-10-01T22:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55654>

Interesting that you mention latitude. I just "Asked Jack" about that with reference to eating seasonal carbs. Does the latitude matter? Do fruits from the same latitudes have similar carb content? Do we even really care about the season, or is it more about just monitoring total carbs? I which case it wouldn't matter eating out of season fruits, so long as you didn't exceed the carb limit for that season.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T00:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55691>

I think it's about eating them out of season not necessarily no carbs. Context of your health environment. I can't eat a lot of carbs yet even in summer but that's ok my carbs right now even w/an occasional apple or dark chocolate is below 25g

The reason you don't want to eat blueberries or bananas out of season is b/c in winter you are in a reduced oxidative state. Eating those shoot your oxidation out if the park

However not everyone does well in cold, just as others don't do we'll in heat context. What do your labs say? What are you vitD3 levels now and again in deep winter? What does your T3 & rT3 look like? What does your lipid panel look like? It all tells a story and tells you what you can/can't eat

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-02T00:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55693>

I just updated my comment to Thekid in ask Jack

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T16:06:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55805>

@Shijin13, as I mentioned in Ask Jack, I understand the problem. I'm trying to zero in on WHY so that I can manage the problem better for me and my family.

I have raging Hashis, so I need to be VLC pretty much all the time until things are better under control. But I'm thinking about my family members who are fairly healthy, albeit mismatched.

Jack mentioned that the brain is literally counting energy intake. I get that. But does the brain really distinguish between an out-of-season carb and an in-season carb? Or between and indigenous, local carb versus and imported on?

This may sound nit-picking to some, but I live in the Middle East where nearly absolutely all of the food available isn't indigenous to this region. Most everything is imported.

So whose season am I to follow? Cause there ain't too much that grows here.

It would make it easier if what we were really driving at was just numbers. That way, if my daughter wants some blueberries in winter, she can have a few, but just not too many that she would exceed the targeted carb intake for the given season.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T16:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55809>

My interpretation is that the light cycles tell your brain at what latitude you are living. Your brain programs your body/gut/cells to deal with the food availability of that season. This program and it's info are stored in our genome from evolution. So if you are in an equatorial region with little seasonal light variance your brain is programming your body for a more or less consistent supply of carbs. You would not require a change in cell membrane fluidity. If you are in a temperate zone the light tells your brain when it's going to get cold and that your carb sources will be ending. You now need increased membrane fluidity which you will get because your 06/03 ratio will be changing. It's really a beautifully orchestrated dance if we let it happen. If you eat stuff when it grows where you live and abide by circadian cycles your brain knows how to run your body. Of course if we are broken by disease our brain and body can

t put it all together.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-02T17:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55816>

I would love to hear more about this, because you simplify and consolidate things nicely for my brain!

In any given season, if my FT3 is great (and RT3 is under control), and my HDL is high, and my LDL is under control (I'm a little hazy here, because I know that pregnenelone is made from LDL, so don't we want to see a decent amount of it? I guess we do, in the context of reasonable FT3 numbers. This would show we have plenty of raw hormone building materials, but we don't want a bottleneck either), then our hormone profiles are probably humming along. I'm not sure about using D3 as a measure of this, in the context of supplementing it orally. Doesn't oral supplementation overide the hormone creation cascade, and "fill the last hormone bucket?" Like a little cheat...

So, what I expect to see in winter is a need for more iodine/seafood to keep FT3 from falling. If I don't do this, then my cholesterol (LDL) would go up, because of a lack of T3 to convert it into pregnenelone. I would see crappier hormone profiles falling out of this scenario, with reduced numbers from pregenelone, all the way down to vitamin D (again, I'm guessing D isn't the best measure given we're supplementing it orally). If I choose to eat bananas all winter long in NH, then what happens? How does the light get it's say in the hormone cascade, that produces the havoc we see in non-seasonal eating? It directs the hypothalamus, which choreographs the entire hormone cascade. This is the point where my understanding gets hazy though. I would love more clarification right here!!

Do I have this all correct?

Then likewise, in the summer, as carbs come into season, the seafood/iodine can take a backseat, and carbs can drive the FT3 levels to produce a similar outcome. How do increased carbohydrates = increased FT3?

> **Shijin13;56848 wrote:** I think it's about eating them out of season not necessarily no carbs. Context of your health environment. I can't eat a lot of carbs yet even in summer but that's ok my carbs right now even w/an occasional apple or dark chocolate is below 25g
>
>
>
> The reason you don't want to eat blueberries or bananas out of season is b/c in winter you are in a reduced oxidative state. Eating those shoot your oxidation out if the park
>
>
>
> However not everyone does well in cold, just as others don't do we'll in heat context. What do your labs say? What are you vitD3 levels now and again in deep winter? What does your T3 & rT3 look like? What does your lipid panel look like? It all tells a story and tells you what you can/can't eat

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T19:03:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55854>

Bingo! That's a nice breakdown Dali. If what you're saying is correct, then latitude is what we're after in this issue. And I would assume you could then choose from the variety of fruits that naturally grow at your latitude.

In my case, we're at about the latitude of Miami. Now I don't feel so bad about the mangoes my daughter likes so much when they come flooding in during the season from our corresponding latitude in Pakistan.

I'm wondering how far north and south of one's latitude one can go I terms of selecting fruits to eat. Any ideas on that?

Thanks.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T19:45:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55874>

If what I'm saying is correct I'm not sure if it really matters that the carbs are local. In a paleo world your carbs would have been local and there is probably other good plant stuff that may correspond to your environment. In the modern world I think it would be about carbs and lipid ratio however they are sourced.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T20:02:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55879>

> **Dali Dula;56999 wrote: My interpretation is that the light cycles tell your brain at what latitude you are living. Your brain programs your body/gut/cells to deal with the food availability of that season. This program and it's info are stored in our genome from evolution. So if you are in an equatorial region with little seasonal light variance your brain is programming your body for a more or less consistent supply of carbs. You would not require a change in cell membrane fluidity. If you are in a temperate zone the light tells your brain when it's going to get cold and that your carb sources will be ending. You now need increased membrane fluidity which you will get because your 06/03 ratio will be changing. It's really a beautifully orchestrated dance if we let it happen. If you eat stuff when it grows where you live and abide by circadian cycles your brain knows how to run your body. Of course if we are broken by disease our brain and body can
>
> t put it all together.
>
>
> TheKid;57045 wrote:** Bingo! That's a nice breakdown Dali. If what you're saying is correct, then latitude is what we're after in this issue. And I would assume you could then choose from the variety of fruits that naturally grow at your latitude.
>
>
>
> In my case, we're at about the latitude of Miami. Now I don't feel so bad about the mangoes my daughter likes so much when they come flooding in during the season from our corresponding latitude in Pakistan.
>

>
> I'm wondering how far north and south of one's latitude one can go I terms of selecting fruits to eat. Any ideas on that?
>
>
>
> Thanks.

Dali breaks it down very well... however, one component your missing is where are you from? Are you from that area originally? or are you from Maine?

I do best in winter and in cold (When at my current Latitude or higher)- while others don't - why going back to my 23&me - my mitochondrial DNA originated during the HEIGHT of the LAST ICE AGE, in Northern Europe/Asia.

I do 2nd best in Guam - which is ~3deg Latitude - but while I was there I was eating low carb a La Zone diet) I was the skinniest and strongest I'd been since graduating college 2yrs prior. sun rises/sets at 6am/6pm yr round. out of season carbs were hard to find due to the importation costs.

I do 3rd best in the middle east - dryer weather, less allergens, less access to fruits/veggies out of season (during Dec - Feb) - I ate mostly meat/veggies, and locally fermented camel's milk (yogurt, lassi, keifer)

In Okinawa which is at ~ 20 deg lat - I didn't do as well - why? I went vegetarian, w/some fish - & ate lots of carbs... (however they were mostly rice/pasta etc - as out of season fruits/veggies were hard to come by b/c of the importation costs)

I think you have to look at the context of who you are, as well as the context of where you're from, and the context of where you are.

so from my n=1; its about knowing where you stand with health (via blood work etc), as well as following optimal circadian cycles, and seasonal cycles. to determine what you can or cannot eat.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T20:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55887>

> **bigknitwit;57007 wrote: I would love to hear more about this, because you simplify and consolidate things nicely for my brain!
>
>
>
> In any given season, if my FT3 is great (and RT3 is under control), and my HDL is high, and my LDL is under control (I'm a little hazy here, because I know that pregnenelone is made from LDL, so don't we want to see a decent amount of it? I guess we do, in the context of reasonable FT3 numbers. This would show we have plenty of raw hormone building materials, but we don't want a bottleneck either), then our hormone profiles are probably humming along. I'm not sure about using D3 as a measure of this, in the context of supplementing it orally. Doesn't oral supplementation overide the hormone creation cascade, and "fill the last hormone bucket?" Like a little cheat...
>
>
>
> So, what I expect to see in winter is a need for more iodine/seafood to keep FT3 from falling. If I don't do this, then my cholesterol (LDL) would go up, because of a lack of T3 to convert it into pregnenelone. I would see crappier hormone profiles falling out of this scenario, with reduced numbers from pregenelone, all the way down to vitamin D (again, I'm guessing D isn't the best measure given we're supplementing it orally). If I choose to eat bananas all winter long in NH, then what happens? How does the light get it's say in the hormone cascade, that produces the havoc we see in non-seasonal eating? It directs the hypothalamus, which choreographs the entire hormone cascade. This is the point where my understanding gets hazy though. I would love more clarification right here!!
>
>
>
> Do I have this all correct?
>
>
>
> Then likewise, in the summer, as carbs come into season, the seafood/iodine can take a backseat, and carbs can drive the FT3 levels to produce a similar outcome. How do increased carbohydrates = increased FT3?
>
>
> LDL -> Pregnenolone (when acted upon by Vit A and T3 (T3 is converted in the gut from FT3 ->T3 - so if your gut is off, there's a limited production of T3)) From LDL -> Pregnenolone -> progesterone & all other steroid hormones. If you're on the survival pathway LDL-> Pregnenolone -> cortisol (pregnenolone steal)
>
>
>
> I think the general idea is that your premise bigknitwit;57007 wrote:**
>
> So, what I expect to see in winter is a need for more iodine/seafood to keep FT3 from falling. If I don't do this, then my cholesterol (LDL) would go up, because of a lack of T3 to convert it into pregnenelone. I would see crappier hormone profiles falling out of this scenario, with reduced numbers from pregenelone, all the way down to vitamin D (again, I'm guessing D isn't the best measure given we're supplementing it orally).

however it doesn't always work like this... my n=1 shows that last winter (not eating epi-paleo but eating seafood at least 3x/week) that my LDL went up, my HDL went up, Trigs stayed the same, saw D tank (from 116 to 64) my TSH went up, my T3 remained the same on the low end, but my rT3 was reduced - causing my T3:rT3 ratio to improve (All w/o medication). this summer, with the addition of carb (seasonal berries) saw my HDL decrease, Trigs increase (not bad but it was an increase), and saw LDL decrease, vitamin D increase to 127, saw TSH stabilize mid range, T3 remain the same, but an increase in rT3 (I'd been on 1/2 grain of Armour for close to 3mths, as well as BHRT progesterone, and tri-est, as well as DHEA and Pregnenolone)

What was the difference for me - CARBS - seasonal ones no less. my body functions best in ketosis, carbs are a limited thing in my world right now, at some point they might be part of what I do in the spring/summer - but I won't know until we test.

Remember, that not everyone is like me. everyone's n=1, ymmv. It took me a long time to understand why Jack says test, test, test... you can't see the patterns that drive your health, if you don't test.

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T20:28:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55888>

@Shijin. Good points. However, the question is whether the Circadian response is a genetic or Epi-genetic phenomenon. or maybe both.

What I understand from Jack is that light flips Epi-genetic switches, so that we would be constantly adapting to the new latitudes we find ourselves in.

But I have really wondered about this too. I mean "if a cat has her kittens in an oven, you don't call them biscuits."

So I wonder if an equatorial African living in Alaska will have the same circadian responses to light that an Inuit would have.

I think it might be an evolutionary response, that can flip the epigenitic switches....

I get what your asking - I think you have to look at light as the primary driver, and foods as a secondary driver. So if you follow the circadian cycles - you can potentially be more lax in the diet (Assuming you are optimal). my question is why would you want to be lax? (rhetorical question only). If my labs say I'm good (optimal window for me) in December - then yeah I might go ahead and have some 100% dark chocolate, but I'm not going to break out the frozen blueberries and cover them with cream. I'll wait until March to see if I can eat them.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-02T22:12:57+0300

This won't work so well if you are broken, but I'm working on getting fixed.

### TheKid — 2012-10-02T22:17:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-stuff.3763/post-55930>

As I said, not concerned about me. It's my family. They are healthy. And my daughter is a real hodge podge of genetics. So if actual genetics are at play, then I need to really dig deep for her.

But I still think that saying "eat local and seasonal" is a short cut to maintaining proper carb levels year around. Otherwise you'd have to always be looking up gram values and measuring stuff.

You'd have to know what typical carb gram values exist in fruits from your latitude. So it's easier to just eat whats local. Unless you live in the middle of the desert where very little is local.

ah...now I see where you're going w/this. My kids didn't get an optimal start to life b/c I had a sub-optimal Pg:E2 ratio (hence migraines, PCOS, not to mention a bunch of other stuff)

the kids are getting the Vit D tested this week to see where they're at - then we'll do it again in Dec/Jan. I know dd has a sub-optimal gut (BS chart 2/3s most of the time, and bad breath) DS is definitely better, but not by much - as the both got my sub-optimal gut flora at birth. my kids look healthy - but I doubt they're as healthy as they could be. we've been working really hard to control what we can (somethings like daycare the dcp is required by law to feed them certain stuff) Chaos, the 4yo is now starting to refuse to eat the things she knows she shouldn't. Havoc, the 2yo isn't quite there but he can tell you if he sees a fruit if its in/out of season - he hasn't connected that if its out he shouldn't eat it. # Thread: OCD Advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jenn — 2012-09-19T20:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52742>

Hi all,

I wondered if anyone here has had any experience with OCD and knows of any specific lifestyle interventions that might help. My 13-year-old son was recently diagnosed (he was also diagnosed with ADHD at age 6) and is really struggling with the obsessive thoughts and constant hand washing. I reluctantly agreed to try Zoloft, and then Prozac, but the side effects were almost worse than the OCD and in any case they did little to help. The only treatment I know of, besides drugs, is cognitive behavior therapy, but he is either unable or unwilling to follow through with it despite having an excellent therapist.

He uses blue blockers/amber lights at night (with some grousing) and has an 8:30 bedtime (up at 5:30am school days). He eats a fair amount of fish (but little shellfish except in clam chowder), but also some grains/wheat, mostly when we go out to eat. He tried gluten-free for a couple of weeks with no obvious benefit (would have been longer, but I was under considerable pressure from him and hubby to relent). He dislikes vegetables and favors starchy foods, dairy, and modest amounts of protein foods, like fish and bacon. He moves around a lot but does not exercise/do sports. Body comp not fat but a little â€œsoft.â€￾ I have not even broached the topic of CT yet, as I am sure it will meet with resistanceâ€¦.did I mention he was 13?

Thoughts? Suggestions? Experiences? Is there a particular blog of Jackâ€™s that might lend some insight into the brain chemistry behind OCD and other anxiety disorders? (maybe the Tourettes one, or the Leaky Gut Rx?) Any tests for allergens, hormones, etc., I should consider? I might add that my hubby is not fully on board with the changes Iâ€™ve been trying, so if you have any cites that back up your recommendations, by all means share! Thank you for any advice you might have.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-19T21:09:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52746>

Jenn,

Is your son on meds for ADHD? From my experience with one of my children ADHD meds increase anxiety and thus OCD.

My other advice would be to test for allergies and celiac (esp. dairy and wheat) and to really limit sugar.

And exercise daily is really important for anyone with depression or anxiety. He doesn't have to spend an hour in the gym, walking or riding a bike will do. He needs to move!

### jenn — 2012-09-19T21:29:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52750>

Hi indigogirl,

Not on ADD meds right now. Earlier this year we tried a course of Intuniv (non-stimulant), shortly after which the OCD symptoms began. They went away after stopping the med, then resumed a couple weeks later. The psychiatrist says there is no connection between Intuniv and OCD, but I'm not so sure.... On one Intuniv forum board I read that Intuniv works by increasing glutamate to the brain, but that in persons sensitive to it (i.e. those with gluten or casein sensitivities) it can precipitate OCD symptoms. But I have no way of assessing the credibility of this claim.

You are totally right about the exercise! I think I know some of the things I ought to be doing with him...but need a push to go with my instincts. Thank you very much for your advice!

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-20T00:07:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52785>

Dr Kalish treats ADD/ADHD with neurotransmitter support. He says he gives tyrosine in his office to patients that cannot focus and it works within 20-30min to help them through the appointment. Not sure about the OCD but sounds like he might be able to help with that as well.....

[http://www.kalishresearch.com/](http://http://www.kalishresearch.com/)

He is great with answering email inquiries

### jenn — 2012-09-20T01:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52802>

Thank you, Souldanzer! I'll check him out. I'm betting OCD and ADHD have common neurotransmitter issues at their core....I've read dopamine is involved with both...

### Momma28 — 2012-09-20T04:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52840>

Hi Jenn,

I find that behavior worsens with the consumption of dairy and grain. My heart goes out to you because I know how hard it is to limit foods once they get into their teens. Your best route is going to be getting him on your team. Try the website MY Aspergers Child for tips. It is $19, I think, and the man was a therapist for teens and then set up the website to help more families. He has a son on the spectrum. His info is good but applied to older children like your son. I got it for help with a two year old on the spectrum.

If he has gluten sensitivity he may also have B12 defficiency because the intestine will be damaged and may not pick it up from food. I apply B12 patches by Vie that I get from Amazon. I also supplement D3 and iodine in the form of Lugol's and they all eat Paleo. They get one cheat on Sunday afternoon for a dessert. There are great recipes on Chocolate Covered Katie and The Spunky Coconut that are dairy/gluten/sugar free. Hope this helps. Remember, you're the only one that's going to do this for him. Let's pray he'll be able to thank you later.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-20T20:57:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52971>

Jenn, I had a very close relative suffer with this. The SSRI's were horrible - side effects terrible.

What seemed to work in the end was taking several mindfulness classes for this person. I would also be very tempted to try out cold thermogenesis. Check out this article:

[http://www.thisviewoflife.com/index.php/magazine/articles/take-a-cold-shower](http://http://www.thisviewoflife.com/index.php/magazine/articles/take-a-cold-shower)

> The biologist Dr. Nikolai Shevchuk, contends that the origin of noradrenergic dysfunction in humans can be attributed to individual genetic susceptibility and a more general evolutionary mismatch between our modern and ancestral environments. He believes that cold-water exposure can be adapted as a therapy for depression and other conditions that may have their roots in noradrenergic dysfunction. Dr. Shevchuk believes that modern humans lack sufficient “thermal exercise” of the locus coeruleus, and that this was not a problem faced by our ancestors.

After reading through several pubmed papers, I'm of the opinion that once we start losing our BAT and their adrenergic receptors that norepinephrine may malfunction and cause anxiety problems. It is just wild idea at this point, but I think it could work or has potential.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-20T21:10:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-52974>

There is a recent podcast with Kalish on underground wellness, had some stuff that applies!

### jenn — 2012-09-20T23:17:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53003>

Momma28,

I think you're right about the dairy and grains. My son was colicky as an infant due to a milk-protein intolerance (via my breastmilk), and it could very well be that casein is still problematic and manifesting in the form of ADHD and OCD. I've been considering the EnteroLab stool test for various allergens to try to clarify the picture....they claim to be more accurate than blood tests.

### jenn — 2012-09-20T23:27:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53007>

villjamur\_stevenson,

Yeah, the SSRIs gave my son sleep problems and complete brain fog, and didn't even take the edge off his anxiety. I'm glad I stopped them before they caused any lasting problems.

I like the idea of CT (and practice it myself; I squish into a 40 gallon outdoor stock tank every morning before work -- this morning 50 degrees F -- and use ice packs at night), but I have the grit to do it and think it will be a hard sell for my son....but then many worthwhile things are hard, at least initially.

I'm considering pushing him to try meditation, which I know Jack practices and recommends...but with the ADHD it would have to be a "walking meditation" as he gets squirrelly if he has to sit still for any length of time. I appreciate your suggestions!

### jenn — 2012-09-20T23:29:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53009>

Cavemam,

I like Sean Croxton and will definitely check out the podcast, thank you!

### jenn — 2012-09-20T23:34:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53011>

To everyone who has replied with their suggestions and experiences so far, thank you so much! Sometimes it feels as though I'm all alone in this, struggling to help my son, and it is encouraging to come here and know that others are willing to help.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-21T15:54:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53158>

> **Jenn;53642 wrote:**
>
> I like the idea of CT (and practice it myself; I squish into a 40 gallon outdoor stock tank every morning before work -- this morning 50 degrees F -- and use ice packs at night), but I have the grit to do it and think it will be a hard sell for my son....but then many worthwhile things are hard, at least initially.

We got our kids an inflatable water slide that runs on tap water. They love it and will play in that thing for hours.

We use our tap water - 53 F. They've been doing CT all summer, and didn't even realize it.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-21T16:10:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocd-advice.3765/post-53168>

Jenn, in terms of meditation you can download meditations ( go to the Chopra center website or google)...instrumental, chants, guided meditations.

When my son was a young teenager these were easier for him than just sitting with his thoughts.

I would also talk him through progressive relaxation.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-19T22:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52753>

OK Brainiacs! Here is your chance to win some home made vanilla!

HACK MY LABS! Tell me what you know! Who ever is closest to what Dr. K says on our consult on 9/29 wins a little package of handmade vanilla from moi. G, if you win - you get something else, but equally awesome.

I will mail it to you! I won't stalk you.

Background:

Female, 44, 210 lbds, 1 round of HCG in June of this year.

Gastric Bypass 12 yrs ago.

HR: Averages 90/60

Pulse: Resting about 60, low activity bout 70

Blood Glucose, low risk for beetees. Fasting 77ish, rarely goes above 120.

Epi paleo with seafood 1X a day on average. . .been doing that for 6 months, mostly Ketogenic with a few fail days (maybe 1X every 2 months?). Consume lots of oysters and a reasonable amount of local and homegrown produce. Do consume HWC and some goats/sheep cheese now and then. Recently started to add in more fermented stuff.

Very circadian focused, blue blockers and minimal lights at night. Get up with the sun and go outside for bright lights. Active with lots of natural lighting. Very low stress - I've spent years engineering my environment and life before I even conceptualized the term optimal as we know it.

At the time of drawing these labs I was on:

B12 Shots

Lots of Iron supps

Mag at night

30-50K a day of Vit-D (a lot of good that did!)

I had done months of CT, but stopped over summer to clear out for the round of HCG and for drawing a hormone panel.

I have recently had a vaginal ultrasound with additional ovary looking to check, not a fibroid or cyst in sight. . .obgy said the ovaries were beautiful.

Symptoms: Hair Loss

Poops: Ideal.

Energy: Consistant and good. Sleep good. Mood reasonable, been pretty cranky and anti-social in the last month.

I have taken a DEXA scan and sent in for the Metramix GI, will add those as soon as they come in.

TAKE YOUR BEST SHOT AND WIN!! WHAT'S SHOULD I BE DOING DIFFERENTLY!

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0AmcYkugLB3O4dDZHdndkTVFCMTRoM2VIMTY5cEhUMFE](http://https://docs.google.com/spreadsheet/ccc?key=0AmcYkugLB3O4dDZHdndkTVFCMTRoM2VIMTY5cEhUMFE)

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T22:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52762>

You've got elevated TPO AB - you're going to need to re-check this at least 2-3x/s over the next year to rule out autoimmune

your T3 isn't great b/c its all going to rT3. your FT3:rT3 ratio is 11

you' need to have a hormone panel run btwn cd 19-21 to see what your Pg:E2 ratio looks like

You're total T is out of control, girl hate to break this to you but you're a testosterone producer - but your Free T is in range so there's a win!.

you LDL is low. you need some LDL to make hormones... you're not making the T3 to convert what LDL you've got to hormones.

but w/o an ASI can't say for sure if you've got pregnenolone steal going on...

You've also got issues w/your Vit D3. - so that means you have issues w/your gut, but we know that b/c of the gastric bypass.

so you some how have to fix your gut the best way you can... what does your K2 supplementation look like? also are you taking any reversitiol sublingulially?

most likely everything is related to your altered gut... as to how to fix it IHNC - but I bet JAck does.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T23:03:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52766>

> **Cavemam;53391 wrote:** Hormone stuff is there. . .pg/e ration is 5.7 according to the calc we've been batting about here!!!!!

yeah... but look at these:

[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Hormones\_estradiol%2C\_progesterone%2C\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.png](http://http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.png)

specifically the Pg one (2nd one). at the beginning of your cycle its low, then it increases... you want to get the measurement at its PEAK and compare it to where you E2 is at the same time, so if your test dates were on CD4 - then you only have 1/2 the picture - where your Pg is at the start of your cycle... you need the CD19-21 to get the real picture.

I had Pg drawn on cd 19 (Aug 11), and cd 4 (jan 12) both times my PG was .5ng/mL - as in Pg didn't elevate like its supposed to in the charts above. w/BHRT it bumped up to 19 ng/dl which is better but not great when compared to my E2.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-19T23:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-52778>

With the elevated TPO, you to probably eat autoimmune although not enough to call it Hashis but enough for eating to change until proven you don't have it (18 mo - testing every 3-6 mo). The FT3 is way too low. Maybe you need more iodine to help in the conversion process. Ditto what Gretchen said about the T4 getting converted into rT3 instead of FT3. How's you temp? I would guess it is a little lower than it normally would be. Your FT4 is also low as it should be at the very least mid to high range. So basically you arent making enough FT4 and what you are making is going to rT3 causing the FT3 to fall. I only know how to read (as best my non-doc brain will allow me) PTH in patterns...no thin slicer here! It helps your calcium levels in your blood. Too much calcium in your blood makes you a crazy person. It happened to my mom and she was literally CRAZY! PTH helps regulate that.

Your iron is out of whack too. Your ferritin (storage iron) is low. Your serum iron is a little low too and should be higher (around 110, IIRC). The % saturation s/b around 35% so you are not storing the iron. Absorption issues maybe?

Your cholesterol looks a lot like mine. I always wondered how it could not be higher with all the fat I eat. It was fat-malabsorption and low stomach acid. Ox bile and HCL is taking care of that! Your HDL should be higher and the lower number is probably back to the gut (leaky).

The Pg & E2 testing needs to be done on cd 19-21 of a 28 day cycle. Then you can figure out the proper ratio. My guess is it is still going to be out of whack and you will need bio progesterone. DHEA is a little low but close to the top quartile at 3. But if the range is age adjusted, you may want to supplement with 7-keto DHEA. It would be nice to have a pregnenolone test in here as well.

Both B12 and Vit D are low. Absorption issues again I assume with all the supps you are taking. Also the K2 helps to allow the D3 to get processed easier. I wonder what your other vitamins look like (choline, other Bs, antioxidants). Maybe a Spectracell Micronutrient test is also in order.

That's all I know!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T23:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/get-your-lab-reading-certification-here-throw-in-your-02-cents-and-win.3767/post-53001>

> **Patty Cakes;53556 wrote:** Destiny try for the thermography, like Seahorse mentioned if they want you to do another one. My old gyn was just starting to do those a few years ago. That is the only thing I would consider nowadays. Also is your estrogen high? Sorry can't remember the details....

Destiny, I think it's really normal (and appropriate) to be afraid. You can't just ignore fear. It can be intelligent. When making the decision, my advice (as someone whose had breast cancer and a lot of mammograms during the process to figure it out) is to make sure your decision isn't based only on fear. It's easy for people to say 'don't do a mammogram' if they've never had any red flags. On the other hand, thermography is a good option. You need to educate yourself on this, however, and figure out what you feel is best. Thermography is not a detection screening tool like mammography is - mammograms can detect tumours and cancerous growth (when done right). Thermography detects breast temperature changes, thought by most to be a pre-cursor to cancer. However, even a thermographer is going to refer you for a mammogram if they are concerned. You can also ask for an ultrasound (another option I also had when mammogram wasn't clear) and MRI is also an option (though, radiation as well). What I would want (and do want for myself) is a doctor who can discuss this with you and help you wade through the risk-benefit analysis. Good luck!

# Thread: Destruction of Thyroid Peroxidase enzyme
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-19T22:30:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52754>

I re-re-re-read B12 this afternoon, this time taking notes...

Here is the part that jumped out at me from a Hashimoto's perspective:

_"Venturi reported in 1985 that iodine was the first inorganic antioxidant to be described in any living system on this planet. He showed that iodine is is collected in cells by proteins in its iodide form and is bound to amino acids to form iodoproteins. It was later discovered that in all vertebrate cells iodide acts as electron donors in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and thyroid peroxidase enzyme. This enzyme is the one that is destroyed in Hashimotoâ€™s thyroiditis by auto antibodies. This helps explain why Hashimotoâ€™s is often seen as the first step in many neolithic diseaseâ€™s that oxidize human tissues to alter cellular signaling. It also helps explain why Hashimotoâ€™s disease is now so common today."_

So, if we don't have any thyroid peroxidase enzyme (destroyed in autoimmune attack), then it seems to follow that the iodide cannot act as an electron donor (lipid antioxidant) since the enzyme to produce this is not present (destroyed). Does this mean the cell is significantly hindered in acquiring the life-saving antioxidant that is iodine? I take replacement T3/T4 meds to offset the lack of hormone output from my gland, but it seems that without this enzyme, my body can't make iodine do what it needs to do to protect my cells.

How do Hashimoto's patients deal with this? If the disease progression is only partial, then I suppose there might still be some enzyme (reduced) around. But once advanced, does our body just self-destruct, and the only thing we can do is slow down the progression of oxidation with a heavily "reduced" diet, plus CT, plus low light, etc? How do we achieve the "reduced" state without being able to use the iodine as an electron donor? How on earth did I manage to produce 4 full term pregnancies with this condition?

Is there a way to reverse this horrendous disease?

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-20T00:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52787>

Am I the only Hashi's person sitting here, re-reading this, and trying to stay calm (minimize my entry into the cortisol pathway!)?? Now I think I understand why Jack says Hashi's is like a cancer diagnosis... I want my Tpo enzymes back! I must be missing something, but the way I read this is that Hashi's will gradually (as antibodies climb) deplete you of your ability to use iodine at the cellular level as an antioxidant. You will be oxidizing and oxidizing and oxidizing some more, and there's not a whole lot you can do about it, except live in a cold, dark, ketogenic place and count your days as your body degenerates. I must be missing something. This is looking horrendously bleak.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-20T00:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52789>

Jack refers to Hashi's as a dynamic disease. I interpret this to mean it will respond negatively or positively to given circumstances. I don't know the answer. Great question.

I have recently come to an awareness of how devastating this disease can be and the myriad factors that influence it. It's rising prevalence is alarming.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-20T00:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/destruction-of-thyroid-peroxidase-enzyme.3768/post-52798>

eat correctly, pay attn to your labs, light, and temperature and you can live long with it........if you fail too......you will be seeing lots of MD's. It is a disease that growing into a massive problem under everyone's noses because they do not seem to understand the dietary links.

# Thread: Getting the fish down- ???

**Posts:** 9

### Meg — 2012-09-20T00:26:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52792>

I am new on the forum but have been Epi Paleo since the blog came out. What I want to know is does anyone else struggle with the methods of getting some of this seafood down (especially raw), by eating/swallowing as fast as you can and how that affects the digestive system? Everything I've read about healing the gut encourages eating slow, letting saliva mix with the food in your mouth etc........ which is not happening with me!

Does the good of the food outweigh the method of intake? Or should I try to slow down and enjoy it.

I am learning to look at each meal as like - what can I put into my body that will be most healing and beneficial to my brain and most days I have no problem with this, occasionally I revert back to the Amercican way of thinking that food should be fun and exciting and I beat this idea down by remebering how cruddy I felt when I was eating that way.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T00:59:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52799>

I eat lots of sashimi and under cook all the fish I make. Not a fan of raw oysters but can swallow a few, no big deal. Plus with the MHSmothies I choke those puppies down as fast as I can! Maybe enzymes would help if you are worried about improper digestion. Sorry I'm not much help. I love fish so it's not that hard for me.

> I am learning to look at each meal as like - what can I put into my body that will be most healing and beneficial to my brain and most days I have no problem with this, occasionally I revert back to the Amercican way of thinking that food should be fun and exciting and I beat this idea down by remebering how cruddy I felt when I was eating that way.

I had a wonderful cooking teacher and she always said - if you don't like something - you probably haven't had it prepared in a good way for you. I think if you just keep trying different fish and different ways of prep you will find things you love. Can someone else in your household just lightly steam some fish for you - a la Jack LaLaine? Things always taste so much better when someone else makes it! Have you tried Inger's recipe for Salmon? It is so delicious. As you said - food is a nutrient and healing for our body and not the focus it used to be - amazing how that has changed for us! Keep us posted on how you are going.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T01:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52819>

I think if you blend up the fish (a la MHS) you don't have to worry about the chewing as it's pre-chewed. Definitely find recipes you like. But with the raw stuff you don't like, blend and slurp quickly has been my motto!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-20T03:22:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52836>

Meg, have you tried ceviche? Using lime juice to "cook" the fish in the fridge?

### Meg — 2012-09-20T03:22:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52837>

Yes, food somehow mysteriously does taste better when you don't cook it but I wish you could see me laughing hard at the "someone else could cook it" idea! My family watches with disgust at the raw stuff but will participate in eating the cooked.

I think I need Ingers recipe for salmon- do you know where I can find it? Is it just salmon & water in the blender?

### Meg — 2012-09-20T03:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52838>

Blend and slurp it will have to be! At least for the raw stuff. Thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T03:52:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52839>

> **Meg;53468 wrote:** Yes, food somehow mysteriously does taste better when you don't cook it but I wish you could see me laughing hard at the "someone else could cook it" idea! My family watches with disgust at the raw stuff but will participate in eating the cooked.
>
>
>
> I think I need Ingers recipe for salmon- do you know where I can find it? Is it just salmon & water in the blender?

Inger's salmon, gratefully, does not require a blender  It is raw salmon, skin off, cut into small slices/pieces, then lime/lemon/vinegar, garlic, coriander (cilantro), salt, pepper and maybe a bit of honey. Somewhere on the forum she made an actual video of herself making it. It's pretty easy - just salmon ceviche really.

+1 on the family watching with disgust. I will bring my MHS to the table and sit with them and they groan "Oh, grossssssss!" (though my husband will now drink one if I make him one)

### caroline — 2012-09-20T05:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-fish-down.3770/post-52856>

> **Meg;53468 wrote:** Yes, food somehow mysteriously does taste better when you don't cook it but I wish you could see me laughing hard at the "someone else could cook it" idea! My family watches with disgust at the raw stuff but will participate in eating the cooked.
>
>
>
> I think I need Ingers recipe for salmon- do you know where I can find it? Is it just salmon & water in the blender?

Inger's Salmon recipe is on page #7 with beautiful pictures and there is good posts by AKMan on fish.

Inger also has a video somewhere on High Meat - probably this thread too. I am not very good at finding stuff but some one else is sure to know.

There used to be all lot of wonderful food pics from another sweedish or Danish girl - but I can't find them at all.

Good luck!

# Thread: Doctor recommendation in NYC please

### PaleoCarol — 2012-09-20T00:33:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-recommendation-in-nyc-please.3771/post-52793>

Hi everyone,

On June 7th I came across this site and started following the plan: the BAB, coconut oil, organic food, NO snacking, bought blackout shades (even for the kitchen), fitover amber glasses, do face in ice water - everything. Four weeks later I had blood work done and my A1C went from 6.4 to 5.7. Now it's 5.5. In the first four weeks I lost 12 lbs. and 6 inches from my waist. (I'm 5'4''and 218 lbs. now.) I was elated and I am really looking forward to my consult with Dr. K. Sept. 29th.

My question is this: I have been unable to find a doctor to work with me in NYC.

Can anyone suggest a doctor? My insurance is Medicare. Thank you so much.

PaleoCarol

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-20T00:47:19+0300

talk to msclman he may know someone.

# Thread: BG6 Questions re: Coffee or Tea and what do you put on your Seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T01:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52817>

I am considering starting the Epi-Paleo Rx to eliminate my inflamation and to possibly reverse some arthritus.

I just read BG-6 today and noticed Dr. Kruse wants coffee to be eliminated to help reduce inflamation. I am okay with that, I will miss it, but do not depend on it. I was wondering if tea and ice tea are allowable substitutes?

Secondly, as I transition into more seafood, I got to wondering what to put on it for added flavor. I always have mixed up either cocktail sauce or tartar sauce, but one is made with nightshades and the other has fat in it. So what do you put on it?

Thanks

L J

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T01:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52818>

Coffee is about context. If you think you need to drop it (that's often wise) green tea is the best substitute. If caffeine is an issue, pre-steep for a minute or two, drain the water and then refill.

Stock up on spices - cilantro, coriander, curry powder, oregano, thyme, mint - and garlic and onions and try different flavours with your seafood. Fat is OK as long as it's not saturated - so you can use olive oil, mac oil or even bacon (mostly MUFA).

Good luck.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-20T01:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52820>

coffee is not inflammatory.....it just can cause a problem with your parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous system balance.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T02:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52823>

Thanks. And back to wikipedia I go.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T04:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52849>

Oy! That made my head spin!

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-20T15:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bg6-questions-re-coffee-or-tea-and-what-do-you-put-on-your-seafood.3773/post-52883>

Okay I have discovered what the parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous systems are for. I took inventory of me since my stroke and diagnosis of the cervical stenosis. I understand that part of my bladder urgency at times was the med I was on (New doctor and new med, less urgency) and part is probably corrupt nerve communication along the parasympathetic path. So giving up coffee may help balance my nerve communications. I know it won't heal it, but it may help. I will give it a couple months to see how I am doing.

I am probably a little off on my conclusion, but it is a theory I can run with now.

# Thread: I'm kind of a baby, so I need some help please.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-09-20T05:37:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52864>

I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:

\- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and

\- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and

\- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?

Thanks!

### Melinda — 2012-09-20T06:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52865>

I use Trader Joe's wild canned 1 lb cans to make a salmon loaf type dish. I don't have a set recipe but the bones and skin disappear as long as I add some celery. It's better than using expensive salmon meat without the skin and bones. Mash fish, add a few eggs, celery, onion, sun dried tomatoes, dill, maybe some rice bread crumbs, shredded cheese, diced pre-cooked bacon, diced black olives etc...mix all and cook. I pat it into a fry pan, lots of ghee and fry it, flip it onto a plate and slide back into the pan to cook the other side or leave it in the pan, add more cheese and broil it until firm/cooked.

### caroline — 2012-09-20T14:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52876>

> **cookworm;53496 wrote:** I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:
>
>
>
> \- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and
>
> \- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and
>
> \- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?
>
>
>
> Thanks!

The skin and bones used to gross me out too- but now I just mash it all up and add green onions and it is good to go! You really can't tell - doesn't change the taste - at least I don't think so. Lately - food just seems to be fuel to me - so I just eat it and move on.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T16:19:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52894>

Recommend that you switch to Wild Planet brand...they have BPA free cans. I eat both the pink and the sockeye. TJ's is supposed to be working on BPA free cans.

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T17:06:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52897>

> **cookworm;53496 wrote:** I love salmon, but can't always afford the fresh stuff, so I am willing to go with wild canned salmon to make my diet more epi-paleo. I don't have a problem with bones, but, I admit, the skin grosses me out. So, I have a three part question:
>
>
>
> \- What are some different ways to enjoy canned salmon? and
>
> \- Is there a way to enjoy the salmon with the skin and bones, carefully finding a way to disguise the skin? and
>
> \- Does mashing the skin with the bones and fish make the salmon itself taste different/unpleasant?
>
>
>
> Thanks!

Just mash it all up. The skin and bones break right down and don't change the flavor of the salmon at all. My favorite way to use canned salmon is to fry a couple pieces of bacon, take them out of the pan, then add in a can of mashed-up salmon. Saute it in the bacon fat a little bit to warm through, then scoop it onto a plate and add the strips of bacon, chopped up. It's good with sauteed kale and a couple runny eggs on top, too...yum!

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T17:45:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52905>

What ealachan said...done the same myself.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52913>

When we were kids and my mom was making salmon patties after she dumped the blob of salmon out of the can she would ask us "who wants the bones?" and all three of us would run to munch and crunch on the bones. We were taught they were a treat like batter from the blender beaters when making cookies.

I still make salmon patties and this is what I eat when I travel. I mash the salmon with some freshly grated (or bottled) horseradish, caramelized onions, good salt, and a sprinkling of walnut meal to hold them together. I fry in a little olive oil (used to use coconut oil but no more). I have also made little salmon balls this same way just a different shape. I also have a variety of organic dried herbs that I use to add a different flavor if I'm in the mood.

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T18:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52914>

> **Patty Cakes;53548 wrote:** When we were kids and my mom was making salmon patties after she dumped the blob of salmon out of the can she would ask us "who wants the bones?" and all three of us would run to munch and crunch on the bones. We were taught they were a treat like batter from the blender beaters when making cookies.
>
>
>
> I still make salmon patties and this is what I eat when I travel. I mash the salmon with some freshly grated (or bottled) horseradish, caramelized onions, good salt, and a sprinkling of walnut meal to hold them together. I fry in a little olive oil (used to use coconut oil but no more). I have also made little salmon balls this same way just a different shape. I also have a variety of organic dried herbs that I use to add a different flavor if I'm in the mood.

Oooh, I love horseradish. Now I need to make some more salmon cakes and try them with horseradish in!

Your talking of crunching the bones made me laugh. I actually don't mind the texture of them anymore, but they seem to kind of stick to my teeth, which drives me bonkers.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:24:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52916>

> **ealachan;53549 wrote:** Oooh, I love horseradish. Now I need to make some more salmon cakes and try them with horseradish in!

You must try freshly grated horseradish! So much better than the prepared bottled stuff. I am assuming here that it is an epi-paleo approved food since it is a root. I can't have peppers so this is pretty much my only ZING!

### ealachan — 2012-09-20T18:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52923>

> **Patty Cakes;53551 wrote:** You must try freshly grated horseradish! So much better than the prepared bottled stuff. I am assuming here that it is an epi-paleo approved food since it is a root. I can't have peppers so this is pretty much my only ZING!

Oh yes, my dad grows it, so he's always got some hanging around. Once I had the actual ground root with just a little vinegar in it to preserve it, I never went back to the bottled stuff. I used to work with an old-timer who kept a bottle of his own home-grown horseradish root in the fridge. When one of our crew got a sore throat or a stuffy nose, out would come the horseradish and he'd make us take a dose straight-up. That stuff would knock a cold out of you like nobody's business!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:40:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52927>

OMG!!! Horseradish up the nose! I'm getting a burning sense up there just thinking about it. Add a raw garlic sandwich to that and you'd be good to go!

### cookworm — 2012-09-20T19:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52939>

ealachan--hey you Raveler!  This sounds delicious (anything with bacon is better!)

These are some GREAT ideas--thank you everybody! I'm not opposed to trying most things...I just can't seem to think outside the box mostly.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-20T22:17:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-kind-of-a-baby-so-i-need-some-help-please.3777/post-52981>

My kids had a really hard time accepting the skin and bones as part of their "meal" for a while, but I think I finally found a way to make it work. I do salmon patties like someone previously mentioned. Just mash up the salmon, bones, skin and all - add to a beaten egg with some sea salt, fresh/dried dill. I used to always coat the patties in pecan meal, but we're trying to really limit nuts these days so now I chop up bacon and mix that into the patties . . . the kids love it! I fry the bacon in a cast iron skillet and leave the grease in their so I can just fry the patties in it to soak up the extra goodness. Works like a charm . . . the bacon flavor / color totally overpowers any skin/bone issues

# Thread: Bpa
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Endless — 2012-09-20T08:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52869>

I'd love to see this as a sticky, so people could check out their canned fish! If anyone has determined through research or other means that a particular brand of canned fish/whatever is BPA free, please post it here!

[http://www.naturalnews.com/037241\_BPA\_nervous\_system\_plastics\_chemical.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/037241_BPA_nervous_system_plastics_chemical.html)

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T17:27:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52901>

Wild Planet...available at Whole Foods and Sweetbay Supermarket. Price varies on the type of salmon and ranges from 3 sumtin to 4 sumtin (6 oz).

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:22:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52915>

_"Trader Joe's Tells Little, Does More

. It is sometimes hard to know whether to be infuriated or pleased with the Trader Joe's approach to food retailing. Stories abound about some of TJ's practices - clear information at the company's web site does not. But a call to TJ's customer relations reveals that canned corn, canned beans, canned fish, canned poultry, and canned beef at Trader Joe's are all packaged in BPA free cans. However, any other products that contain corn or beans or fish, etc. and other items - for example in soups or chilis - are not in BPA free cans, the company said. No labeling here, either."_

From: [http://www.treehugger.com/green-food/7-companies-you-can-trust-to-use-bpa-free-cans.html](http://http://www.treehugger.com/green-food/7-companies-you-can-trust-to-use-bpa-free-cans.html)

But how do we know they haven't substituted BPS for the BPA?

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T19:40:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52942>

That article is over 2 years old.

Someone here contacted TJ's in the last few months, and was told that their fish is NOT in BPA free cans yet.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T19:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52949>

Just emailed Trader Joe's about their cans. Will update upon reply.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T20:22:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52959>

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-20T22:07:12+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-09-20T22:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-52984>

Natural Value tuna is BPA free. $1.99 for a can of wild caught yellowfin tuna at my locally owned HerbMart.

[http://naturalvalue.com/](http://http://naturalvalue.com/)

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-10-09T03:32:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-57026>

Here you go...email reply from Trader Joe's Customer relations folks:

Thank you for contacting us. Here is the deal with BPA. First, regarding Tetra, all Tetra Pak is BPA-free.

Second, every glass jar item has a metal lid. All metal lids do have a layer of BPA coating. However, there is another coating put on after that. There is no direct contact of BPA to food. We have multiple supplier testing results showing there is no BPA detected from metal lids.

All our canned fish (and our canned chicken and beef too) are now in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Sardines, Crab, Cherrystone Clams & Oysters (our suppliers are working for a 2012 solution).

All our canned fruits, beans and vegetables (including tomatoes, and the Organic Canned Pumpkin when it returns this Fall) are in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Mandarins, Hatch Chilies, Artichokes, Organic Baked Beans (expecting transition next Fall).

All of our canned Soups and Stews (and including Joe's Os) are in cans that DO have BPA. Some of our suppliers are expecting they will be able to make transition next year.

Lastly, Coconut Milk and Coconut Cream is in a BPA-free can.

Regards,

Nikki

Customer Relations

### skline@peak.org — 2012-10-09T03:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bpa.3778/post-57027>

I've been buying brisling sardines in olive oil, source Poland -- hoping that since BPA is outlawed in

Europe, maybe it's outlawed in Poland as well?

or not ...

>
> Europe, maybe it's outlawed in Poland as well?
>
>

> **WannaBeOptimal;58328 wrote:** Here you go...email reply from Trader Joe's Customer relations folks:
>

>
> Thank you for contacting us. Here is the deal with BPA. First, regarding Tetra, all Tetra Pak is BPA-free.
>

>
> Second, every glass jar item has a metal lid. All metal lids do have a layer of BPA coating. However, there is another coating put on after that. There is no direct contact of BPA to food. We have multiple supplier testing results showing there is no BPA detected from metal lids.
>
>
>
> All our canned fish (and our canned chicken and beef too) are now in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Sardines, Crab, Cherrystone Clams & Oysters (our suppliers are working for a 2012 solution).
>
>
>
> All our canned fruits, beans and vegetables (including tomatoes, and the Organic Canned Pumpkin when it returns this Fall) are in BPA-free cans EXCEPT: Mandarins, Hatch Chilies, Artichokes, Organic Baked Beans (expecting transition next Fall).
>
>
>
> All of our canned Soups and Stews (and including Joe's Os) are in cans that DO have BPA. Some of our suppliers are expecting they will be able to make transition next year.
>
>
>
> Lastly, Coconut Milk and Coconut Cream is in a BPA-free can.
>
>
>
> Regards,
>
> Nikki
>
> Customer Relations

### Mery — 2012-09-20T15:16:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gain-muscle.3779/post-52884>

LR started 19-3-2012. Iâ€™ve been complaining about gaining weight even though I did not realised it that Iâ€™ve gained muscles and bones.

Iâ€™m not very observant like my husband said. I did notice before that my tummy was much firmer even though I was so obsessed about not being able to fit in my clothes that I forgot to look more closely to my arms and legs. My skin is not longer loose. No more bingo wings on my arms or flabby legs.

Iâ€™ve always had weak and flaccid muscles, no matter how much exercise I did, they didnâ€™t get firm, but now without any exercises my whole body is muscular.

It is nice to feel this way, my husband wants to do the same as he has got weak muscle like I used to have.

Now they're like...a layer of pudding over rock. LOL I can even feel abs if I poke deep enough into my Pillsbury Dough Boy belly, and...this is going to sound so silly, but...I can make my boobs bounce by flexing my pectoral muscles. ### Destiny — 2012-09-26T13:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gain-muscle.3779/post-54386>

The 10 Top Tips When Buying And Using Body Fat Scales

1\. Use them to measure your progress only. Don't compare your body fat percentage to tables or to your friends score. It is probably inaccurate no matter what the manufacturers say.

2\. Choose body fat scales that have the right profile for you. If your children are going to use them make sure they can be calibrated for children. If you're an athlete (+10 hrs of strenuous exercise a week) same thing applies. This is important. It makes a big difference.

3\. Measure out some water one hour before you test yourself. Make sure you always drink the same amount of water one hour before you test yourself.

4\. Measure yourself at the same time of day for each test.

5\. Your skin temperature affects the electrical current used by the scales. It's difficult but try to test yourself in a similar room temperature each time.

6\. Don't test yourself after exercising. When you exercise you sweat and when you sweat you lose water. This affects your hydration levels and hence... the results.

7\. Thoroughly clean the foot pads, preferably with alcohol and then dry them off each time you test.

8\. Buy the most expensive body fat scales you can afford. Accuracy will almost certainly increase with price.

9\. If ever you get the chance, have your body composition measured by a professional using skin fold calipers. It will give you a frame of reference as to how accurate your scales are.

10\. Read the page on body fat calipers for an alternative you could consider.

# Thread: Calling all Hashimoto's people
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 261
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-20T16:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52895>

How did BG12 change the view you have of your health and your plan going forward? I keep looking around for other people feeling the way I do (like all that knowledge just crushed my head), and I'm not seeing it! After the 3rd read of it (while note taking), suddenly, it all came crashing in together and I "got" it, "get" it, whatever. And it's not good!

All this reading about the life of the cell, inflammatory versus reproductive pathways, ketosis, epi-paleo, CT, all of it. I think it's getting into my thick skull. We (Hashi's people) live in the inflammatory pathway all the time, and there doesn't seem to be a way to get out of it (I'll be looking though!). We can only minimize our stressors putting us there (epi-paleo, CT, light, low stress living). Last night going to bed the thought crossed my mind I'd better get my affairs in order (did I mention I'm 36 and have 4 young kids?). This morning my resolve is stronger (adding in more CT, low FT3 or not!), getting into the dark with sundown more than just using the blue-blockers. Diet is already pretty clean.

Anyone here of cases of people reversing their Hashimoto's? How about reversing other auto-immune conditions? I think Terry Wahl did it, right?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-20T17:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52898>

I think your final statement is key - CAN one get out of the Ipathway?

Along the lines of thoughts can change your DNA? Guess what, goes both ways. I bet we don't know all the ways nature has set up for us to heal ourselves, and a constant mental focus on what is wrong may just be doing us as much harm as say - buying sardines in oil instead of water (or whatever minute detail people are stressing over).

I think if you are letting all of this knowledge weigh you down, instead of offering you hope and freedom - it might be time to try and live a little outside the "Worry about health" pathway and enjoy the life you DO have.

I know it's much easier for me to say, and I don't suffer from any chronic stuff like you do (yet?) but life is ultimately very short, and enjoying the life you have is the key to happyness.

### MartiD — 2012-09-20T17:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52903>

I'm recently diagnosed with Hashi's, still hunting for the right Doctor as I am not convienced the T4 only script was the right option for me. I know there is alot of controversy over the Iodine and Hashi's. Besides eating a Epi-Paleo diet best I can afford, I had also been taking Chlorella tablets. Within a few weeks I really started to feel really bad, very anxious, rapid heart rate, extrememly "hyper" feeling so I discontinued it and feel 1000% better! There is alot of "internet" talk about how increasing iodine or taking iodine supplements is not good for all Hashi's patients and I am definately one of them. I haven't had much seafood in the last 10 days but now that the Chlorella has cleared my system I am going to start adding it back and see how I feel. This is just my experience and probably is not that of everyone with Hashi's. I think you just need to figure out what works well for you.

I had mentioned on another thread that I would love to hear from thyroid people who are finding success with all of the factors...prescription of either T3, T4 alone, compounded, Armour plus CT and epi-paleo, AI Diet, etc. We have lots of people still having issues but I am hoping someone out there is finding success after more than a year using some or all of these protocols.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-20T18:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-52911>

As someone well acquainted with Hashis (5 years now) I have found both natural dessicated thyroid (NP Thyroid, Thyroid-S, and Naturethroid) as well as T3 only to be effective treatments for raising my FT3 and causing me to feel better. After finding Dr. K and the information in the blogs, forum, etc I have changed my treatment to not only the meds but including the AI seasonal epi-paleo, circadian living, balancing hormones (gotta keep getting that estrogen lowered), CT (now up to 5x/wk), etc and will add more as things are revealed.

I have read of people "getting rid" of this condition but what exactly does that mean? The antibodies are now within range? I have a friend whose TPO antibodies were over 200 four years ago and now they are just within range. Is she cured? I don't think so because one false move with her diet, lifestyle or something else that will cause inflammation. thus possibly causing her antibodies to increase. I know that I have to do all that is available to me to fix what I can and live in this moment, not where I might be a few years from now. I have super duper long telomeres so I don't want to live a long painful life. I am so much better than I was 5 years ago that I am positive I will continue to be better off as I continue on my path toward optimal.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-21T08:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53130>

> **Cavemam;53533 wrote:** I think your final statement is key - CAN one get out of the Ipathway?
>
>
>
> Along the lines of thoughts can change your DNA? Guess what, goes both ways. I bet we don't know all the ways nature has set up for us to heal ourselves, and a constant mental focus on what is wrong may just be doing us as much harm as say - buying sardines in oil instead of water (or whatever minute detail people are stressing over).
>
>
>
> I think if you are letting all of this knowledge weigh you down, instead of offering you hope and freedom - it might be time to try and live a little outside the "Worry about health" pathway and enjoy the life you DO have.
>
>
>
> I know it's much easier for me to say, and I don't suffer from any chronic stuff like you do (yet?) but life is ultimately very short, and enjoying the life you have is the key to happyness.

+1

I may or may not have Hashi's, am definitely hypothyroid and have had a cancer diagnosis. And I agree with what you've said. Using knowledge to make yourself miserable or make you feel like you are doomed is not a recipe for success. But ignorance is not bliss either. I think the point of the latest blog - as well as the specific science of it - is to point out that many diseases are growing at rapid rates and we are ignorant of their causes and etiologies. There isn't a health condition that you shouldn't take seriously. They are all progressive if you continue down the pathway that caused them. It's just that we view some of them as more grave than others. Diabetes, cigarette smoking, cancer, autoimmunity, stress... the list goes on. It can all kill you. And you can live with/overcome all of it.

Maybe our particular genetic & epigenetic make-ups and switches mean we will always be prone to certain things flaring up if we're not careful. But it doesn't have to make you miserable.

I know what being afraid is like. It sucks. But I also know what being alive is like. It's great.

So I use this information to help me live and live well. It is not my only tool. And it can't make me happy. But it can be very helpful. But it has to be empowering to be helpful. I think getting to that point is different for everyone.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-21T08:19:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-hashimotos-people.3780/post-53131>

> **MartiD;53538 wrote:** There is alot of "internet" talk about how increasing iodine or taking iodine supplements is not good for all Hashi's patients and I am definately one of them.
