# Thread: Ezekial Bread
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### debbiemikus — 2012-09-21T00:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53044>

Has anyone that is trying to follow a gluten free diet had success with this bread? I would love to try it because it is from sprouted grains and should not have the bad lectins, etc that conventional breads do. Love to hear from you! Thx

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T02:42:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53059>

Would love a discussion on why no. Since carb load is an n-1, what's the bad here. . .

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-09-21T02:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53062>

I used to eat "healthy" Ezekiel bread and cereal every day .... that stuff is addicting, and I was always hungry .... so I ate more. It was also very inflammatory for me.

Within 2 weeks of stopping use of both Ezekiel products, stiffness in my back that made getting out of bed difficult "magically" disappeared. My n-1 shows that my body does not like Ezekiel products (or any grains for that matter).

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T02:51:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53063>

> **Cavemam;53694 wrote:** Would love a discussion on why no. Since carb load is an n-1, what's the bad here. . .

Well, in a word, grains. Still has 15 g carb per slice and contains gluten and gliadin (4.8 g protein per slice). If you need to have bread, I'd experiment with coconut flour...it makes good biscuits and scones.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T03:22:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53070>

For the record, this is an academic endeavor, not a "I was thinking of eating it" thing. I like to be as accurate and informed as possible. . .

I think carbs have been done to death, almost the same for gluten. . .but not everybody has a gluten intolerance. . .so for someone without a gluten intolerance (or does that really exist?) what is the harm?

So that leaves Gliadin - I've found such conflicting information on why it's bad, what it does and if it's a quantity issue - so I was hoping for more real info other than what is quickly becoming our default answers

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T03:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53079>

\[

> **Cavemam;53705 wrote:** For the record, this is an academic endeavor, not a "I was thinking of eating it" thing. I like to be as accurate and informed as possible. . .
>
>
>
> I think carbs have been done to death, almost the same for gluten. . .but not everybody has a gluten intolerance. . .so for someone without a gluten intolerance (or does that really exist?) what is the harm?So that leaves Gliadin - I've found such conflicting information on why it's bad, what it does and if it's a quantity issue - so I was hoping for more real info other than what is quickly becoming our default answers

Well, I think there is plenty of evidence that gluten damages the gut even in people who are not symptomatic, it "abrades" epithelial cells, causing leaky gut, affecting immunity and leads to vitamin and mineral deficiency. Cordain does a good review of the literature in "The Paleo Answer".

### Lyndra — 2012-09-21T04:53:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53107>

From their website: INGREDIENTS: Organic Sprouted Whole Wheat, Filtered Water, Organic Malted Barley, Organic Sprouted Whole Millet, Organic Sprouted Whole Barley, Organic Sprouted Whole Lentils, Organic Sprouted Whole Soybeans, Organic Sprouted Whole Spelt, Fresh Yeast, Organic Wheat Gluten, Sea Salt.

I don't want something with added gluten nor soybeans. No doubt sprouting is "better" but still not worth it. I do find it interesting there's no sugar of any kind - I thought that was necessary to "feed" the yeast.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-21T21:44:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53263>

> **Lyndra;53742 wrote:**
>
> I don't want something with added gluten nor soybeans. No doubt sprouting is "better" but still not worth it. I do find it interesting there's no sugar of any kind - I thought that was necessary to "feed" the yeast.

I used to make homemade bread all the time without sugar. Believe me, the yeast absolutely loves the flour.

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T22:12:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53271>

> **Nonchalant;53898 wrote:** I used to make homemade bread all the time without sugar. Believe me, the yeast absolutely loves the flour.

Agree. My sourdough starter was just yeast, flour and water. It would take off like crazy when I fed it more flour. Plenty of sugars in the wheat all on its own.

### MartiD — 2012-09-21T22:27:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53274>

I've discovered at even "gluten free" isn't always AutoImmune acceptable because it can be filled with corn or oats. I have a recipe for break that is coconut flour, almond meal and flax seeds and eggs. Not totally ideal with the flax seeds and eggs but better than going totally without.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T04:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53357>

> **Cavemam;53705 wrote:** For the record, this is an academic endeavor, not a "I was thinking of eating it" thing. I like to be as accurate and informed as possible. . .
>
>
>
> I think carbs have been done to death, almost the same for gluten. . .but not everybody has a gluten intolerance. . .so for someone without a gluten intolerance (or does that really exist?) what is the harm?
>
>
>
> So that leaves Gliadin - I've found such conflicting information on why it's bad, what it does and if it's a quantity issue - so I was hoping for more real info other than what is quickly becoming our default answers

I believe Gliadin is the protein found in gluten. That is the killer and yes it really does exist. Ask my gut when it bloated everyday from eating the stuff. A lot of people have a gluten intolerance and don't even know it.

### janagram — 2012-09-22T06:07:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53363>

I ordered a loaf of almond bread and a loaf of coconut bread from Julien bakery. Paleo bread! It is about what you'd guess....ok smothered in almond butter....we'll see, maybe with a fried egg...

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T15:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53380>

> **MartiD;53909 wrote:** I've discovered at even "gluten free" isn't always AutoImmune acceptable because it can be filled with corn or oats. I have a recipe for break that is coconut flour, almond meal and flax seeds and eggs. Not totally ideal with the flax seeds and eggs but better than going totally without.

Marti, As far as I know, eggs are a no-no with AI unless you get tested and it says ok. Flax seeds aren't that good either. You might not be helping yourself in the long run by feeding those desires for bread. I would find something else that really pleases you that you know for certain you can have and use that as your crutch. What about coconut milk (use only the thick stuff, not the water) with pumpkin, spices and stevia all blended up and left to chill. Then eat it like a pumpkin mousse. That might even be better than this bread.

### PrimalPam — 2012-09-22T16:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-53392>

The problems I had prior to moving to epi-Paleo was in trying too many of the Frankenfoods. The Atkins bars, the shakes, the "gluten-free breads". I still had the mindset of "eat all you want and still lose weight!! Amazinggggg!!!".

I've just moved away from any of these types of products. And anything that has "Bread" in the title is an automatic delete for me. It just keeps my mind....clean. Like, I don't have to worry whether it's AI friendly or not. I.Just.Don't.Eat.It.Anymore.

### James Gerard — 2018-10-01T19:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-252086>

This is my first post on here. This is an old conversation, but if anyone still has any ideas about eating ezekiel bread.. it is probably a good idea to stay away from sprouted wheat grain. There are substances in sprouted wheat that can lead to cancer by causing aneuploidy in liver cells. Benzoxazinoids are the culprit. Here is a link to the abstract. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16644106](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16644106)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-10-02T19:40:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ezekial-bread.3793/post-252180>

I would not eat it in any season.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Flux question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-09-21T02:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53057>

Hello...any of you flux users, ever had a problem with the software? Mine doesn't turn colors, and I can't uninstall or delete the app because it's telling me the app is OPEN when it's not working. Ugh!

Has this ever happened to anyone else? I can't seem to get an answer from the company....

### mafixit — 2012-09-21T03:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53066>

Mine seems to be working fine.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T03:32:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53077>

I had that on an old computer - I think since it is running all the time it can suck down too much mem or speed and won't go right.

### Michael — 2012-09-21T08:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53134>

> **hope2conceive;53692 wrote:** Hello...any of you flux users, ever had a problem with the software? Mine doesn't turn colors, and I can't uninstall or delete the app because it's telling me the app is OPEN when it's not working. Ugh!
>
>
>
> Has this ever happened to anyone else? I can't seem to get an answer from the company....

Yeah, it would have to be running on start-up. So it will always be going in the background: I guess it would never be _not_ working, in that sense, although it might not be doing much most of the time.

And because of that you couldn't uninstall it until you explicitly quit it.

I have it on my Mac. I guess you'll be using Windows. You could perhaps try re-booting. Alternatively, you could call up a list of the running processes, see if you can identify the process and kill it from there. Then you could re-start the program manually.

Control + Alt + Delete to call up the Task Manager, isn't it? Someone here will probably know the name of the process, although it may have an obvious enough name.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-21T10:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53137>

go to device manager or go to the start up menu by typing msconfig and just disable it from starting at the beginning. Afterwards then delete/uninstall it. This is assuming you have a Windows computer and not a MAC.

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-23T20:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53656>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53762>

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-24T22:33:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-53892>

Screens are cheap these days and the latest Linux distros, such as the Linux Mint I'm using, are very slick and easy to use. Only a few mouse clicks were needed to install and set up Skype and Skype recording for my upcoming consult with Dr. K. If only getting my labs done was as easy...

### Michael — 2012-09-25T08:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-54064>

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-25T09:29:19+0300

### KetoWarrior — 2012-11-19T08:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-question.3795/post-65287>

>

>
> I know this is a very long shot, but I thought I'd ask, anyway.

After two months, I understand I'm reanimating a zombie thread and apologize but thought the usefulness of this info might outweigh the breech of forum etiquette.

I run dual-screen setup on Debian testing with XFCE and KDE window environments. I could not get F.Lux to work properly but have had great results with [Redshift](http://http://lifehacker.com/5567172/redshift-makes-your-screen-easier-on-the-eyes-at-night) which does the same thing and is available for a number of distributions. See [http://jonls.dk/redshift/](http://http://jonls.dk/redshift/)

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-19T11:47:31+0200

# Thread: Who's Taking Alpha Lipoic Acid and How Much?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mambo — 2012-09-21T03:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-53092>

I've been taking 600mg of Alpha Lipoic Acid (ALA) daily for 2 years for my neuropathy (diabetes-related and suspected carpal tunnel syndrome). My neurologist at the time suggested that I could take more, if I wanted to. I also read about a clinical trial in Germany where neuropathy was rolled back somewhat after administering mega doses (don't know how much) of ALA intravenously.

After recent flare-ups, I upped my dose to 900mg for a week and then tried to go up to 1,200mg. Then I came upon an interview with Dr. Burton Berkson, who pioneered the use of Low Dose Naltrexone (LDN) and ALA in cancer treatment. [Dr. Berkson says that at very high doses of ALA, monkeys have died because their mitochondria "fell apart". He says 90 mg/kg could kill a primate. As for humans, he's never gone above 15mg per kilogram.](http://http://www.prescription2000.com/Interview-Transcripts/2010-01-22-burt-berkson-lipoic-acid-naltrexone-transcript.html)

So if you weigh 160 lbs., your upper limit is 160lbs. = 72.6kg; 15 x 72.6 = 1,089mg. That's less than 1,200mg! If you are female and weigh 110 lbs., the upper limit is 750mg. As for the lethal dose that could wreck one's mitochondria, it's 4,500mg.

Now, that seems very hard to believe. ALA comes in capsules of either 300 or 600mg per pill. If true, many people are dosing above what the Father of ALA says is the ceiling.

So what then is the optimal dose if you want to treat neuropathy? Cancer? Autoimmune diseases? Anyone gone above 1,200mg and seen any side effects?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T04:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-53108>

Well I had no idea about this.... I think I take 500mg twice a day. For months now.

### JerryB6768 — 2012-09-25T23:31:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-54250>

It depends on the dosage form of the ALA product. With tablets and powder-filled capsules, the ALA is poorly absorbed and taking above 600 mg is probably not a concern. I personally use a solubilzed product called ThioGel that has a 200-300% higher bioavailability than powdered products.

### shah78 — 2012-09-25T23:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-54251>

Question for jerry and Mambo? Do you have amalgam fillings? I'm chelating mercury out of my brain (Cutler Protocol) and I can't take more than 50 mg per day and I have to divide the dose into 6 mg. servings every three hours or I'll get wicked side effects. How do you take such massive amounts.I'm utterly amazed!

### mambo — 2012-10-11T01:24:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57467>

Go to Swanson or iHerb and any ALA you see come in 300mg or 600mg per capsule. Where do you purchase 50mg doses?

> **shah78;54957 wrote:** Question for jerry and Mambo? Do you have amalgam fillings? I'm chelating mercury out of my brain (Cutler Protocol) and I can't take more than 50 mg per day and I have to divide the dose into 6 mg. servings every three hours or I'll get wicked side effects. How do you take such massive amounts.I'm utterly amazed!

### shah78 — 2012-10-11T01:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57468>

@MAMBO: 21st century pills from swanson.

### mambo — 2012-10-11T07:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whos-taking-alpha-lipoic-acid-and-how-much.3796/post-57533>

I guess I don't know the difference between regular Alpha Lipoic Acid and R-Fraction Alpha Lipoic Acid. I always took the regular one ranging from 300 to 600mg. I'm now on 300mg. Did it help my neuropathy? Don't think so. But then supplements are supposed to let things not get worse, not better. So it's preventive, not ameliorative. By that standard, I really can't judge the effectiveness of Alpha Lipoic Acid. I only recently heard about its use for cancer patients and the combo therapy with LDN.

> **shah78;58778 wrote:** @MAMBO: 21st century pills from swanson.

# Thread: Cold Tub options
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### edwardfries@netcommander.com — 2012-09-21T08:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53133>

I'm new here and I really want to get started on the cold thermogenesis. I tried to search for a topic like this so I'm sorry if this was already answered because I didn't think of the right key words to try. The house I'm in has showers with things that look like tubs, but are not really tubs since to lay flat your chest is at the upper end of the sides on the supposed tubs so immersion of the torso is not possible in either shower. I guess this is from cheaper installs in houses where people mostly shower these days. At the coldest the water never hits less than 70 degrees so a cold shower is not an option either. I would gladly by an outdoor cold tub but I found only one on the internet that was specifically designed to be a cold tub.

[http://www.coldtub.com/](http://http://www.coldtub.com/)

It looks incredible and I was thinking if it was maybe 5 thousand I would do it, 10 thousand would be hurting but possible. It was twice that expensive so I don't know how easy it may be to make a jacuzzi into a cold tub or what other options you guys may have but I would love to hear them

PS I live in Texas so it doesn't get real cold here except maybe 2 weeks out of the year, that's why I was going for something that could cool the water instead of buying lots and lots of ice daily.

### Ray — 2012-09-21T19:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53230>

People are buying the Rubbermaid stock tanks and using ice from their freezer to bring down the temperature as necessary. Some have rigged up refrigeration units, but it seems like just throwing some ice in would be cheaper and not too hard.

Here is a thread on this site with some info: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?990-Stock-tank-tips-amp-tricks](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?990-Stock-tank-tips-amp-tricks)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-21T20:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-tub-options.3800/post-53237>

I like this tub. It's like a hot tub for the cold....a very large ice chest in the shape of a circle. I want one. I'm still struggling to get my whole body in the tub. It won't fit! SO I alternate baths one day it's my torso and upper legs and the next day it's legs and lower torso. Not happy about this method...

# Thread: Question about oysters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-21T15:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53150>

Oysters have never been my thing before. I have always loved seafood, but I stuck mostly to fish, shrimp, crab, etc. I know I desperately need the ugly little oysters, but have a few questions about eating them.

Since raw is so much better, is it ok to eat already shucked oysters raw? I'm thinking of these: [http://www.ilovebluesea.com/shuck-oysters-tub?acc=a5771bce93e200c36f7cd9dfd0e5deaa](http://http://www.ilovebluesea.com/shuck-oysters-tub?acc=a5771bce93e200c36f7cd9dfd0e5deaa)

I ordered them before and they seemed to be of good quality (based on my limited knowledge), but I was lightly cooking most of them. I think I did eat a few raw, but then I read something on here about not eating frozen oysters raw. These were never frozen though, so I just want some opinions on what all you seafood experts think.

I do plan on freezing these for later use though as it's a good bit cheaper to order 3 containers and get free shipping. So, what is the opinion then on whether or not they can be eaten raw after I freeze them? Last time I put about 10-20 in a small baggie, froze them and then just thawed and ate throughout a day or two.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53176>

My advice is to "start" your oyster journey with freshly shucked SMALL ones. I think this time of year you can get littler ones. The big once can be overwhelming in the "omg I'm eating snot"department. The little ones, easy to suck down from the shell without chewing.

My fish guy told me with lots of emphasis. . .DONT EAT FROZEN RAW. I didn't ask if it was just a foodie thing, or what - just took that advice to heart.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T17:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53184>

Is that a safety thing? Or texture? Because most of the time, freezing is recommended for eating other foods raw, to destroy parasites and such.

I have ZERO access to fresh oysters, that I am aware of.

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53185>

Yeah, not a good idea to eat pre-shucked previously frozen oysters raw. The idea is that raw ones you shuck yourself have their own liquor in them and are super fresh, where as the frozen ones have been sitting around intermingling with a bunch of other oysters' liquor and just aren't the freshest thing ever. It's kind of akin to eating fresh home-made beef tartar or carpaccio (really good, not likely to make you sick) vs. eating raw ground beef that came from a processing place 3 states away (gods only know what's actually in it, not a good idea to eat raw).

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53186>

> **MamaGrok;53819 wrote:** Is that a safety thing? Or texture? Because most of the time, freezing is recommended for eating other foods raw, to destroy parasites and such.
>
>
>
> I have ZERO access to fresh oysters, that I am aware of.

Safety. You can tell a sick / dead oyster when you shuck it. If it was a machine or a person who maybe didn't care so much when they were shucking a whole bunch of oysters to meet a daily quota, you never know quite what you might be getting.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T17:37:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53189>

I've been eating oysters like crazy for a few months and my spectracell just showed a very good level of zinc!! No zinc supps at all at any time

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T17:44:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53194>

So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?

I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?

I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:46:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53195>

> **MamaGrok;53829 wrote:** So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?
>
>
>
> I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?
>
>
>
> I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

I have never had an oyster sickness - and I eat a ton of oysters, always have. In restaurants I order when they shuck them there.

I have learned that stores like WF and even seafood vendors have to have the damn things flown in. I think the idea is as long as they are alive you are good to go.

### ealachan — 2012-09-21T17:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53199>

> **MamaGrok;53829 wrote:** So if the idea is never to eat an oyster raw that you don't shuck yourself, I suppose that's the same as saying never to eat them raw in a restaurant?
>
>
>
> I suppose having a good source (like the equivalent of US Wellness Meats) would be an exception, then?
>
>
>
> I don't avoid beef carpaccio or raw oysters in restaurants, and never get sick.

Well, no. The idea is that, in a restaurant, you trust that they know what they're doing and they're preparing things correctly. I eat sashimi and raw oysters in restaurants and have never had a problem, but I'm theoretically paying extra to have someone who knows what they're doing preparing them for me.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-21T18:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53203>

> I think the idea is as long as they are alive you are good to go.

Aha! Now I understand! Thank you! So if you eat raw oysters, they should be alive shortly before prepared and eaten?

Smoked oysters in a can would be fine, then, since I assume they're not raw?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T18:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53206>

> **MamaGrok;53838 wrote:** Aha! Now I understand! Thank you! So if you eat raw oysters, they should be alive shortly before prepared and eaten?
>
>
>
> Smoked oysters in a can would be fine, then, since I assume they're not raw?

yups! ....

### kathylu — 2012-09-21T20:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53238>

Oysters need to be freshly harvested and shucked and kept very cold (on ice) to eat them raw. Never from the jars of previously shucked raw oysters! I'll only eat them raw from a restaurant I trust and on the few occasions when we dredge for oysters ourselves in the winter (I live near the Chesapeake Bay...great oyster beds!).

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-21T20:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53245>

I buy the Ekone brand shucked (but not frozen) oysters from Whole Foods and eat at least 30oz (3 jars) of them a week--always raw. I also eat 3 lbs/week farmed rainbow trout or wild Alaskan salmon from Sprouts sashimi-style raw, even after days of open air dry-aging in my refrigerator right next to my grassfed chuck steak that gets the same treatment (and gets eaten the same way--raw). I have never gotten sick from eating all this raw seafood (or beef or offal).

I have, however, gotten violently ill from well-cooked supermarket ground beef before.

Google "raw paleo" and check out the forums of people who have eaten raw or raw paleo for decades and never get sick. It's not about the presence of germs or viruses, it's about the state of your body and gut flora.

Perhaps I've just gotten extremely lucky with my raw seafood (and raw beef and raw offal) up to now, perhaps my gut flora is much healthier since the ground beef episode in my pre-Kruse days (blech.....I will never touch ground beef again, ever...\*vomit\*), or maybe my zonulin-powered leaky gut is absorbing ocean viruses as we speak to give me and my future progeny a wider library of epigenetic programs to choose from to adapt to coming environments.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T21:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53257>

I recently had food poisoning. I don't know what item was to blame. I had a gag reaction to everything basically that was protein afterwards. But I'm noticing that the thing I'm having the most avoidance right now is not the fish, raw fish, raw frozen oysters that I ate that day...... it's the cooked liver I had for dinner.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T06:21:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53365>

I will stick to eating oysters raw only if they are alive...not going to risk the pre-shucked ones. That would have been the only way our predecessors ate them. Not like a mammoth, that you'd have to store and eat more slowly ;-)

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-22T08:37:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53369>

Your concern is understandable, and certainly prudent. Though as many raw foodists would point out, at the time period Epi-Paleo is targeting, our ancestors wouldn't have eaten cooked anything, ever.

If whole, unshucked oysters weren't cost prohibitive at the amounts we eat, I would certainly be eating them instead. All I financially have access to is the Ekone brand canned (plastic jar, actually), but they seem very fresh. Ekone is a small company with less than 50 employees, so I trust their QC far more than a bigger, higher-volume producer. I'm lucky with that, living in SoCal and the oysters getting here overnight from Washington state, and never frozen. If you live further inland, then getting shucked oysters fresh might not be possible.

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-22T15:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53379>

Just checked their website and they only ship smoked produce if you order on line...lots of other ingredients in the smoked oysters so it won't work for me. You ARE lucky living so close to them.

I'm finding that the cheapest oysters I can buy fresh are 1.25 each. But it adds up if you really want to chow down on them 2 or 3 times a week. Still, Vermont is in a pretty good spot as far as seafood with Maine just over the mountains, so I won't complain.

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T16:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53397>

> **CuiviÃ«;54004 wrote:** Your concern is understandable, and certainly prudent. Though as many raw foodists would point out, at the time period Epi-Paleo is targeting, our ancestors wouldn't have eaten cooked anything, ever.

True, but our ancestors would have been cracking their own oysters open right on the beach and eating them straight from the shells, not getting them pre-shucked from plastic containers that had sat around under undetermined conditions for an undetermined amount of time.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T19:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53431>

I wish I had better access to live oysters....I would love to eat them every day. I do buy frozen oysters and eat them very lightly steamed as often as possible.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-24T19:05:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53822>

> **Patty Cakes;54078 wrote:** Have you tried their smoked oysters?

No, haven't. My whole all-raw kick was about concern over AGEs and other carcinogen by-products of cooking, at up until recently I was eating 1-2lbs of grilled/seared steak a day. So now that I have gone raw I have avoided anything smoked.

It sounds delicious, though. I LOVE the taste of seared and smoked meat. Alas, for now I need to avoid such things. Perhaps every now and then as a treat.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-24T19:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53834>

> **ealachan;54036 wrote:** True, but our ancestors would have been cracking their own oysters open right on the beach and eating them straight from the shells, not getting them pre-shucked from plastic containers that had sat around under undetermined conditions for an undetermined amount of time.

Again, good point. But this is the only financially un-insane access to regular amounts of oysters, and given that raw seafood is best for promoting the gut flora response we want for Epi-Paleo/EPCOTx (as well as the fact I love raw), I'm willing to take the risk to eat them raw. Whole Foods has good storage/handling practices (or so they claim) and I always buy the jars that are the most covered in ice and avoid any that look oxidized (turned pinkish instead of the usual grey/white).

Raw is still more evolutionarily appropriate, but like you said, with modern food access realities, there are added elements that we can't control. It will have to be a personal decision.

Personally, I have never gotten sick since going raw, nor since eating both steak and seafood seared/blue from quality sources. I have gotten very sick from well-cooked food from conventional sources (traditional supermarkets, restaurants). FWIW, I wouldn't dare eat anything raw--seafood or otherwise--from a conventional grocery store.

Pre-existing gut health may also be a factor. I was strict paleo for nearly 2 years before I went rare, 3 years with lots of pro-biotic usage before going all-raw.

I had some cooked wild Alaskan salmon last night and got really nauseated, borderline having to vomit. Never once, ever, got sick eating it raw. I fear the heat oxidized the PUFAs (including our sought-after DHA) and made it rancid.

Eating this particular brand from this particular store raw happens to work for me. YMMV with different brands, store practices, geographic location, or gut health.

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T20:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-oysters.3802/post-53839>

Sure, I was just playing devil's advocate, mostly. It's an annoying habit I picked up from my husband...

FWIW, I've never gotten sick from eating anything raw either (knock on wood). I routinely munch on pieces of raw (supermarket) beef while I'm prepping steaks for dinner, and I always have my steak blue in the middle as well. I also eat raw uncured bacon sometimes (weird I know, but I like it for some strange reason, always have), and have no problem tasting my meatloaf, meatball or sausage mix raw to make sure I like the seasoning, as well. Aside from undercooked salmon and rare tuna steak, I've yet to do raw fish at home, but I have no issues with it in a restaurant setting and would happily make my own sashimi at home if I had half a clue what I was doing!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Food allergies, desensitization, reactivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T15:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53155>

I thought I'd post an interesting story and see what people say about it.

A friend of mine's son is allergic to peanuts, and she is very strict about keeping him (and her house, and his classroom) nut free. He also has significant environmental allergies, and other lower level food allergies (egg, tree nuts, maybe more). The boy eats an "upgraded" SAD. Cereal for breakfast, regular lunch/dinners, although she does her best to buy organically, and most of their meats come from pastured animals.

1.5 years ago, she began the boy on a peanut desensitization trial program. Every day, he consumed an increasing (dose increases every 2 weeks, symptom dependant) amount of peanut, from 3mg/day all the way up to 3 peanuts/day. The goal was never to have him eating peanuts again, but to be able to handle living in a non-nut-free environment - handle cross contamination with reduced risk of anaphylactic reaction. He did pretty well on the protocol, but was never able to get beyond 2 peanuts/day without having reactions after each dose (small reactions, but reactions none the less). The protocol had him eating tons of foods (starches) before the dose, and stay still and quiet for 2 hours afterwards.

I have been thinking about this a great deal. I think they want the kids still and quiet to keep cortisol levels from increasing. Doesn't increasing cortisol increase gut leakiness? She found that even playing video games was too much during this time and would lead to more reactivity. What is the rationale behind having them eat so much starchy food beforehand? She said the straches seem to work like a barrier in the stomach, buffering the dose from the gut.

How much would an epi-paleo diet improve the success rate for these kinds of kids? I suppose all other foods put the cells in a more inflammatory path, leading to chronic oxidation. This boy was not able to move past 2 peanuts, and has stayed that dose for 6ish months. Another boy had been doing the maintenance dose for many months but had an anaphylactic reaction one evening from the dose. He had sustained an injury while wrestling earlier in the day, and had taken ibuprofen. NSAIDs also increase gut leakiness, I think, which would lead to more peanut getting through the brush border..? I wonder if having good HDL levels would also be a predictor of better tolerance? Vitamin D levels would also be important, but I'm not sure I exactly know why, except that it's an immune system modulator.

I'm just trying to put the biology pieces together here to have a solid hypothesis about what's going on, things the parents could do to help the situation, and why those things would help.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-21T17:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53177>

Not much input here but wanted to say that playing video games imo is one of the worse things to do for managing cortisol so I"m not really surprised he didn't do well with that. People \*think\* they are sitting still and resting, but they don't take into account the stuff that happens not so obviously while playing these things. Lots of cortisol and adrenaline flowing from the game and wanting to win and stuff and those stress hormones have nowhere to go b/c your sitting still, a very unnatural combination for our bodies.... we're supposed to move when stress hormones are up.

The other thing is that if the boy had an injury that required pain med treatment that also means his cortisol/adrenaline was already elevated that day, so the peanut dose could have pushed things over the limit, pain meds or not.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-21T19:11:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergies-desensitization-reactivity.3803/post-53218>

> **Souldanzer;53812 wrote:** Not much input here but wanted to say that playing video games imo is one of the worse things to do for managing cortisol so I"m not really surprised he didn't do well with that. People \*think\* they are sitting still and resting, but they don't take into account the stuff that happens not so obviously while playing these things. Lots of cortisol and adrenaline flowing from the game and wanting to win and stuff and those stress hormones have nowhere to go b/c your sitting still, a very unnatural combination for our bodies.... we're supposed to move when stress hormones are up.
>
>
>
> The other thing is that if the boy had an injury that required pain med treatment that also means his cortisol/adrenaline was already elevated that day, so the peanut dose could have pushed things over the limit, pain meds or not.

I fully agree with you here about the exercise injury already elevating his cortisol and therefore increasing his gut leakiness.

What I wonder about is how helpful these kinds of desensitization treatments are. They have the potential to improve quality of life (child can now fly on an airplane, etc), but I wonder about the biological cost. Is the child now living "even more" in the oxidation pathway, with increased inflammation and pregnenelone being shunted to cortisol, etc? Or after a time, does the immune system learn to accept what it used to attack so ferociously? What other markers could be used to screen a child as a good (or not appropriate) candidate for this kind of protocol? What supplements (or foods) would increase the likelihood of success in this kind of a program. Nobody wants to feed their child 2 peanuts everyday to improve quality of life now, only to find out later on that bigger problems have been the "side effect." It's a difficult place to be as a parent.

At the same time, this mother has said she would feed her son fish everyday, and that at one point he was eating 2 cans of sardines/day, and now he refuses fish of all kind. With an allergic kid, this is very difficult. I have a child who refuses a lot of different foods (mostly fruits/veggies), and for years I would push on it (somewhat). Later, intradermal allergy testing showed he reacted to a lot of them. He is very sensitive, and intuitively knows what he should eat, and what he shouldn't (also gravitates heavily to fish and beef, not oysters, sadly). I try to respect what he himself knows, but it would be much more difficult it the foods he was refusing were the very ones I thought might stand a chance to heal him (the seafoods).

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-11T16:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-57587>

I did some snooping and testing, it looks like when someone drops out - or opens a new window and double opens the webcast, it leaves them up as a Guest. . . .so I'm unsure if we had unannounced guests last time, but there is a possibility we didn't. I'm a little dissapointed frankly. I'll show a little more skin this time and see if it helps our hit counts.

At this point, I don't think we are popular enough to need to worry about security - but let's keep investigating better formats. I didn't see a lot of hands waving excited to do a full multi-person video conference, and once we get more than 10 people it becomes a tad ordealish (read gotta pay). I do think there is probably a BETTER medium than spreecast, as the chat window is so small.

I liked this as it took me all of 1 min to set up and it's intuitive, anybody can use/run it. Seems like really good platform in general, no real techy glitches and it's free!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-14T18:36:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58121>

> **BJK77;59554 wrote:** A real campfire + Malbec is where this needs to go . . . no degradation! A live group meet-up would be great one of these days.

I agree. Since that isn't something likely to become a frequent enough community engagement - how about we try for a.m. or mid-day chat next time? A cold bath chat?

I think being able to "see" is a nice aspect of the process. . . but we have to go with what people are comfortable with and perhaps the "both" option does seem to be the best of our choices.

Personally, I like the laughs and jokes as much as the info - so I kinda do like the format of speakers + chat. . It keeps the flow with fewer awkward pauses imho .Iinput from others? My thought is when it's live the fun stuff is that, fun without really harming or mis-interpreting the message. I mean, if we can't joke about all of us old ladies and sex and the merits of cougarific, what else is there!

I don't want to move into territory of being disrespectful to anybody talking, so I think there has to be a certain comfort level of "we are among friends" kind of vibe going on.

And we need more of the guys to join us.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-14T19:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58137>

I agree on the live meet up...that would be such a dream!

Not just for the campfire at night but also just cooking and eating together with folks who GET it about eating....and don't have to have cakes, donuts and other junk food around to have a great time!! We could hike together, jump in the lake or river together (most lakes and rivers near where I live, you're not allowed to ugh!)

Wouldn't it be fun to share recipes and other practices we do/use...Maybe even have a real retreat....practicing implementing stuff that would benefit everyone on this path....especially for newbies who get to feel empowered in making changes (if they are ready)...but also just share on a deeper level too....

I can see it now....a real epi-paleo retreat living in tune with nature at a camp or resort....where the food and lifestyle is getting us closer to optimal one meal and one CT at a time...we all go to bed with the sun and up with it too...sleeping in tents or even outside.

I would TOTALLY love that...

As to time for future chats, I too think doing it earlier is a great idea. I actually ended up completely darkening my PC and just listening to the people on camera; I missed the chat part but I am so early in the leptin Rx, I didn't want to jeopardize (or as little as possible) my (hopeful) progress on the circadian front, LOL. Though I have to say - Cavemam, Kathylu, Gretchen, KiwiLauren, y'all looked AMAZING!!!! And it was really great to 'see' you. Here's to hope for thick, beautiful hair!!

You have NO idea how great it is to know you guys are out there too....though I am SO new here, I feel like you guys are my tribe and I so love the inspiration y'all give me to push myself (and not be called crazy for the way I eat!).....woohoo!!

### JanSz — 2012-10-14T22:31:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58182>

> **Lyndra;59617 wrote: Anybody remember last night what Dr. K said a low alkaline phosphatase reading indicates? I know it signals poor libido but did he mention a mechanism?
>
>
>
> > **hope2conceive;59620 wrote:** Lyndra, low ALP means your zinc is low. I only know because I have had this  and zinc is needed for lots of stuff, including libido. He just posted something on the forum about this...will see if I can copy in here...
> >
> >
> > Lyndra wrote:
>
> **
>
> > Thanks, just found this on another thread:
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > Jack Kruse said:
> > >
> > > low alkaline phosphatase is a sign of a loss of Zn and other brain specific nutrients......it also is tied to obesity, osteoporosis and hypothyroidism........anything that lack the brain specific nutrients in food destroys signaling in the thyroid and in the brain........that is the physiologic linkage to both organs. A human brain can not form without great functioning thyroid that is filled with brain specific nutrients.
>
>
>
>
>
> Alkaline Phosphatase (25.00 - 150.00)
>
> mid point average(25, 150)=87.5
>
> range (150-25)=125
>
> 25% below mid point =87.5-0.25\*87.5=65.625
>
> 25% above mid point =87.5+0.25\*87.5=109.375
>
> dr Kane flags items +-25% of range

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-15T00:53:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58203>

I'd like to put three things out for discussion re: upcoming group chat/modern campfire calls:

1) As others have stated, let's try to find a time when it's daylight for most everyone. When I do consults with Dr Kruse it is usually mid morning here and early afternoon there. Are there folks from Europe trying to get on these calls? That will add another dimension. But perhaps we can alternate times. Something like a Saturday afternoon for the US folks maybe?

2) I would like to encourage everyone to take a turn on camera who isn't feeling too shy to do this. I realise some do not want to be on camera at all and that's totally OK. At the same time, I think there are some who would be OK with being on camera but don't necessarily feel they have something 'special' to say. What encouraged me to get on camera (all I did was ask a question!) was how lovely it was to connect with the faces of others. I'd love to meet more of you that way.

3) I know in advance of our last call many people submitted ideas/topics/questions they wanted addressed. I'm really keen to hear the opinions of others, but I'd like to make sure these things are prioritised while also being flexible with our time (and making sure we can joke around -like Cavemam, this aspect is the most appealing part to me... I like connecting with you all in a way that isn't all about serious stuff and heavy thinking). I think we are all going to default to listening to whatever Dr Kruse has to say and I always gain so much from him. With these calls, I want to equally hear from everyone else, too. When Dr Kruse is there I totally want him to be answering our questions (if he's OK with that), but I want the content to be driven by the whole group (some of which was submitted in advance), not just by what's on Dr Kruse's mind. I hope that doesn't sound ungrateful. I am aiming to have something different here with these calls, something that is both social and learning. Does that make sense?

Please feel free to disagree. I'd like to hear everyone's thoughts.

I love that....I totally love the social/fun piece of it (though I'm still so new) too....though I also crave the learning too

### kathylu — 2012-10-15T16:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58298>

I like the idea of a meet-up too! Probably impractical for most of us though. Colorado in the summer is a real draw...I'd try to make it. I could also visit my cousins in Longmont, so there's extra incentive.

I can see two major results from these chats. One is the support and camaraderie formed when friends get together to talk. It's really nice to be able to put a face on the people who post at the forum. Also, the support is great. It is wonderful to be able to discuss the details (humor and frustrations) of this lifestyle with people who really get it!

The second result could be enhancing our understanding of the science behind the epi-paleo lifestyle. I have a suggestion about this. If you have a question, be willing to come on screen and discuss it. Also, do your homework first. If you have a question about metformin (for example), research metformin. Understand how it functions in the body and what biochemical pathways it affects. You may answer your own question that way, but if not, you will be prepared to discuss the details in a meaningful way.

I like the idea of submitting general topics in advance...that way we can all do our homework! Also, I noticed that when Jack joined us, the energy of the chat changed, people were more reluctant to speak. That's understandable, since we are all a little in awe of Dr. K, but I would still like to see the chat be a discussion among us all. So maybe we could all look at where that feeling comes from and work to reduce it.

### kathylu — 2012-10-15T16:25:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58300>

This is probably not the right place to post this, but I complained (again!) about my trouble with cycloset and nausea. Gretchen suggested that I try to eat more fat at breakfast and Dr. K seconded that. It's funny, but that hadn't occurred to me, I was actually reducing the fat in my diet because of the nausea (it just didn't appeal to me). Anyway, I did an about-face and started to eat a seafood meal drenched in paleo mayo (olive oil) and sure enough, it helped! I guess sometimes we have to get out of our own way.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-15T17:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58316>

I'm loving all this feedback on the chat! I hope everyone feels comfortable saying what they think here. Please do it, even if you are uncomfortable!!

I think one of the things sub-optimal in our world today is people don't seem to be able to communicate and disagree and offer/take/reject others opinions and feedback in a respectful manner that allows for real connection.

The strength of any community and relationship is how they handle disagreement. On the internets this usually ends in a flame-war situation, so I'm excited that we seem to be on the right path.

So please offer your opinion and feelings - we won't all be agreeing with you. we will have different opinions - and I think we can navigate those things and get past them.

### curves64 — 2012-10-15T18:56:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58330>

I am salivating at the chance to have regular "in person" discussions. I'm a total extrovert so communicating without being able to see, hear, taste and touch you feels unnatural for me. This is the first forum I've ever been remotely attracted to but I feel like I didn't read the rule book, and don't know how to communicate in this way. Most of the time I hang back rather than participate. So, please count me in, and let me know if I can help facilitate in any way. I love the intelligence, compassion and open-mindedness in which you all explore these ideas.

### curves64 — 2012-10-15T19:20:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58339>

> **Cavemam;59843 wrote:** How Ironic! Curves, welcome to introvert world! I think by nature of this format, it attracts a ton of introverts and seems odd to extroverts. Now you can take that uncomfortable feeling and have a little connection with the next introvert you are around in person - we are uncomfortable in the "in person"!

You have no idea how hard that lesson has landed. I want to reach out and hug every introvert I meet, but you all probably wouldn't like that -- am I right? LOL. Give me a room full of people any day, and I'll make 10 new friends. Here on the forum, I feel completely vulnerable and uncomfortable. It made me think of my daughter and me. She is an introvert with respect to the way she processes information. I'd want to "talk things over," and she'd want to be left alone to process things by herself. Made my head hurt until I figured out there is no right or wrong. we just approach things from a different perspective.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-15T20:39:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58369>

I see a couple of purposes in the chats. One as kathylu & shijin suggest, using it as a chance to have an in-depth conversation about a health topic. That means studying in advance and being prepared. The other purpose is building the "tribe" as Cavemam put it. For me, that's just random chatter that isn't too scripted so there's no "performance anxiety" about whether you know enough to have the floor and speak. Can we satisfy both needs in one chat? That certainly happens in real life (imagine sitting around the campfire and sometimes you're deadly serious and a moment later laughing till you hurt).

mtg only were a meeting talking about fuelingyourferrari and what were doing to get to optimal real using its not a destination but a journey. Very way of the peaceful warrior w/o the veganism

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-15T23:59:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58416>

You asked for respectful disagreement, Mam, so here I go out on a ledge....

I actually don't think our self-organised calls are the place for Dr Kruse to air his complaints. Every one of us reads everything he writes on the forum and blog. If he wants to complain about things, we will all read and listen. For me, the calls are about mutual-support and shared-learning. They are without a hierarchy... we can all be on camera (or not), we can all ask questions (or not), we can all give answers (or not). One of us is not more qualified than any other, though we each have strengths and weaknesses. This is not aimed as criticism of Dr Kruse in any way, but I think if you join one of our calls it should be because you want to learn and you want to connect with others.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-16T00:02:47+0300

### curves64 — 2012-10-19T00:03:07+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-59049>

Slightly disconcerting!

# Thread: Tell me about your liver.   Not the one you eat (I hope).
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T17:26:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53182>

I've seen posts about monitoring liver function. I'm clueless on this. Discuss?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-09-21T17:39:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53190>

AST and ALT are serum tests that can help you monitor your liver function. elevated levels can be signs of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease or simply show the liver is not functioning at optimum levels.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-21T20:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53242>

There is also the liver detox phase 1 & 2 testing to dtermine liver functionality in detoxification. I know nothing about this aspect either. Anyone please share.

### AKMan — 2012-09-21T21:48:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53264>

ALT and AST seem to be a standard lab. Back in my SAD days, these were both elevated slightly out of range. Ranges for ALT are 30-65 and AST are 50-136.

As I progressed further into metabolic syndrome, gaining weight, my triglycerides went up to 400's, TSH was 45, ALT was 100, AST was 150.

I went for an ultrasound of my liver, and they said I had non-alcoholic liver disease (NALD). I was on meds for trigs, chol, bp, hypothyroid at the time. Nothing changed until I started eating paleo--all this went away within a year of ditching grains, sugar, veg oil. ALT now 48, AST now 20.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-21T21:50:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53265>

> **AKMan;53899 wrote:** ALT and AST seem to be a standard lab. Back in my SAD days, these were both elevated slightly out of range. Ranges for ALT are 30-65 and AST are 50-136.
>
>
>
> As I progressed further into metabolic syndrome, gaining weight, my triglycerides went up to 400's, TSH was 45, ALT was 100, AST was 150.
>
>
>
> I went for an ultrasound of my liver, and they said I had non-alcoholic liver disease (NALD). I was on meds for trigs, chol, bp, hypothyroid at the time. Nothing changed until I started eating paleo--all this went away within a year of ditching grains, sugar, veg oil. ALT now 48, AST now 20.

Woah! Now that is some amazing info, so nice to see success!

### AKMan — 2012-09-21T23:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53296>

> **Cavemam;53900 wrote:** Woah! Now that is some amazing info, so nice to see success!

I knew I had a liver problem because my liver actually hurt. I could feel it when I was sitting, and I couldn't sleep on my stomach or right side. I figured it was liver cancer or something, so I just ignored it since I didn't want chemo...crazy, I know, but that's how men think. Glad it was just diet-related and easily reversed.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-09-22T01:46:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53330>

Actually AST and ALT are not measures of liver function. They are enzymes AST (aspartate amino transferase) and ALT (alanine amino transferase) normally in the liver cells. Cell damage leads to leakage so the serum levels rise. Liver function can be arrived at by looking at levels of serum proteins, ammonia level, or a bromosulphalein excretion test.

### Joann — 2012-09-22T03:07:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53345>

alkaline phosphatase is another enzyme looked at for liver

### Stavity — 2012-09-23T18:58:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53615>

From my understanding the liver doesn't feel pain, but if enlarged it can lay on nerves. I am getting tested for HCV next week. I hope with all I have been doing the tests come out well. I know I feel better since eating seafood twice a day. No pain on right side--gone, gone, gone.

### Jude — 2012-09-24T03:20:58+0300

> **Stavity;54257 wrote:** From my understanding the liver doesn't feel pain, but if enlarged it can lay on nerves. I am getting tested for HCV next week. I hope with all I have been doing the tests come out well. I know I feel better since eating seafood twice a day. No pain on right side--gone, gone, gone.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T19:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53827>

> **Patty Cakes;53877 wrote:** There is also the liver detox phase 1 & 2 testing to dtermine liver functionality in detoxification. I know nothing about this aspect either. Anyone please share.

I had this done recently, and just went over the results with my doctor last week. It's a bit overwhelming, due to the scientific lingo and terms, and I'm still absorbing it. It's an expensive test but only needs to be done once since genes don't change over time. However, as we all know here from Dr. Kruse, the genes' _expression_ can change. I proudly offered the word "epigenetics" when my doc began to explain how we can compensate for a defective gene.

So it turns out I'm missing one gene entirely: GSTM1, a glutathione gene. Glutathione is the body's strongest anti-oxidant and protects against cancer. This is very helpful to know, as I've had cancer twice.

I also have a few defective genes, two of which, CYP1B1 and COMT, signify impaired estrogen metabolism. This means my doc will not prescribe BHRT for me, although if I lose more weight she may consider it, since right now my fat cells are over-producing estrogen, and it's not being properly metabolized. This is also a cancer risk.

I also have defects in NAT2 (cancer risk), GSTP1 (also glutathione-related), and SOD2 (mitochondria)

For each defect, the health risks and ways to minimize risk are included. For example, for me, smoking is strongly discouraged. It says I'm at much higher risk than most people from smoking. So it's good I quit! But I should also avoid charbroiled food and eat tons of cruciferous veggies (so strongly recommended throughout the test that my doc suggests I take a good broccoli seed supplement to really flood my system with all that veggie goodness).

She also told me to take NAC, sulforaphane, and consider some type of glutathione supplementation, depending on my ability to afford it, as there are several types, such as foot creams that are compounded by a pharmacy and even something you breathe into your lungs every day. I believe I read on the forum that CT improves glutathione production, which I hope is true. If so, I've already made progress on upping my glutathione.

So I consider the test very worthwhile. It cost $470 plus $50 for the blood draw. It took me a while to come up with the money, but I'm glad I did.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:01:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53841>

Thank you RoseKelly. That's a lot of info in that test. Thanks for putting it down here for all of us. I wonder if the genes would show up on the raw data of the 23andme test. I have that one already done.

You may want to look into DIM for some "veggie goodness"! It helps with metabolizing of estrogen as well.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T20:13:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tell-me-about-your-liver-not-the-one-you-eat-i-hope.3805/post-53848>

Thanks, Patty. My doc mentioned DIM but recommended sulforaphane as even stronger. I was just about to order it a few minutes ago, and I discovered it actually is the broccoli sprout extract I should be taking. I thought they were two different things. Not.

But yeah, DIM helps estrogen metabolism too, so why not take that too?

Now to budget all this!

> **Patty Cakes;54504 wrote:** Thank you RoseKelly. That's a lot of info in that test. Thanks for putting it down here for all of us. I wonder if the genes would show up on the raw data of the 23andme test. I have that one already done.

### chewingthefat — 2012-09-21T19:30:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crazy-fatty-acid-profile.3807/post-53223>

Just got back my fatty acids profile. Not quite sure of what to make of it. My ND thinks I should stop the krill oil (taken only when I eat non grass fed beef-I used to take it 2/day a few months ago) and the fermented cod liver oil (used on the days I do not eat liverworst/braunschwieger which I eat 2 times a week). I do take Evening Primrose oil as it has helped me hormonally. She didn't think I should stop it? I've been gluten-free for more than 2 years, paleo for most of it, and have been eating sea food once at day (mostly) for 3 months. I can't believe the palmitic acid is low as I eat a ton of it daily? And saturated fat is low?!? I only cook with CO and uncured bacon grease and use olive oil for salad dressing? Lots of fats on my menu...I do admit to occasionally (once every couple of weeks) eating a rotisserie chicken with nice crunchy skin...I craved it when my gut was healing from celiac. I'm a bit on the lean side.

The other items were normal range, but these were out-of-range:

Palmitic acid 1252 L 1480-3730 nmol/mL

Lineoleic acid 4147 H 2270-3850 nmol/mL

EPA, C20:5W3 365 H 14-100 nmol/mL

Arachidonic ACI 1636 H 520-1490 nmol/mL

DHA, C22:6W3 1206 H 30-250 nmol/mL

Triene Tetranene 0.0008 L 0.010-0.038

total Saturated Fat 2.2 L 2.5-5.5 mmol/L

total Monsatur 1.7 1.3-5.9 mmol/L

total Polysatur 7.7 H 3.2-5.8 mmol/L

Total W3 1.8 H 0.2-0.5 mmol/L

total W6 6.0 H 3.0-5.4 mmol/L

Total Fatty Acid 11.7

### chewingthefat — 2012-09-22T18:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crazy-fatty-acid-profile.3807/post-53412>

Thanks for your reply, JanSZ! I really wanted to nail down how I need to tweak my supplement intake. I'm working on getting to the point of none...but not sure I can do that with AI. Tempted to quit all of the fatty oil supplements and test again in 6 mos, but I'm afraid of back-tracking on my progress. It's only been recently that I've started to feel "normal" again...meaning not crashing in the middle of the day and living in a total fog. Thyroid antibodies are better, too.

I assumed the High sat fat and low palmitic was the result of a largely keto diet...am I right? Probably need to add even more in the way of dietary fat?

But not everything can be solved by just working on them.

As far as palmitic and other Saturated fats

sources are

meat, diary, coconut oil, palm oil

...

When I test at Quest Diagnostics, they group it as follows:

21 ---- Saturated Fatty Acids(Meat, Diary, coconuts,palm oils

22 ---- C15:0 Pentadecanoic

23 ---- C16:0 Palmitic

24 ---- C17:0 Margaric (Heptadecanoic)

25 ---- C18:0 Stearic

26 ---- C20:0 Arachidic

27 ---- C22:0 Behenic

28 ---- C23:0 Tricosanoic

29 ---- C24:0 Lignoceric

///

> All that it tells you is that
>
> Evening Primrose oil (EPO) is affecting your levels of Steroid Hormones.
>
> No wonder, EPO contains GLA -----> it highly influences 5Ar---->which in turn influences progesterone and DHT levels.
>
> I had these done at Quest. I had a full work-up, but left off the ones that were in normal range.

You may get better input if you scan and post exact copy of your complete Fatty Acid report, all pages, however insignificant they may look.

Would save lots of guessing.

Even better if you would add all available tests.

There is a lots of variables.

# Thread: Help me catch up!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MamaGrok — 2012-09-22T01:00:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53313>

I just had my first consultation, and Jack's on with someone else right now, so he can't come here and make fun of me yet.

Educational consult revealed to me: Hashi's. Apparently responsible for everything, from the cravings to the diarrhea, fatigue to ... everything else.

Well, now I need to catch up. I've been mostly off the forum all summer, living my life and enjoying the increase in energy I've had. And I missed a few things. So any of you out there who have 5 minutes to sum up for me, I'd really appreciate it! I plan to read a whole bunch of back blog posts, and already hit most of BG12, but here's what I think I'm missing out on:

\- Epi-paleo - does this just mean paleo with a higher percentage of seafood than most do? How high? How much raw? How much shellfish vs fish fish? We don't care about the contamination, or what?

\- Iodine is king. Are we just eating more seafood, or seaweed, or supplementing? Are we concerned with selenium status first?

\- What's the consensus these days on best everyday CT? I have a great river very close that's 50 degrees year-round, but can't go without hubby, and he's not always available. AKman's brown fat burner thing? MS cooling vests? What can I use during my daily life around the house? Looking normal while doing so is a big bonus.

\- Um, what is ASI?

Hrm, that's all for now. I'm really beat up right now. But I guess I should be seeing the light at the end of the tunnel does exist ... just a lot further away than I had thought it was.

Thank you for your help! I will be catching up reading in due time, but I need a jump start.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-22T01:13:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53319>

MamaGrok!!! Good to see you again!

Sorry about the Hashi's. \*hug\*

I have to run, but you might want to start eating some sort of seafood everyday. You will find that you will come to like it more than you used to.

Don't worry about mercury for now, but yes, taking selenium or eating a brazil nut every other day would be good.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-22T01:31:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53326>

Hashis isn't the end of the world although it's not the best news. Been dealing with it for five years now and Jack's advice seems to be the best. Never felt better.

ASI is Adrenal Stress Index. Obviously something you need to get??? Stressed adrenals go hand in hand with Thyroid issues. Are you still taking the ThyroGold or have you moved onto something else.

Also selenium helps with the hashis attacks on your thyroid. I rotate now 200/400mg every other day to help with that.

Seafood every day all day is great. I'm able to get lunch (if I have it) and dinner as seafood (preferably shellfish, crustaceans). I think ocean fish then fresh water fish come next in order of importance. Oysters are the best most nutrient dense. Also include ocean veggies.

That's just a little snippet but I'm sure others will add to that. Good to see you back on here!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T02:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53333>

MG, I've missed you and your great wisdom. Jack has a blog on the epi-paleo diet. I think it is one of the BG series. He did a webinar but I think he did a blog too not all that long ago.

Did Jack comment on if you have celiac or not? I was wondering from your GI Effects test you had done this year. A lot of us have scored high on Anti-gliadin sig and thought you had too.

Glad to hear you have had some energy and have been enjoying your life.

### Marccus — 2012-09-22T07:46:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53367>

hey that's great you come back after a long time and now i hope you will be regular on the forum .

### Inger — 2012-09-22T10:43:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53374>

MG,

there was this thing about eating dirty vegetables from the garden.. to get good bacterias in...

There can never be too much seafood, Jack said in a post we can eat only that, when someone asked. The most perfect food for human.

I guess something raw every day is great. No need to be all raw. Fish eyes and brain and skin and all will give us the perfect nutrition. Like oysters and shrimps eaten with head and skin.

Iodine the natural way is best. Eating lots of algae and everything from the sea is much better than supplementing. I make me algae-water these days and drink it all day long. So very good.

### angieonthegogo — 2012-09-22T19:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53428>

>

MG welcome back to the fold!

Supplementing is good but eating seafood is best, the more the better but go by your labs as it may be necessary. I can only do one meal/day for budgetary reasons but I can surely feel a difference if I skip a day. Pay special mind to the light cycles. Blue blockers on when sun goes down, minimize/eliminate artificial lights sources especially at that time. Get AM sun first thing in the morning if possible, just a few mins can get things going.

I'm considering ice pack thingy AKMan posted about. Seems in the long run to be an economical choice while still allowing for mobility.

Inger - how do you make your algae water?

### Inger — 2012-09-22T21:15:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53448>

Angie,

I take dried seaweed and just toss it in water, let it sit for a few hours and drink it throughout the day. Often I make it in the evening ant let it sit on the counter and drink my first glas in the morning.

I use all kind of seaweed. Then I eat the seaweed when all the water is gone.

### angieonthegogo — 2012-09-22T22:06:38+0300
