### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-22T22:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-catch-up.3811/post-53467>

> **MamaGrok;53948 wrote:** I just had my first consultation, and Jack's on with someone else right now, so he can't come here and make fun of me yet.
>
>
>
> Educational consult revealed to me: Hashi's. Apparently responsible for everything, from the cravings to the diarrhea, fatigue to ... everything else.
>
>
>
> Well, now I need to catch up. I've been mostly off the forum all summer, living my life and enjoying the increase in energy I've had. And I missed a few things. So any of you out there who have 5 minutes to sum up for me, I'd really appreciate it! I plan to read a whole bunch of back blog posts, and already hit most of BG12, but here's what I think I'm missing out on:
>
>
>
> \- Epi-paleo - does this just mean paleo with a higher percentage of seafood than most do? How high? How much raw? How much shellfish vs fish fish? We don't care about the contamination, or what?
>
>
>
> \- Iodine is king. Are we just eating more seafood, or seaweed, or supplementing? Are we concerned with selenium status first?
>
>
>
> \- What's the consensus these days on best everyday CT? I have a great river very close that's 50 degrees year-round, but can't go without hubby, and he's not always available. AKman's brown fat burner thing? MS cooling vests? What can I use during my daily life around the house? Looking normal while doing so is a big bonus.
>
>
>
> \- Um, what is ASI?
>
>
>
> Hrm, that's all for now. I'm really beat up right now. But I guess I should be seeing the light at the end of the tunnel does exist ... just a lot further away than I had thought it was.
>
>
>
> Thank you for your help! I will be catching up reading in due time, but I need a jump start.

I've missed you!

Lots of us dealing with Hashi's (or potential Hashi's, not ruled out yet), so welcome back. I'm sure he told you to keep testing antibodies (how were your levels?) as it's a fluid disease that can be managed.

Re: your questions, I don't think there are 'rules' but I'll give you my general take-home messages:

Epi-Paleo means as much seafood as you can eat. There is a whole blog about it with the JK Epi Paleo food pyramid. Most of us, I'd say, eat at least one big seafood meal a day. Ideal, have something raw every day. The more raw the more DHA. If you're cooking it all, eat more. The now-famous MHS (mackerel head smoothies) are a cheap and easy way to get raw seafood in you. Also, don't put saturated fat with your seafood. Stick with mac oil or olive oil (or even bacon which is mostly MUFA) to eat with it. If you need/like/want sat fat (as I do) eat it at another, separate meal.

Iodine is best received from food (and there is a list of all the seaweeds in the epi paleo blog post - I think BG5). There are some who have wondered about supplementing until deficiency status goes away, but I know JK prefers directly from food (and there are risks with supplementing.)

Dr K has repeatedly suggested to 'pre-load' with selenium before doing the iodine, so I'm guessing this means making sure you've got good Se levels, too. You can supplement a la Brownstein protocol or you can eat Brazil nuts (1 per day - I do this even though I'm AI, not sure all would though). There is, of course, a lot of Se in seafood, so ideally this sorts the problem.

As far as CT, I think anything is good. Whatever you can do, whenever you can do it. Bathtubs, ice packs, cold rivers, cold vests. I know there is a thread by Trisha on the cooling vest she uses and has found really great.

You've already been told about ASI.

Hang in there. And so lovely to see you back.

Lauren

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KA24 — 2012-09-22T01:49:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53332>

I stopped CT in June and have only done it twice in the last 3 months. I've focused a lot more on training and other stuff since then, and have also been incredibly busy with work and building a new house. However, I've been planning on this self-experiment with CT for awhile and wanted to get everything going on the first day of fall. I've purchased a 100-gallon drum/tank for cold soaking and put it in my garage gym. Keep in mind, I'm going to jump right into it, without any re-adaptation time. Here is what I plan on measuring.

**Controls

_CT Sessions - 3 times per week for 6 weeks, or 18 total sessions

_Water temperature - I can usually get the water down to around 50 degrees with ice. I have 2 thermometers and will make sure the water is similar for each session.

_Length of soak - Each soak will be 30 minutes long of complete submersion (except for the head and two hands)

**Variables

_CT Time of day_ - I will try to control this as each session will be in the evening, but it's not feasible to do it the exact same time each day

Pre-CT meal/supplements**_ **- I am going to test different pre-CT meals weekly. I am not sure exactly how detailed I will get with this, but it definitely won't be 100% consistent session to session.

**Measurements******

Body measurements**_ **- this will basically include different body measurements affected by the CT.

Blood sugar**_ **- I plan on taking pre-CT and post-CT blood sugars 5-minutes before and after each session. I am a former Type 2 Diabetic who has a very good record of previous blood sugars.

Strength levels** - this is more of an observational thing, but I want to see if certain strength elements show significant improvement as the CT goes on

Hypothesis**

I think it's pretty safe to say that everything mentioned has been shown to be affected by CT. I want some concrete numbers for myself. I believe it will improve Glucose levels, body composition and strength gains.

Any recommendations for someone wanting to track this stuff? Anything I'm not accounting for? What to add, subtract, tweak, etc?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-22T02:08:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53334>

i'm not sure if you have the time to do this, but the literature suggests two session per day, of 1/2 time is better than one full time CT exposure. At least in mice.

### KA24 — 2012-09-22T02:19:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53335>

How far apart for each session? I think it's an interesting thought. Do you have any links or studies that reference this? I'd be interested in the margin for benefits and how they are measured on two sessions per day...

### freesia — 2012-09-22T06:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-53366>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;53969 wrote:** i'm not sure if you have the time to do this, but the literature suggests two session per day, of 1/2 time is better than one full time CT exposure. At least in mice.

Would love to see the study you read. Looking forward to tweaking my CT for next Autumn and Winter. Benefits from 1 x per day have been great but I'm happy to improve on it

Thx

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T04:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54018>

I think I may incorporate the twice a day thing. I'm still wondering how much time in-between would give max benefits.

Tonight, I did an abbreviated session after doing some chest and back lifting in the garage. Went 15 minutes and it felt great. I was definitely sore. Got cold enough outside last night that I kept the water at 59 degrees, makes it great to have the 100-gallon drum in the garage (no need for ice).

After 15 minutes, I got out and despite being pretty exhausted from the lifting session, I was able to do 30 straight Kettlebell snatches with a 75 lb Kettlebell. Generally, 35-40 is my max effort with this weight, and that is usually with some pre-workout supplement and it's usually a max set. Although I felt "numb" from the cold, I was really felt some additional strength capacity. I know I started another thread about this before and plan to really do some testing later in the winter on cold training, but it was cool to get a glimpse of. I really do think there is some adaptation that can be made for additional strength gains in the "numbness window" right after CT.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-25T15:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54103>

sorry, I posted that and then went away. Let me find the studies and I'll post them here.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T17:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54117>

Thanks for the reminder about Autumn, KA24. I sure didn't miss the first day of Spring, but I forgot about 9/22. Time to get serious.

Are you going to drink icewater before doing the CT?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-25T17:03:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54119>

i'd like to say I'm normally more organized than this - lol. But this happens more than I'd like to admit.

I've been searching through my horde of papers and this is all I'm able to find at the moment:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691)

> Two 2-h cold exposures daily were sufficient to increase basal metabolic rate and nonshivering thermogenesis to the same level as continuous cold exposure, whereas one 4-h cold period per day did not result in a significant increase of both parameters.

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T19:35:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54156>

> **Nonchalant;54821 wrote:** Thanks for the reminder about Autumn, KA24. I sure didn't miss the first day of Spring, but I forgot about 9/22. Time to get serious.
>
> Are you going to drink icewater before doing the CT?

Experimenting with different pre-CT foods/supplements. I have some data about these foods and effect on blood glucose, and will compare the effect they have while doing the CT. I generally don't use the ice water as I haven't noticed much of a difference. I've actually tried some experimenting with drinking a hot beverage both before and during CT.

### KA24 — 2012-09-25T19:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54158>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;54823 wrote:** i'd like to say I'm normally more organized than this - lol. But this happens more than I'd like to admit.
>
> I've been searching through my horde of papers and this is all I'm able to find at the moment:
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2155691)

Interesting. I'm going to print this one out. Interesting that it's two 2-hour exposures though. I wonder if the benefit decreases reverse-exponentially as the time goes down.

For instance, 4 hours is of greater benefit compared to 1 hour. However, i wonder if two 30-minute sessions would be better than 1 hour. Thanks for the link.

### philerup — 2012-09-26T04:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/autumn-starts-tomorrow-9-22-6-week-ct-experiment.3813/post-54332>

Yes, thanks Villjamur for the link. I have been doing sort of a 2x per day cold exposure using gel packs on the BAT areas. I do about 1 hour each time. It seems to at least keep my body composition stable as I haven't done much exercise, eat the same amount (or often more), and perhaps even got leaner.

Good luck with your experiment KA24. I'm interested in seeing how you do.

# Thread: Cycle changes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-09-22T02:48:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53341>

I'm wondering if anyone else has experienced changes in their cycles when starting implementing many of the things here.

Since my consult in early Aug., I've been eating seafood/fish 1-2 times per day (most days) and eating mostly epi-paleo, wearing blu-blockers at night, eating 4 hours before bed, and have added supplements, including DHEA.

My most recent cycle was about 23 days, when normally I'm at 30+. I usually have PMS, cramps - this time, nothing (which was really exciting!). But, the cycle is also longer than normal. I'm assuming that this is a good sign that things are changing, but also checking that maybe it's not.

My sleep has gotten better, and even if I wake up during the night, I feel so much better during the day- I'm not getting the tired feeling during the day like I was. The only thing is I'm still getting cravings and really wish they would stop.

I haven't started any other hormones - I have an appointment upcoming to talk about BHRT, which I'm really looking forward to. I'm guessing there will be more changes coming once I add BHRT!

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T16:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53399>

I've definitely noticed changes in my cycle, yes. I used to run about 30 days, quite regular, pretty bad PMS the week before it started, heavy flow the second day. After a couple months doing the Leptin Reset, my PMS was noticeably better, and cramping was much less. This last time (since starting to eat more seafood and tightening up my light exposure), I was at 29 days, had barely any PMS symptoms in the week before my period, and not one single cramp. My period because a non-event, which was GREAT. Hoping the trend continues!

For what it's worth, I'm not doing any BHRT, either.

### diane — 2012-09-22T17:02:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53400>

Thanks so much for the reply ealachan! I had assumed I would see cycle changes, but this was pretty fast and pretty different - so wasn't sure what to make of it.

I'm definitely going to do BHRT - given my age (45) and my labs. I actually had an appointment this week, but had to reschedule because of an immovable work meeting - and the closest appointment was almost a month away! I guess more and more people are getting on the train LOL.

My sense is that a lot of the change was from seafood - plus some of the supplements I'm taking for leaky gut. And seeing that you had a similar reaction - that might be the key!

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T18:34:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53411>

I'm not actively avoiding BHRT, for what it's worth...I just a) haven't had any tests done yet and b) am only 32 years old.

I agree that the changes were pretty fast for me, too. I had only really been ramping up the fish in earnest for about two weeks when my last period showed up, and the difference was pretty noticeable.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T19:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53425>

My cycles have been regular before starting the LR, but TOM was very short and heavy. This month I had much more cramps and clotting, and it lasted for 8 days! I'm hoping it's more of a detox thing than a permanent change.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T23:38:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53488>

I went for DEEEEEEP Ct after the Webinar - I was doing 29-35 degrees for 30-45 mins a show 4X a week. I ended up with a 15 day bleed and freaked out. Got all the goods checked out and was back to norm when I stopped CT for the summer. I figure it was 35 year old broken down crap coming out!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-23T01:11:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53508>

My cycle has been all over the place since starting the LR and working my way toward optimal since I began June2011. Initially I didn't have a cycle - what nursing a 14mth old. 2 months into the LR I started cycling again, and discovered that my migraines were 100% hormonally driven. I started CT mid Feb - saw some changes in my cycle length, added BHRT saw my cycle bounce from 32 days to 26 days. I've also seen my migraines decrease, but not go away. I've also seen the length of my TOTM go from 5-10days down to 3 days w/1 day of spotting. I'm cycling ever 26/27 days. I need more Pg. as I'm still estrogen dominant, along w/having PCOS.

### MsYo — 2012-09-26T02:23:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-54309>

My cycles are 28 days like clockwork. I was being pressured into a hysterectomy (44, 2 myomectomies, and fibroid pressure). DIM, SSS tonic (iron) and vitamin D was a noticeable improvement in less than a month. Added a low dose progesterone and started paying attention to circadian rythms. My periods are beyond "normal" in flow and length. My weight has stopped bouncing all over the place too. I'm looking forward to my educational consult to conquer that since all other issues are progressing NICELY.

By the way, Ealachanm I had my first myomectomy at age 28..... chances are my hormones were off then but no one checked. Make sure you check especially if you have other issues.

### margor_j — 2014-02-07T03:21:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-108810>

hi girls,
when i first went to paleo (about 4 years ago) i had drastic improvements in my period within 1-2 cycles. however, as i started to get fatter and fatter and puffing up like a beeyatch, they went haywire again as well.
iv always had a 'problem' period - with PMT, bloating, cravings, headaches (omg, the headaches are HIDEOUS) BUT despite all that, regular as clock work, almost down to the hour.

so, im about 3 months into this site now and doing my best in all areas - including having relocated to a much better place, daily earthing on the beach, meditation, more water etc..

iv just finished a period. id say the PMT was about 60% reduced - no tears, and although i felt big, i didnt feel as big as usual (showered with the lights off tho, lol!) and it started, and then i had nothing for two days and then we were off. thats not happened a lot to me but yeah, i have had that before. but i had no cramps, no back pain and i didnt get the henous headaches that usually hit me at the end of it - which i did the month before. they can last up to a week - i wake up with them and theyre so hideous, i have to get out of bed. i cannot lie in bed.. like a vice around my head.

im only recently back onto transdermal progesterone (dr tim). id say iv upped seafood tho im not really too keen on scoffing too much tinned tuna, but im doing my best! its still seafood, right? tho a hit of tuna with avocado a good breakfast does make!

oh, the other thing that can go awry for me is sleep - before my period. which hit me full force this last period - but also, i was in a hotel, so who knows what was around me (emfs - tho there was no wifi in my room). but i wonder about flying to - i can travel up to 32 hours to go to work, but this was close - a 30 min flight. (i do the grounding JK recommends while im up in zee air) i still had the coffee cravings and cravings for carbs a couple of days BUT this time around i felt like living death for the two days prior - which is also new for me. zero energy, headache and SO fatigued it was ridic - the second i started my cycle, that all lifted. wtf!? hormones.. lol

mags

# Thread: Stomach Pain - Help!

**Posts:** 5

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T03:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53354>

Here we go again. I have had a pain in my gut (beneath the ribs to the navel and to the left and to the right) all day. It was finally subsiding this evening and then I ate dinner consisting of blue gills fried in bacon grease with salt and pepper. Now my gut is hurting all over again. What is going on? Almost seems a little better to lay on my left side. If I can't even eat fish right now, what the hell can I eat?

Thanks for any input.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T15:00:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53378>

No takers on what's going on? Last night was pretty bad. Woke up every 2 hours with pain. Wondering if I have gastritis? I had this about 7 years ago. Anyone know anything about gastritis? If I do, what do I eat? I am eating burger right now and we'll see how things fare. Pretty scared as food seems to make it flare up.

### Destiny — 2012-09-22T15:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53387>

> **vkiernan;53989 wrote:** Here we go again. I have had a pain in my gut (beneath the ribs to the navel and to the left and to the right) all day. It was finally subsiding this evening and then I ate dinner consisting of blue gills fried in bacon grease with salt and pepper. Now my gut is hurting all over again. What is going on? Almost seems a little better to lay on my left side. If I can't even eat fish right now, what the hell can I eat?
>
>
>
> Thanks for any input.

That sounds like my reaction to Betaine HCL. I had to stop taking it after a week on it. After a few days of stopping, I was good.

Since then, I have been taking Ox bile, no problem with that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-22T20:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53437>

I'm just here to give you support. I know nothing about gastritis. Big hug!!!

### vkiernan — 2012-09-22T21:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stomach-pain-help.3815/post-53449>

I am a lot better today stomach wise. Yesterday was bad and kinda scary not really knowing what was up. Must have been a touch of stomach flu or something. I slept a lot yesterday and last night and am really drained today. Going to lay low and take a nap soon.

# Thread: Can't decide if I should do it again or keep trying the hard way!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LSWood@eoni.com — 2012-09-22T03:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-53356>

A year ago I did a successful round of the Bodyshapers (real HCG, sublingual, which gets a "positive" on pregnancy tests). I am female, 60+ and had no other weight-loss success despite multiple other attempts, so I was thrilled, not to mention that the HCG Diet Info Forum (and Colleen Coble! THANKS!) introduced me to Jack Kruse, the Leptin reset and Paleo. Here it is a year later, I've only regained about 5 of the 15 lbs, but it seems like it's all fat around my middle (I'm about 147-148 now, 160 when I started last year). Part of me wants to keep plugging away eating Paleo, etc. (the hard way) but I'm really tempted to go another round of the Bodyshapers HCG (the easy way). I did CT for a while last spring but didn't get great results and didn't really do it 100% with ice packs in the tub - my immersions were only about 60 degrees. I know not to attempt HCG and CT at the same time, according to Colleen's N=1.

I liked doing it in the fall - lots of fresh veggies and strawberries at the farmer's market and it set me up for eating more ketogenic in the winter.

Any thoughts/advice? Especially regarding using the Bodyshapers again - I see it's a different company but since it worked so well and will get here quickly I'd prefer using them again unless anyone knows any reasons I should be concerned about using them again.

I still have some sleep issues and not great energy, and I've been following all the threads regarding those problems, so just concerned regarding the potential impact of HCG. They were pre-existing before HCG so I doubt that should actually be of concern but thought I'd mention them while I was at it.

Thank you all!

### tellmisty — 2012-09-26T07:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54374>

Consider that your PG-E2 ratio is off, making you estrogen dominant, which is causing excess estrogen to be stored in fat cells in your belly. Detoxing estrogen, in this case, is what will strip your middle of the bulge. You need labs and the support of a good functional medicine doctor to tackle this. It's the first thing that's ever successfully removed stubborn belly fat for me, and as I learned about it, I was surprised at how common it was and how little it's discussed for women ...

### caroline — 2012-09-27T12:36:44+0300

Detoxing estrogen, in this case, is what will strip your middle of the bulge. You need labs and the support of a good functional medicine doctor to tackle this. It's the first thing that's ever successfully removed stubborn belly fat for me, and as I learned about it, I was surprised at how common it was and how little it's discussed for women ...

Thanks Misty for talking about this. I have just discovered that I am down 2 sizes but my weight has not changed - in fact I am up 2 K. All of this really does work. No one could be more surprised than me - I have been on every diet know to mankind. They usually worked when I was younger but as I got older the middle bulge just kept getting bigger. Now I know it is my PG-E2 ratio etc. Thanks Dr. K.

### AdrianaDr. Radler-Pohl — 2012-09-27T13:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54716>

I think that this Pg/E2 ratio thing is what most women need. Esp those who are not optimal with their bodycomp. The off-bodycomp ist the body talking to us. Still I find it not easy to get things adjusted. here in Germany it is so difficult to find a Doc who is familiar with the whole hormone stuff. and you need often prescriptions - so I find myself in a situation I want to change but it takes so long to find the right help ..... ### caroline — 2012-09-28T01:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-decide-if-i-should-do-it-again-or-keep-trying-the-hard-way.3816/post-54892>

I so absolutely agree - My bulging middle is what started me on this journey. I really thought that something was the matter but when my Docs would test they would say I was fine. Have you done a consult with Dr. K? I got lucky yesterday and went to a new Doc. He was very interested and watched my Skype consult with Dr. Kruse. He agreed to do scripts for what Dr. K recommended and said he will do further research and work with a compounding pharmacy. At Least this is a start for me.

It is hard in Australia as well. I was able to order tests from ZRT Labs and this new Doc showed me an on line pharmacy where I could order the progesterone that Dr.K recommended. I got the name of this new Doc from a compounding pharmacy so I would suggest you give that a go. It is a huge step forward to do an email consult with Dr. K about what labs you need and then do a skype consult. I found that it really helped to show the new Doc the skype consult because then he knew I wasn't dealing with some online Whacko. If you want the name of the online pharmacy - PM me. Good luck!

# Thread: Greetings from New Hampshire.. doing research on Leptin for my situation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cramernh — 2012-09-22T05:32:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-53359>

Hello!

I am a married 38yr old from New Hampshire with a 19yr old heading off to college. I spent the last 15+ years working two industries: culinary and healthcare. Now that I have an empty nest, I remain in my healthcare position. I am an advanced certified medical reimbursement specialist within a large provider/health system network. I use my clinical background to help with the appeals process and battle insurances to get medical claims paid.

When Im not battling for medical claims and the headaches associated with 38220's always needing a modifier 59, I am a bassoonist with a private university's orchestra department.

I keep up with my previous cheffing background as my husband seems to think Thanksgiving occurs 365 days per year, LOL!

I am currently a patient being treated for PCOS, hyperinsulinemia, metabolic syndrome, and dealing with the residual healing of alopecia... I spent a number of years trying to find practitioners who had a background with IR/PCOS and Metabolic syndrome, unfortunately there are not many out there.

I have learned so much about myself from this one Endocrinologist I am seeing however, I truly believe not all the appropriate testing has been done.

I have a very clean dietary intake where I consume 100% clean foods. I have learned about the effects of the bread/grain group of foods and how it has affected me. It is one year that I have been without that family of foods and in my first three months: I have completed erradicated my diabetes risk from 11.2 to 5.2 and successfully sustained that for exactly one year (today makes it one year exactly). My cholesterol also dramatically improved in the first three months, to the point of shocking not just the Endo, but it compelled my Dermatologist and PCP to call me and ask "WHAT DID YOU DO?" They almost did not believe me when I told them that I completely rid my home of all breads, pasta, rice, corn and flour. I havent had a can of soda in over 12 years...

But - the real issue I have going right now is that my weight is NOT dropping. For one year, I have followed the guidance of the Endo: 1600 calories per day, no more than 120g of carbohydrates daily (each meal no more than 40g)... My total cholesterol panel is proof that for one year, I clearly have made excellent food choices... but with all of the exercising I am doing at the gym... I just cannot drop this weight.

I have begged the Endo to consider other testing as I feel there may be other things we simply have not reviewed yet.

I was downright INSULTED that she would even suggest Qsymia to me. I complied with food changes willingly and have successfully stuck to that for one year - Qsymia would be for someone who does not comply with food portions/modifications.. I was angry she would even consider recommending to me a damn drug.

Out of all the tests I have had since being diagnosed, I have never once had leptin considered, glucagon considered and it boggles my mind why this Endo thinks a quickly-approved weight loss drug would help.... Im about to fire her to be honest.

I do hope there are others here like me and Im just wondering about Leptin resistance and whether or not I fit in this category of being possibly considered for...

Thank you for any future replies!

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-22T15:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-53385>

Welcome from over the border in Vermont! Have you had a chance to read about the tests suggested for looking at your inflammation levels, hormone profile etc? How about your light cycles and CT? Good food choices are part of the picture, but not the whole picture. Certainly you've come to the right place to get lots of information from the blogs and the forum!

### Huck — 2012-09-22T15:23:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-53386>

Welcome. I wouldn't be to quick to fire her. There's not very many great docs around and yours sounds trainable!

This site is kind of like a treasure hunt. All, or at least most, of the answers you need are here someplace. Read, read, read. And when you can't find it or have questions, just ask.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-22T17:07:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-53401>

Welcome. I will give you the advice I give everyone if they are not already doing it. Don't consume any calories within 4 hours of sleep. You can find more about this in the blog concerning circadian cycles. I missed this for a long time and once i put it into practice things changed dramatically. good luck. And if you haven't already seen it check out the leptinRX.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T18:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54133>

Hi Cramernh, welcome. With Autumn here, you may want to reduce those carbohydrates way back. And for winter, reduce them even further, close to zero.

For your thyroid, I'd want to know what your Free T3, Free T4, and reverse T3 levels are. Also you should check for antibodies. I don't see a Vitamin D level in your results above. Is the CRP number a regular CRP (which checks for infection) or a highly sensitive (cardiac) CRP?

Your LDL is low, so maybe your thyroid is working very well and converting the cholesterol to various hormones like it should. But you mentioned that you were on prescription medicines. You aren't taking a cholesterol-lowering drug, are you?

I would guess that your weight loss stall is caused by a hormone imbalance, or by toxins hidden away in your fat cells. If your body gets rid of the fat, it would release the toxins as well.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54138>

Hiya!

I know that from previous labs (I do my best to keep a copy of each-drives them batty! LOL), I have not seen a Free T3/T4 or the reverese T3 ever done so that is my next step. Thankfully my Vit D levels are within normal limits (had to call the Endo back for that separate result). Unfortunately the CRP is the standard... so Ill be adding HS-CRP to the list.

My LDL is low thanks to getting rid of the bread/grains group of foods as well as potatoes... NOOOOOO no cholesterol-reducing medications because I successfully lowered it by the food changes I made.... Im the only one in the family thus far who has been successful in doing that and never needed the medication for it.

My testosterone/estrogen levels have been stabilized for one calendar year thus far.....

My Endo has no idea what to consider next for testing... thats why she recommended that damn drug the FDA was so quick to approve... I told her "HELL NO are you KIDDING?!" I was insulted by it to be honest.... She admits to having no other patients with an HDL of 86.... I am her first.... despite my diagnoses of PCOS, Insulin Resistance, Metabolic Syndrome, etc....

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T18:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54139>

As of right now, the Endo recommended this:

...and yes, I am very well aware that Endos have no full nutritional training, just the basics to help diabetics and other pts out.

1600 cal per day (almost 300 below my BMR)

no more than 120g of carbohydrates daily - not to exceed 40g per meal

no refined carbs (hence my willingness to just eliminate all breads, pasta, rice, corn, flour and potatoes)

I am an avid rabbit food lover, and I have a 64oz mug of iced water at my side all the time.

I cook 100% clean, 100% of the time....

Ocella, Metformin, Fosinopril, Spironolactone, D3, Calcium Citrate, B complex, Biotin, Fish oil.... That is what I am taking right now

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T22:09:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54232>

Cramernh, the 50g of protein is just for breakfast. As far as meat goes, make it seafood if possible. Yes to all visible animal fat (but I still have difficulties with visible beef fat myself.) Just eat whatever protein and fat is needed to keep you happy until the next meal, with no snacking. People that regain their leptin sensitivity will move their largest meal to the evening, instead of morning. Happy to have you with us, btw.

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T22:52:39+0300

I LOVE LOVE LOVE the idea of no snacking..

Im still trying to figure out how the hell Ill be able to get exercise in during the later part of the day... I can definitely do that on the weekends... but during the week is chaotic with my schedules... for the longest time, I had been working out at 5am for an hour because it is convenient to get in there and put a solid hour's worth of exercise in, come to work to shower here, and the voila: ready to start my shift.... Yes, in case you are wondering, I did read on Dr. Kruse's excerpt regarding no exercise before/after breakfast... lol

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T22:16:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54548>

OK... I just received an email from the Endocrinologist this afternoon.. she confirmed that I did have the HS-CRP, it came back within normal limits. She also confirmed the last thyroid screening done (including reverse) all came back normal as well. She also referenced my hormonal levels are all within normal limits as well from the June 2012 draw (her office didnt send me page three evidently)

Im ready to punch a hole in the wall out of frustration.... I dont know how much more compliant I can be. My head is killing me from doing all kinds of research on my conditions, in fact, Im lucky to understand about 85% of it...

I dont want to throw my hands up on this - I know I have come so far and maintained well, my labs are proof that I am compliant and I just have to keep reminding myself of that fact: if I were jerking around and eating poorly, my labs wouldnt look so damn good like the have been for the last calendar year.

Very frustrating

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-26T22:55:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54558>

Did she give you the exact figures or just tell you within "normal limits"? This was my downfall for years...not having the labs right in front of me and going by the "everything's fine" I heard from the Dr.'s. Being in the top quartile is very different than being "within normal limits." You could try posting them here and see how other's respond.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-27T00:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54591>

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T00:35:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-54592>

only within normal limits/normal, etc.

Here is a copy-paste from her email reply to me:

**_I am glad you are doing your research.Here is the breakdown on your questions:Hs-crp- non-specific marker. It helps determine risk for ischemic heart disease. It may help understand the risk but may not be related to what you are dealing with currentlyDHEA- checked in 2010 was normalSalivary cortisol has the same specificity as 24 hour urine cortisol (which is still the gold standard) for ruling out Cushings or cortisol excess. You had the 24 hour urine studies done prior which was normal. TSH and FT4 levels done previously were normal.T3 levels help understand the thyroid disease - but in most cases do not give any further information if your TSH and Free T4 levels are normal. T4 gets converted to T3 in your body and most often checking the T4 levels is a good indicator of how your T3 levels are.Reverse T3 is not helpful and in general is only checked when you have abnormal thyroid function tests from an illness not related to an underlying thyroid condition.Naturopathic literature suggests otherwise - but based on my experience reverse T3 has never been helpful.CLA - natural supplement - which I have never prescribed before and not really have experience with seems to be something that may help (or not) as based on reading that I had done the results in few studies was helpful for weight loss and in some studies it did not show benefit.Based on what I see it seems to be a fairly well tolerated med, however can increase insulin resistance and changes lipids.Unfortunately, I cannot come up with a good answer for your persistent issues. I can only ask you to continue. I will be happy to answer any questions you have_**

### rwhite — 2012-10-18T16:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-58911>

Welcome to the forum> I am sure you will get a lot out of it.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T22:48:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-new-hampshire-doing-research-on-leptin-for-my-situation.3817/post-59022>

Well, "normal" lab results mean that you are in the same boat as most of the other people in the US.

Your doctor should provide paperwork that was sent from the lab to the doctor, giving the normal range and exactly where on that range you are.

Dr. Kruse does not want people to be in the normal range for HS-CRP. He wants us to be below normal. He wants no systemic inflammation at all, in fact.

# Thread: A nice way to get lots of Iodine throughout the day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 37
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-09-22T08:42:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-53370>

I am doing algae-water these days. I love it.

I just take a glass jug and fill it with water, add some algae, I have many different kind and I vary them. Today I have carrageenan, yesterday it was wakame. I drink the water when thirsty, I ususally drink one jug / day. I eat the algae left too, together with my meal when the water is gone. Sometimes I use the same algae for 2 days, I just fill fresh water in the can.

It feels really good! I am imagine this must be the healthiest water ever...

Sometimes I use the same algae for 2 days, I just fill fresh water in the can.
>
> It feels really good! I am imagine this must be the healthiest water ever...
>
>
>

You know that is a really great idea. I have never seen anyone do that before but it makes perfect sense. Are you using dried seaweed or do you have access to fresh?

### Inger — 2012-09-22T21:22:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-53450>

Caroline, I am using dried, because that is what I get here..

My carrageenan water today was so tasty, kinda thickish and nice rose' colored! Very nice!

@ Patty Cakes.. you see.. I am a lazy girl..lol That is why I come up with easy things like MHS and seaweed water.

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-23T05:57:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-53558>

> **angieonthegogo;54084 wrote:** How do you make your tea?

I pour hot water over a teaspoon or two of crushed arame. Let it steep, stir, and sip. I have also mixed arame with green tea. very good. I am finding a good way to introduce and accustom myself to the seaweed flavors is to make broths or teas with just the seaweed and sip it. I have done this with bladderwrack, kombu,wakame,hiziki, kelp and dulse. They are all different and interesting.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-29T16:07:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55198>

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-29T17:54:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55227>

Caution: Shameless show-off about one of my kids to follow!

One of my children is very intuitive about what he eats (the other 4 aren't). He has been that way since the beginning. He has plenty of issues too. If only I had gotten out of his way sooner, and known what I know now, I bet things would be different... Hashimoto's diagnosed (thanks to me at age 3), Celiac diagnosed (again, thanks only to me, at age 5).

Since age 5 (me feeling very defeated by all of this), I tried to get out of his way, and let him eat more what he wants (within parameters, of course), and less of what I thought he should eat. He's been happy to eat mainly fish and beef. His vegetable choices are few, and often they will change dramatically after a few months time. His fruit choices are few, and often only a serving or so of each fruit per season, often when we go out picking. Then he won't touch it again, often until the next season. He has always eaten limited grains (even the Weston Price soaked grains I used to make). He will have dairy, preferring the raw milk I pick up, and generally only cheeses that come from outside the country (KerryGold). He goes long stretches without wanting to eat (does not feel the need to snack). He's not perfect. He'll still love a piece of cake if it's offered to him. Sugar seems to destroy his intuitive sense, at least temporarily.

At the point of his celiac diagnosis 4 years ago, he was anemic, hypothyroid (because the old endocrinologist believed in keeping him at the highest TSH point to better stimulate his own gland - don't even get me started talking about this it makes my blood boil), mentally fragile, speech delayed, completely miserable. 4 years later, he is academically working 1-3 years ahead of his grade level, he sprints faster than any of the children in his school (small school where the grades 2-6 are taught together. He is in 4th grade, but can work at whatever rate suits him), can spontaneously do 15 pull-ups, whereas most of the other children struggle to do 1-2. All this with 2 auto-immune diseases. I blame the seafood! Now if only I could get him to put an oyster in his mouth...

Shameless show-off now finished. If only I could find the way for my other children to tap this intuitive sense that he seems to have so well. I would love to hear how Inger evolved this intuition. My impression from reading her posts is that it wasn't always this way...? They are all eating some form of fish daily now...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-29T18:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55232>

So glad you wrote this! I love it! Proud mommy feelings are the best thing in the world - my intuition is that happyness is good for your health, too.

None of us here are coming from perfection - it doesnt exist, and for all those times fate landed you on the negative side of the coin flop just means that you got the positives a coming. Life finds balance. If your pendulum swung one way, it will always swing back later.

Anywhooo - I wanna see a happy pic of the brood.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-29T20:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55248>

### nonchalant — 2012-10-01T23:51:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55682>

> **ealachan;56835 wrote:** What do you mean by you feel it in your thyroid? I don't seem to have had any adverse reactions since I started eating a lot of kelp every day, but I'm curious what i should look out for.

A feeling of tightness. Like something gently pressing on your throat. Like a tshirt that is up too high in front.

I put a small amount of sea salt in my palm and eat it, and drink plenty of water, and the feeling soon goes away.

I felt this way when taking lugols. I ignored it, and continued, and ended up with a bromaderma on my face in a week.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-04T19:58:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-56266>

BTW, Inger, I liked the seaweed water. I put some dried seaweed in a quart jar, added filtered water, and set it in the fridge. I think I added too much seaweed, because it expanded and pretty much filled the jar. The taste was a nice ocean flavor.

My excess iodine symptoms have abated. I'll probably try some next week, but it will just be a tablespoon of the water next time. Or perhaps I'll measure the seaweed; be less generous.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Brighton catches up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brighton-catches-up.3820/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-22T09:38:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brighton-catches-up.3820/post-53373>

Just down the coast here there's the town of Brighton. The names's a contraction of Brighthelmstone, and this was a tiny fishing village till the Prince Regent decided it would make a suitable place for the seabathing cure in the early 19th century and built a Moghul-cum-Chinese palace at the cost of what would now be a medium-sized country's national debt. I suppose it kept some craftsmen in work. I don't know whether paddling in the sea cured the royal gout. It seems to have had less effect on the regal girth than an epi-paleoRx would have done, judging by some of the Gillray cartoons:

[http://www.britishmuseum.org/explore/highlights/highlight\_objects/pd/j/james\_gillray,\_a\_voluptuary\_un.aspx](http://http://www.britishmuseum.org/explore/highlights/highlight_objects/pd/j/james_gillray,_a_voluptuary_un.aspx)

Anyway, for the past half-dozen or so years Brighton has been allowed to refer to itself as a "city".

And it's catching up fast. Yesterday a man died in front of his son after crashing his van in the city. While a woman who was passing "struggled to save his life" with artificial respiration her purse was stolen.

# Thread: Seaweed Scone recipe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T18:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/post-53407>

Here is the Scone recipe that was asked after. . . .

It's the basic recipe, and I tried it with added dried seaweed and chopped dark chocolate - awesome, lots of seaweed and you would never know it was there.

2 cups almond meal

Â¾ tsp baking soda

Â½ tsp sale

3 tbs maple syrup

1-egg

Mix up all the ingredients, and pat out into a round, bake at 350 till done.

Variations:

I like adding cinnamon or pumpkin spices.

You can add drier additions like crushed seaweed â€“ if adding dry stuff I would increase egg to 2.

Â½ cup of additions like larger chunks of toasted pecans or chopped dark chocolate or dried fruit always works well.

This isnâ€™t chemistry like regular baking, so you probably wonâ€™t mess it up by experiementing!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-22T18:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-scone-recipe.3824/post-53417>

I would like to end this article by a short anecdote that will perhaps contribute to convince the most sceptical scientists among you of the value of polyphenols. In 1934, DÃ©sirÃ© Cordier (1858-1940), founder of the world famous Cordier wine trading house, organized the first “longevity festival” in Saint-Julien-Beychevelle (MÃ©doc, France), after having noted that life expectancy in this wine-producing area near Bordeaux was 45% higher than the national average. All the centenarians from MÃ©doc came to celebrate wine as the “elixir of life”… A votre santÃ© !

### chocolate — 2012-09-22T20:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/older-polyphenol-article.3826/post-53434>

> Loved the last paragraph!
>
> am not entirely convinced that I will never be able to digest a glass of wine again. I just have a mess to clean up, first.
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19930765](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19930765)Abstract
>
>
>
> Sucrose increases postprandial blood glucose concentrations, and diets with a high glycaemic response may be associated with increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes and CVD. Previous studies have suggested that polyphenols may influence carbohydrate digestion and absorption and thereby postprandial glycaemia. Berries are rich sources of various polyphenols and berry products are typically consumed with sucrose. We investigated the glycaemic effect of a berry purÃ©e made of bilberries, blackcurrants, cranberries and strawberries, and sweetened with sucrose, in comparison to sucrose with adjustment of available carbohydrates. A total of twelve healthy subjects (eleven women and one man, aged 25-69 years) with normal fasting plasma glucose ingested 150 g of the berry purÃ©e with 35 g sucrose or a control sucrose load in a randomised, controlled cross-over design. After consumption of the berry meal, the plasma glucose concentrations were significantly lower at 15 and 30 min (P < 0.05, P < 0.01, respectively) and significantly higher at 150 min (P < 0.05) compared with the control meal. The peak glucose concentration was reached at 45 min after the berry meal and at 30 min after the control meal. The peak increase from the baseline was 1.0 mmol/l smaller (P = 0.002) after ingestion of the berry meal. There was no statistically significant difference in the 3 h area under the glucose response curve. These results show that berries rich in polyphenols decrease the postprandial glucose response of sucrose in healthy subjects. The delayed and attenuated glycaemic response indicates reduced digestion and/or absorption of sucrose from the berry meal.

This study included the sucrose. I want the berries without sucrose. I wish I knew what to think. I think they can only help.

# Thread: Canadian First Nations Traditional Foodways
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-22T21:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53463>

There's some information on that in this documentary about Dr. Jay Wortman's work among First Nations peoples on the west coast in Canada, where he works trying to cure diabetes and other metabolic syndrome diseases with a low-carb diet.

Interestingly, one of the staples of the traditional diet was "oolikan grease" - an oil high in monounsaturates made from tiny fish, whose numbers are now unfortunately threatened.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;bjTmdvFH3gQ\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bjTmdvFH3gQ\[/video\]\[/video\]

Most of the documentary is taken up with what they did to combat the health problems but what historical information is given is interesting.

Jay Wortman has, apparently, travelled around Canada, interviewing elderly indigenous people, trying to find out more about their ancient foodways.

I heard he had offered to speak at AHS and was snubbed. After all why would anyone want to hear a medical doctor who works firsthand with people's problems and who has also personally done oral history investigations into the diets of pre-agricultural populations?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-22T22:36:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53469>

he was snubbed..........they do not want the message out that something else might be better.

### maggie — 2012-09-22T23:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53486>

I remember reading about this a couple of years ago and being completely fascinated learning about the existence of the Grease Trail--like the Silk Road! ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grease\_trail](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grease_trail) It was a trading route between coastal and inland peoples that made these valuable foods like fermented oily fish available to noncoastal tribes. IIRC, these foods were particularly valued because they were considered necessary for fertility. I wonder how many more of these traditional trading patterns there are that just invisible to us. I mentioned this in a thread on the Evolutionary Psych blog after Dr. Harris made a comment to the effect that a lot of hunter gatherers never ate a fish in their lives, but nobody picked up on it. I think this would be an awesome subject for a book--studying evolutionary nutrition through ancient trade routes.

I think Dr. K's writings are providing an epistemological lens that will bring a lot of seemingly unrelated stuff into focus--I remember the articles that were being discussed here on the baby who survived the morgue and about European peasants who slept the winters away. Anyway, fuel for the imagination if nothing else...

**

That's dramatic language. "Tethered** to the water". When people who know about brain chemistry speak of water-sourced foods as having that kind of importance, one had better take notice.r />

That's egg on the face for people who talk a lot about our evolutionary past, but who don't know that the most important phase in it took place around water.

As for consumption of fish and shellfish, the interesting thing is that on this issue even the mainstream knows better. Here is advice on diet from Britain's National Health Service:

> A healthy diet should include at least two portions of fish a week, including one of oily fish.
>
>
>
> That is because fish and shellfish are good sources of many vitamins and minerals. Oily fish – such as salmon and fresh tuna – is particularly rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids, which may help to keep your heart healthy.
>
>
>
> Most of us should have more fish in our diet, including more oily fish. ... Shellfish includes prawns, mussels and langoustine ... a good source of selenium, zinc, iodine and copper

[http://www.nhs.uk/Livewell/Goodfood/Pages/fish-shellfish.aspx](http://http://www.nhs.uk/Livewell/Goodfood/Pages/fish-shellfish.aspx)

### Michael — 2012-09-23T17:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53598>

> **Jack Kruse;54214 wrote:** CW does realize the benefit of fish........you wonder why paleo does not? I have my own ideas.

I could take a stab in the dark and might very well be going astray.

I think Cordain is probably correct to say that a lot of people in the dietary field are fairly rudderless. They lack any clear overarching theoretical framework.

However, I think this might mean that they expect any foodstuff might be "good" or "bad" with no particular rhyme or reason to it. If they're told fish is good and it doesn't seem to conflict with anything else they already believe, they'll be quite ready to accept that. Someone like Crawford at London Imperial publishes some papers and what's in them does filter down. And maybe dieticians don't think about _why it should be that we seem to be almost _dependent on shellfish and fish; but they're quite prepared to accept that it's desirable that they should be in the diet and take a handful of immediate reasons for that - that they're good sources of DHA, Selenium, Zinc, etc.

Paleo's problem might be that it _does_ have a framework - but it's not the right one. Or rather it has a aspiration to be in line with evolutionary biology. But in practice something more than a vague genuflection in the direction of natural selection is necessary. By itself that tends to be too abstract to tell you much. You need more models than that, and you need them to be closer to the phenomena. So, for example, you need a model for how encephalization took place. If you assume that's explained by what paleoanthroplogists have been speculating for getting on for a century now (plus a few updatings along the way), you're going to be badly out.

I have a feeling that this is a generalized problem with Cordain, learned and interesting as he undoubtedly is. He puts his faith in the models he's using and doesn't see that if there are problems with them, then it puts a big question mark against his conclusions. Here's another one: I have a hard time believing that early man in East Africa was eating a Zone-style 40:30:30 macronutrient ratio (and, moreover, that that wouldn't vary seasonally). I don't know where his model might be wrong, but I have an intuitive sense that it probably is. He's taken to saying to interviewers that the Paleo Diet isn't "his" diet but simply the human diet and how people have been eating for 2.6 million years. I don't know that's so much a statement of humility as a backhanded claim that what's he's offering is beyond challenge_. But in truth there remains a gap between "what men and women were eating in the Upper Paleolithic" and "The Paleo Diet™ ", the latter only approaching the former insofar as the models in use from which the reconstruction has been attempted are valid.

I enjoyed The Paleo Answer_ and thought it a superb book. However, if it were everything it should be it would carry a chapter specifically on fish that would discuss the shoreline hypothesis and the importance of the high level of brain-specific nutrients in seafoods. It would also carry quite specific recommendations to eat these foods regularly, too. Unless that's explicitly done the average reader is not going to take the message to heart.

I think one of the reasons why the average reader wouldn't is a species of romanticism. It seems to me that there's a popular image of the "caveman" as someone who carries a club and eats a lot of meat (probably raw). Now I've nothing against meat, and nothing against eating it raw, but it's possible to be over-influenced by images. I think meat is perhaps seen as macho.

That macho image of the caveman is probably what the anthropologists who suggested that the Venus figurines were being made by palaolithic men to masturbate to had in their minds. Only they thought of this hairy individual with his raw meat in his hand and _shuddered._ Others, of course, find the thought of machismo appealing.

This site was recently posted by someone at MDA. It's interesting that it both sells meat and says it's friends with various crossfit organizations:

[http://www.paleowales.co.uk/friends-of-paleo-wales](http://http://www.paleowales.co.uk/friends-of-paleo-wales)

Flogging yourself silly at a gym is, of course, macho, too.

Perhaps an over-civilized culture that tends towards vegetarianism and thinks that at best a very little meat, especially if there's no blood or bones and you can't see a vein, is partly to blame. A certain sort of person thinks, "Sod that." And some kind of counter-reaction like this is probably to blame for that rather odd bacon meme. Bacon is certainly tasty and "comforting", but it's been vilified so long as notoriously fatty and dangerously nitrate-laden that people probably wish to cock a snook at the nay-sayers.

So I think meat, and a lot of it, probably has a romantic image for some people. Therefore, best to say explicitly that there is an alternative that they ought consider at least X times per week.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T18:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53604>

Cordain's........book was the best paleo book I have read to date. No doubt. But Cordain is a BONE COLLECTOR in his training.......and there in lies why has this blind spot in his research and writings. Kuipers, Cunnane, Crawford, and Tobias all have evolved to a new plateau of understanding. Cordain and Eaton got traction in the paleo community because their idea's are superior to the SAD, no doubt.......but being superior to CW does not imply optimal for homo sapiens.

I am aware of this.......no one else appears to get that in paleo. I am a brain surgeon. I know something about how to build and brain and how it works in detail.......he has not a clue about it and neither does his students who have also written books. This is why their blind spot exists. They are not aware they even have a blind spot truthfully. I say read books from people who know about this blind spot and then come talk to me and see if I am wrong about spot lighting this glaring weakness?

This paleo sphere has zero trouble pointing the finger at modern medicine faults.........but they hate it when a guy like me, points to THEIR blind spots.......and those blind spots show you that the FOUNDATIONAL work of Cordain and Eaton.........maybe built on a lot of faulty assumptions. That is a tough swallow for some of the "current leaders" when your career and lively hood is based upon a meme that may not be 100% correct. **It is only directionally accurate.** My job is to help people.......not sell a book or a theory. My beliefs are based upon what I have uncovered for me, my family and my patients. I am sharing it with the world right here and you get to decide what to do.

The world of modern medicine can be a dangerous place to visit for any human; not because of the physicians, hospitals, corporations, and government rules that are inherently sinister. It is this way because of the people in those groups who refuse to do anything about altering it. They just are not aware of what they do not know and they refuse to look for a better way. I view the current meme of paleo in the same exact light now.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T02:45:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53951>

nice..............because I like this thread so much I am going to give you a preview of a blog coming in about 12 months........

**CPC: 12Autism**

ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms. Introgression from "archaic" hominids (adapted for less demanding social environments) is conjectured as the source of initial intraspecific heterogeneity because strict inclusive fitness does not adequately model the evolution of distinct, copy-number sensitive phenotypes within a freely reproducing population.

Evidence is given of divergent encephalization and brain organization in the Neanderthal (including a ~1520 cc cranial capacity, larger than that of modern humans) to explain the origin of the autism subgroup characterized by abnormal brain growth.

Autism and immune dysfunction are frequently comorbid. This supports an admixture model in light of the recent discovery that MHC alleles (genes linked to immune function, mate selection, neuronal "pruning," etc.) found in most modern human populations come from "archaic" hominids. The manner in which BDNF and melatonin are programmed and de programmed to do this on a nanoscopic level are just being elucidated by modern science.

Mitochondrial dysfunction, differential fetal androgen exposure, lung abnormalities, and hypomethylation/CNV due to hybridization are also presented as evidence of this phenomena...............What we do know now is that we have more control of these diseases than we currently think if we understand how to alter our behavior before the offspring is sprung.

### Michael — 2012-09-25T09:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-54067>

> **Jack Kruse;54626 wrote: nice..............because I like this thread so much I am going to give you a preview of a blog coming in about 12 months........
>
>
>
> CPC: 12Autism**
>
> ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms. Introgression from "archaic" hominids (adapted for less demanding social environments) is conjectured as the source of initial intraspecific heterogeneity ...

OMG. So it's suggested that it's a reversion to something much more archaic.

And this also makes a nice tie to the other thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4177-Interesting-paper-on-DHA](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4177-Interesting-paper-on-DHA)

That makes perfect sense. My understanding is that it's often (always?) linked to an inability to filter things out. There's so much information coming in all the time, via the senses, you can't attend to it all -- or not and get on with any complex task.

So, for example, the autist Stephen Wiltshire, originally written about by Dr. Oliver (Awakenings) Sacks many years ago can "draw Rome from memory":

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jVqRT\_kCOLI\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;jVqRT\_kCOLI\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jVqRT\_kCOLI\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;jVqRT_kCOLI)

There's a woman who's a professor somewhere - autism doesn't always preclude attainment - who checks out livestock situations for farmers. So she can walk a route the animals would follow to a slaughterhouse, and she knows why they will get spooked and stop at a certain place. It will be something quite minor that a normal person would miss - maybe they can see a light at that point on their walk - something like that. The point is said to be that she can see everything, like the animals would do, and doesn't "filter out" what seems to be irrelevant, as we do.

If you're doing what the animals do, it would make sense that that's something archaic.

The GAPS protocol is interesting stuff. But you get the impression reading it that Dr. Campbell-McBride feels that the brains of autists are getting poisoned in real time by waste metabolic products and so on, and if only their guts could be sealed and these substances stop crossing the blood-brain barrier all would be well. She probably doesn't actually say that. She probably says that the term "autism" probably covers different things, and that the symptoms (or some of them) may be reversible for some (but not all) patients.

It does seem more likely that in at least some cases this is something intractable and permanent. It seems intuitively likely, as soon as someone's said it, that this is something much more archaic getting "switched on". And what's going to have switched on the archaic trait? Something environmental, maybe pre-natal, the biggest factor here likely being food choices, though disturbed light-cycles might be another.

It would be the ultimate irony if the conditions of the modern world were causing our offspring to revert to something more archaic.

There is nowhere in the paleosphere where anyone would give you a taster of something to come as interesting as this. Nowhere.

### AKMan — 2012-09-25T22:16:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-54236>

> **Jack Kruse;54626 wrote:** ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms.

From today's news:

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-boosting-natural-marijuana-like-brain-chemicals.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-boosting-natural-marijuana-like-brain-chemicals.html)

American and European scientists have found that increasing natural marijuana-like chemicals in the brain can help correct behavioral issues related to fragile X syndrome, the most common known genetic cause of autism.

Fragile X syndrome is caused by a mutation of the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. People born with it are mentally disabled; generally experience crawling, walking and language delays; tend to avoid eye contact; may be hyperactive or impulsive; and have such notable physical characteristics as an elongated face, flat feet and large ears.

\---so maybe our ancestors smoked pot??

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-29T19:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55238>

> **Michael;54269 wrote:** Some of that is dubious. On page 70 we're told that "man lived and thrived on a diet composed of eighty to ninety percent protein". A surprising claim which the authors look to have taken from Boyd Eaton.
>
>
>
> But by comparison the brain seems curiously absent from Cordain's books.

It seems to me that many use the word protein when they should be using the word "meat". Meat contains protein but protein is not meat. In that context people that ate 80-90% meat would have been getting a good amount of fat and lots of other compounds from the meat. It is unfathomable how the bulk of early man's diet would have consisted of 90% amino acids or am I missing something?

This reminds me of a tv documentary in which archeologists have located one of the very earliest cities on earth. The city was inland but there were fishing folk on the coast who traded sea products for agricultural products grown by the inland folk. I believe it was in South America somewhere.

### AKMan — 2012-09-29T20:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55251>

An article was in our paper last week. It talks about Eskimo Potatoes. There is a plant here in Alaska that extends well into the arctic called H. Alpinium [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eskimo\_potato](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eskimo_potato) that the Eskimos used as food. Apparently this tuber was collected in large numbers and eaten by the Inuit and others when food was scarce.

So maybe the Inuit wasn't all-meat as thought.

"A named place: Troth Yeddha� goes before geographic board next month

Published September 23, 2012

By Jim Kari Fairbanks Daily News Miner

Community Perspective For more than 75 years, numerous Athabascan experts from the Tanana Valley, such as Moses Charlie, Peter John, Howard Luke and Evelyn Alexander, have proudly shared the place name for the ridge that underlies the University of Alaska Fairbanks campus.

Troth Yeddha�, or �Indian potato ridge,� refers to Hedysarum alpinum, a plant with an edible root that is commonly found in Alaska stream beds. This is the only indigenous vegetable with food value for Alaska�s Athabascan people."

# Thread: Pls hack my thyroid labs...

### DarleenMB — 2012-09-23T00:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53494>

Just got back from a trip to Colorado to find my lab results in my inbox. Any thoughts?

TSH 1.460 uIU/ml **Reference Interval (RI)** = 0.450=4.5000

T4 7.5 ug/dL ; RI= 4.5-12.0

T3 uptake 31%; RI = 24-39

Free Thyroxine INdex 2.3; RI 1.2-4.9

T4, Free (direct) 1.11 ng/dL; RI = 0.82-1.77

Reverse T3, serum 24.6 ng/dL, RI = 13.5-34.2

T3 95 ng/dL; RI = 71-180

T3, Free, Serum 2.4 pg/mL; RI = 2.0-4.4

Now according to one site I looked at my free T3 "should" be 3.5 to 4.2 and my RT3 should be less than 16.

But I look at those reference intervals and am scratching my head. My cortisol results were:

AM 6.84 (ref range 18-35)

PM 0.44 (ref range 0.9-9.2)

And those numbers are all I have at the moment via phone from my compounding pharmacist who got them in an email from the lab. He did say there was a note the values are LOW (really?!) but that in '07 they were lower than that.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-23T00:25:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53501>

Your ft3 to rt3 ratio is 10 and needs to be over 20; this means no t3 in your cells. Your ft4 is kinda low too. Looks like you have a low cortisol issue to deal with. Need to see that morning sun as soon as it rises and soak it up and let your body know it is am.

### DarleenMB — 2012-09-23T00:45:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53503>

### vkiernan — 2012-09-23T02:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pls-hack-my-thyroid-labs.3831/post-53520>

Jack's blogs mainly. Go to StopTheThyroidMadness.com and read, read and read some more. [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) You can put your own numbers in and get your ratio. Make sure you choose the correct u/m otherwise your ratio won't be correct. This would be a great place for you to learn a lot about the thyroid. Also read Jack's Hormone 101 blog. [http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/](http://http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/)

Glad to hear you are getting the morning sun. Jack says most adrenal issues are in the brain and not in the adrenals themselves.
