I am not a doctor.

My view is that at this time, you should leave your thyroid hormones be, and work on improvement to your steroid hormone panel.

Among most doctors and people frequenting boards you will find more (moral) support to directly increasing your poor cortisol levels. (I (mostly) do not support this approach, because the is much better way.)

You already doing a lot by minding what dr J preaches,

that is

circadian rhythm via light/dark management and proper eating,

but

you are still missing on hormone testing and corrections.

I do not recall your age, but you already have Medicare.

That solves many financial obstacles for you (until this election) and possibly after elections (if you vote right).

There is more, but you can have very good start if your doc will write for you a series of scripts as below.

Results from those tests plus proper supplementation to incrementally correct deficiencies should make lots of improvement to your Steroid Hormone Panel.

To be done at LabCorp

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

3 ------- 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum (Free Cortisol, Serum Cortisol, CBG)

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

11 ------- Androstenedione, LCMS--Test Number: 004705 CPT Code: 82157

12 ------- Testosterone, Total, Serum Test Number: 004226 CPT Code: 84403

13 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound--Test Number: 143255 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

14 ------- SHBG

15 ------- Dihydrotestosterone (DHT)--Test Number: 500142 CPT Code: 82651

16 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

17 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

18 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

19 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

............................................................

Salivette tubes (item # 51.1532) and

specialized patient collection kits

(item # 38330G)

255.3 Other corticoadrenal overactivity

255.8 Other specified disorders of adrenal glands

255.4 Corticoadrenal insufficiency

You do not have to that that cortisol test now.

Now we know already, it sucks.

Do that test when you are closer to acceptable levels.

//

Real Hummers run on diesel.

.

..

# Thread: Optimal Bribery!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-23T00:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53504>

I read sometime ago, that despite CW, bribery really works for teaching kids things.

So, I had that in mind this afternoon when. . .

I finished by cold bath, advised my son it was time for him to hit the Cold Tub (last time he did 10mins all on his own).

He wasn't really in the CT mode, wanted to quit and not sit down after putting his feet in.

So, I bribed. For each Minute you stay (up to a max of 15) you can have free choice on the ipad. Sitting outside after the bath.

he's listening to an audiobook, planning his junk ipading, and gettin some 65 degree CT.

Winter is coming. . .

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T00:50:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53505>

yeah what works for dogs works for kids........ and husbands.......

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-23T01:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53510>

some days we use bribery to get the kids to eat dinner.... some days its bribery to clean up their toys... when they're 4 & 2... what you bribe them w/is different... these days its the promise of grilled squid for lunch hehehehehehe

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T01:37:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53511>

[http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf](http://http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf)

LOL

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-23T02:05:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53518>

> **Jack Kruse;54153 wrote:** [http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf](http://http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf)
>
>
>
>
>
> LOL

Heh, I'm considering the positive physical benefits of CT as the type of reward that could be considered self driven. . meaning he will feel there is benefit in the action.

Besides, last time I checked it was a bad move to hold your 6 yr old in a tub of cold water against his will

### diane — 2012-09-23T16:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53586>

Interesting - I've never believed that bribery works long term. Rewards - yes, but not every time, change it up and they need to be tied to intrinsic motivators. The issue with bribes is that there is always the choice to take the bigger and better bribe - or to negotiate for more.

I don't have kids, but have dogs. And I don't train my dogs with treats. It may take longer to actually figure out what makes your dog tick, but once you do and you connect with that - the results and bond are amazing. Then the learning part is easy.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-23T20:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53653>

I learned quite a while that rewarding kids for chores, cleaning up, etc, produced a horrible result, and horrible atmosphere. Everyone pits against each other, nobody does an ounce more than his "share," and every bit that I want them to do is like pulling teeth. Everyone screams about the unfairness of the whole thing while it's ongoing, and finally, I feel like dumping their reward on their heads! And this is when everyone gets a "reward." Ugh!!!

I do have expectations though. Like if they want to watch their TV show after dinner (this will need to move to earlier now that it's getting dark earlier), they need to have completed their music practice (lessons they all voluntarily have asked to have on instruments they picked). And if they want to continue with the lessons, they are responsible for completing the practising (20 min/day is the sweet spot for my age bracket). Another good one that works really well in my house has to do with making lunches for school. If you don't participate in the lunch making, you will not have a lunch the next day!! It's tough to follow-through with it though. I had to 2 weeks ago for my six year old (compromised and sent her with a can of fish only). I don't like to think of it as punishment, just "natural consequences."

Back to CT. I find that if I leave the bathtub full, they will often times partake, even compete with each other!!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-23T21:03:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53665>

hehe clearly I'm the mercenary mom! I have no qualms about it as far as CT goes. In reading through these other posts - I guess we have established clear "expectations" for most things. . .and don't really bribe in general, but for some reason this one feels like the good outweighs the bad! In all kinds of educational studies - they HAVE found that learning for a particular task is improved with an appropriate reward. I realize it does not speak to overall behavior and what that sets the person up for later in life. . . .

I'm going with it on this one as I see the reward (outcomes of CT) to have both components (external/internal) of a reward. He clearly still feels a sense of accomplishment, knowing that he CAN do it (bragging to daddy after that I DID IT!). . .Your cautions are noted however , and I will for sure be watching out for that.

I am going to use random reward though, I would never directly reward every time. As I think I've said before, it's probably a very good thing I never got myself in a postion to experiment on people. I'm sure I would be breaking all kinds of laws of state as well as of humanity.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-24T06:37:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53751>

> **Cavemam;54146 wrote:** I read sometime ago, that despite CW, bribery really works for teaching kids things.
>
>
>
> So, I had that in mind this afternoon when. . .
>
>
>
> I finished by cold bath, advised my son it was time for him to hit the Cold Tub (last time he did 10mins all on his own).
>
>
>
> He wasn't really in the CT mode, wanted to quit and not sit down after putting his feet in.
>
>
>
> So, I bribed. For each Minute you stay (up to a max of 15) you can have free choice on the ipad. Sitting outside after the bath.
>
>
>
> he's listening to an audiobook, planning his junk ipading, and gettin some 65 degree CT.
>
>
>
> Winter is coming. . .

awesome idea! I'm totally using this with my 8 year old. We really limit media time, so this might work well. When we first started CT in the pool last spring he thought it was fun and novel, but after a couple times decided he would rather not be cold. Hubby and I CT'ed in the pool yesterday at 63 degrees and I couldn't get him interested at all!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-24T18:15:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53808>

> **Trisha;54409 wrote:** awesome idea! I'm totally using this with my 8 year old. We really limit media time, so this might work well. When we first started CT in the pool last spring he thought it was fun and novel, but after a couple times decided he would rather not be cold. Hubby and I CT'ed in the pool yesterday at 63 degrees and I couldn't get him interested at all!

That was my experience, he would be in the right frame of mind for it about 1X every two weeks. . .and now the whole family is CTing, we run one bath, then take turns so I want him doing it when it's "cold bath time".

At first it was fun, and we enjoyed the game of timing how long he stayed - and I would stay in there cheering him on, but once we hit 10 mins, he had zero interest in continuing. I totaly get how bribes can be a negative - but I'm going to experiment with this one.

So we limit media too. He gets 1/2 hr a day of media, and that is when he cooperates and willingly participates in "being part of the family" (aka chores).

So as a practical "stay at home working mother" thing, I work from home and he only goes to school 3 hrs day - so when I have phone calls that can't have a noisy boy in the background, he gets the ipad and can do freeplay (usually spongebob or angrybirds).

I don't like that, but it's our little routine and it keeps me home with him instead of at an office.

# Thread: Hard to understand
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-09-23T15:58:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53580>

A friend of mine, suddenly dropped dead at 56 yrs old. That is so hard to understand. He was always positive, friendly, in a good shape, active full life, no health problems.

He was the last one I would ever expect this to happen. There are so many others I know that are good candidates but not him.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T17:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53597>

I dont know why this is hard to understand..........time is not promised and nothing is guaranteed......we all know it but when it happens and we see it it shocks us........it should not. It should motivate us to live in the now, not in the past or the present. I see people die every week in the prime of their life..........maybe that is why I look at it differently than you.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T19:57:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53635>

A family member's work partner got a new job. He had a "beer belly" as the only indication of disease and switched to nightshift in the new job. 2 weeks later he suddenly dropped dead.

Needless to say, this family member is now not only listen to my never ending monologues about optimal health and the importance of \*light cycles\* but asking quite a few questions and coming up with surprising suggestions on how to improve his state of health. He has a beer belly.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-23T20:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53640>

I remember a chapter title from a book I read a long time ago. **_Death as an Advisor_**

My dad died when I was 22 of stomach cancer. No one had died in my family before that. I now try to use death as an advisor. It's sad when someone dies an early death. Hopefully that will bring "life awareness" to those around them.

I'm sorry about your friend Destiny. Always hard to have happen around us. I'm glad you are helping yourself and setting the example to those around you that striving for optimal is a better way to live.

### Destiny — 2012-09-23T20:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53642>

> **Jack Kruse;54239 wrote:** I dont know why this is hard to understand..........time is not promised and nothing is guaranteed......we all know it but when it happens and we see it it shocks us........it should not. It should motivate us to live in the now, not in the past or the present. I see people die every week in the prime of their life..........maybe that is why I look at it differently than you.

I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-23T20:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53645>

> **Destiny;54284 wrote:** I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

This is why I now believe in the labs. They don't lie. A person that looks good and feels good on the outside may not be that way on the inside. I had a friend drop dead in his sleep (ok I guess he didn't drop but dead nonetheless) at 42, the _picture_ of health. Yah, I guess not too healthy.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T20:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53647>

> **Destiny;54284 wrote:** I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

there is a lot of people who think I am the picture of perfect health.... when I touch on having health issues their eyes get real big and the unavoidable answer is "but you look so healthy and I KNOW you eat SO healthy??? that can't be true."

### Destiny — 2012-09-23T20:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53650>

> **Patty Cakes;54282 wrote: I remember a chapter title from a book I read a long time ago. _Death as an Advisor_**
>
>
>
> My dad died when I was 22 of stomach cancer. No one had died in my family before that. I now try to use death as an advisor. It's sad when someone dies an early death. Hopefully that will bring "life awareness" to those around them.
>
>
>
> I'm sorry about your friend Destiny. Always hard to have happen around us. I'm glad you are helping yourself and setting the example to those around you that striving for optimal is a better way to live.

It must have been really a lot to deal with when you were only 22 yr old............ I like the "life awareness" statement.

### debbiemikus — 2012-09-24T00:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53700>

Destiny,

My condolences on the loss of your friend, but I do understand how it can happen if we listen to CW. As I mentioned on here a few weeks ago, I asked my husbands cardio how he would know if there were any problems cardiac wise, his answer was to "ask him how he feels". That is why I love this site and JK advice so much. CW are a bunch of dumba...sses. OK, enough of that soapbox.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T04:31:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-56405>

This thread now takes on a whole new meaning for the person who started it..........

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-10-06T02:59:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-56552>

> **Patty Cakes;54282 wrote: I remember a chapter title from a book I read a long time ago. _Death as an Advisor_**
>
>
>
> My dad died when I was 22 of stomach cancer. No one had died in my family before that. I now try to use death as an advisor. It's sad when someone dies an early death. Hopefully that will bring "life awareness" to those around them.
>
>
>
> I'm sorry about your friend Destiny. Always hard to have happen around us. I'm glad you are helping yourself and setting the example to those around you that striving for optimal is a better way to live.

I know this to be true from experience. My first husband died in a car accident at 27, I had just had my 25th birthday and we had a 7 month old son. When someone close to you dies it makes you value the gift of life and realize how important it is to love and care for those around you. There are no guarantees for anyone. I no longer dwell on the what ifs that used to haunt me, but try to savor moments with my family and friends with the awareness that our tomorrow's are limited.

# Thread: Saw this article this AM about early man
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-23T17:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53592>

This was posted in my feed at facebook this morning. I thought it was interesting and placed the knowledge of organized hunting further back in history.

[http://m.guardian.co.uk/science/2012/sep/23/human-hunting-evolution-2million-years?cat=science&type=article](http://http://m.guardian.co.uk/science/2012/sep/23/human-hunting-evolution-2million-years?cat=science&type=article)

To me, the implication is that our species may be even older than 2 million years or the development away from the coastal zones was faster.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T17:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53595>

"What was not clear was the source of that meat." again this article is using the savanna hypothesis as its starting point and perspective. We already know from a physiology standpoint you cant build a human brain from and ape brain eating meat alone..........and there in lies the blind spot. You must look at the problem not as a bone collector ( all the people cited here were or are bone collector's) you need to look at the problem from a brain physiologic perspective to see what makes us different. Evolutionary metabolic neurologic taxonomy is where that problem is solved. Read Cunnane, Crawford etc.........then come talk to me. Lipid neuro chemistry is the missing link for bone collectors. This is why their theories do not add up.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-23T19:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53621>

Thanks Dr., I had forgotten that you had mentioned lipid nuero chemistry development. I just knew it did not ring quite true with what you have been teaching.

### AKMan — 2012-09-23T19:58:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53638>

Up in Northern Alaska, there are stone fences made by the first Inuits to corral caribou into an area they could be slaughtered and then heaped in piles where they would remain frozen for the winter months when food was scarce.

Seems to me, as soon as early modern man realized how smart they were, they started doing things like this which eventually led to our decline. Once we started living together, we had to come up with ways to feed everybody...organized hunts, herding, agriculture. This may have taken us away from our early start by the sea, but homo lived pretty well like this for a long time. As soon as we started grinding wheat, fattening cows, and processing sugar and oil it went to hell pretty fast. Then artificial light and the iPad. The smarter we get the dumberer we is.

So the multi-biliion dollar sports industry uses what since the can't use steroids? Sleep/Cold. We need to sell them some epi-paleo lessons.

"We've had sleep people come in, talking about how important sleep is, how important it is to get eight hours of it," Ryan said Friday. "We bring in these specialists for a reason. I just think it's wise to do that, listening to people with a lot more knowledge than myself about it.

"If we can gain a little advantage, then we're going to look for it." The players received handouts earlier in the week about how sleep helps performance. Meetings and practices were shortened a bit, and the change in the schedule was welcomed by the players."

# Thread: Interesting paper on DHA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-23T20:25:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53654>

Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA): An Ancient Nutrient for the Modern Human Brain

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/)

Among other things:

> The late archaic humans (Neanderthals) sourced protein predominantly from the red meat of wolves, large felids and hyenas. There was no evidence of fresh water aquatic species or marine sources of protein in the bone collagen of the Neanderthal samples. In striking contrast, seafood consumption was a â€œstapleâ€￾ of the diet of the early modern humans (Mid-Upper Paleolithic period). Depending upon geographical region, freshwater or marine sources of protein constituted between 10-50% of the diet for the early modern humans. Freshwater sources occurred along rivers and included fish and/or water fowl and marine sources were coastal and included fish, shellfish and small slow-moving animals such as turtles/tortoises. When the radio isotope values were aggregated, the between-group difference in the values was statistically significant (p = 0.005).

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T20:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53658>

truth bomb alert? Maybe why the Neanderthals died out.........they forgot fish!

### AKMan — 2012-09-23T21:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53672>

From the abstract:

"Modern humans have evolved with a staple source of preformed docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in the diet. An important turning point in human evolution was the discovery of high-quality, easily digested nutrients from coastal seafood and inland freshwater sources. Multi-generational exploitation of seafood by shore-based dwellers coincided with the rapid expansion of grey matter in the cerebral cortex, which characterizes the modern human brain."

Hmmmm....where have we heard that? This is a full-text paper, hard to find these for free. Everybody should read this. Good find!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-24T03:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53722>

> **Jack Kruse;54300 wrote:** truth bomb alert? Maybe why the Neanderthals died out.........they forgot fish!

But they had bigger brains right? How does that tie in?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53726>

Maybe you need to go back and re read the earlier BG post where I point out that in the paleolithic man has lost 130 gms of brain tissue........another fact that Cordain et al........does not realize.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53727>

From BG 2 CAVEMAM:

Another one of my thought experiments comes into focus:

This begs the question, why are we all focusing in on the paleolithic template when the pot of gold maybe before this era when our guts first were naturally selected for leakiness? This made me wonder, was the current version of the paleo diet really the most Optimal diet for humans or do we just merely function better on it when we compare it to a post agricultural diet of today?. In my view, there is an Epi Paleo solution for modern humans where we may even do better than subsisting on a modern paleolithic diet? The reason for this is that we can never heal a leaky gut because a leaky gut is a human trait built in by evolution. This is why the paleo diet works because it limits its leakiness to reasonable levels but I don’t think it does for Optimal levels of functioning. Why? Our brain has been shrinking since the late paleolithic. This tells me we are moving away from optimal.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53728>

BG 2 is so solid.........it's not funny. It is foundational for the entire QUILT

Our past, and homo’s prologue, is fossilized in our modern genome…….not in our bones.

And that story begins in the neurochemistry of our brain's construction.

### Michael — 2012-09-24T08:44:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53760>

>
>

>
>
>
> Here's another interesting quote from Cunnane:
>
>
>
>
> > Despite having somewhat larger brain size subnormal brain function also occurs in autistic children today
>
>
>
> _Survival of the Fattest, p. 287
>
>
>
> The encephalization event happened way before Neanderthals and Homo sapiens existed as separate species.
>
>
>
> Here's the thing: once that explosive brain-growth had happened -- serendipitously, and merely because a few Australopithecines had wandered out of the trees and down to the shoreline (not_ the savannah) where they began eating the food that propelled it -- once that growth had happened the creatures were then dependent_ on those same nutrients. They not only propelled brain-growth; they maintained brain-health.
>
>
>
> "This may have kept early humans tethered to the water in central Africa where there was a constant food source of DHA," says professor Floyd Chilton.

If you've got a Ferrari, do you put low-octane fuel in its tank?

Somewhere along the line the Neanderthals, or their immediate ancestors, had forgotten about fish and in the end maybe that just did for them.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T15:52:03+0300

### MonteD — 2012-09-24T16:40:58+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T18:13:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53806>

Size is not totally important but what is very important Monte is to know that when homo entered the Paleolithic his brain shrunk......and that has massive implications to Eaton and Cordain's work. It should tell the paleo world that something else maybe important that they are missing.......this is where they end and where my work begins because the reason they are blind to it is because it is a brain story. That is why I am so focused in on this aspect. As a neurosurgeon I have the experience, education, and clinical expertise to understand this nuance better than a paleoanthropologist or a radiologist. This is not minimizing their contributions.......it is merely adding to their work but the current leaders in paleo have decided to make it a more personal battle over dogma. That bothers me a lot. Dogma is what got us into this mess to begin with.

In theory, there is no difference between theory and practice; In practice, there is. I get that and I don't believe the paleo leadership does because for the most part they are not clinicians.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-24T19:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53828>

Thank you so much for posting this, Michael. It may have given me a hint.

"Linoleic acid (LA) and alpha-linolenic acids (ALA) are the two traditionally recognized essential fatty acids. Linoleic acid (LA) was the first to achieve nutritional essentiality status, some 80 years ago \[14\]. It is required for the normal function of the stratum corneum ceramides, which prevent trans-epidermal water loss. Dietary deficiency is characterized by severe scaly dermatosis that is reversible by administration of LA but not ALA \[15\]. Deficiency is not common in the Western diet, which is characterized by excess intake of LA."

I'm going to apply LA on my hand rash I've had since Spring. Rubbing on coconut oil isn't helping, and now the rash has enlarged and progressed to what looks like eczema now. I've been avoiding linoleic acids for almost a couple years now. I have applied olive oil twice today, and the itchiness has abated. I'll continue, and look for some evening primrose oil or grapeseed oil to try topically as well.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-24T19:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53830>

> **Monte;54440 wrote:** Size isn't the only thing that matters... Complexity and efficiency are more important.
>
>
>
> Take a look at the modern curve of cpu progression. It would make sense others follow similar patterns.

Ah, but brains do NOT organize and process info the same way computers do. Sticking to that model is why AI went nowhere. . brains and neural networks and feedback loops is an essential difference.

Size is relevant (we are going on the argument that bigger mammal brains related to intelligence are proof of epi theory). But it sounds like an operation story not a creation story?

Here we are now in the same position as Neanderthals in theory. A big brain that is not being operated properly.

I have a theory, Getting too smart for our own good is simply another form of pandemic to correct an imbalance in the natural order.

Nature says ooops, back a few steps. Need to course correct.

### Michael — 2012-09-24T19:55:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53837>

> **Cavemam;54492 wrote:**
>
> Here we are now in the same position as Neanderthals in theory. A big brain that is not being operated properly.

It looks like we may be.

There's an interesting idea that perhaps the thyroid in Neanderthals was not as good as ours, so that their need for iodine was even higher than ours. I guess that's something that no-one will ever be able to prove, but it's an intriguing thought. Essentially it would mean that having spread into inland areas and stopped eating seafood and food from rivers, the impact of that movement, unknown to them, would be devastating.

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T17:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54121>

Im actually a musician. I play Bassoon (chief), clarinet, bari sax, piano, flute and mallet percussion. I perform with the SNHU Orchestra here in southern NH.

At work I do advanced medical billing, coding and appeals and a very large component requires the use of music to concentrate on each section of my responsibilities. If I am composing an appeal (mostly on my largest claims: neuro, GI, Endo, Derma, etc), I rely on classical music to focus on reading the clinical documentation. Thankfully my clinic experience really does help (Im the only person from school who scored 100% in A&P, hehehehe) with the appeals but often I need classical compositions to analyze and compose the appeals letter. Its very rare that I need an LMN (letter of medical necessity) unless the insurance asks for one.

The easier components of my position are certain genres...

I even have music compiled for when I am not feeling well. Once per month I have a power migraine that 1000mg of advil just numbs it a bit... so Ill listen to meditative music and it truly does help me to remain productive.

On the crazy side, everyone knows me for my 70s and 80s BIG time! LOL!

Interesting. The classical music seems to give you greater access to your analytical and concentration abilities. Maybe there is some sort of association cortex between the auditory system and whatever else is needed in the brain? Allows you to use more of your brain for the task?

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T17:34:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54128>

> **Nonchalant;54828 wrote:** Interesting. The classical music seems to give you greater access to your analytical and concentration abilities. Maybe there is some sort of association cortex between the auditory system and whatever else is needed in the brain? Allows you to use more of your brain for the task?

When I started playing music (we are talking 1984 mind you), learning became so much easier for me. Moving forward from there, listening to music was was a requirement to be capable of studying for my tests/quizes, reading books and pages of lessons, etc. I could not learn anything without it. I could not retain the information and my 'off days', teachers could tell whether or not I studied without the music. But, by incorporating music, I retained materials so much better, that often I did not have to refer back to the books.... When I went for Medical Assisting for both clinical and administrative, I never once referred to my books... took a few notes here and there but was able to retain the information so much faster when there was music in the background playing.... Very weird for me..... but not so much when my daughter also developed the same traits as well. She had a 3.95gpa in high school thanks to using music during her study times, and even was in the band during her freshman year of high school (She had my music director also)...

Right now, I could still recite the last appeal on a pt's account that I did 30 days ago as if I had just composed it this morning... the reasons it originally denied and the departments I had to speak with pertaining to Synvisc-1 and the ramifications BCBS-Massachusetts has with coverage criteria... all because of using music as a tool to get the job done...

Its a really powerful tool

### Michael — 2012-09-25T19:47:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/music.3850/post-54170>

How about some music from the eccentric Australian pianist and composer Percy Grainger?

I'm something of a Grainger fan: there often seems to be an irrepressible mirth and sense of fun in his music. He was once seen walking along a London street, every so often nipping off the pavement and up and down the basement-stairs of the houses in time to a tune that was obviously running in his head.

Besides how could we not honour him here?

> Grainger would sleep naked in the winter with his windows open ... However, this is just the beginning of his sadomasochism.

Here he is playing his own arrangement of the March-Jig from Stanford's Four Irish Dances.

# Thread: AI Food AVoidance Add-On
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-23T22:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53689>

Can we discuss this a bit? In addition to the foods we don't eat because they're not epi-paleo friendly, an individual with an auto-immune condition also avoids egg, all dairy (except ghee), nuts, what else? Are there some foods we avoid temporarily that we may "get back?" What are they? Fruit? How do we know they are safe for us? We test our antibody levels after x months of consuming the food? Watching for more obvious reactions following ingestion I would assume...

But what I really want to gain better insight into is the _why_ of these extra avoidances. What does eating egg (or dairy, or nuts, or whatever no-no) do in body of the auto-immune person that worsens disease? I assume they worsen antibody levels, but how? If there is a blog that I am forgetting, I would love to be redirected.

### LinD — 2012-09-23T23:42:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53698>

For starters:

[http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/)

What is a leaky gut syndrome? It is the complex biochemical reactions that occur in the gut appear to be the genesis of where inflammation initially passes in to our body. We need to realize this and avoid eating the things that cause this inflammation. As I have said for close to five years now this means strict avoidance of omega 6′s, all grains and especially wheat of any kind and very limited fructose (fruit or synthetic sources) The gut associated lymphatic tract (GALT) is the first place where our immune systems interact with the outside world. This occurs right below the intestinal brush border and is our first line of defense. It seems to me that evolution has dictated that this is precisely where the battle between health and disease begin in humans and why our immune system is set up ready on that battle front.

So clearly defense number one is to avoid the dietary sources of inflammation I mentioned above. Many of the low carb diets out there for weight loss completely negate their benefits because they fail to limit omega six fats.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-24T01:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53709>

everybody is so focused on the food with this..... is there other things that will make ABs go up? Other than iodine without enough selenium.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T02:03:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53711>

On the heals of re-reading the leaky gut blog, and at the risk of looking really thick skulled (again), I will ask more:

So, we avoid nuts because of their O6 content. What about once our O3/O6 ratios are improved? Is there something else that makes the nuts a bad source of O6s in the diet? What about eggs makes them inflammatory? I get it that nightshades contain a compound (solanine, if I remember right) that is inflammatory and so will never be a good choice for us. What is in dairy that is inflammatory?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:52:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53729>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53731>

A1 Dairy is not good for any human........if you got Hashi's it can kill you slowly and softly below your ability or your doc ability to even know it. Go read Keith Woodford's book on it. Read up on DPPIV and BCM-7. Then you'll have your answer.

### Lyndra — 2012-09-24T04:06:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53734>

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-24T04:39:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53741>

> **Souldanzer;54363 wrote:** everybody is so focused on the food with this..... is there other things that will make ABs go up? Other than iodine without enough selenium.

I'm not sure if increased oxidation will increase ABs, but as Jack explained to me in consult it will increase inflammation and guess what increases oxidation? Light. Jack says if you have Hashi's and live in N. America you should spend your summers in Australia.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T04:47:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53743>

BG 12 says what? Anything that puts you in the survival pathway ( oxidative pathway) is bad for your health and longevity..........This is not just a dietary story folks.

Each blog gives you a pice of the pie..............You have to tie it together........but the fragments are there for you to examine.

### LinD — 2012-09-24T06:29:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53750>

> **Jack Kruse;54387 wrote:** A1 Dairy is not good for any human........if you got Hashi's it can kill you slowly and softly below your ability or your doc ability to even know it. Go read Keith Woodford's book on it. Read up on DPPIV and BCM-7. Then you'll have your answer.

Part of an Amazon review of Keith Wooford's book;

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1603581022?ie=UTF8&force-full-site=1&ref\_=aw\_bottom\_links](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1603581022?ie=UTF8&force-full-site=1&ref_=aw_bottom_links)

"The danger from A1 milk is in its protein structure. The beta-casein in A1 milk has a structure that allows a short molecule called beta-casomorphin-7, or BCM-7, to split off during digestion. This small protein is the "devil in the milk", and it can cause various problems, ranging from juvenile diabetes, autism, schizophrenia, allergies, to auto-immune diseases such as MS and Parkinson's.

The evidence is quite compelling. The conclusion is that we should not drink A1 cow milk, or eat products made from it."

Dang... And we grew up consuming tons of dairy and cereal. I was doomed. (MS diagnosis, age 28)

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T20:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53865>

In my mind, nuts seem strikingly similar to grains, and I'm wondering why one would be more permissible (the nuts) than the other (the grains)? Carb content?

Why are eggs excluded for auto-immune people? I assume this is another chapter of the leaky gut story. I need to go get that book that was cited somewhere...

### LinD — 2012-09-25T01:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53930>

> Why are eggs excluded for auto-immune people? I assume this is another chapter of the leaky gut story. I need to go get that book that was cited somewhere...

I had NO idea eggs were of my list, but only after eating 3-4/day for about 8 weeks.... and an MS symptom appeared (and still hasn't gone away after close to a year now. This autoimmune no-no of eggs is all over the place, but I never realized it! Look at Robb's book, Cordain, Sisson, .....

Too bad because I really like them.

### chewingthefat — 2012-09-25T20:57:47+0300

### kposs — 2012-10-07T00:01:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-56639>

Hi had the same question after seeing suggestions here about limiting eggs.

### chewingthefat — 2012-10-07T02:55:01+0300

# Thread: September Webinar - EPCOTx Protocol: Re-engineer Your Gut Flora
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/september-webinar-epcotx-protocol-re-engineer-your-gut-flora.3852/>

### tellmisty — 2012-09-24T02:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/september-webinar-epcotx-protocol-re-engineer-your-gut-flora.3852/post-53713>

Hey People,

This month's webinar is going to introduce you to Jack's newest protocol, the EPCOTx Protocol. This protocol is specially-designed to help you reverse leptin resistance and heal your leaky gut, which as you all know, is the foundation for brain health. Leaky gut is basically tied to cellular oxidation and cellular aging. This protocol will help you rebuild your gut flora create the healthiest gut (which will give you the healthiest brain) possible. The EPCOT Protocol works because it helps lowers inflammation, reduce oxidation, and stay in reduced, pro-gestation pathway.

**This protocol is especially effective in helping reverse:

Crohn's

SIBO

Candida, GERD or helicobacter infection

Skin problems

Eating Disorders

Hormone imbalances

Obesity

Infertility

Hashimotos

Dr. Jack will outline the fundamentals of this protocol and give us all very specific instructions on how to tweak our diets to achieve optimal results. We've jam-packed this webinar with a LOT of information you can use \*right now\* to fast-track you to optimal!

Here's the info:**

Title: September Webinar - EPCOTx Protocol: Re-engineer Your Gut Flora

Date: Tuesday, September 25, 2012

Time: 7:00 PM - 8:00 PM CDT

This month, we have pre-recorded the webinar for you and we are trying out a new provider called Spreecast. Spreecast will enable us to do a video Q&A. So this month, watch the webinar before we gather on Tuesday. Dr. Kruse will give a quick overview of EPCOT and we'll spend the hour doing questions and answers.

You may [sign up to be a Karnivore](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=LPZEZUWEWZGKA) to be able to attend the webinar ($48 / mo) or you may [sign up for the webinar only](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=Q49VJVMZNLVZ4) ($38 one-time - you may attend live, but you will not be able to access the recordings).

As always, if you have questions, email me: [misty@JackKruse.com](mailto:misty@JackKruse.com).

See you Tuesday!

Misty

# Thread: I call foul
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-24T03:35:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53723>

I was at a farm today, and watching the chickens. Then I realized - I don't think I've eating chicken since June. Not by design, it wasn't anything I tried doing. Just happened.

I remember the first time Jack said "foul is foul" months ago, I was allll like - oh no he didn't! But here I am cluck free - except for the systematic ingestion of their babies.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-24T03:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53724>

Yep same here. Chicken has no appeal. We still have a bird or 2 in the freezer but I have no desire for them. Might as well thaw them out & cut them up for the dog

What's funny is my grandfather used to call chicken foul. The only fowl he'd eat was pheasant

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53725>

From my FB page: [http://thechart.blogs.cnn.com/2012/09/19/report-worrisome-levels-of-arsenic-in-rice/](http://http://thechart.blogs.cnn.com/2012/09/19/report-worrisome-levels-of-arsenic-in-rice/)

And you thought rice was a "safe" starch according the current paleo meme right? Now FDA says levels of Arsenic in rice is "worrisome." Lung and bladder cancer are potential results from Arsenic toxicity. Rice is grown in water and ground water is the carrier of the Arsenic. How many products are in your house that contain Arsenic? Do you know? Arsenic in feed for poultry results in chicken poop with Arsenic. Then that poop is spread in the water that is used to grow rice. If you read Brain Gut 6 ([http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/)) you would not be eating any fowl.

The world of modern medicine can be a dangerous place to visit for any human; not because of the physicians, hospitals, corporations, and government rules that are inherently sinister. It is this way because of the people in those groups who refuse to do anything about altering it. They just are not aware of what they do not know and they refuse to look for a better way

So ask yourself now what foods in your house contain rice? Baby food for one. Be smart and avoid the "safe starch", rice. When you know better you do better.

### Huck — 2012-09-24T04:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53738>

Are farm ducks OK or just wild ducks? Wild ducks eat seafood, farm ducks don't.

### AKMan — 2012-09-24T07:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53759>

If you get a wild duck, eat it all...meat, gizzard, heart, liver, kidneys, lungs. Put the rest (head, bones, skin, feet) in your crockpot covered in water and make some awesome stock. Wild duck ain't farmed duck!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:17:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53852>

In my house, we have really never been fans of fowl but ate it for variety. We haven't had chicken in so long. Duck I wouldn't mind every now and again but the duck fat is pure heaven! Hey, I wonder if duck fat is sat fat or other.....do you think it would work with shrimp/fish preparation?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53854>

_Duck fat is high in beneficial unsaturated fats...........At 33%, duck fat's saturated fat content isn't terribly low, she points out. In fact, it's on par with chicken fat (about 30% saturated fat) and pork fat (39% saturated fat)._

[http://articles.latimes.com/2011/mar/27/health/la-he-nutrition-duck-fat-20110320](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2011/mar/27/health/la-he-nutrition-duck-fat-20110320)

I'm going to try duck fat with our fishy meal tonight!

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T20:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53857>

Ugh, my mom was OBSESSED with chicken when I was growing up. Of course, she was under the impression that beef and fatty pork would kill you instantly (hyperbole, but still...), and fish was out of the question because she hated to cook, so we had some iteration of boneless, skinless chicken breast seriously like 4 or 5 nights a week for YEARS (and I spent all of those years getting fatter and fatter...hmmm). I do still eat chicken sausage fairly regularly because we like it and it's a good brand with no weird stuff in it, but other than that, I'm quite happy to have basically kissed cluckers goodbye.

### Destiny — 2012-09-25T02:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53947>

We used to eat chicken all the time. I have not made any chicken for a while.

I don't know what possessed me but I made an organic chicken yesterday. We could not finish it.

It did not taste good at all. No more chicken! Not even once in a blue moon.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-25T16:01:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54107>

> **Destiny;54622 wrote:** We used to eat chicken all the time. I have not made any chicken for a while.
>
>
>
> I don't know what possessed me but I made an organic chicken yesterday. We could not finish it.
>
> It did not taste good at all. No more chicken! Not even once in a blue moon.

I feel this way... and for some reason turkey is doing the same thing for me - just the thought of it yuck! NO.DESIRE.TO.EAT.FOWL.MEAT.

I now understand why we had LAMB every year at Christmas instead of turkey. FOWL SUXs. and tastes like crap

### kathylu — 2012-09-25T16:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54110>

Well, I disagree. Maybe it's because of the way I roast it, but I think chicken is pretty tasty! We'll continue to eat it for variety, though I have to admit I don't eat it often. Mostly because there's only so much I can eat in a day and the epi-paleo diet means eating a lot of uncommon foods...so not much room left for anything else.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-25T19:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54152>

My kids used to love chicken and ask for it daily. I really haven't been making it much since I read Jack's statement "fowl is foul." I think I may have cooked it once a month or so since then and I didn't even eat it - just no desire for it. Now that I'm thinking about it, the kids are just not requesting it like they used to either.

I did order a turkey for Thanksgiving back in July so I suppose we'll have that, but I think I'll also come up with some yummy seafood dishes as well so we have a nice variety.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-25T21:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54213>

Had chipotle today and put it on salad out of curiosity. Wasn't tasty, or bad - kind of a bland neutral.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-01T18:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55594>

If you can find REAL chicken you're in for a treat. A real chicken is about 2-3 pounds, slender and tasty beyond belief. What you all have been eating in the past is frankenchicken. And don't tell me they don't use hormones to make 'em bigger. They are tough, flavorless, just nasty.

We had a small family grocer here in town (and how I wish they hadn't closed up shop) who carried the real deal. The first one I ever bought from them I cooked up in our baby rotisserie (which I just realized sounds like it was meant to cook babies in ... it's the actual NAME of the appliance, just to clear things up) and we ate the entire thing in one sitting it was SO good. I've tried doing a frankenchicken in the rotisserie and it's still just plain nasty.

My grandparents raised chickens and we had a fresh one every Sunday.

If you're near a Mennonite colony you might be able to get the real thing. I'm pretty sure that's where Bino's (the grocer) got theirs.

### kathylu — 2012-10-01T19:05:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55598>

So true Darlene! As a child, we had "Sunday chicken dinner" well, every Sunday. We raised them ourselves and there is a far cry difference in texture and flavor when you eat a chicken that can eat it's natural diet, mostly bugs, seeds, grasses and fruit with a little supplemental grain. You also learn exactly where your food comes from, a valuable lesson for everyone.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-01T19:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55609>

I'm a voice of dissent too. I love chicken. I limit it to about once/10 days or so, for variety. We usually have a seafood breakfast, and a leftover lunch (kids pack for school). I almost always find high quality birds (pastured). The meat is far more "developped" for lack of a better word, than the stuff from the grocery stores. That stuff is almost chalk-like in texture. My kids don't really like it either. Just this morning I picked up raw milk (from Jersey cows), and drove past the pastures where the chickens were being pastured under movable coops. Sadly, the man who handles the cows (whom I've known for years) got attacked by one of the bulls two weeks ago, and is in intensive care down in Boston. He is stable, but sustained many injuries. The bull is already slaughtered... I suggested they feed it to him at the hospital lol! Poor man...

# Thread: The real cause of the French Paradox.......is epi-paleo......not paleo.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T04:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-53736>

The current US Institute of Medicine recommendation for ALA intake is 1.6 g/day for men and 1.1 g/day for women, 10% of which can be consumed as EPA + DHA \[33\]. This proportion represents the current mean intake of EPA + DHA in the American diet, approximately 100 mg per day. In most Western countries, the current recommendations for the prevention of coronary heart disease from health bodies are approximately 500 mg per day from EPA + DHA, the equivalent of two oily fish meals per week \[33\]. When nutrients are consumed, either as fortified food or supplements, for the purpose of the prevention and/or treatment of disease, the nutrients are also increasingly known as â€œnutraceuticalsâ€￾ \[34\]. The nutraceutical dosages of EPA and DHA are generally much higher than their physiological or basal levels, which remain largely undetermined. During pregnancy, some recommend 200 mg/day DHA, while intakes up to 3 g per day of DHA have been shown to be safe \[35,36\].

France is the only country where recommendations specifically for DHA are provided by health bodies at 120 mg for men and 100 mg for women per day. A recent survey of 4884 French men and women found that on average this target was far exceeded by estimated intakes of 273 mg/day for men and 226 mg/day for women \[37\]. In addition, the total long chain omega 3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA) intakes in France are in line with the recommended nutraceutical doses for the prevention of heart disease, at an estimated 497 mg/day for men and 400 mg/day in women. The French estimates for preformed DHA (250 mg/day), predominately from seafood, are much higher than estimates from other Western countries, such as 70 mg/day in the US, 90 mg/day in Australia, and 170 mg/day in Germany. These observations may in part contribute towards the â€œFrench Paradoxâ€￾, the lower incidence of heart disease despite the diet rich in saturated fatty acids \[29\].

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-24T04:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-53742>

I just finished reading the whole paper. Great stuff. DHA. The section on neurodevelopment is stunning. Not only is breastfeeding critical, but the mother's DHA intake is also critical.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T04:49:23+0300

Just wait til BG 13 when I begin to teach you about the science of bioenergetics..........more science paleo forgot to study.

# Thread: Hello from Ontario, Canada
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### smarcello@live.com — 2012-09-24T06:24:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-53749>

Hi there,

I've come to Dr. Kruse after being told by a dear family friend about the site and about Dr. Kruse's phenomenal learning site.

I am writing to you concerning my mother, she is 51 years old and has suffered 3 strokes, and 4 TIAs through out her life. Originally, all the doctors were dumbfounded at how such a healthy woman could experience these strokes. The neurologists attributed the strokes to the fact that my mother is a migraine sufferer, and there is a relation between migraines and stroke. After months of appointments she was then told the strokes may have been caused by the "hole in her heart", and after 4 years of searching for the right PFO closure device to help her-- she was able to get the surgery.

We were told in 2010, after the surgery, she must stay on Cholesterol medication and Blood thinners for life, but that she should not experience another attack (if that was the problem).

Since my mother's last stroke in 2008, she was left with a constant throbbing pain in her head-- she describes it as a heart beat pounding in her head...which eventually turns into a headache by the end of each day. She's been unable to concentrate for long periods of time, and has been experiencing migraines even more frequently (a few a month).

Moreover just this month, my mother experienced another TIA. They believe it may not have developed into a stroke because of the blood thinners she's taking. Since the TIA, the last 2 weeks she has had 4 days of debilitating migraines -- days only Tylenol 3's, dark rooms, and sleep will help!

The doctors attributed this last episode to the migraine she was experiencing for 5 consecutive days before the TIA. In my opinion, the diagnosis of having a migraine does not help provide a root cause. There must also be a cause for the migraines then... shouldn't there?

I feel helpless watching her suffer every day.

I feel like we need help now, the doctors we've seen have tried medications after medications to stop her headaches, throbbing, and migraines--- nothing has worked, they caused more negative side effects than anything. We then saw a naturopathic doctor to get detoxed, tested for allergies, Vitamins/Nutrient tests, etc. -- there was a some improvement but the chronic pain remained.

I'm wondering where to go from here? I'm not sure if this is the right place to have come, but I feel good putting my trust in a doctor who is trying to help patients with only positive energy and a true desire to help better the lives of his patients!

Thank you for reading!

~ Stef

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T20:52:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-53870>

You will need to pay attn to the next blog that comes in the monster Brain Gut series......it will begin to open the door to this problem. I would also tell you to get her B12 and B5, B6 levels, and her homocysteine level.

After reviewing all the most current blood work for my mom, those levels have not been tested (except Vitamin B12, two years ago) So I will get in touch with a clinic asap and set up an appointment.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T01:07:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54281>

Stef, glad to have you here. Sorry to hear about your mother's problems. I hope she can talk to Dr. Kruse soon. You might also want to read about the Leptin Reset, especially about controlling exposure to light while the sun is down. Does she watch much TV or is she on a computer much? The bright blue light from these devices, as well as fluorescent and LED lights can aggravate migraines.

### mafixit — 2012-09-26T03:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54327>

I am also here because at 48 years young I have been diagnosed with 'chronic migraine' which causes daily headaches and 24/7 dizziness for the past 1-1/2 years. No one yet has tried to help me figure out the cause of the migraines - they only want me to take everything from calcium channel blockers to anti-depressants to Botox to 'control' them. Most of the doctors I have seen have suggested this is 'hormonal' but have not given me any ways to correct whatever imbalance is lurking in my system, until here and now. I have seen Mayo Clinic PCP's, Neurologists, ENTs, etc.

I don't want to 'control' these migraines, I want to be rid of them. So here I am, at the suggestion of my chiropractor. I am getting together my suggested labs and have hopes that we will find a root cause of my migraines and dizziness. I have started to read all suggested articles and am frequenting the forums on a daily basis. Fingers are crossed. I am willing to do what it takes.

My thoughts are with your mom. You are a special daughter for taking such a deep interest and caring so much to come here.

Karen from Arizona

### smarcello@live.com — 2012-09-26T06:06:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54355>

Thank you for your reply.

I find it so interesting that you brought up the blue light from all of the LED lights and TVs, I was just given that advice recently. Thank you for pointing right to the article that I can read more about it.

... and yes, unfortunately, the TV becomes her "relaxing" tool when she gets headaches --- perhaps not such a good idea it seems...

### smarcello@live.com — 2012-09-26T06:13:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54357>

Karen, I'm so sorry to hear that! I really do understand your pain and frustration. We've gone through the same process with my mom, all with out real answers.

I hope we can both get to the root of the problems! I wish you the best of luck Karen.

... I'm starting to get to reading all of the articles too! I feel we are at the stage of doing what it takes to feel GOOD AGAIN!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T18:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54465>

> **stefm;55087 wrote:** ... and yes, unfortunately, the TV becomes her "relaxing" tool when she gets headaches --- perhaps not such a good idea it seems...

As a first step, you can reduce the brightness of the TV, and change the colors to a "warmer" setting or turn down the blue a bit. She also might be interested in wearing the orange Uvex brand goggles while watching TV or after dark. They cost about $8 at Amazon. They might make a huge difference for her migraines. And also improve her sleep.

So I looked for a solution.... and found  10 days later no sign of viral infection. Immune system kicked in to full gear.

# Thread: Glutathione
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T20:00:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53838>

So I was just about to hit send on an email to my doc about how glutathione is increased in cold water swimmers, this from an abstract I found on the forum. [Uric acid and glutathione levels during short-term whole body cold exposure](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0891584994900302)

Since glutathione is a major anti-oxidant, I want a lot of it, and this abstract suggests my ocean swims might be helping me make more of it.

But then over to the right I saw links to other articles about cold exposure. Clicking on one or two, I find abstracts of studies suggesting the opposite. For instance, [Chronic cold exposure affects the antioxidant defense system in various rat tissues](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0009898103001712), and others like it that show up on the right.

But it looks like these tests were done without any adaptation. Just plunged rats into cold and kept them there for a while. So adaptation is the key to resisting the negative effects and getting the positive ones, right?

Also, rat studies. I know Dr. Kruse has said rats do not equal humans. Does anyone have other tests about cold adaptation improving glutathione production in humans? Now I'm curious. Thanks.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T22:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53889>

I got it right. In [this article](http://http://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736\(05\)76723-4/fulltext), the conclusion is that acute cold immersion produces different effects than regular cold water swimming. "These findings indicate that glutathione metabolism and function might be impaired during acute cold water immersion but can be preserved during chronic or repeat immersion." Not only preserved, but improved, according to the other study.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T22:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53891>

I just saw this.......I think CT 4 and 6 answer this. Chronic adaptation is the key to success for inflammation control and optimal performance as time evolves. This will be tangentally hit in BG 13.

### freesia — 2012-09-24T22:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53895>

> **Rosekelly;54559 wrote:** I got it right. In [this article](http://http://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736\(05\)76723-4/fulltext), the conclusion is that acute cold immersion produces different effects than regular cold water swimming. "These findings indicate that glutathione metabolism and function might be impaired during acute cold water immersion but can be preserved during chronic or repeat immersion." Not only preserved, but improved, according to the other study.

This probably helps explain why I found CT challenging the first couple of weeks then it just got sweeter and sweeter - like the seasoned CTers told me it would...and I couldn't imagine!

PS Chronic adaptation. So worth it!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-25T00:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53922>

I like that phrase...._Chronic Adaptation._

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T03:06:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53956>

Patty I hope you remember this when you read BG 13 for the first time.........it will be another massive part of the Quilt. Honestly BG is the masterpiece work in the Quilt.......it is what separates us from all other mammals.......and explains why our biology differs from all others.

### freesia — 2012-09-25T03:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53965>

Here are some stats on human cells and cold adaptation (apologies if posted before)..

It's all fascinating to me

I had HBOT therapy in the past and experienced upregulations in some of the same enzymes.

Proteomic analysis of endothelial cold-adaptation. Michael AJ Zieger, Mahesh P Gupta and Mu Wang.

[http://rd.springer.com/static-content/0.5340/pdf/572/art%253A10.1186%252F1471-2164-12-630.pdf?token=1348532036867--c3a702c301631e83877e1590ae79e7e9ab4dbef7afb6cf877abc45c050f40b347aad6adb80a3b295b87b06260de554d459c1b1f230db0983314b9d62a33d5202&doi=10.1186%2F1471-2164-12-630&contentType=article](http://http://rd.springer.com/static-content/0.5340/pdf/572/art%253A10.1186%252F1471-2164-12-630.pdf?token=1348532036867--c3a702c301631e83877e1590ae79e7e9ab4dbef7afb6cf877abc45c050f40b347aad6adb80a3b295b87b06260de554d459c1b1f230db0983314b9d62a33d5202&doi=10.1186%2F1471-2164-12-630&contentType=article)

I think there is another possibility. Most are reporting that it is with latter babies that this is a problem. Somewhere on this forum Dr K talked about how subsequent pregnancies deplete women of DHA and if they aren't eating the right diet (and matching circadian cycles, etc). In other words, if we don't refuel and refeed our bodies, our own health suffers. I would guess that it takes high levels of immunity and health to breastfeed optimally. We know the quality of our milk is effected. And it may even be a dual-directional problem... our milk is not up to scratch therefore our babies sleep more poorly and need more milk which isn't up to scratch. Also, if our DHA starts to be trashed (along with our hormone panel, as MamaGrok points out) then our inflammation rises. Higher inflammation means our 'tolerance' for a lot of things goes down.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-26T06:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54367>

\[h=1\]Oxytocin and social affiliation in humans\[/h\]

-   [Ruth Feldman](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0018506X12000098#)

-   Department of Psychology and the Gonda Brain Sciences Center, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel
    Abstract

A conceptual model detailing the process of _bio-behavioral synchrony_ between the online physiological and behavioral responses of attachment partners during social contact is presented as a theoretical and empirical framework for the study of affiliative bonds. Guided by an ethological behavior-based approach, we suggest that micro-level social behaviors in the gaze, vocal, affective, and touch modalities are dynamically integrated with online physiological processes and hormonal response to create dyad-specific affiliations. Studies across multiple attachments throughout life are presented and demonstrate that the extendedoxytocin (OT) system provides the neurohormonal substrate for parental, romantic, and filial attachment in humans; that the three prototypes of affiliation are expressed in similar constellations of social behavior; and that OT is stable over time within individuals, is mutually-influencing among partners, and that mechanisms of cross-generation and inter-couple transmission relate to coordinated social behavior. Research showing links between peripheral and genetic markers of OT with concurrent parenting and memories of parental care; between administration of OT to parent and infant's physiological readiness for social engagement; and between neuropeptides and the online synchrony of maternal and paternal brain response in social-cognitive and empathy networks support the hypothesis that human attachment develops within the matrix of biological attunement and close behavioral synchrony. The findings have conceptual implications for the study of inter-subjectivity as well as translational implications for the treatment of social disorders originating in early childhood, such as autism spectrum disorders, or those associated with disruptions to early bonding, such as postpartum depression or child abuse and neglect. This article is part of a Special Issue entitledOxytocin, Vasopressin, and Social Behavior.

* * *

\[h=4\]Highlights\[/h\]►A conceptual model of bio-behavioral synchrony is proposed for the study of human affiliation. ►Affiliation is expressed by unique physiology and behavior synchronized online among partners. ►OT supports the three prototypical social bonds in humans— parental, pair, and filial. ►Brain, genetic, and hormonal markers of OT interact with behavior to support bonding across life.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-26T16:04:12+0300

Also, if our DHA starts to be trashed (along with our hormone panel, as MamaGrok points out) then our inflammation rises. Higher inflammation means our 'tolerance' for a lot of things goes down.

In my case, my fifth baby found me giving the best milk to date (not fat-deficient, and the proof was in the teeth that didn't rot out like all the other babies' had!), and my omega 3 was higher than ever (after several years of working on the 3:6 ratio). My body was very, very depleted in babies 1-4, but I worked hard for three years before the last conception, with sugar addiction being the main remaining problem (and of course, as I now know, untreated thyroid disease). I think that for me, the hormones are the last remaining bastion of dysfunction after years of working on these issues.

Interestingly, this baby slept like a rock from the beginning. She's the only one who never needed to nurse much at night, which was very fortunate for me, since I couldn't stand it!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-26T18:09:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54444>

I was able to nurse #1 all through my pregnancy with #2, although less and less as the pregnancy progressed. By 20 weeks I couldn't stand more than a minute of it and we were done. I tandem nursed them for a year (no engorgement problems!!), although I only "tolerated" nursing the older child (the latch felt gross, compared to the infant's latch). With the other pregnancies, I had to wean the older child pretty quickly because I absolutely could not stand the feeling of the child nursing, pretty much from the very beginning of the pregnancy. I never had a problem nursing anyone through the night though - and all of them nursed all night long until pretty much until weaning...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-28T00:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54879>

For me, oxytocin comes best in it's natural package. With children, that means within the relationship (just like with partners). In other words, if the relationship is bad, then there is a problem. I am speaking only for myself here. My children are my greatest blessings and my best teachers. They are also my main motivation. If I didn't have them, I know my drive to be healthy, stay healthy and live well would be reduced. If there are times when I start to see my children solely as stressors in my life, I know our relationship (and my mind/heart) needs work. Because I can be crushed by a bus tomorrow and the state of my hsCRP isn't going to matter to my children... but how our relationship was will. Not to say that I'm not working hard on inflammation, thyroid, etc. Just to say that right here, right now is just as important... I don't want to wait until I'm 'all better' to have love in the centre of my life.

### billy — 2014-10-31T08:48:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-145753>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20626426](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20626426)
"this small nine amino acid peptide is involved in a wide variety of physiological and pathological functions such as sexual activity, penile erection, ejaculation, pregnancy, uterus contraction, milk ejection, maternal behavior, osteoporosis, diabetes, cancer, social bonding, and stress, which makes oxytocin and its receptor potential candidates as targets for drug therapy."

Yeah but can if fix my sink?

Exogenous! And this article is 4 years old... I hadn't heard of oxytocin before, this place is pretty cool.
