### Craig Craddock — 2014-12-01T08:31:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-148914>

Hi folks
This is my first post here, but I have been reading and studying Dr. Kruse's work for several years.
Regarding oxytocin, I can share my experiences in using it approximately every other day for the past three years. I obtain 20 iu troches of oxytocin from International AntiAging Systems in UK. I have purchased "smart drugs" from them for 15 years. I use 1/3 to 1/2 of the troche or lozenge when I go to work in the supplement department of the local coop, or when I want to be more at ease socially. I believe that my early overenthusiastic use of oxytocin contributed to or facilitated my having an extramarital affair. This controverted the results of some recent study which suggested that men using oxytocin were less likely to stray from their mates. I am more cautious about how much and with whom I use it now. I do feel at ease and connected with people more-so when I take the oxy. A very good book available from my same source is Passion Sex and Long Life, The Incredible Oxytocin Adventure, by Dr. Thierry Hertoghe. There are cautions and contra-indications, but overall very encouraging. Cost is approx $5.00 per lozenge, or around $100.00 for 24 troches, including shipping from UK.
It's great to get your oxytocin pumped by touch, physical contact, lovemaking, etc., but if you aren't getting it that way, then exogenous supplementation may be worth the turn-on, so that you can feel that warmth and connection, if you have been missing it. I am a bit shy about posting this here, but Dr. Kruse has created a great educational forum with his blogs and webinars, so I am just going to take the risk of coming out of my oxytocin cave, and present this as my small contribution to our colloquy.
FYI: I am 69, in good health, take many herbs and supplements, since I have been in natural foods retail since 1970, living in Ashland, OR for the past 34 years.
If you are interested, here is the website: [http://www.antiaging-systems.com/search/o](http://www.antiaging-systems.com/search/o)
PS: My photo was taken after I took two troches of oxytocin and noticed that my hair was beginning to stand erect, so I banded it and took the selfie in disbelief.
I think that my humor has improved too--more willing to be playful.

# Thread: I received my new set of labs today and thought I would share for educational purpose
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T02:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-53953>

My labs are pretty good but I thought I would share with you one of the things I have learned about my own biology that seems to be unique to me.........for 8 consecutive yrs in Sept my Vitamin D craters suddenly as the light cycle changes. I was 98 in the beginning of August and I had my labs done on Sept 22 and my D has fallen to 55 on 10,000 IU of D3 and 15 mgs of K2.

My DHEA also fell by 50%.........in the same period.

My testosterone also dropped about 15%.......

My IGF-1 is stable.

My ASI is rock solid as usual.........

My gut is humming along and my TSH is pristine and my free T3 is in the top quartile of the range and my pregnenolone remains stable..........

What does this all means for me? It means I drop all fruit and I begin my ketogenic climb in my diet while ramping up my seafood and uping my vitamin D3 levels and the most important move............

I begin daily CT..........and I go deep. This move alone will bring me back to allostasis........at least it has for the last 5 yrs. I'll let you know how I roll on my next lab draw.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-25T22:25:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-54239>

O and I just thought. While Jack is always striving for Optimal but yet stating the importance of minding the seasons and cycles, it's important to remember his past and where he was coming from---broken. He said b/c of his 23andme labs indicates why he should have to alter his D levels even if there might be a natural evolutionary explanation. See perhaps in evolution there is the normal cellular inflammation etc. but maybe for him he needs to intervene since if left alone it could continue to plummet which is NOT in tune with evolution. So yes, he's Optimal but I don't think we should expect him to look like a god or something.

It's all about knowing yourself and for many people this requires a 'reverse' process of past damage. So while using evolution and ancient pathways harnessed by using the cold, it is important to understand that these are tools and a guide to help with that reversal process. It is not exactly possible to just 'copy' literally what ancient humans did...because they did not live in our current conditions with broken-ness. This is why Jack emphasizes to lose one's dogma. I hope my understanding is as good as I feel it is at this point.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T23:01:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-54241>

My intention with that question was different. I'm working on building a picture in my mind on natural vitamin D status/cycles during the different times of the year, disease states, etc. I'm also interested in knowing whether or not he has any patients/edu consult people that he would consider optimal who do not experience that sudden drop in vitamin D at some point in the fall or winter.

I'm also noticing that he is still supplementing vitamin D at pretty high doses. Which could simply be related to spending most of the day inside and not diving for oysters in the ocean all day.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T23:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-54242>

> **Gladina;54944 wrote:** O and I just thought. While Jack is always striving for Optimal but yet stating the importance of minding the seasons and cycles, it's important to remember his past and where he was coming from---broken. He said b/c of his 23andme labs indicates why he should have to alter his D levels even if there might be a natural evolutionary explanation. See perhaps in evolution there is the normal cellular inflammation etc. but maybe for him he needs to intervene since if left alone it could continue to plummet which is NOT in tune with evolution. So yes, he's Optimal but I don't think we should expect him to look like a god or something.
>
>
>
> It's all about knowing yourself and for many people this requires a 'reverse' process of past damage. So while using evolution and ancient pathways harnessed by using the cold, it is important to understand that these are tools and a guide to help with that reversal process. It is not exactly possible to just 'copy' literally what ancient humans did...because they did not live in our current conditions with broken-ness. This is why Jack emphasizes to lose one's dogma. I hope my understanding is as good as I feel it is at this point.

Pretty spot on......

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-09T05:41:56+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-mystery-for-jack-dark-dna.3885/post-53957>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-dark-dna-brain-day-night.html#ajTabs](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-dark-dna-brain-day-night.html#ajTabs)

Long stretches of DNA once considered inert dark matter appear to be uniquely active in a part of the brain known to control the body's 24-hour cycle, according to researchers at the National Institutes of Health.

"We are only beginning to understand how the pineal gland helps maintain the body's 24 hour rhythms."

# Thread: Mastitis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastitis.3890/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-25T03:55:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastitis.3890/post-53986>

Alright, not sure where to put this but I decided to try here. I never had mastitis or any trouble with plugged ducts with my first two kids. But with my twinsies...yowsers, I've had it three times now! The first time was when they were just 6 weeks old. I went to my ob and she gave me an antibiotic. I had my second bout of it just 16 days ago (babiesare now 15 months). I did my research and decided to try mega doses of vit C, D, and a bunch of raw garlic (blech!) before going to the doctor and being given an antibiotic. It worked! Fever subsided, body aches went away, breast healed, all was well. Then just 3 days ago I got it in the OTHER breast! My little cocktail has once again done its magic. Still a bit of soreness in my breast, but I'm sure it will be gone by tomorrow.

SO, there are two reasons I'm writing this. 1) I wanted pass along the info in case you find yourself in a similar situation and old like to avoid antibiotics. 2) I'm wondering if there is a reason I've gotten this twice in just a couple of weeks? I'm. Assuming its simply because I have twins, who up until just about a month ago, were almost exclusively breat fed. they all of a sudden ot interested in food and drastically cut down on nursing. Anything I might be missing? I really don't want to deal with this again. It knocks me out of comission for about two days!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-25T04:18:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastitis.3890/post-54008>

It's the increase in solids. Nurse first then offer solids.

How many times/day are you. Nursing?

Yes the VitC, D3 & garlic does the trick every time.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-25T04:21:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastitis.3890/post-54010>

I don't have a set number of times I nurse, I've always just nursed on demand. I nurse each of them probably 3-4 times during the day and still a couple times at night. The night time nursing is loooooong and drawn out. They prefer for me to not get any sleep

### freesia — 2012-09-25T04:23:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mastitis.3890/post-54013>

Just some endorsement for your cocktail here, I used massive doses of Vit C when I had mastits and duct problems and I managed to avoid antibiotics too.

I got plugged ducts each time the babies dropped demand. My midwide recommended lecithin as a preventive and it seemed to work really well...not sure exactly how. Anyway I was happy not to have to deal with those particular issues again....completely wiped me out!!

Hope you're in the clear now

# Thread: New website goes live at 4 am CT (ish) on Tuesday, September 25
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 51
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tellmisty — 2012-09-25T04:09:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54002>

Hi ya'll,

Okay, we are getting ready to take the new site live tomorrow morning. A few things of note:

This is your LAST CHANCE to [buy consults at the current prices](http://http://jackkruse.com/educational-consults/). Once the new site goes live, the price of consults will depend upon your membership level.

If you want to buy an email lab consult, [you may do so until the new site is live without being a member](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=TM7EZCKMA4MM2). Once the new site is up, Karnivores (new name: Silver Klub) can buy email consults at $99, and Klub members (new name: Gold Klub) get a free email lab consult with their membership.

If you've paid for a Karnivore membership and want to upgrade, there is no way for us to pro-rate your membership, unfortunately, with the old site (this is a PayPal limitation). We are changing this with the new site, though ... we've set up a different merchant solution that will give us some additional flexibility so we can prorate for you if you want to upgrade. Unfortunately, you won't be able to upgrade to the Kruse Klub (Gold Klub) and get the included monthly consult (at $248 monthly). If you want the monthly consult included (best price for consults!), [this is your last chance to upgrade.](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=X3GB45N5XXLPL)

The new site is going to be transitioned over in stages. First, we're putting the new template with a better-organized membership area. Over the course of the next two weeks, you will see content on the site modified and a lot of new content come online. The forums will be transitioned over in the next couple weeks, too; we are going to do our best to link your forum profile with your member login so you will have ONE login instead of two.

If you have any issues with the new site, you will be able to open a support ticket rather than having to email me for help. These tickets will be easier to track.

This process isn't going to be perfect ... we will have to iron out the kinks as we go, so be patient with us! My marketing team is awesome ... they are working so hard on this project and we are stoked about the new functionality we'll have in the new site. We are making a LOT of things a lot easier, working to make content more accessible, etc.

During the webinar tomorrow night, we'll take a few minutes to review the site's functionality with you and answer any questions at the end. At some point, I'll be making a call for feedback, too, so we can get your help in polishing things out.

Looking forward to talking to you guys tomorrow night on the webinar!

Misty

### tellmisty — 2012-09-25T09:31:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54069>

Alright; I don't think launching a new website is Epi-Paleo. ;-) My circadian biology is probably jacked for the next few days ... \*sigh\*

Been working all night with the programmers ... finished everything I could tonight and am going to bed. Thank God for the wonderful guys who are bringing this home ... once we get things moved over, we'll have to start a thread to express our gratitude to them for all their hard work!

I'm building out the Getting Started section tomorrow with Dan ... it's gonna rock. We've been sketching it on paper for weeks; it will come to life tomorrow! All member levels are in, framework is SO much better ... can't wait for you guys to experience it bug-free! Hopefully that will be, well, tomorrow. At least for the first round of roll-outs.

No more consults at current prices, and no more new Kruse Klub registrants until tomorrow ... all have been deleted off the site to make way for the switch. See you on the other side!

> **DarleenMB;55358 wrote:** Wasn't under a rock, dear. I was OUT OF TOWN. thanks for trying to slap me down. Didn't work.

Darlene, I'm not trying to "slap you down," but rather point out that we did our part in communicating the changes. It seem that what you were trying to do is "slap ME down." Whether or not you want to acknowledge it, your post was rude and disrespectful, and it's no way to try to get help. I have answered HUNDREDS of emails over the past week, supporting our members and doing my best for this community. I tend to be very patient with our members and any issues that they're having because I truly want to help, but I'm not going to tolerate rudeness and ingratitude. So take that for what it's worth. No one owes you anything here. This is an I-want-to-be-here community, and you can leave at anytime. Spreading negativity and vitriol is not an option.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-27T14:46:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54725>

> I have answered HUNDREDS of emails over the past week,

Honestly not trying to slap anyone here but if there is that much confusion going on then may I respectfully suggest there is a problem somewhere else then with members' brain fog. I don't know how many emails you usually answer, but I, for one, am witnessing the same questions over and over again being asked.

You can't see what you are not looking for. Maybe there is a blind spot? You can either get upset at the things you don't like or you can welcome them as more knowledge. And knowledge is power as one wise member on here taught me once. There used to be a very relaxed and welcoming spirit on this forum. In the past weeks/months upset and anger has been building. If I were you, I'd be curiously investigating what happened instead of getting distracted by the personal attack you are appear to be perceiving.

Depersonalize. You're attempting to fix a problem, not a person. Leave that part up to Dr K, he's got the required thicker skin for that

### tellmisty — 2012-09-27T17:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54747>

>
>
>
> You can't see what you are not looking for. Maybe there is a blind spot? You can either get upset at the things you don't like or you can welcome them as more knowledge. And knowledge is power as one wise member on here taught me once. There used to be a very relaxed and welcoming spirit on this forum. In the past weeks/months upset and anger has been building. If I were you, I'd be curiously investigating what happened instead of getting distracted by the personal attack you are appear to be perceiving.
>

>
>
>
> Depersonalize. You're attempting to fix a problem, not a person. Leave that part up to Dr K, he's got the required thicker skin for that

Souldanzer, the forums are not a place to air your grievances. Period. None of us on the technical side are monitoring forums. I have made my email address available and get plenty of people emailing. There is nothing, NOTHING you've said on the forums, souldanzer, that we are not aware of and working on fixing. This entire project--the new website--is about addressing all the issues that I've seen and members have reported. It's annoying that you'd insinuate that we need to listen or "be curious." Helloooooo ... new website ...

It is not helpful or useful for people to come on the boards to bitch and complain over and over again. Good constructive feedback sent to me or communicated via support will be addressed. We have communicated openly and consistently all the changes that are coming and we're doing the best we can in the meantime. No one is entitled to anything. If you don't like the experience, you don't have to stick around, and in the meantime, we are revamping everything and doing our best to make it better.

You seem to be suggested that it's okay for people to be rude or disrespectful, that they're "entitled" and we need to tolerate it and "toughen up." Jack nor I need to put on tough skins so that people can be as rude and impatient and disrespectful as they want to be while making their requests. The negativity you are describing does not need to be tolerated by me or Jack or any of the other members.

I understand the frustration, and as PROOF of my understanding and Jack's understanding, we have a new website and programmers working around the clock. If anyone has a problem or issue, open a support ticket or email me.

Also, for anyone having trouble actually getting ON to the blog itself, it looks like the "blog" link in the header on the forums is toast, but all the other ones appear to redirect to the new site. If you're on the forums and you click the "blog" link in the header, you'll just get a bunch of code crap, but if you hit the "Home" link in the header, it will take you to the main [www.jackkruse.com](http://http://www.jackkruse.com) page, where you will (presumably) see blog entries.

Just figured I'd share my experience so to help out any others who may have been having trouble.

# Thread: Help me justify a ASI to my PCP
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-25T04:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54011>

Soooooo,

Will you-all help me figure out HOW to describe to my PCP that I need to get an ASI on top off all these other tests?

The reason in my head is that something is keeping me from converting steroid/cortisol hormones. . thus low D, P, E.

The real thing I need to get a grip on communicating is WHAT knowing the ASI numbers will mean as far as what I do to treat it?

If all the things " to do" to work on this pathway are behavior/food things - there probably isn't much more I could be doing.

I kind of want to know this too. . . the only thing I can think of is that perhaps the Cytomel if my circadian stuff looks off.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T04:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54019>

You don't need him for that. You buy it online. It's never covered by insurance.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T04:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54022>

Tis post makes no sense to me.......you order your own ASi and tell your doc to kiss your ass.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-25T05:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54026>

> **Souldanzer;54701 wrote:** You don't need him for that. You buy it online. It's never covered by insurance.

Hubby's was covered - fep blue (federal employee blue cross). I can't remember the reason. . .but I like to be an expert on these things before having the conversation with Doc.

What's wrong with exploring an if/then scenario? If test comes out to be XXX then what? I'm clearly sub-optimal because I've not seen the light on this. . .

Unless it's only the light (thus cycloset for resettin) that we are talking about here.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-25T05:06:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54027>

> **Jack Kruse;54705 wrote:** Tis post makes no sense to me.......you order your own ASi and tell your doc to kiss your ass.

A great man once told me to question everything. Fortunately I already had that policy, so here I am, questioning everything.

### veerji — 2012-09-25T15:09:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54097>

And saturated fat is low?!? I only cook with CO and uncured bacon grease and use olive oil for salad dressing?

### JanSz — 2012-09-25T17:58:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54132>

Spend $$ on oysters. Have insurance pay for Salivary Cortisol

get more samples, create better profile.

LabCorp

Salivary Cortisol, MS

Test Number: 500179 CPT Code: 82533

eight (8) serial samples

Salivette tubes (item # 51.1532) and

specialized patient collection kits

(item # 38330G)

255.3 Other corticoadrenal overactivity

255.8 Other specified disorders of adrenal glands

255.4 Corticoadrenal insufficiency

\=======================================

//

# Thread: Show me your Labs: Find a Match?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T07:24:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54057>

Is anyone else starting to feel like labs are some of the most personal things to share with others? Lol. I'm feeling very private about my steroid hormone panel..... I have to look at ALL the labs posted on here. I just think it's so cool and tells you so much about a person. Way more than a first date would ever tell you. I think Match.com needs to add a section for labs and hormone panels. It's like, i can see what you eat, I can see your sex drive, I can see your if you're a stressed out person, .... etc. and you can't lie either. You can write and say a lot of things about yourself... but if your T is in the toilet and your cortisol is in the sky.... I probably don't want to date you no matter how cute you might appear on your profile pic.

Maybe I'll become a matchmaker for singles who uses labs to make the matches, heehee. What do you all think?

### Paradox — 2012-09-25T08:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54061>

Ohhhhh. Now that is such a good idea.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T08:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54063>

Immune systems..... didn't we learn recently that we tend to feel attracted to the people who have an opposing immune system for our own? So we can expand our immunity and pass on to progeny?

How do you test for immune system particulars? Then pick a boy and a girl with opposing ones... Match!  Match the sex drive, too, b/c that'll be a constant source of arguments otherwise

How about gut floras? I think those would be perfect.... we want variety. The more variety girl and boy have together the better. So maybe GENOVA and Metametrix?

I'm getting into this

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T08:20:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54065>

I could create different categories for certain lab results.... then you either want opposing (immune system and gut flora) or similar (hormone panel/sex drive and ASI and food choices).

Maybe look into 23andme to put neanderthals with neanderthals... we don't want to mix species here.

Ok. I really gotta go to bed  Now what happens if one person decides to optimize and the other one doesn't?

### vkiernan — 2012-09-25T14:39:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54085>

Soul, this is why you didn't sleep last night. This is crazy good. You are ahead of your time and I wouldn't doubt if it actually happens some day!

### vlynnb — 2012-09-25T19:40:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54163>

Yes, Soul, your lifestyle must be optimizing your brain to come up with this.

Similar to the reasons my late family physician husband told my kids they would never be able to marry within our small community, he knew too much about every patient, even if they didn't admit to their real lifestyle.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-26T06:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/show-me-your-labs-find-a-match.3899/post-54359>

gah, my brain comes up with all sorts of stuff...... I got a notebook for all the stuff I want to create and do! My problem is making it HAPPEN...... somehow I'm a bit not so useful in that department. How do you bridge the gap from creativity and imagination to reality??

Anyways. Not really planning on doing this  but I find it fascinating. Any couples wanting to submit their poop test results for preliminary data collection? I want it all, your labs, his labs..... look for patterns. If I can find about a hundred of you, I'm all set  I need an eval of how well you get along as well. Or maybe some divorced couples' labs.... now those will be kind of impossible to find.

I should better go back to trading technical analysis patterns instead of people's poop test pattern matching.........lol.

# Thread: What's up with broccoli?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SueC — 2012-09-25T20:02:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/post-54177>

I'm still new here and I'm trying to figure out what's the rap against broccoli and other Brassicaceae family veggies. I know they contain goitrogens that negatively affect the thyroid, but I thought from info I've found elsewhere that goitrogens are disabled by cooking.

So are goitrogens the issue? Anybody know if it's true that cooking disables them? Are there other issues I'm not aware of?

THANKS for any help. I've learned a ton from the members willing to post -- not to mention Jack!

### ealachan — 2012-09-25T20:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/post-54179>

I believe it's only an issue for people with Hashi's, but don't quote me on that one.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T20:37:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/post-54187>

it is a goitrogen......so if you have any form of hypothyroidism you need to discuss with your doc.

### SueC — 2012-09-25T20:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/post-54194>

THANKS MUCH, Dr. Jack!

Anybody know about the cooking issue?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T20:52:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-up-with-broccoli.3905/post-54196>

if you cook them then they are less goitrogenic.

I'm doing about 1/4 teaspoon in my coffee each day now. But, I would like to know what is a minimum dose that would work for some of the benefits of CO? I myself will improve on how much I can tolerate I'm sure. My bf will try to do better with it when I show him the article. But until then, is my small amount benefiting me?

# Thread: CT in the South
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SueC — 2012-09-25T21:09:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54209>

I'm curious. Any of you who consider yourselves cold adapted and live in hot climes? I'm wondering if CT makes you warmer, does it also make you miserably hot is 90 degree summers? I'm a post menopausal woman who still occasionally get a hot flash or two and I'm just wondering about body heat in the wicked temps of summer. (Obviously the benefits outweight the detriments, just wondering.....)

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T21:37:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54216>

No, actually I think I am better able to tolerate the heat now. This past summer several times I'd hear people complain, but it wasn't really bothering me. That is a complete turn-around, because I was always the first to complain.

### Larry — 2012-09-25T21:59:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54225>

> **Nonchalant;54920 wrote:** No, actually I think I am better able to tolerate the heat now. This past summer several times I'd hear people complain, but it wasn't really bothering me. That is a complete turn-around, because I was always the first to complain.

I could not agree more. We had a very hot summer & I took it like it was nothing compared to family and friends.

### Larry — 2012-09-25T22:02:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54226>

> **Nonchalant;54920 wrote:** No, actually I think I am better able to tolerate the heat now. This past summer several times I'd hear people complain, but it wasn't really bothering me. That is a complete turn-around, because I was always the first to complain.

I could not agree more. We had a very hot summer & I took it like it was nothing compared to family and friends.

### SueC — 2012-09-25T23:26:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54248>

Wow! Who'da thunk it? Gives me even more incentive. THANKS!

### Larry — 2012-09-25T23:30:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54249>

I know, it is pretty wild. It is certainly counter intuitive but it signals to me that the body as a whole is working much better.

### Eric — 2012-09-26T04:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54331>

Well, up here in PA for the summer, we were in the 90's, I would blast my A/C on full power, and the thermometer would say it was only 70'.

Even at full blast, at night I had to use thin blankets or sheets, because my trunk was radiating heat. It was nice knowing my metabolism was jacked up. It sucked cause I was like a hot coal!

Now that it's in the 50's out, and I've laid off CT for a bit (only cold showers for a week or two), it's not quite so bad at night.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-26T05:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54342>

I was cold adapted before summer hit, and even though we hit the 90's in mid-May and stayed there till August this year (longest hot summer I can remember), I was far more comfortable than others seemed to be, whether working in the heat or sitting inside in excessive air conditioning.

My thermostat just works better now. It keeps me cool when it's hot out and warm when it's cold.

### Birdy — 2012-09-28T02:09:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-54899>

ct'er in a hot climate and it was a cinch this summer for the most part. I did have trouble the first few weeks of ct, weird temp swings. But another thing, whenever it was super hot, I'd just head for the cold tub and that made things much more comfy for hours afterwards. I feel like I am experiencing summer just like a kid. I used to run around and do everything, whether it was "too hot" or "too cold".

### hazyjane — 2012-09-29T23:37:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-in-the-south.3907/post-55290>

I live in the South and I think I tolerate heat a bit better than I used to.

It was also fun going back up to Michigan and not being at all cold in the lakes up there (the water was warmer than usual, but I still noticed that others would complain they were chilly when the wind kicked up, while I was totally comfortable). I'm excited that my ground water temp is finally dropping again so I can do my cold showers and baths without the hassle of ice.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Latest cholesterol results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/latest-cholesterol-results.3908/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jonnyh — 2012-09-25T21:10:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/latest-cholesterol-results.3908/post-54210>

So I just had my blood tested, did the usual Full blood count, iron, PSA, thyroid function, all come back in range, CRP was 1, waiting to hear the vit D result.

Fasting blood glucose was 5.2(93.6).

My cholesterol is worrying though, the LDL is still high, though since my last test in feb 12 the HDL has gone higher, and trigs have dropped, so not all bad.

Here are my results, with Feb 12 results in brackets:

Total 372(370)

LDL 266(272)

HDL 92(82)

TRIGS 65(77)

Trigs/HDL Ratio 0.70(0.93)

Total/HDL Ratio 4.04(4.48)

LDL/HDL Ratio 2.8(3.31)

I guess on the whole the trend is good, and going in the right direction, but I'm still thinking the LDL is very high.

I eat a paleish style diet, eating more fish lately, here's a rough outline of what I eat:

Salmon, , sardines, mackeral, cod, haddock 4-5 times a week,

beef and lamb once a week,

Lambs Liver once a week,

small sweet potato a day,

1 apple a day,

lots of veg(mainly kale, cabbage, spinach, carrotts),

couple of squares of dark chocolate(85%) a day,

3-4 avocadoes a week,

raw milk kefir everyday(250ml),

2-3 eggs 5 days a week,

2 TBSP of greek yoghurt a day

small amount of cheese(matchbox size) most days

EVOO on most meals

2cans coconut milk a week.

small amount of Kerrygold butter a day

Handful macadamia nuts, 1 brazil nut, small amout pumpkin seeds a day.

Organic double cream some days as a dessert.

Small glass of red wine with evening meal.

1 black coffee a day, 3-4 cups green tea, rest water.

Cinnamon, turmeric on most meals

small amount of maca powder on veggies on evening meal

Supplements:

500mg krill oil, 1g Vit C, 5000iu Vit D, 500mg Mg Citrate daily

Any advice would be appreciated regarding my high LDL, though it has come down a tiny amount, is there anything in my diet that stands out to be 'not good'?

Also anyone any tips to lower LDL?

BTW I lift weights once or twice a week, cycle twice a week, fair amount of walking, so fairly active.

I'm 5'9", 141 lbs right now.

Many thanks

### shilohman — 2012-10-01T20:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/latest-cholesterol-results.3908/post-55634>

> **bigknitwit;56765 wrote:** This is interesting to me. I still do not have a full handle on reading cholesterol/trigs numbers, and context. Why might weight loss result in increased serum LDL?

### shilohman — 2012-10-01T21:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/latest-cholesterol-results.3908/post-55637>

Your HDL is high/optimal. Don't worry about your LDL, your HDL is protecting you from your LDL. Do as JanSz says and eliminate the fish oil supplementation. If you can get an NMR cholesterol test and not the VAP. The NMR will give you a breakdown of your LDL, look for your VLDL values, these are anti-inflammatory.

### Michael — 2012-09-26T09:27:50+0300

Yeah, those animations are really clever. As to the seafood, I guess at that point he was keeping it up his sleeve.

Occasionally there'd be a hint, like when he'd said that a particular low-carb researcher "didn't get it" although he kept citing the Inuit diet. In retrospect I guess "it" was probably that a tremendous amount of that ketogenic diet is seafood-based. Some Inuit did take a fair bit of big game, but fish was a major part of the diet. Of course, they live somewhere cold, and dark in winter, as well ...

And thinking about it now Stefansson, perceptive as he was, didn't see the significance of that - not in Canada nor Iceland. Yes, there was little to no bread available in early-medieval Iceland, but also a heck of a lot of the food was sourced from the _sea_, and maybe that was important, too.

# Thread: what are the signs of hypothermia?  and neoglucogenesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-09-26T01:17:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54285>

this is going to be the first cold thermogenesis through the winter for me. i am all alone.

so i am worried about hypothermia being an issue. jack mentioned it is a win win situation when dealing with cold air and cold baths. makes me feel more comfortable knowing that, but just in case i go overboard without realizing it or body starts feeling to cold, do you know anything about hypothermia creeping up on you.

also neoglucogenesis is something else that i am worried about. with almost no carbs or no carbs at all during the winter, i wonder if this pops up its nasty head on me. the body using protein as a fuel source.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-26T01:24:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54289>

IDK all the symptoms of hypothermia, but I think getting drowsy and disoriented are symptoms and that's why it's so dangerous.

Google it, I'm sure sure you'll come up with more!

### KA24 — 2012-09-26T01:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54290>

Gluconeogenesis is not a BAD thing. It's actually a very good adaptation that the body would die without. However, you have to match your fuel intake with what you expend. Especially if you are doing consistent CT. Without carbs, this means increases in fat. There are many recommendations out there you can find, but it's crucial that you keep your fat intake on the higher side. This won't stop Gluconeogenesis, but it will keep your body from really relying on it. You're correct, you don't want to really be in prolonged states of requiring protein conversion for energy.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-26T01:37:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54293>

Doesn't Jack say to have a meal high in fat before you CT?

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-26T02:01:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54298>

problem with googling is that the info might be wrong or outdated. shivering is one thing it says, i should say strong shivering.

this isnt just about CT, it is dealing with the cold in general. since i wont be using any heating. if things get too bad, i will just start to cover up and get warmer.

what is the limit of cold air temp that you can withstand after being cold adaptive?

jack says you can walk outside with just a regular shirt or t-shirt during the winter in an interview with Sean Croxton/Underground Wellness. it wasnt mentioned how cold is too cold to walk outside with summer clothes.

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T02:05:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54301>

I started out CTing alone as well. Set a time limit and don't exceed it the first few times, like 10 minutes. Take note of how you feel while in cold water and afterwards. Do some thinking games and warm up if you feel you are getting goofy. Two strange, advanced symptoms of hypothermia are 'paradoxial undressing' where the victim sheds all their clothing which worsens the problem, and 'terminal burrowing' also known as the 'hide-and-die' syndrome. ( [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypothermia](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypothermia))

It may be worth it for you to get a digital body-temp thermometer, I have one that you stick in your ear, and chart you temp for a week before undertaking CT. Then compare your temp during and after CT. If you are getting into the 95-96 range, you are in danger of hypothermia. Most see an increase in body temp after a few sessions. If you don't, you may need some advice.

Good luck and report back!

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T02:11:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54305>

> **x\_danny\_x;55026 wrote:** what is the limit of cold air temp that you can withstand after being cold adaptive?
>
>
>
> jack says you can walk outside with just a regular shirt or t-shirt during the winter in an interview with Sean Croxton/Underground Wellness. it wasnt mentioned how cold is too cold to walk outside with summer clothes.

You'll know, and it will be different for everyone. I tell people to just dress warm enough to prevent frostbite. Last year I was able to sit in an ice-filled tub of 33 degree water for an hour. Could have been longer, but gotta have a life, too. I would work outside, shovelling snow or running snow blower wearing only shorts, gloves, boots, hat in temps around -10F for at least an hour, then I'd put on a light jacket and stay out several more hours. Neighbors still think I'm nuts!

Take it slow and play it smart. A lot of this is in the mind.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-26T02:42:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54319>

so the body has to be a constant 98 degree no matter how cold? i was thinking of buying one of the skin thermometer.

i cannot eat coconut oil which is solid. the fat that I have to consume are animal fat and like ghee? what about natural yogurt that is made from milk fat?

### KA24 — 2012-09-26T03:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54326>

The pre-CT meal, from my experience, is optional. It's one of those things that I would try CT with and without it (along with many other things I'd test out) and just roll with it.

### Eric — 2012-09-26T04:05:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54330>

Last winter I put on at least 5lbs of fat.

I like to experiment. Curiosity, basically. And all this fervor over 'intermittent fasting' got the better of me. So I messed with it, and I figured if I'm starving myself for 16hrs a day, I deserved a 'reward' for it. That meant Christmastime junk food. During the feeding window. Also, I was injured, so no exercise. And no CT.

So I put on _at least 5lbs of fat over that month. Maybe more. (I'm 38)

As spring approached, I knew I had to blast it off. I started exercising (power endurance, largely kettlebell work), but it was slow coming. So I decided to go "all out" and reintroduce CT, using the cool fat burner... but at this point, I'd had the working model in my hands (as opposed to the working prototype from the fall before, which while effective, needed improvements). I was gonna do whatever it took to friggen force the fat off LOL!

Anyhow, to "brute force" all that fat off, I used the device on 'hardcore' intensity (full shivering) for 2 full hours at least twice a week. Then I'd take it off, and lounge (ie, game on the computer) for 2-6 hours in the same room. Usually, that meant oscillating back and forth into shivering for the first hour, then sensations of overall chilled coolness for the rest of the session.

On the other days, I'd go to "high Moderate" intensity, ie, just short_ of shivering. So I did full shivering for 2 days a week, then strong goosebumps, bordering on shivering, for the other 3-4 days a week.

Anyhow, over those few months, my waistline dropped nearly 4 inches.

**I don't know if I was ever "hypothermic" or not, but I'm still here to tell you about it! ;D**

ps. if you go and do something dumb, don't blame me!!!

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T05:05:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54341>

> **x\_danny\_x;55047 wrote:** so the body has to be a constant 98 degree no matter how cold? i was thinking of buying one of the skin thermometer.
>
>
>
> i cannot eat coconut oil which is solid. the fat that I have to consume are animal fat and like ghee? what about natural yogurt that is made from milk fat?

That's why it's good to get a baseline before you start. Figure what your normal temp range is, then make sure you don't fall below it. The skin thermometer is good to judge your exposure, but not your core temp. Skin exposure is best at 55 degrees--core needs to be around 98.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-26T05:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54346>

> **AKMan;55071 wrote:** That's why it's good to get a baseline before you start. Figure what your normal temp range is, then make sure you don't fall below it. The skin thermometer is good to judge your exposure, but not your core temp. Skin exposure is best at 55 degrees--core needs to be around 98.

Do some thinking games and warm up if you feel you are getting goofy. Then compare your temp during and after CT. If you are getting into the 95-96 range, you are in danger of hypothermia. Most see an increase in body temp after a few sessions. If you don't, you may need some advice.
>

>
> Good luck and report back!

95-96 rang? My normal body temp is 96.6 and very rarely fluctuates above 97.2. I have seen it go don to 96.1 while not CTing.

### ealachan — 2012-09-26T16:37:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54415>

> **Trisha;55182 wrote:** 95-96 rang? My normal body temp is 96.6 and very rarely fluctuates above 97.2. I have seen it go don to 96.1 while not CTing.

Don't temps that low generally indicate a thyroid issue?

### KA24 — 2012-09-26T16:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54417>

It can, but not always. First off, low body temperature is a relative term. It can differ from person to person, it's more important to know the set point temperature that your body regulates. This can shift from day to day, hour to hour. For most, this is impossible to know for sure unless you collect a lot of data and control many things. There are also other causes. Insulin resistance can be one, but again, that's a very broad diagnosis. Insulin resistance cause by Diabetes Type 2 for instance, would be negative. Insulin resistance, short-term, caused by a Ketogenic cycle in the diet or fasting can be a positive thing. There are also supplements and medication that can cause changes in body temperature. Finally, you have to account for measurement error. I've taken my temperature with different thermometers and using different placement methods and gotten a wide variety of readings. As I said before, with many readings using different methods and times, you may be able to get a better idea of what it is for you, but there is still some margin of error there.

I do think it is smart to monitor body temperature both during CT and outside of CT. I think the same thing about blood glucose. With enough data, I think one can identify to a better degree if there is another problem going on. Nothing is more important than how you feel during and after CT, and what changes you may notice as a result.

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T17:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-signs-of-hypothermia-and-neoglucogenesis.3914/post-54434>

> **Trisha;55182 wrote: 95-96 rang? My normal body temp is 96.6 and very rarely fluctuates above 97.2. I have seen it go don to 96.1 while not CTing.
>
>
> I was just using 95-96 range with the speculation that her normal temp is 98.6.
>
>
>

It can differ from person to person, it's more important to know the set point temperature that your body regulates. This can shift from day to day, hour to hour. For most, this is impossible to know for sure unless you collect a lot of data and control many things. There are also other causes. Insulin resistance can be one, but again, that's a very broad diagnosis. Insulin resistance cause by Diabetes Type 2 for instance, would be negative. Insulin resistance, short-term, caused by a Ketogenic cycle in the diet or fasting can be a positive thing. There are also supplements and medication that can cause changes in body temperature. Finally, you have to account for measurement error. I've taken my temperature with different thermometers and using different placement methods and gotten a wide variety of readings. As I said before, with many readings using different methods and times, you may be able to get a better idea of what it is for you, but there is still some margin of error there.
>
>
>
> I do think it is smart to monitor body temperature both during CT and outside of CT. I think the same thing about blood glucose. With enough data, I think one can identify to a better degree if there is another problem going on. Nothing is more important than how you feel during and after CT, and what changes you may notice as a result.

Good explanation! Thanks!

# Thread: Factor X Smoking Gun--Myelination
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/factor-x-smoking-gun-myelination.3915/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-26T01:30:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/factor-x-smoking-gun-myelination.3915/post-54291>

Jack talks about this quite frequently. This study examined differences in primates and humans concerning brain development. So much for us being 99% identical!

A mixed background group of US researchers has found in studying chimpanzee brains, that development of myelin, the fatty sheath that covers the connections between nerve cells, occurs at a different rate than for humans, and as they write in their paper published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, the differences might account for the greater instance of mental disorders in people than in both chimpanzees and other primates, such as macaques.

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-myelin-differences-chimps-humans.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-myelin-differences-chimps-humans.html)

Human babies are born with almost no myelin at all. Shortly after birth, they have a spurt of sorts, with a lot of developing occurring and continuing on slowly thereafter for some thirty years. Other primates, in contrast, begin developing myelin while still in the womb and it keeps up just until the animal reaches sexual maturity, at which point, it stops completely.

The researchers speculate that the differences in myelin development may explain some of the unique mental abilities of people, as being born with little to no myelin, **allows for more openness to learning from the environment as opposed to relying on information passed on through genes**. They also suggest those same differences might be putting people at more of a risk of developing mental illness though, as the possibility of something going wrong during the process grows greater over longer periods of time.

### marylouise — 2012-09-26T01:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.3916/post-54294>

Hello everyone, I am marylouise. I am 61 years old. I started my new way of eating. March, 2011. I have lost 137.8 pounds, so far. I started off at 300 pounds. Today I weigh 163.8. I would not have lived much longer had I continued to eat refined carbs and processed food. I was on 2 different types of insulin and had to use a go-cart to go shopping. I could not go outside and enjoy Gods beautiful world. I had very limited time left to live. From heart problems to breathing problems.

I need spell-check and it doesn't seem to be here??

I had stopped losing and tried hard to restart weight loss. This went on since March. I have been on LC Induction for one year. Then I tried lowering protein and calories and bamm, I started losing again. On my new woe to date, I have lost 9.8 pounds. I started this new woe 8-21 of this year. And it is fantastic!

I restrict calories and have a UD and a DD. I eat 1.300 calories on my UD and 600 to 650 on my DD. I keep my carbs from around 10 to 25 max. I have started to raise them very slowly. I use a site calculator to record every bite I eat or drink.

I no longer use any aide to control blood glucose

It is a pleasure to met ya'll

I will use my picture as soon as the site will let me.

marylouise

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-26T16:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.3916/post-54407>

I don't think there's any post minimum for an avatar. Just hit your Settings button at the top of the page and search around.

The WOE here is basically

\- lots of seafood & meat (& eggs if no autoimmune disease)

\- SEASONAL veggies & fruits, so your carbs will be highest in the summer, lowest (nothing) in winter

\- dairy is mainly fermented, and only for those with no autoimmune disease and certain other good health markers

\- plenty of coconut oil, animal fats, & cold olive oil

\- exercise is simple - lift heavy things & sprint once in a while, once you're leptin sensitive (there is a nice page somewhere on the blog explaining how to tell this)

\- cold thermogenesis - starts with face dunking in ice water, moves to cold baths. It is MUCH easier than it sounds (almost all of it thought it horrifying and impossible at first) and the benefits are beyond your wildest dreams

Most of us are tackling these things a little bit at a time, so everyone's in a different place. 100% compliance is not required for discussion!

There is no

\- worrying about carbs in non-produce foods

\- obsessing over weight

\- processed food

The main focus is health, health, health. Labs & symptoms help guide our progress. Weight is only one symptom; it is \*not\* the be-all, end-all. However, many of us have lost a LOT of weight along the path.

As you can tell, there are similarities between Atkins & this, b/c Dr. Atkins hit on the truth that carbs play a major role in weight loss. but Atkins alone (just pure low carb, and maybe a focus on avoiding processed food) is unlikely to hit the other health markers we're changing here, everything from thyroid to sexual dysfunction, eczema to degenerative disc disease.

I'm sure I forgot something, lol.

If we get digestion trouble like this from a new food or increase in a particular food.... does that really mean we need to in fact eat more of it so our gut flora will stay varied and in good numbers... kinda like prepared for anything coming in at all times? Could this be seen as a "test" for our own gut flora.... you should be able to eat a great variety of epi-paleo foods without this happening, and if we can, it means we have lots of different bugs in good numbers?

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T19:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veggies-and-gut-troubles.3918/post-54482>

I read that there is about a 2 week lag in the new gut flora building in numbers sufficient for proper digestion. Another problem I see, is that vegetables should come with some bacteria that can take up residence in your gut, however, lots of the veggies we buy at the supermarket have been zapped with radiation and washed with chemicals to remove all bacteria that may make us sick and it keeps the veggies fresh longer. So, the newly introduced veggies may be so sterile that there is no bacteria in it.

I would like to see more discussion on this, ever notice that you can buy a head of lettuce at the store and it will keep for weeks? Pick a head from the garden and it turns brown and soft in a couple days. That's a clear sign it's been irradiated.

I buy organic and most of the veggies I eat really are from my garden right now  Some I wash a little, if the dirt is caked on and visible. The greens I never bother, the herbs even less. Used to feel bad about my lack of washing but I guess that was my primal voice silently directing me

I haven't had that happen with veggies keeping forever from the store? I hate buying from the store b/c things always look to me like they're ready to go to the chickens or compost  I'm spoiled from my garden I think.

If your head of lettuce from your garden goes bad that quickly you might be storing it incorrectly (give it an ice water dip the second you cut it, dry very well, store in airtight container/bag). My garden veggies keep the longest if stored correctly.

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T20:20:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veggies-and-gut-troubles.3918/post-54488>

> **Souldanzer;55256 wrote:** I haven't had that happen with veggies keeping forever from the store? I hate buying from the store b/c things always look to me like they're ready to go to the chickens or compost  I'm spoiled from my garden I think.

Maybe it's a regional thing. The vegetables that get shipped up here to Alaska are probably treated differently than what you guys buy in the states. Back 10 years ago, we couldn't get good veggies here, everything was spoiled as soon as it was on the shelf, now it looks great and lasts forever.

How many know about this?: [http://www.health-alternatives.com/food-supply-report.html](http://http://www.health-alternatives.com/food-supply-report.html)

Food irradiation is a process whereby the food is exposed to high levels of radiation in order to kill insects, bacteria and mold, and make the food last longer on the store shelves. Although the idea of radiating food sounds quite unappetizing to most people, it has been practiced in the USA since the 1960's, when the Food & Drug Administration approved the irradiation of wheat and white potatoes. During the 1980s, the FDA approved petitions for irradiation of spices and seasonings, pork, fresh fruits, and dry or dehydrated substances. Poultry received approval in 1990. The FDA approved irradiation for red meat in 1997.

However, studies have shown that irradiating microorganisms like E. coli and salmonella may give rise to even more dangerous, radiation-resistant strains of bacteria. Under laboratory conditions scientists found that one particular type of bacteria can survive a radiation dose five times what the FDA will allow for beef. In tests, scientists exposed this bacterium to enough radiation to kill a person several thousand times over; the bacteria survived. Before you get a false sense of security from the idea that food irradiation makes food much safer to eat - radiation is completely ineffective against viruses, and does absolutely nothing to clean the food of waste products and other unsanitary matter often left on beef, chicken, and lamb as the result of filthy and inhumane slaughterhouse conditions.

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T14:38:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veggies-and-gut-troubles.3918/post-54724>

Also, youre damn right I went up to that woman and let her know how disgusting it was to see her cough in her hand and then manhandle the oranges. She thought it was perfectly fine where people dont eat the outer rinds.

I told her "ohhhhhh yes they do. Even if they didnt, the minute they cut into that orange with your filthy sick germs, YOU ARE GONNA GET OTHER PEOPLE SICK"... I was so frigging mad.
